1.28: ke-mareh haqeshet asher yihyeh beanav beyom hagashem ken mareh hanog saviv hu mareh demut kevod YHWH: 'Like the appearance of the bow that is in the cloud on the day of rain, so was the appearance of the brightness all around. Such was the appearance of the likeness of the glory of YHWH.' Ezekiel's theophany of the divine chariot-throne (merkabah) is the foundation of Jewish mystical speculation. His careful qualification of language — 'likeness of the glory of YHWH' rather than 'glory of YHWH' — maintains divine transcendence even in the vision. John of Revelation reuses Ezekiel's visionary vocabulary (the four living creatures of Ezek 1 reappear in Rev 4:6-8; the rainbow around the throne in Rev 4:3 echoes Ezek 1:28), grounding the Christological throne-vision in the Ezekielian framework.
1.1: The opening date formula places Ezekiel in 593 BCE (the thirtieth year, probably Ezekiel's own age — the year a Levite was to begin priestly service, Num 4:3). The setting is the Chebar Canal in Babylonia, among the first exiles deported in 597 BCE. The phrase marot elohim (marʾôt ʾĕlōhîm) — 'visions of God' — signals that what follows is not ordinary prophetic speech but direct visual encounter with the divine; the plural marʾôt (visions) is the standard term for visionary experience in later Hebrew prophecy (cf. Dan 8:1; Zech 1:8).
1.2: galut (gālût): 'captivity, exile.' The technical term for the exilic community — those carried away from the land. Ezekiel's call comes in the midst of the exile, not in the land; the divine glory is not confined to Zion but can be encountered at the Chebar River in Babylon. This is theologically radical: YHWH is present with the exiles, not merely with those who remain in the land. The Joiachin deportation (597 BCE) is the reference point — the fifth year of King Jehoiachin's exile.
1.3: yad YHWH (yad Yhwh): 'the hand of YHWH.' The formula for prophetic compulsion — the overpowering divine energy that takes hold of the prophet (cf. 1 Kgs 18:46; Isa 8:11; Jer 15:17). In Ezekiel it appears with particular frequency (1:3; 3:14, 22; 8:1; 37:1; 40:1), marking each major visionary episode. The hand of YHWH functions as both a physical sensation and a metaphor for the divine initiative: Ezekiel does not choose to enter the vision — it seizes him. hayah (hāyâ) 'was/came upon' — the perfect tense in Hebrew here marks a completed decisive event.
1.4: ruach searah (rûaḥ sĕʿārâh): 'stormy wind/whirlwind.' The theophany begins with cosmic storm — the same elemental context as Sinai (Exod 19:16-18) and the Job theophany (Job 38:1). anan gadol (ʿānān gādōl): 'great cloud' — divine cloud always signals the Shekinah presence (Exod 16:10; 1 Kgs 8:10-11). esh mitlaqaqhat (ʾēš mitlaqqaḥat): 'flashing/flickering fire' — the continuous participle suggests ongoing motion, not a single flash. hashmal (ḥašmal): one of the most discussed words in biblical Hebrew; the LXX renders it ēlektron (electrum, a gold-silver alloy) — a gleaming, luminous metal. The Mishnah restricts public teaching of this passage (m. Hagigah 2:1), reflecting the mystery surrounding Ezekiel's throne-vision.
1.5: arba hayyot (ʾarbaʿ ḥayyôt): 'four living creatures.' Hayyot (plural of ḥayyâ, living creature) becomes in Ezekiel the term for the beings supporting the divine chariot-throne; they are identified as cherubim in chapter 10. In John's Apocalypse (Rev 4:6-8) the same four creatures appear surrounding the heavenly throne, picking up Ezekiel's vision directly. The number four represents the four directions — the divine throne encompasses the totality of spatial reality.
1.6: Each creature has four wings — two spread upward meeting above, two covering the body. The four-wing configuration differs from the seraphim of Isaiah 6 (six wings) but shares the function of covering and flight. The wings touching one another above forms the kappōret-like space of the divine presence, recalling the cherubim whose wings overshadowed the ark (Exod 25:20). Ezekiel's beings are thus mobile cherubim — the portable ark-throne of the God who travels to Babylon with his people.
1.7: The feet are described as regel yeshara (regel yĕšārâ): 'straight/single foot' — possibly hooves rather than articulated feet, gleaming like polished bronze (nechoshet qalal, nĕḥōšet qālāl). Bronze/copper is consistently the metal of divine judgment in Ezekiel and Daniel (Dan 10:6; Rev 1:15). The sparkling bronze feet that move without turning sideways represent the directional omnipresence of the divine will.
1.8: Human hands under their wings — yedot adam (yĕdôt ʾādām) — the creatures have both animal/celestial features and human hands, combining heavenly and earthly registers. The four-sided symmetry (hands on all four sides, faces on all four sides) means the divine chariot has no front or back — it operates equally in all directions simultaneously. This is a theological statement: divine action has no preferred direction, no blind spot.
1.9: Their wings touch one another — the connected wings create a unified covering over the expanse between them. When they move they go straight forward without turning (lo yisabu belekhtam, lōʾ yissabbû bĕlektām) — the Hebrew emphasizes directional constancy. The chariot-throne moves in any direction while the creatures themselves maintain their orientation; it is the wheels, not the creatures, that change direction. This engineering-like precision in the description reflects Ezekiel's priestly attention to exact sacred-space geometry.
1.10: The four faces — pene adam (pĕnê ʾādām): human face (front right); pene aryeh (pĕnê ʾaryēh): lion face (right); pene shor (pĕnê šôr): ox face (left); pene nesher (pĕnê nešer): eagle face (back). The four faces represent the four domains of living creatures (human, wild animal, domestic animal, bird) — the divine throne encompasses all creaturely modes of existence. Irenaeus (2nd century) identified these four faces with the four Gospels (human=Matthew, lion=John, ox=Luke, eagle=Mark — or in various orderings), a tradition that shaped medieval Christian iconography of the four evangelists.
1.11: Two wings spread upward and touched another's wings above; two wings covered the body. The spreading wings above form the firmament space (the expanse, rāqîaʿ, mentioned in v. 22-26) while the covering wings preserve modesty — the covering of the divine presence is always paired with disclosure (cf. the seraphim covering faces and feet in Isa 6:2). The creatures' posture enacts both revelation and hiddenness simultaneously.
1.12: Each creature goes straight forward — wherever the spirit goes, they go. ruach (rûaḥ) here means either 'spirit' (animating will) or 'wind' (direction) — the ambiguity is probably intentional. The creatures' motion is Spirit-directed: they do not move by their own initiative but by the ruach's leading. This pictures the total responsiveness of the divine chariot-mechanism to the divine will — no friction, no lag, no resistance.
1.13: Their appearance was like burning coals of fire (like the appearance of torches, legaḥălê ʾēš), flashing up and down among the creatures. Fire moving among the living beings connects to the divine fire that accompanies theophany throughout the OT (burning bush, pillar of fire, Sinai). The fire that 'moves back and forth' (mithallek) is the same participle used for God 'walking' in the garden (Gen 3:8) — the divine presence in perpetual active motion.
1.14: The creatures dart (ratsot, rāṣôʾ) and return (shov, šôb) like the appearance of a flash of lightning (bazaq, bāzāq). The darting-and-returning motion of lightning is the closest natural analogy for the otherworldly speed of the divine chariot-creatures. The darting and returning movement also evokes the rabbinic image of the ma'ase merkabah (chariot work) — the mystical contemplation of this passage that became foundational for Jewish mysticism.
1.15: As I looked at the living creatures, I saw a wheel on the earth beside each living creature — ophan (ʾôp̄ān): wheel. The four wheels beside the four creatures introduce the chariot's mobile infrastructure. arba panav: the four of them — each wheel has four faces (is equidirectional). The wheels are the interface between the heavenly chariot and the earthly surface, enabling movement in any direction.
1.16: The wheels' appearance: ke-ein tarshish (kĕʿên taršîš): 'like chrysolite/beryl' — a yellow-green gemstone (Tarshish stone; Dan 10:6 uses the same). The four wheels all have the same form. ophan betoch ophan (ʾôp̄ān bĕtôk ʾôp̄ān): 'wheel within a wheel' — the most discussed image of the vision. The concentric-wheel structure probably represents a gyroscope-like mechanism enabling movement in any direction without turning. This image became iconic in Jewish mysticism and has been widely noted as an analogy for complex mechanical systems.
1.17: When they moved they went in any of the four directions without turning (lo yisabu belekhtam): the directional freedom of the wheel-within-wheel system. Four-directional omnidirectional movement without pivoting is mechanically impossible for ordinary wheels — the vision describes a supernatural mobility system. The theological point: God's governance of history moves in any direction simultaneously without the limitations of fixed orientation.
1.18: gavoah (gābōah): 'height/loftiness.' The rims of the four wheels were tall and awesome (yira, yîrāʾ, inspiring fear). The rims were full of eyes (einayim, ʿênayim) all around the four of them. Eyes on the rims: the divine chariot's wheels are all-seeing; every movement is watched in all directions simultaneously. In Rev 4:6 the four living creatures are 'full of eyes all around and within,' combining the eyes of the chariot creatures and the eyes of Ezekiel's wheels.
1.19: When the living creatures moved, the wheels moved beside them; when the creatures rose from the earth, the wheels also rose. The total synchrony between living creatures and wheels: the organic beings and the mechanical system move as one unified entity. This synchrony will become the basis for the visionary language of divine-human integration in Ezekiel — the prophet's movements are eventually synchronized with divine instruction in the same way.
1.20: Wherever the ruach wished to go, they went — wherever the ruach went, the wheels went with them, for the ruach of the living creatures was in the wheels. ruach hahayyah beopanim (rûaḥ haḥayyâ bāʾôp̄annîm): 'the ruach of the living creature in the wheels.' The animating ruach pervades both the organic creatures and the mechanical wheels — there is no division between the spiritual and the material in the divine chariot. This anticipates Ezekiel's pneumatology: the ruach that animates the chariot is the same ruach that will animate the valley of dry bones (37:9-14).
1.21: When they rose, the wheels rose; when they stopped, the wheels stopped; when they lifted themselves from the earth, the wheels lifted alongside them. The triple correspondence seals the synchrony described in vv. 19-20. The repetition of the pattern is characteristic of Ezekiel's priestly precision — like the tabernacle instructions of Exodus that repeat specifications in both command and execution forms, Ezekiel repeats the chariot's movement principles to establish their reliability as observable law.
1.22: raqia (rāqîaʿ): 'expanse/firmament.' The same word used for the sky-dome of Gen 1:6-8. qerach hanora (qeraḥ hannôrāʾ): 'awesome crystal/ice' — qeraḥ means frost or ice; the radiant crystal platform stretched over the heads of the living creatures like the icy brightness of the sky. The expanse is simultaneously the floor of the divine throne-platform and the ceiling of the creaturely domain — marking the boundary between the earthly and heavenly realms. The awesome (nôrāʾ) quality emphasizes that this boundary inspires reverent fear.
1.23: Under the expanse the wings of the living creatures stretched toward one another; each creature had two wings covering its body. The structural arrangement is now clarified: wings spread upward touching above the expanse; wings downward covering the body below. The upward-spreading wings that touch above the crystal expanse form the supporting structure for the divine throne; the body-covering wings maintain the creatures' modesty before the divine holiness seated above them.
1.24: The sound of their wings was like the roar of mighty waters (qol mayim rabim, qôl mayim rabbîm), like the voice of the Almighty (qol Shaddai, qôl Šadday), like the sound of an army camp. The triple simile for the sound: (1) the cosmic ocean (waters above; Gen 1:7), (2) El Shaddai's own voice, and (3) a military host. Shaddai as the divine name connects to the patriarchal traditions (Gen 17:1; 28:3); its use here alongside the more characteristic Ezekielian Adonai YHWH gives the sound an archaic patriarchal resonance. John's vision of the living creatures in Rev 4:8 picks up the thunderous sound tradition.
1.25: When they stood still, they lowered their wings — the sound ceases when motion ceases. The dynamic responsiveness of the chariot (sound when moving, silence when stopped) mirrors the pattern of divine communication in Ezekiel: when the hand of YHWH is active, the prophet speaks; when the hand withdraws, the prophet is silent (3:25-27). There was a voice above the expanse (qol mimal laragia) — the threshold between movement-noise and divine speech is the expanse that marks the creaturely-heavenly boundary.
1.26: even sapir (ʾeben sappîr): 'sapphire-like stone.' The lapis lazuli (the actual identification of biblical sappîr) forms the appearance of the throne. The sapphire throne appears in Moses's vision (Exod 24:10: 'under his feet was something like a pavement made of lapis lazuli, clear as the sky') — Ezekiel's vision is a more elaborate and personal encounter with the same throne-reality. demut kisse (dĕmût kissēʾ): 'likeness of a throne' — Ezekiel uses demut (likeness) and mareh (appearance) throughout to maintain appropriate distance from unqualified assertion: he sees the likeness of the throne, not the throne itself. demut kemarat adam: 'likeness like the appearance of a human' — the enthroned form above the sapphire throne looks human. The anthropomorphic divine form will become central to Jewish mysticism (Shiur Qomah literature) and Christian incarnation theology.
1.27: hashmal (ḥašmal) again — from his loins upward, and again downward, surrounded by fire. The bipartite division of the enthroned figure — gleaming metal above, fire below — corresponds to fire both as judgment and as holiness. The brightness (nogah, nōgah) all around: the same word used in v. 4 for the brightness surrounding the initial theophanic cloud. The full circle of light that surrounds the enthroned figure is the divine kavod radiating outward — the glory both conceals and reveals the divine person.

36.26: venathati lachem lev hadash veruach hadasha etten bekirbechem vahashirothi et-lev haeben mivsarchem venatati lachem lev basar: 'And I will give you a new heart and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh.' The new heart-new spirit promise is the Ezekielian new covenant (parallel to Jer 31:31-34). Lev hadash (new heart): the decision-making center (lev) of human personhood is replaced — not repaired, not improved, but new. Ruach hadasha (new spirit): YHWH's own Spirit placed within (v. 27: 'I will put my Spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes'). This is Pentecost prophesied — the Spirit's indwelling that replaces external Torah-motivation with internal Spirit-empowered desire and ability to obey.
36.1: veatah ben-adam hinabbe el-harei yisrael ('son of man, prophesy to the mountains of Israel'): the mountains of Israel are addressed directly as the recipients of the restoration oracle. Ch 36 opens as the counterpart to the ch 6 judgment oracle that was also addressed to the mountains (6:2-3). The mountains that received the sword, famine, and plague of judgment now receive the promise of life and flourishing.
36.2: yaan amar ha'oyev aleichem heach bamot olam ('the enemy has said of you: Aha! the ancient heights have become our possession'): Edom (from ch 35) and other enemies have exulted over Israel's desolated heights. bamot olam ('ancient/eternal high places') — the mountains Israel has inhabited since the patriarchal era. The enemy's gloating ('heach!' = a cry of malicious satisfaction) is the theological occasion for YHWH's response.
36.3: yaan yaan shomate ('because — yes, because you are desolate'): the doubled yaan for emphasis — a construction that intensifies the causal connection. The desolation of the land (from the Babylonian conquest) is real and acknowledged; YHWH's oracle does not minimize it. But the enemy's contemptuous seizure and mockery of Israel's land becomes an affront to YHWH that generates the restoration.
36.4: The comprehensive address: mountains, hills, ravines, valleys, desolate wastes, abandoned cities — the whole topography of ruined Judah is addressed. The oracle covers every part of the landscape, leaving no part of the land excluded from the restoration promise. The specificity of the address (seven categories of terrain) emphasizes the thoroughness of the coming transformation.
36.5: beeesh qin'ati dibbarti ('in the fire of my jealousy I have spoken'): qin'ah (H7068, jealousy/zeal) — YHWH's jealousy is not a weakness but an expression of covenant fidelity. The same qin'ah that executed judgment on Israel for idolatry (5:13; 8:3) now burns against the nations that have seized the covenant land. yaan shim'chat kal-levav uveuzat naphesh legarsh otah — 'because of wholehearted joy and bitter contempt with which they made my land their possession': the malice and contempt (not merely opportunism) of the nations' action triggers the jealous response.
36.6: davari al-admat yisrael ('speak about the land of Israel'): YHWH's jealousy for his land — the land is inseparably connected to his covenant identity. The nations' contempt for Israel is simultaneously contempt for YHWH, since the land is his. This theological point drives the restoration: YHWH acts to vindicate his own reputation, not merely to restore Israel out of sentiment.
36.7: im-lo haggoyim asher-lachem misaviv hemah yesu kalimatam ('the nations around you will themselves bear their disgrace'): the reversal-formula — those who shamed Israel will be shamed. The same vocabulary (kalimah, H3639, shame/disgrace) applied to Israel in exile is redirected to the surrounding nations. YHWH's oath (nesa yadi, 'I have raised my hand') formalizes the reversal.
36.8: veaitem titenu anpechem uvliyitem piriechem ('you mountains will put forth your branches and bear your fruit for my people Israel'): the mountains addressed as living entities bearing fruit — the same personification as in ch 6 (where they were addressed as recipients of judgment). The mountains are commanded to participate in the restoration: ki qerovim lavo ('for they are soon to come'). The returning exiles are the 'soon' — the restoration is not distant.
36.9: ki hineni eleichem upeniti aleichem venirashtem venigraztem ('I will turn to you and you will be tilled and sown'): panah el (H6437, to turn toward/face toward) — YHWH's face turning toward the mountains as the active agent of restoration. Tilling and sowing are the signs of cultivated settlement versus the desolation of abandoned fields. The agricultural renewal is the sign of human return.
36.10: vehirbeiti aleichem adam ('I will multiply people on you'): the reversal of the depopulation caused by exile, sword, and famine. venishvu hecheharim uhivnyu habamot ('the ruined cities will be inhabited and the waste places built'): charubot (H2723, ruins/waste places) repopulated — the archaeological landscape of conquest reversed by divine restoration.
36.11: vehirbeiti aleichem adam uvehemah verebu veparu ('I will multiply people and animals on you; they will increase and be fruitful'): the Genesis 1:28 fertility mandate (peru urevu, 'be fruitful and multiply') applied to the restored land. The restoration recapitulates creation: the fruitfulness that was interrupted by the exile-curse is restarted by YHWH's direct action. kaqdemonichem asiti ('I will make you as in your former state'): restoration to pre-exile conditions, but — as subsequent verses clarify — actually surpassing the former state.
36.12: veholachti aleichem adam et-ami yisrael ('I will bring my people Israel on you'): the possessive construction — ami yisrael ('my people Israel') emphasizes the covenant relationship that the exile appeared to have dissolved. veyerishuch ('they will possess you'): the land-possession verb (yaresh, H3423) that constitutes the fulfillment of the ancestral promises from Num 13-14 through Deut 30:5.
36.13: koh amar adonai YHWH yaan yomerim lach ochelet adam at ('because they say of you: you devour human beings'): the accusation against the land — that it consumes its inhabitants (a pagan understanding of why successive peoples were expelled from Canaan). ocheleth adam — the land as a man-eater, like the slanderous report of the spies (Num 13:32: 'the land devours its inhabitants'). YHWH addresses and denies this accusation about his own land.
36.14: lachen adam lo-tocheeli od uggoyech lo-teshakeli od ('you will no longer devour human beings and no longer bereave your nation'): YHWH's direct rebuttal of the accusation — the land will not 'eat' its inhabitants. The implied theology: the expulsions and deaths were not the land's inherent hostility but YHWH's specific judgments that have now run their course. The land's character is restored alongside the people's restoration.
36.15: velo-ashmi'a elayich od klimat haggoyim ('I will no longer cause you to hear the insults of the nations'): the end of the international shame that accompanied exile. cherpath ammim lo-tis'i od ('you will no longer bear the reproach of the peoples'): cherpah (H2781, reproach/shame) — the same shame that Job mourned and the Psalms cry out against. The restoration removes not just the physical desolation but the shame that accompanied it.
36.16: The shift to the theological explanation of exile (vv.16-21) — the 'because' section before the 'therefore' restoration. YHWH speaks directly: the exile happened because of Israel's conduct and deeds.
36.17: ben-adam beit yisrael yoshvim al-admatam vayetamu'u otah bedarkam uvealilotam ('son of man, when the house of Israel dwelt in their own land, they defiled it by their ways and their deeds'): the historical summary of why the land was given over. ketumah haniddah hayetah darkam lefanai ('their way was before me like the impurity of a menstruous woman'): niddah (H5079, menstrual impurity/isolation period) — the strongest cultic impurity language in the Levitical system (Lev 15:19-30). Israel's conduct was ritually defiling to the land itself. The land as covenant space shared YHWH's holiness and could not tolerate Israel's conduct.
36.18: vaeshpoch chamati aleichem ('I poured out my wrath on them'): shaphach chemah — the standard Ezekielian phrase for divine wrath-execution. al-hadam asher-shapechu al-haaretz ('for the blood they shed on the land'): blood-pollution of the land (Num 35:33: blood pollutes the land) — the legal basis for the exile. The land could not tolerate innocent blood; YHWH executed the legal requirement of expulsion.
36.19: vaeputzem baggoyim vayyizaru baarasot ('I scattered them among the nations and they were dispersed through the countries'): the Deuteronomic curse executed (Deut 4:27; 28:64). The scatter-verb putz (H6327) and zarah (H2219, winnow/scatter) — both agricultural metaphors for the violent dispersal of a community.
36.20: vayavo el-haggoyim asher-bau sham vayehalelu et-shem qodshi ('when they came to the nations where they went, they profaned my holy name'): the remarkable claim — Israel's continued existence as a scattered people was itself a profanation of YHWH's name. chilel shem qodshi (H2490 + H8034 + H6944, profane holy name) — the nations observed exiled Israel and concluded 'YHWH could not protect his people.' The exile that was meant to honor YHWH's justice was being interpreted by the nations as YHWH's weakness or failure.
36.21: vaechamal al-shem qodshi ('I had concern/compassion for my holy name'): chamal (H2550, spare/have compassion/be concerned for) — YHWH's motivation for the restoration is explicitly his concern for his own name's reputation, not Israel's merit. The restoration is an act of theological self-vindication. This is the most theologically direct statement of YHWH's motivation in the entire OT: the nations were getting the wrong message; YHWH acts to correct it.
36.22: lachen emor leveit yisrael koh amar adonai YHWH lo lemankhem ani oseh beit yisrael ki im-leshmi haqadosh ('therefore say to Israel: not for your sake am I acting, house of Israel, but for my holy name'): the explicit statement of YHWH's motivation — emphatically not for Israel's sake but for his own name. lo lemankhem is placed first for maximum emphasis. This is the theological anti-sentimentalism of the OT: the restoration is not sentimental return because YHWH loves Israel; it is YHWH acting to vindicate his own reputation among the nations. Israel is the vehicle, not the ultimate purpose.
36.23: veqiddashti et-shemi hagadol hamechullal baggoyim ('I will sanctify my great name which has been profaned among the nations'): qiddesh (H6942, sanctify/hallow) versus chilel (H2490, profane) — the key antithesis of this oracle. veyadu haggoyim ki-ani YHWH: the recognition formula applied to the nations — the restoration of Israel serves YHWH's self-disclosure to the entire world. behiqadshi bachem leeyneichem ('when I show myself holy in you before their eyes'): YHWH's holiness displayed in Israel's restoration functions as a sermon to the nations about who YHWH is.
36.24: velaqachti etchem min-haggoyim ('I will take you from the nations'): the second Exodus vocabulary — laqach (take/gather) from the nations parallels the first Exodus from Egypt. uqibatzti etchem mikol-haaratzot ('and gather you from all the lands'): the ingathering of the scattered, reversing the diaspora of v.19. The return from Babylon is the near fulfillment; the ultimate fulfillment awaits the complete ingathering.
36.25: vezaraqti aleichem mayim tehorim uteharthem ('I will sprinkle clean water on you and you will be clean'): zaraq (H2236, sprinkle — the priestly sprinkling verb used in sacrificial purification, Lev 1:5; Num 19:13-20; the red heifer waters). The purification of the restored people is accomplished by YHWH himself performing the priestly act of sprinkling. mikol-tum'oteichem umikol-gilluleichem ataher etchem ('from all your impurities and from all your idols I will cleanse you'): comprehensive cleansing covering both ritual impurity (tum'ah) and moral/idolatrous pollution (gillulim).
36.27: vet-ruchi eten beqirbechem ('I will put my Spirit within you'): the interior dimension of the new covenant — the Spirit placed beqerev (within, in the midst of). ruach (H7307, spirit/breath/wind). The new heart of v.26 receives the animating Spirit of v.27. veeth-chuqqotai tishmeiru vaasitem ('I will cause you to walk in my statutes and you will keep my ordinances and do them'): the Spirit's work is specifically defined as enabling Torah-observance. The failure of the old covenant (Israel could not keep the commandments) is addressed by the interior gift of the Spirit who works obedience from within.
36.28: veyashavtem baaretz asher natati laavoteichem ('you will dwell in the land I gave to your fathers'): the land-covenant renewed. vihyitem li le'am vaani ehyeh lachem le'Elohim ('you will be my people and I will be your God'): the covenant formula (H1961 + H5971 + H430) — the most fundamental statement of covenant relationship in the OT (from Lev 26:12 through Jer 31:33; Ezek 11:20; 14:11; Rev 21:3). The covenant threatened and dissolved by the exile is reconstituted under the new heart/Spirit arrangement.
36.29: vehoshati mechal-tum'oteichem ('I will save you from all your uncleanness'): yasha (H3467, save) — the salvation vocabulary applied to the cleansing from impurity. Salvation and purification are linked: the same act that restores the covenant relationship removes the defilement that interrupted it. veqarati el-hadagan vehirbiti oto ('I will call to the grain and multiply it'): YHWH summoning agricultural abundance — the reversal of the covenant curses of famine and blight.
36.30: The restoration of agricultural abundance — fruit, grain, the end of famine. The reversal of the curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28: the land will produce its fruit so that Israel will no longer suffer the disgrace of famine among the nations. The nations that noted Israel's famine as evidence of divine abandonment will see its reversal as evidence of divine restoration.
36.31: uzchartem et-darkeichem hara'im ('you will remember your evil ways'): the remembrance of sin as part of the restoration, not as pre-condition but as consequence. Israel will remember its evil ways and deeds and loathe itself (qot, H6962, feel loathing/disgust) in its own sight. Genuine restoration produces self-abasement, not self-satisfaction. lo-lemaanchem again — 'not for your sake': the restoration does not generate pride in Israel but self-examination and shame.
36.32: lo lemankhem ani oseh ('it is not for your sake that I am acting'): the formula repeated from v.22 — the divine motivation is reasserted at the conclusion of the restoration oracle. The repetition brackets the entire oracle of vv.22-32 with the same theological claim. YHWH acts for his own name; Israel is the beneficiary but not the cause. boshtem vekhalmuni ('be ashamed and humiliated'): shame as the appropriate response to undeserved grace — the restoration should produce not pride but deep shame at the contrast between what Israel deserves and what YHWH gives.
36.33: koh amar adonai YHWH beyom tahhari etchem miqqol avonoteichem ('on the day I cleanse you from all your iniquities'): the day of cleansing as the trigger for the restoration of the cities. YHWH's act of moral cleansing and the act of urban reconstruction are simultaneous — the inner renewal produces the outer restoration. The land's desolation was a consequence of Israel's moral defilement; cleansing produces rebuilding.
36.34: vehaadamah hanneshamah teaved tachath asher haytah shemamah le-eynei kol avar ('the desolate land will be tilled instead of being a desolation in the sight of all who pass by'): the public nature of the restoration — those who passed by and saw the desolation as evidence of divine abandonment will see the tilled land as evidence of divine restoration. The restoration is a visible theological statement to the watching world.
36.35: vaomru haaretz halezut hayeta keGan-Eden ('and they will say: this land that was desolate has become like the garden of Eden'): Gan-Eden (the garden of Eden) as the highest possible standard of agricultural beauty and fertility. The restored land compared to Eden is the ultimate statement of what YHWH intends for the covenant land — not merely pre-exile normalcy but Edenic abundance. The garden destroyed by the Serpent and closed by cherubim is the type for the restoration.
36.36: veyadu haggoyim ki ani YHWH ('the nations will know that I am the LORD'): the recognition formula on the lips of the nations — their observation of Eden-like Israel will produce knowledge of YHWH. The nations learn who YHWH is not through theological argument but through observable transformation of Israel's land. baniti hacharubot natati nezerot ('I YHWH have rebuilt the ruined places and replanted the desolate areas'): YHWH as builder and farmer — the divine craftsman who rebuilds what was demolished and the divine gardener who replants what was uprooted.
36.37: The parabolic comparison: Israel's small population (like a few sheep in Jerusalem's pasture, before the exile) versus the restored great flocks filling the cities. The restored cities compared to the festive sheep-market in Jerusalem before major feasts — dense, noisy, full of life. The desolation reversed into abundance that exceeds the former state.
36.38: veyadu ki-ani YHWH: the chapter closes with the recognition formula for the seventh and final time in ch 36. The seven-fold repetition of yadu ki-ani YHWH in this chapter is structural: the entire oracle is oriented toward the epistemological outcome. Every act of restoration — cleansing, Spirit-giving, agricultural abundance, land rebuilding — serves this single end: that YHWH will be known as YHWH.

6.1: Standard prophetic introduction marking a fresh oracle. The phrase wayehi devar-YHWH elai ('the word of YHWH came to me') is Ezekiel's most frequent commissioning formula, occurring over 50 times in the book. Each occurrence marks the beginning of a discrete divine communication event, not merely an internal impression.
6.2: harim Yisrael ('mountains of Israel'): the mountains are addressed as if persons — a rhetorical device heightening the indictment. The high places on Israel's ridges were the actual sites of illicit worship; addressing the terrain itself implicates the geography of apostasy. Cf. 36:1 where the same mountains receive a word of consolation — structural reversal in the book.
6.3: bamoteichem ('your high places'): bamah (pl. bamot) designates open-air elevated shrines, condemned from Leviticus 17 through Kings. Here YHWH announces that the sword will destroy the very sites where the people worshipped false gods — judgment through the mechanism of defeat and military desecration of the sanctuaries. Nevatim ('demolished'): razed, used of altars.
6.4: chammanim ('incense altars'): etymology debated — possibly from chammah (sun), designating sun-pillars or incense stands associated with solar worship. The term appears in Leviticus 26:30 as a target of divine wrath. gillulim ('idols'): from gll (to roll), cognate with gelal (dung pellets) — a deliberately contemptuous coinage used 39x in Ezekiel alone. YHWH refuses to use a neutral word for the objects of Israel's devotion.
6.5: gufei bnei Yisrael ('corpses of the Israelites'): the ultimate irony — the bodies of worshippers laid before the idols they served. The verb natati ('I will put/give') makes YHWH the agent, not a passive observer. The dead scattered before their idols enacts a kind of dark liturgy: the worshippers finally brought fully into the presence of their gods.
6.6: yeshamu ('they shall be desolated/made waste'): Niphal of shamam, appearing 5x in this chapter. Shamam describes the condition of a land utterly stripped of population, agriculture, and life — the covenant curse of Leviticus 26:31-32 enacted. The high places that were crowded with worshippers will be as empty as the idols themselves.
6.7: yeda'tem ki-ani YHWH ('you will know that I am YHWH'): the refrain formula that punctuates all of Ezekiel — occurring ~72 times in the book. The goal of judgment is not annihilation but recognition: YHWH's actions are revelatory. The same formula is used for both judgment oracles (nations will know YHWH) and restoration oracles (Israel will know YHWH). Knowledge of YHWH is the telos of all divine action in Ezekiel.
6.8: palitim ('escapees/survivors'): from palat (to escape, deliver). The remnant that escapes the sword is not preserved through their own merit but scattered among the nations — a partial rescue that simultaneously continues the humiliation. YHWH preserves a witness to his judgment even in the dispersion.
6.9: lev zona ('whoring heart'): the marriage metaphor of apostasy that runs through Ezekiel 16, 20, and 23 is introduced here with its central term. Zana (to whore/commit harlotry) in prophetic usage describes covenantal infidelity — the exclusive relationship with YHWH violated by attraction to foreign gods. eineihem ha-zonot ('their eyes that whored after'): the eyes as the organ of desire that initiated the apostasy.
6.10: veyadeu ki-lo chinnam ('they shall know that not for nothing'): the survivors will finally understand that the destruction was not random or unjust — it was the enacted covenant curse for specific covenant violations. The recognition formula here adds the crucial element of justified judgment: YHWH acted with moral coherence, not capriciousness.
6.11: haka yad verego regel ('strike with the hand and stamp with the foot'): prophetic gestures of grief and horror — possibly also mimicking the executioner's final blow. The prophet embodies the divine anguish over the apostasy even as he announces judgment. Ezekiel's sign-acts throughout the book make his body the medium of prophetic communication.
6.12: The three modes of death announced — pestilence, sword, famine — are the classic biblical covenant-curse triad (Lev 26:25-26; Deut 28:21-22). The formula ve'yadeu ki-ani YHWH closes the triad, ensuring even the judgment itself serves the purpose of divine self-revelation.
6.13: raach nicho'ach ('pleasing/soothing aroma'): the technical term from Leviticus for the aroma of acceptable sacrifice that ascends to YHWH. Here the phrase is used bitterly — the pleasing aroma rising from the high places, under the trees, on the mountains, was worship given to other gods. Every element of true sacrifice (the aroma, the elevation, the open-air altar) is present but directed to the wrong recipient.
6.14: nosh'mamah umeshimmah ('desolate and waste'): the doubled desolation intensifies the completeness of the coming devastation. The phrase midbar diblata ('wilderness from Diblah/Riblah') marks the geographical extent of the destruction from south to north — the wilderness of Riblah marking the northern border of the land. The yeda'tem formula closes the chapter, making recognition of YHWH the final word.

7.1: The oracle-reception formula again — but the message of chapter 7 is qualitatively different from everything before. This chapter is a sustained lament over finality, with a distinctive elevated/poetic style that breaks from Ezekiel's usual prose. The shift in register signals the gravity of what follows.
7.2: qets ('the end'): the word Amos used for the northern kingdom's fate (Amos 8:2) is now applied to the southern. Qets denotes a terminal point — not merely a bad period but the cessation of something. arba kanfot ha'aretz ('four corners of the land'): totalistic formula — every direction, nothing excluded. The end comes from all quadrants simultaneously.
7.3: shafachti appi ('I will pour out my wrath upon you'): aph is both 'nose' and 'wrath' in Hebrew — the image of flaring nostrils as the original somatic register of anger. The pouring metaphor suggests wrath as accumulated liquid finally released, uncontainable and unstoppable. mishpatim ('judgments'): the deeds that fill the "end" are the divine verdicts — YHWH's legal actions against covenant breach.
7.4: lo-tachon einecha ('my eye will not spare you'): the verb chus (to have compassion, spare) is used negatively here — the divine compassion that normally withholds judgment is explicitly withdrawn. Ezekiel 8:18 and 9:5 repeat this formula. The removal of divine mercy is itself a judicial act, not arbitrary cruelty but the withdrawal of a staying grace that was repeatedly refused.
7.5: ra'ah achat ra'ah ('a disaster, a singular disaster'): the repetition of the word creates an ominous emphasis — 'a disaster, one disaster.' The LXX reads kakía mía kakía. This singular unique disaster will be unlike the repeated warnings and partial judgments that preceded it. This is the final act.
7.6: heqitz elayich ('the end has awakened against you'): qetz (end) is personified as a sleeping predator now roused. The verb huqitz (Hiphil of quts) means 'to awaken' — judgment was dormant, patient; now it stirs. The wordplay qetz/qits (end/summer) echoes Amos 8:2's famous vision.
7.7: hatsefira ba'ah ('the dawn has come, the doom has come'): tsefira is rare — possibly 'morning' or 'crown/garland' (hapax in this sense) — pointing to an ironic dawning not of light but of doom. The day dawns but brings darkness. lo-har hechim ('not a joyful shout on the mountains'): the mountains that should echo with the joyful shout of harvest (Isa 16:10) will be silenced.
7.8: qerov lishpoch chamati ('soon I will pour out my fury upon you'): chamath (fury, heat of anger) — a stronger term than aph — denotes the burning intensity of the divine anger that has been holding back. The second person singular address makes this intensely personal: YHWH speaks directly to the land/people as if face to face.
7.9: lo-tachus einecha velo echmol ('my eye will not spare and I will not pity'): the doubled refusal of mercy — both the visual mode of compassion (chus b'ayin, spare with the eye) and the emotional compassion (chamal, pity/have mercy) are withdrawn. The yeda'tem formula again: judgment serves revelation.
7.10: mateh parach hazadon tsats ('the rod has blossomed, pride has budded'): unusual agricultural metaphor. The 'rod' is likely the rod of punishment/affliction that has now 'blossomed' — it has come to full growth and is ready to execute. Zadon (pride/arrogance): the bloom of Israel's arrogance has triggered the bloom of its judgment. Nature's growth cycle becomes the metaphor for moral consequence.
7.11: hachamas qam lemateh resha ('violence has grown into a rod of wickedness'): the violence that filled the land has become the instrument of its own destruction — a rod that punishes. This theme of sin becoming the agent of its own punishment is central to prophetic theology. Lo-meylahem ('nothing of their wealth') — no remainder survives.
7.12: ba ha'et higi'a hayom ('the time has come, the day has arrived'): the prophetic 'day of YHWH' language — a day that has been anticipated is now arriving. ha-mocher al-yismach ('let not the buyer rejoice'): commercial transactions will be meaningless — the land itself is forfeited, and buying property is pointless when everything is about to be seized by the invader.
7.13: ki-hazon el-kol-hamonah lo yashuv ('for the vision concerning its whole multitude will not turn back'): the divine vision-word is irrevocable — once spoken and set in motion, it cannot be recalled or softened. The prophetic word in Ezekiel functions as divine performative speech: it brings into existence what it declares.
7.14: taq'u vataqoa ('they blow the trumpet'): the war trumpet signals mobilization, but no one goes to battle — the will to fight has been divinely removed. The trumpet sounds into an emptiness that reveals the completeness of judgment: no military response is possible because YHWH's wrath has paralyzed the defenders.
7.15: The three-fold judgment of sword, pestilence, and famine again (cf. 6:12), but here applied to specific locations: sword in the field, pestilence and famine within the city. The geography of doom is complete — there is no safe quarter. The siege creates an inner circle of starvation while the sword awaits those who try to escape.
7.16: umiltetu vehayu ('and those who escape, they will be'): even the escapees are covered in divine judgment — they will be on the mountains 'like doves of the valleys' (col-yon ha'amaqim), each mourning alone. The comparison to solitary mourning doves captures the total breakdown of community: isolated, grieving, without purpose.
7.17: kol-yadayim tirpenah ('all hands shall go limp'): the paralysis of terror — the inability to act, fight, or flee. The idiom raphah yadayim (limp/feeble hands) recurs in Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Zephaniah as the somatic register of total demoralization. berka'ayim telaknah mayim ('knees run with water'): the physical sign of extreme fear, incontinence as the body's ultimate surrender.
7.18: chagor saq ('gird themselves with sackcloth'): the mourning rite of sackcloth — worn against the skin as a sign of grief and abasement. ufachad yechasse ('terror will cover them'): pachad (dread, terror) — the paralyzing fear that removes the capacity for resistance. vel-kol-panim bushah ('on all faces shame'): universal shame as the condition of the defeated.
7.19: kashpam batchutzot yashlichu ('their silver they will throw into the streets'): the ultimate economic collapse — wealth becomes worthless. kesef niddah yihyeh ('their silver will be like a rejected thing'): niddah (menstrual impurity) — silver described as ritually unclean and untouchable, the ultimate devaluation. beyom evrat YHWH ('on the day of YHWH's wrath'): the first explicit use of the 'day of YHWH' formula in Ezekiel.
7.20: tzvi adyo lega'on samu ('the beauty of his ornaments — they set it for pride'): Israel's beautiful temple treasures (the tzvi, glory/beauty) were used to make idols. The divine gift of beauty — architecture, sacred objects, wealth — was perverted into the material of false worship. The very blessings became the instruments of the betrayal.
7.21: unatativ byad hazarim levalah ('I will give it as plunder to strangers to desecrate'): YHWH actively hands Israel's treasured things to pagan desecrators — not as abandonment but as a deliberate act of judgment through the agency of enemies. The 'most wicked of the nations' as YHWH's instruments echoes the Habakkuk theodicy: how can YHWH use the unrighteous to judge his people?
7.22: utsefuni lo-ye'asem ('my treasured place shall be profaned'): tsafun (that which is hidden/treasured/secret) — the innermost holy space, the sanctuary. YHWH turns his face away, allowing the profaners to enter and desecrate what he has until now protected. The divine withdrawal of protection makes the violence possible.
7.23: asoh harattok ('make the chain'): a command to Ezekiel to forge a chain — a sign act of coming captivity. The land is full of bloodshed (mishpat damim) and the city full of violence (chamas). The chain anticipates the exile: Israel will be led away bound.
7.24: miqdasheihem yechullalu ('their sanctuaries shall be desecrated'): plural miqdashim — all the holy places, not just Jerusalem. The word challal (desecrate/profane) is from the root meaning 'pierced/opened' — to violate what was sealed and protected. The divine protection of the holy place is withdrawn, and the sacred is exposed to the profane.
7.25: tavo charchurah ('anguish comes'): charcharah (trembling/anguish) — a rare term that combines physical and emotional distress. They will seek peace (shalom) but there will be none — the divine gift of shalom has been permanently interrupted. The false prophets' cry of 'peace, peace' (13:10) is answered by this declared absence of peace.
7.26: chazon me'at yavoh ('calamity upon calamity shall come, and vision will follow vision'): in a savage irony, the moment judgment falls is when visions multiply — but they are visions of doom, not salvation. Torah mekohen tovad ('instruction from the priest is lost'): the three channels of divine communication — vision (chazon from the prophet), instruction (Torah from the priest), counsel (etsah from the elder) — all simultaneously fail. The complete collapse of covenant mediation.
7.27: hamelech yitabel ('the king will mourn'): the king himself becomes a mourner — dressed in garments of mourning rather than royal robes. The total inversion of the social order: the priest loses Torah, the elder loses counsel, the king loses power, the people lose security. yadayim am ha'aretz tirpenah ('the hands of the people of the land will go limp'): the paralyzing terror now extends through all social strata. Divine judgment equalizes everyone in helplessness.

8.1: yad Adonai YHWH naflah alai ('the hand of the Lord YHWH fell upon me'): the phrase yad YHWH (hand of YHWH) marks ecstatic prophetic experience — a divine force seizing the prophet for vision or transport. Compare 1:3; 3:14, 22; 33:22; 40:1. The elders of Judah sitting before Ezekiel in exile suggests he has become a recognized spiritual authority among the exiles even before the formal destruction of Jerusalem.
8.2: demut-mar'eh esh ('something with the appearance of fire'): the figure that appears is identical in description to the throne-figure of chapter 1 — the deliberate verbal echo connects this vision to the divine chariot. From his loins downward: fire; from his loins upward: gleaming brightness like hashmal (electrum/amber). This is a theophanic appearance of the divine, not an angel.
8.3: tziur haqin'ah ('the image of jealousy'): qin'ah (jealousy/zeal) is the covenantal emotion YHWH declares in the Decalogue: 'I, YHWH your God, am a jealous God' (Ex 20:5). The idol placed at the gate of the altar provokes YHWH's jealousy by occupying the sacred space where only YHWH should be acknowledged. The idol is identified by the response it provokes in YHWH.
8.4: kevod Elohei Yisrael ('the glory of the God of Israel'): the kavod that appeared in chapter 1 at the River Chebar is now seen in Jerusalem — still present, still resident, but about to observe the abominations that will cause it to depart. The kavod's presence here sets up the drama of chapters 8-11: the glory witnesses the unfaithfulness and then withdraws.
8.5: miznafon la-sha'ar ha-mizbeach ('north of the altar gate'): the north was considered the direction of divine approach (cf. Job 37:22; Isa 14:13). An idol placed at the north gate of the altar — the most theologically charged direction — is a maximum affront to YHWH. The cardinal points of the compass become the geography of apostasy in this chapter: north (v5), inner court (v6), entrance of the outer court (v7-12), north gate of the inner court (v14), east gate of the inner court (v16).
8.6: lemarchaqeni me'al miqdashi ('to drive me far from my sanctuary'): YHWH frames the abominations in terms of their effect on him — they push him away from his own house. The divine presence is sensitive, not automatic; persistent covenant violation creates a moral/relational incompatibility between YHWH and the space that was dedicated to his presence. Judgment is the consequence of the people successfully driving YHWH away.
8.7: chayim chur bachomah ('a hole in the wall'): Ezekiel is commanded to dig through the wall — a prophetic act of investigation. The wall conceals something; what is hidden is worse than what is public. The inward/concealed nature of the abomination (the 70 elders in a secret chamber) represents the systemic nature of the apostasy: it is not just a public display but an internal practice of the leadership.
8.8: The command to dig (chathor-lecha) precedes the reveal — the prophet as investigator of hidden sin. The image of tunneling through a wall to see hidden chambers anticipates the revelation that follows. The progressively revealed layers of apostasy (idol at the gate, animals painted inside, women weeping, men facing east) represent a kind of tour of unfaithfulness moving from exterior to interior, from obvious to occult.
8.9: re'eh et-hato'evot hara'ot ('see the great abominations'): YHWH directs Ezekiel's vision — he sees what YHWH wants him to see. The tour of abominations is not Ezekiel's discovery but a divinely guided revelation of exactly how deep the apostasy runs. Each scene is observed at YHWH's prompting: 'come and see' (bo ur'eh) — v9, 13, 15, 17.
8.10: kol tavnit remes uvehema sheketz ('every form of creeping things and beasts and abominations'): the inner chamber is covered with murals of unclean animals — the precise categories prohibited in Leviticus 11. The 70 elders worship before images of everything that Israel is forbidden to eat. The Levitical purity system is being systematically inverted in the secret worship chamber.
8.11: shivim ish miziqnei beit Yisrael ('seventy men of the elders of the house of Israel'): the council of elders — 70 men as in Numbers 11:16-17 where 70 elders received the Spirit. The legitimate governance structure of Israel has been co-opted for secret idol worship. Ya'azanyahu ben Shafan: named — the son of Shaphan the scribe (2 Kings 22:3), who read the discovered Torah scroll to Josiah. The family of reform is implicated in apostasy.
8.12: bemachshak ish becheder maskito ('each in his room of idols/carved images'): maskiyot (carved images/rooms of imagery) — each elder has a private chamber of devotion. The corporate apostasy at the temple is matched by individual private idolatry. Their theological rationale: ein YHWH ro'eh otanu ('YHWH does not see us') — YHWH has abandoned the land. The premise for the secret worship is the claim that divine oversight has ceased.
8.13: The divine 'come and see' formula repeated — this is only the second of four scenes. Each scene is worse than the previous. YHWH is systematically showing Ezekiel the cumulative weight of Israel's unfaithfulness, building to the fullest possible indictment before announcing the judgment of chapters 9-11.
8.14: nashim yoshvot mevakot et-haTammuz ('women sitting weeping for the Tammuz'): Tammuz is the Mesopotamian god of vegetation/fertility (Sumerian Dumuzi), whose annual death and descent to the underworld was mourned by women in ritual lamentation at midsummer. The cult was explicitly Babylonian — Ezekiel's exilic audience would recognize it immediately. The north gate of the inner court is devoted to foreign fertility-cult mourning.
8.15: The third 'come and see' — greater abominations to come. The tour moves from animal-worship murals (8:10), to Tammuz-lamentation (8:14), to solar cult (8:16) — roughly tracing the geography of the temple courts from outer to inner, and also the chronological history of Israel's apostasy from Canaanite (bamot) to Mesopotamian to Egyptian/solar influences.
8.16: esrim vachamisha ish ('twenty-five men'): the number may correspond to the 24 priestly divisions plus the high priest — the entire cultic leadership. mishtachavitim qedmah lashemesh ('bowing down to the sun'): qedem (east) — they face east, away from the Holy of Holies, toward the rising sun. This is the ultimate liturgical inversion: the worshippers of YHWH turn their backs on his dwelling to face a heavenly body.
8.17: shalchu et-hazmorat el-appam ('they put the branch to their nose'): a notoriously obscure gesture — possibly a ritual act in solar or fertility worship (holding a plant toward the face in salutation or offering). Some emend to 'nose' = face, others read it as a deliberate obscenity. Whatever the gesture, YHWH frames it as a provocation: they are outraging him and then returning to do more provocation. Chamasam ('their violence') — the sins accumulate.
8.18: lo-tachus einai velo echmol ('my eye will not spare and I will not pity'): the identical refusal of mercy from 7:4, 9 — but now pronounced in the context of the observed abominations. The justice of the upcoming slaughter in chapter 9 is grounded in the evidence of chapters 8: YHWH has seen everything in the secret chambers and at the east gate. veqare'u be'oznai qol gadol ('they cry in my ears with a loud voice'): even loud prayer in the coming crisis will not be heard.

9.1: paqudat ha'ir ('those charged with the punishment of the city'): paqudat (oversight/charge/punishment) — the root paqad is both 'to visit' (in care) and 'to visit' (in judgment). Six executioners approach from the north — the direction from which Babylon will come. The summoning of divine executioners frames the coming slaughter not as human atrocity but as divinely authorized judgment.
9.2: keli mashchet beyado ('a weapon of destruction in his hand'): each executioner carries keli mashchet — an instrument of shattering/destruction. The one exception is the man levush habadim ('dressed in linen'): priestly linen (bad) marks him as a different order of being — a priestly-scribal figure who marks the saved, not executes the judged. qeset hasopher ('writing case of the scribe'): an ink-holder for marking.
9.3: ukhvod Elohei Yisrael na'alah ('the glory of the God of Israel went up'): the glory begins its departure. From the kerub (cherub) on which it rested — its traditional seat above the ark in the Holy of Holies — it moves to the threshold of the temple. This is the first stage of the glory's withdrawal that will culminate in 10:18-19 and 11:23. The departure of the glory is the most theologically catastrophic event in the entire book.
9.4: vesimta tav al-mitzchotam ('put a mark on the foreheads'): tav (תָּו) is the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet, and in paleo-Hebrew script was written as an X or + shape. The mark identifies those who ne'enachim v'ne'anaqim ('sigh and groan') over all the abominations done in Jerusalem. The criterion for salvation is not ritual performance but grief-identification with YHWH's perspective on the apostasy. Those who mourn over sin are marked as those who share divine values.
9.5: acharav amar b'oznai ('to the others he said in my hearing'): Ezekiel hears the divine command to the executioners, not merely as a vision but as audible speech in his ears. The command to follow the scribe through the city mirrors the Exodus: the blood on the doorposts marked the saved; here the ink-mark on the forehead marks those who are passed over. The executioners begin with the elders before the sanctuary — judgment begins at the house of God (cf. 1 Pet 4:17).
9.6: el-zaqen bachur u'vetulah vetaf u'nashim harogu lemashchit ('old man, young man, maiden, infant, and women — kill to destruction'): the totalistic list — no age or sex is exempt from the judgment. The sole exception: veal-kol-ish asher-alav hatav al-tiqrabu ('do not come near any man on whom is the mark'). Ubeit hamiqdash tachelu ('from my sanctuary you shall begin'): judgment starting in the temple — the most horrifying reversal, since the temple was supposed to be the place of refuge.
9.7: tumeu et-habayit ('defile the house'): the executioners are commanded to defile the temple — the very space that was supposed to be the holiest place in Israel is made a slaughterhouse. The chatzerot mil'u chalal ('fill the courts with the slain') creates a spatial horror: the courts for sacrificial worship become the courts of corpses. YHWH himself commands the defilement of his own house as the final statement of judgment on Israel's apostasy.
9.8: veten Ezekiel el-panav ('and I fell on my face'): Ezekiel's intercession — the same posture of pleading as Moses (Num 14:5; 16:22). ha'atah meshalecheta et kol she'erit Yisrael ('are you going to destroy all the remnant of Israel?'): the prophet's one intercessory cry in the midst of the vision. Unlike Jeremiah (7:16; 11:14), Ezekiel is not explicitly told not to intercede, but the answer he receives makes clear that the judgment cannot be arrested.
9.9: avon beit Yisrael uYehudah gadol meod meod ('the iniquity of the house of Israel and Judah is very, very great'): the double intensifier meod meod (very, very) drives home the disproportionality — this is not slight unfaithfulness but massive, accumulated, comprehensive covenant violation. YHWH names two specific theological failures: (1) the land is full of blood (dam); (2) the city is full of perversion (mateh). Both are covenant violations with capital consequences in Torah.
9.10: gam ani lo-tachus einai velo echmol ('I also — my eye will not spare and I will not pity'): the formula from 7:4,9 and 8:18 now from YHWH's own lips in direct speech to the interceding Ezekiel. The 'I also' (gam ani) is devastating in its context: Ezekiel has just shown compassion ('will you destroy the remnant?'); YHWH responds with the divine reciprocity of derakham beroshham natati — 'their way I repay on their heads.'
9.11: ani asiti ka'asher tzivitani ('I have done as you commanded me'): the linen-clad scribe reports completion. The chapter ends on the note of the one mission accomplished: the mark has been placed. Against the total devastation of the city, the marking of the remnant is the sole exception. The divine plan that preserves a remnant while executing judgment on the apostate majority is achieved with precise obedience.

10.1: raqia asher al-rosh hakeruvim ('the firmament that was over the heads of the cherubim'): the same raqia (expanse/firmament) that appeared in 1:22 above the four living creatures. The identification of the creatures here as keruvim (cherubim) — which will be made explicit in v. 20 — bridges the throne vision of chapter 1 to the temple context of chapters 8-11. even sapir kemar'eh ('like sapphire stone in appearance'): the sapphire-blue throne platform connecting to the throne-chariot.
10.2: galgelei esh ('burning coals of fire'): the man in linen is commanded to take fire from between the cherubim and scatter it over the city — the same coals that will be scattered over Jerusalem as judgment. The fire comes from within the divine chariot, making the judgment itself an emanation of divine holiness. What was the fire of divine presence becomes the fire of divine judgment when directed toward an apostate city.
10.3: ha'anan male et-hechatzer hap'nimit ('the cloud filled the inner court'): the glory-cloud (anan) fills the inner court just as it filled the tabernacle at its dedication (Ex 40:34-35) and the temple at Solomon's dedication (1 Kgs 8:10-11). But here the filling of the cloud precedes the departure of the glory — it is a last manifestation of divine presence before withdrawal, not an inaugural occupation.
10.4: varam kevod YHWH me'al hakerub al-miftan habayit ('the glory of YHWH rose from upon the cherub to the threshold of the house'): the second stage of the glory's departure — from the cherub (the ark/mercy seat) to the threshold (the doorway of the temple). The glory is moving toward the exit. vatimale habayit et-he'anan ('the house was filled with the cloud'): paradoxically, as the glory prepares to leave, its visible manifestation intensifies. Departure is preceded by a final overwhelming display.
10.5: qol kanfei hakeruvim nishma ad-hechatzer hachitzonah ('the sound of the wings of the cherubim was heard to the outer court'): the sound is compared to kol El Shaddai bedabro ('the voice of God Almighty when he speaks'). El Shaddai — the patriarchal divine name (Gen 17:1; 28:3; Ex 6:3) emphasizing divine power and sufficiency — appears here as the register for the thunder-sound of the cherubim's wings. The chariot is audible as divine speech.
10.6: The man in linen obeys the command to take coals. His movement — from the outer area to standing between the wheels — marks him as able to navigate between the human-accessible outer courts and the divine-presence space between the cherubim. He is a mediating figure: priestly linen, scribal tools, access to the divine inner sanctum, commissioned for judgment on the city.
10.7: yishlach ish halomed et-yado el-toch ha'esh ('the cherub stretched out his hand into the fire'): the fire that the linen-clad man is to take is given to him by a cherub directly — the divine agent of judgment hands the judicial fire to the priestly agent of judgment. The chain of delegation (YHWH → cherub → linen-man → city) distributes the agency of judgment while maintaining its ultimate divine source.
10.8: vayera el-hakeruvim tabnit yad adam tachtat kanfeihem ('there appeared on the cherubim the form of a human hand beneath their wings'): a detail repeated from 1:8 — the human hands beneath the wings of the living creatures. In chapter 1 this was part of the initial vision; here the identification reinforces that these are the same figures. The human element in the divine chariot emphasizes the relational/personal nature of the divine presence.
10.9: ofan echad etzel hakerub echad ('one wheel beside each cherub'): the four-wheel structure of the chariot repeated from 1:15-21. kemar'eh even tarshish ('like the appearance of tarshish stone'): tarshish stone is variously identified as beryl, chrysolite, or topaz — a lustrous greenish-blue gem. The same description in 1:16; Daniel 10:6; and Rev 21:20 places tarshish consistently in the register of divine-appearance glory.
10.10: The four wheels are identical to each other (mareh echad laarba'atam) — no hierarchy, no differentiation among the wheels. This uniformity emphasizes the perfect integration and unified movement of the chariot. The divine throne-chariot has no structural hierarchy among its wheels, just as the four living creatures were coordinate rather than subordinate to each other.
10.11: el-ha-rosh yelu acharav yelu ('toward the head they go; after the head they go'): direction of movement determined by the head — wherever the rosh turns, the entire chariot turns without rotating. This describes perfect responsiveness to a single will: the chariot is omnidirectional precisely because it is perfectly subordinate to the divine will directing it.
10.12: kol-besaram vegabotam veyedeihem vekanfeihem vehaofanim mele'im einayim saviv ('their entire bodies, their backs, their hands, their wings, and the wheels — all full of eyes around'): the omni-perceptive nature of the divine chariot — eyes on every surface. The divine presence is omniscient as well as omnipresent. Nothing in the created order is outside YHWH's perception; the chariot's many eyes embody the truth that 'his eyes are on all his ways' (Jer 16:17; Prov 15:3).
10.13: lagalgal qora lahem ('to the wheel it was called 'the whirling thing''): hagalgal — possibly an intensified form of ofan (wheel) emphasizing rapid rotation. Ezekiel notes what the creatures themselves call their wheels — an insider detail that gives the vision the quality of overheard divine speech, not merely observed phenomenon.
10.14: arba panim le'echad ('four faces belonging to one'): the four faces of each creature listed — but here, significantly, the first face is hakerub (the cherub face) rather than the shor (ox) of 1:10. The substitution has generated extensive scholarly discussion: possibly 'cherub face' = the ox face seen from the cherubic perspective, or possibly a deliberate difference marking the Jerusalem temple context. Either way, the identification of the creatures as cherubim is being established.
10.15: vatinase hakeruvim ('the cherubim rose up'): the first movement of the chariot-cherubim since the vision of departure began. They are identified: hi hachayyah asher ra'iti binehar-kevar ('it is the living creature that I saw by the River Chebar'). Ezekiel explicitly connects the two visions — the creatures of chapter 1 are the same as those in the temple vision. The continuity of the divine chariot in both locations affirms that the same divine presence was at the river and in the temple.
10.16: beseet hakeruvim et-kanfeihem larus me'al ha'aretz lo-yisabbu haofanim miletselam ('when the cherubim went, the wheels went beside them; when the cherubim lifted their wings to rise from the earth, the wheels did not turn from beside them'): perfect synchronization — the wheels do not act independently of the cherubim. The whole chariot moves as a unity, every part responsive to the same animating spirit.
10.17: ki ruach hachayah bahem ('for the spirit of the living creature was in them'): the ruach (spirit/breath/wind) of the living creatures animates the wheels — they share a single animating principle. In 1:20-21, this same ruach was the key to the chariot's movement. The divine chariot is not a mechanism but a living, spiritually unified entity — which is why it moves in perfect responsiveness to the divine will.
10.18: vayetse kevod YHWH me'al miftan habayit ('the glory of YHWH departed from over the threshold of the house'): the critical moment — the glory leaves the temple building. From v4 (glory at the threshold) to v18 (glory departing from the threshold), the stage is set: the divine presence has now moved outside the building entirely. This is not merely the ark being captured (1 Sam 4) — it is the voluntary withdrawal of YHWH's presence from his own house in response to its defilement.
10.19: vaya'amdu petach sha'ar beit YHWH hamizrachit ('they stood at the entrance of the east gate of the house of YHWH'): the glory moves to the east gate — the gate through which the sun rises and toward which the apostate priests had been bowing (8:16). The deliberate choice of the east gate for the glory's penultimate resting place may be a reversal: the gate that was used for solar worship now receives the true divine glory as it exits. Compare 43:1-4 where the glory returns through this same east gate.
10.20: hi hachayyah asher ra'iti tachat Elohei Yisrael binehar-kevar ('it is the living creature that I saw under the God of Israel by the River Chebar'): the explicit identification, stated plainly and definitively. The creatures beneath the divine throne at the Chebar River and the cherubim in the Jerusalem temple are the same. This retrospective identification validates both visions simultaneously and affirms that Israel's God is not geographically limited — he was on a moveable throne at the Chebar River, and that same throne now departs from Jerusalem.
10.21: arba panim le'echad ve'arba knafayim le'echad ('four faces for each one and four wings for each one'): Ezekiel catalogs the identifying features of the cherubim to clinch the identification with the chapter 1 creatures. The enumeration is precise: four faces, four wings (vs. Isaiah's six-winged seraphim in Isa 6), with human hands beneath the wings. The consistent anatomy across the two visions is the proof of identity.
10.22: udemut peneihem hemah hapanim asher ra'iti al-nehar-kevar ('the likeness of their faces — they were the faces that I saw at the River Chebar'): the chapter ends with the full identification confirmed. The vision has now come full circle from chapter 1: the living creatures of the throne-chariot are the cherubim of the temple; the same divine kavod was present in Babylon and in Jerusalem; and now it is departing from Jerusalem. The next move of the glory (11:23 — to the Mount of Olives) completes the departure sequence.

2.1: ben adam (ben ʾādām): 'son of man / son of Adam.' The most characteristic divine address to Ezekiel, used 93 times in the book (more than any other OT book). Ben ʾādām means simply 'human being' — it is the term that marks Ezekiel's creatureliness before the divine presence. The contrast is deliberate: the divine being enthroned in chapter 1 has the demut kemarat adam (likeness like a human) while the prophet is addressed as ben ʾādām (a human being) — the human below and the anthropomorphic divine above, both using the vocabulary of human form. In Daniel 7:13 the phrase becomes a messianic title ('one like a son of man'); in the NT, Jesus's consistent self-designation 'the Son of Man' draws on both traditions.
2.2: ruach baah vi (rûaḥ bāʾâ bî): 'the Spirit came into me.' The Spirit that animates the chariot-wheels (1:20-21) is the same Spirit that now enters the prophet. The entry of the ruach into Ezekiel is the link between the visionary encounter and the prophetic commission: he cannot stand without the Spirit entering him (cf. 3:24). The divine voice speaks first, then the Spirit enters to enable response — the order of grace: word, then enablement to hear it.
2.3: Sent to the children of Israel, to the rebellious nations (goyim hamordim, gôyîm hāmōrĕdîm). The term marad (mārad) — to rebel — is stronger than the more common pāšaʿ (transgress); it carries the connotation of open political revolt against a sovereign. Israel is addressed as goyim (nations/Gentiles) — a shocking term, usually reserved for foreign peoples. Their rebellion has characterized them 'to this day' (ʿad hayyôm hazzeh): persistent, ongoing, unreformed. The fathers rebelled and the sons have not stopped.
2.4: Israel is azei panim (ʿazzê p̄ānîm): 'hard of face/impudent' and hazaq lev (ḥĕzaq lēb): 'hardened of heart.' The two body-part metaphors describe spiritual resistance: the face (what one presents to others) is hard/impudent — unashamed, uncowed; the heart (decision-making center) is hardened against the word. Paul's description of the unregenerate heart in Romans 1:21 (their foolish hearts were darkened) and the hardening of Israel in Rom 11:7 both draw on this OT vocabulary of ḥĕzaq lēb.
2.5: 'Whether they listen or not' (yishmehu veIM yichdalu, yišmĕʿû wĕʾim yeḥdālû): the prophetic mission is not conditioned on success. The prophet is called to speak the divine word regardless of reception — a theme that will be intensified in ch. 3. This is the hermeneutic key to Ezekiel's entire ministry: the word goes forth whether it is received or rejected; the prophet's faithfulness is the only variable under his control. ki beit meri hemah (kî bêt mĕrî hēmmâ): 'for they are a house of rebellion' — the label beit meri (house of rebellion) recurs throughout Ezekiel as the characteristic designation of his audience.
2.6: 'Do not be afraid of them, do not be afraid of their words' — even when briers and thorns are around you and you sit on scorpions. The three images of the hostile audience: saravim (sārābbîm, thorns/briers), qotsim (qôṣîm, thorns/spines), aqravim (ʿaqrabbîm, scorpions). The scorpion image is particularly striking: not just passive thorns but actively dangerous creatures. The prophet is called to sit among them without flinching — divine fearlessness as the mark of genuine prophetic calling (cf. Jer 1:17; 1 Kgs 18:15).
2.7: Whether they listen or refuse — they are a rebellious house (beit meri). The repetition from v. 5 forms a frame around vv. 5-7: the commission is unchanged by the audience's likely rejection. The Hebrew perfect yishmehu (they will listen) and imperfect yichdalu (they will refuse) are both uncertain futures — the prophet cannot know which will occur. But the commission stands either way.
2.8: 'But you, son of man, hear what I say to you — do not be rebellious like that rebellious house; open your mouth and eat what I give you.' The contrast between Ezekiel and Israel: Israel is the beit meri; Ezekiel must be the opposite. The command lo tehieh meri (lōʾ tihyê mĕrî): 'do not be a rebel' — explicitly applying the label they bear to the possible failure mode of the prophet himself. The prophet is not immune to the temptation of rebellion; the command is necessary because the commission is costly.
2.9: A hand was stretched out to me, and in it was a scroll (meghillat sefer, mĕgillat sēp̄er). Meghillah is a rolled scroll (from gālal, to roll). The scroll is stretched toward Ezekiel by a hand — presumably the hand of the enthroned figure of chapter 1 (whose hand of judgment/commission recurs throughout). The scroll contains the divine message that Ezekiel must internalize before proclamation.
2.10: Written on its front and back — panim veahor (pānîm wĕʾāḥôr): front and back, both sides. The contents: qinot vehageh vehi (qinôt wahegeh wāhî): 'lamentations and mourning and woe.' Three words for grief, all expressing the negative content of Ezekiel's prophetic message. The double-sided writing means the message is completely full — there is no space for anything else. The divine word to Judah is entirely lament. John's little scroll in Rev 10:8-10 is the same meghillah tradition: the scroll eaten by the prophet contains judgment that is sweet to receive but bitter in effect.

3.1: The command to eat the scroll is repeated — the double command (2:8 and 3:1) emphasizes the necessity of internalization before proclamation. melet bitnek (millēʾ biṭnĕkā): 'fill your stomach/belly with it.' The belly (beten) — the interior of the person — must receive the scroll completely before the mouth can speak. The bodily metaphor is deliberate: the word of God must become part of the prophet's physical existence, not merely an external message he relays. Cf. Jeremiah's word as 'fire shut up in my bones' (Jer 20:9).
3.2: Ezekiel opens his mouth and the divine hand feeds him the scroll. The passive construction — 'he gave me the scroll to eat' — maintains the divine initiative: Ezekiel does not choose to eat, he is fed. The word of God is not self-generated by the prophet but received and then re-expressed. This is the model for all biblical inspiration: the human vessel receives what comes from outside.
3.3: In my mouth it was like honey (devash, dĕḇaš) for sweetness. The sweetness of the scroll is a genuine and full sweetness, not ironic — the word of God is intrinsically delightful even when its content is judgment (cf. Ps 19:10: 'sweeter than honey and drippings of the honeycomb'; Ps 119:103). The prophet who tastes the sweetness of the word can proclaim judgment without bitterness — the tragedy of the message is held within the sweetness of being entrusted with it.
3.4: 'Go to the house of Israel and speak my words to them' — the commission restated after the scroll-eating. The message that Ezekiel was caused to eat is the same message he must now deliver: there is no gap between the received word and the proclaimed word. The digested scroll becomes the prophetic speech. This is the ideal of prophetic integrity: the prophet speaks not his own thoughts about God but the very words he has internalized.
3.5: 'You are not being sent to a people of foreign speech (shefat am, śĕp̄at ʿam) and difficult language (lishan kaved, lāšôn kābēd).' The 'heavy tongue' (literally 'heavy language') is the same idiom Moses used for his own speech impediment (Exod 4:10: kĕbad peh, heavy of mouth). Ezekiel is not sent to those whose language he cannot understand — his commission is specifically to his own people who share his language but refuse to understand.
3.6: 'If I had sent you to foreign peoples of unfamiliar speech and difficult language, they would have listened to you.' The shocking comparison: pagan foreigners would have been more receptive to the divine word than covenant Israel. This is the same pattern Jesus invokes (Matt 11:21-24: Tyre and Sidon would have repented) and Paul develops (Rom 1:18-32; the Gentiles who have the law written on their hearts). The accessibility of the word to outsiders heightens the culpability of insiders who resist it.
3.7: 'But the house of Israel will not listen to you because they will not listen to me — for all the house of Israel is hazak-metsach (ḥĕzaq-mēṣaḥ): hard of forehead (impudent/brazen) and qesheh-lev (qĕšê-lēb): stubborn of heart.' Two anatomical metaphors: forehead (public face of stubborn refusal to bow) and heart (inner decision-making center). The double hardness means they are resistant both externally (will not appear to yield) and internally (do not want to yield). The compound picture is of complete spiritual calcification.
3.8: 'But I have made your face as hard as their faces, and your forehead as hard as their foreheads.' The divine response to Israel's brazen resistance: God hardens the prophet's face to match — not in stubbornness of sin but in unflinching proclamation. natatih paneika chazaq linegev penehem (nātattî p̄ānêkā ḥāzāq lĕneged p̄ĕnêhem): 'I have made your face strong against their face.' The prophet's prophetic courage is not natural human bravery but God-given resistance to human intimidation.
3.9: shamir (šāmîr): 'adamant/emery' — the hardest stone known in antiquity, used to cut other stones. Your forehead like adamant, harder than flint (challamish, ḥallāmîš). The escalation: adamant harder than flint. The prophet who must stand against the stony resistance of Israel is given a forehead of harder stone — divine hardness against human hardness. 'Do not be afraid of them and do not be dismayed at their looks, for they are a rebellious house.'
3.10: 'Son of man, take all my words into your heart (levaveka, lĕbābĕkā) and hear with your ears.' The double command — heart first, then ears — reverses the ordinary sequence (ears hear, then heart receives). The divine word requires not just auditory reception but internalization: the prophet who will proclaim must first have the word lodge in his heart-center.
