This is the basis for the new covenant promise of Jer 31:33 (law written on hearts) which Ezekiel parallels in 36:26-27.
3.11: 'Go to your own people, to the exiles, and speak to them. Say to them, “Thus says the Lord GOD” — whether they listen or they refuse (yishmehu veIM yichdalu).' The phrase 'whether they listen or not' recurs (2:5, 7; 3:11) as the theological framework for prophetic ministry independent of results. Ezekiel is sent first to the exiles — those already in Babylon — before the fall of Jerusalem (in 586 BCE). His ministry is among the community that sees themselves as abandoned by God, teaching them to find the divine presence even in exile.
3.12: The Spirit lifted me up (vatisani ruach, wattissāʾēnî rûaḥ), and behind me I heard a great rumbling (qol raash gadol, qôl raʿaš gādôl): 'Blessed be the glory of YHWH from his place' (baruch kevod YHWH mimqomo). The doxology is the response of the chariot-beings to the divine departure — the chariot moves when the Spirit lifts the prophet. Baruch kevod YHWH is a formal liturgical doxology; mimqomo (from his place) — an unusual phrase that may mean 'wherever he is,' reflecting the new theology of the mobile divine presence that is not confined to Zion.
3.13: The sound of the living creatures' wings touching one another, and the sound of the wheels beside them — the full auditory impact of the departing chariot, described as a great roar. The prophet experiences the sound of the divine departure even as he is being sent on mission. The departure of the chariot from the Chebar to commission a prophetic mission is itself a theological statement: the divine glory moves to empower the prophet who moves toward the exiles.
3.14: vaelech mar uvachamat ruach (wāʾēlēk mār ûvaḥămat rûaḥ): 'I went in bitterness (mār) and in the heat/rage of the Spirit (ḥămat rûaḥ).' The prophet's emotional state: bitter, hot with prophetic compulsion. Mār (bitter) is not spiritual bitterness or resentment but the visceral pain of one tasked with proclaiming judgment to his own people. yad YHWH alai hazqa (yad Yhwh ʿālay ḥāzĕqâ): 'the hand of YHWH was strong upon me' — the divine compulsion that overcomes the prophet's personal grief and sets him in motion.
3.15: Arrived at Tel-abib (tel aviv, tel ʾābîb): 'mound of the spring flood' (probably from Akkadian tēl abubi, mound of the great flood — a tell raised by the primeval flood-disaster; later the name of the first Hebrew city in modern Israel). The prophet sits with the exiles and remains among them overwhelmed (mashim, maštîm or mĕšōmēm) for seven days. The seven-day stunned silence before speaking parallels the seven-day mourning/ordination period (Lev 8:33) — the prophet undergoes a kind of ordination through communal sitting before he speaks.
3.16: At the end of seven days, the word of YHWH came to Ezekiel — the prophetic reception formula that will introduce the new commission of watchman. The seven-day waiting period is ended not by the prophet's initiative but by the word's arrival: divine speech determines the timing of prophecy, not the prophet's readiness. This is consistent with Ezekiel's overall pattern of passive reception followed by active proclamation.
3.17: tsopheh (ṣōp̄eh): 'watchman.' The specific term for the sentinel stationed on the city wall to watch for approaching threats. Ezekiel is appointed as a watchman (nātattîkā ṣōp̄eh: I have appointed you a watchman) for the house of Israel. The watchman metaphor defines his role: not priest (though he is one), not king, not intercessor, but a sentinel responsible for warning. The watchman analogy establishes the life-or-death stakes of prophetic proclamation: failure to warn means complicity in the death of the warned.
3.18: 'If I say to the wicked, “You will certainly die,” and you give no warning — that wicked person will die for his iniquity, but I will require his blood from your hand.' damim (dāmîm): 'blood (guilt)' — the plural of dām used for bloodguilt, legal responsibility for death (Num 35:27; 2 Sam 16:8). The prophet who fails to warn is held legally responsible for the spiritual blood of the unwarned. Paul uses this exact framework in Acts 20:26: 'I am innocent of the blood of all of you, for I did not shrink from declaring the whole counsel of God' — Paul fulfills the watchman obligation of Ezek 3:18.
3.19: 'But if you warn the wicked and he does not turn from his wickedness — he will die in his iniquity, but you will have saved your life (nafshecha hitsalta, nap̄šĕkā hiṣṣaltā).' The watchman's faithfulness determines his own fate, not the wicked's choice. The double outcome: the unrepentant wicked person dies, but the faithful warner lives. This is the covenantal principle of individual moral responsibility — each person bears their own iniquity, but the prophet who fails to warn bears theirs alongside his own.
3.20: 'And when a righteous person turns from their righteousness and commits iniquity, and I place a stumbling block before them, they will die.' mikhshol (mikšôl): 'stumbling block' — YHWH placing a stumbling block before the would-be sinner who turns from righteousness is a concept that has generated theological debate (divine permission vs. divine causation of sin). The context in Ezekiel emphasizes individual responsibility: the turning from righteousness is the sinner's own act; the stumbling block is the consequence, not the cause. Paul uses proskomma (stumbling block) in Rom 14:13 and 1 Cor 8:9 in the related context of causing others to fall.
3.21: 'If you warn the righteous not to sin, and they take warning and do not sin, they will surely live because they took warning — and you will have saved your life.' The fourfold case structure (wicked/unwarned, wicked/warned, righteous-who-falls/unwarned, righteous/warned) establishes the comprehensive scope of the watchman's responsibility. The final case — the righteous who is warned and remains righteous — is the positive conclusion: the ministry of warning can preserve righteousness as well as call back the wicked.
3.22: yad YHWH (yad Yhwh) again — the third occurrence in the book (1:3; 3:14; 3:22), marking the beginning of the next visionary episode. The divine hand comes upon Ezekiel in the valley (biq'ah, biqʿâ) — the same Hebrew word used for the Valley of Dry Bones in ch. 37. The valley is the place of open divine encounter outside the city, outside the temple precincts — reinforcing the theme of mobile divine presence.
3.23: The glory of YHWH was there (kevod YHWH) — just like the vision at the Chebar (ch. 1). The second appearance of the divine chariot-glory confirms the identity of the Chebar vision and establishes continuity: Ezekiel has not been abandoned between the initial call-vision and this commission to silence and confinement. He falls on his face as before (1:28) — the prostration of the finite creature before infinite holiness.
3.24: The Spirit entered into him (vatavoh bi ruach) and set him on his feet — as in 2:2, the Spirit must enable standing before the word can be received. 'Go, shut yourself inside your house.' The divine command to confinement: after seven days of communal sitting among the exiles, now a period of isolation. The stages of Ezekiel's ministry: vision → commission → communal sitting → isolation → sign-acts. Each stage is divinely ordered.
3.25: 'Cords will be put on you and you will be tied with them so you cannot go out among the people.' The physical confinement of the prophet — he will be bound by the community he was meant to serve. Whether literal or figurative (the social pressure of a hostile community keeping him confined), the bound prophet is the sign of a bound nation: Israel is in exile (the bonds of galut), and Ezekiel's immobility enacts the nation's captivity.
3.26: 'I will make your tongue stick to the roof of your mouth — you will be silent (ne'elem, neʾĕlam) and unable to rebuke them.' The divine-imposed dumbness (illem) will be Ezekiel's condition until Jerusalem falls (33:22: 'my mouth was opened and I was no longer silent'). The dumbness is not total — Ezekiel speaks throughout (his oracles continue) — but he cannot speak freely for himself or as an intercessor (tokiah, rebuke/argue their case). His dumbness is the sign that the divine case against Israel is closed to human appeal.
3.27: 'But whenever I speak to you, I will open your mouth, and you will say to them, “Thus says the Lord GOD.” Whoever listens, let him listen; and whoever refuses, let him refuse — for they are a rebellious house.' The final restatement: the prophetic word depends entirely on divine initiative ('when I speak to you, I will open your mouth') and is entirely unconditioned by audience response ('whoever listens, let him; whoever refuses, let him'). The watchman has delivered the warning; the outcome belongs to those who hear.

4.1: levena (lĕbēnâ): 'brick/tile' — unbaked clay tablets were the writing material of Mesopotamia (cuneiform tablets); here Ezekiel inscribes (chuqita, ḥuqîtā) Jerusalem onto the clay, turning the Babylonian medium of record-keeping into the visual surface for Israel's judgment. The sign-act (ôt) transforms the prophet's body and surroundings into a prophetic performance — enacted oracle rather than spoken oracle. The use of a clay tile is culturally resonant: Babylon conquered by its own medium.
4.2: The siege works: dayeq (dāyeq, siege wall), solelah (sōlĕlâ, ramp/embankment), machanot (maḥănôt, encampments), karim (kārîm, battering rams). All four elements of ancient siege warfare arrayed against the clay Jerusalem. The enumeration is technical — Ezekiel has seen siege warfare in his lifetime and describes the full complement of ancient military siege engineering. The sign-act forces the exiles to watch their city being besieged in miniature, months before the historical siege will be complete.
4.3: machavat barzel (maḥăbat barzel): 'iron griddle/pan' — placed as an iron wall (qir barzel, qîr barzel) between Ezekiel and the clay city. The iron wall between prophet and Jerusalem represents the divine separation — YHWH has erected an iron barrier between himself and the city, shutting off the normal channels of prophetic intercession. The sign concludes: zeh ot (zeh ʾôt), 'this is a sign to the house of Israel.' The sign is not merely illustrative but constitutive — it participates in the reality it depicts.
4.4: nasa avon (nāśāʾ ʿāwôn): 'to bear/lift up iniquity.' This is one of the most important theological terms in the OT — the verb nāśāʾ (to carry, lift, bear) with its object ʿāwôn (iniquity, guilt, punishment). Ezekiel lying on his side bearing Israel's iniquity is a prophetic sign-act of substitutionary bearing: the prophet physically enacts the weight of covenant guilt through his constrained body. The same verbal combination (nāśāʾ ʿāwôn) appears in Isa 53:11-12 (the Servant bears the iniquity of many) and Num 14:34 (you shall bear your iniquity forty years). The 390-day performance is the physical vocabulary of covenant guilt-bearing.
4.5: Three hundred and ninety days (shalosh maot vetishim yom) = the years of Israel's iniquity. The number 390 has been variously calculated: as the sum of years from the division of the kingdom (931 BCE) to the end of exile; as the period from the Exodus to the temple dedication minus the 40 wilderness years; or as a typological number rather than a precise historical calculation. The key theological move is the day-for-year principle (yom lashanah yom lashanah, yôm lāšānâ): Ezekiel's physical ordeal compresses centuries of covenant history into months of bodily suffering.
4.6: Forty days for Judah's iniquity — a day for each year. The 40-year connection to the wilderness wandering (Num 14:34 explicitly uses the same day-for-year equation) casts the Babylonian exile as a second wilderness: a period of purging and preparation for a second entry into the land. The temporal symbolism is not precise chronology but covenantal typology.
4.7: 'Turn your face toward the besieged Jerusalem with your arm bared' — zeroa chasufah (zĕrôaʿ ḥăśûp̄â): 'bared arm,' the idiom for divine power in action (Isa 52:10: YHWH bares his holy arm before all the nations; 53:1: to whom has the arm of YHWH been revealed?). The prophet's bared arm facing the clay Jerusalem embodies YHWH's own bared-arm judgment, being executed through the Babylonian siege. The prophet's body is the divine action made visible.
4.8: 'I will put cords on you so that you cannot turn from side to side until you have completed the days of your siege.' The immobilization that was announced in 3:25 is now specified as part of the sign-act: the physical constraint on Ezekiel's body enacts the constraint of the siege on Jerusalem. The immobility is simultaneously the prophet's sign-act and his personal bearing of the city's judgment.
4.9: The mixed-grain bread: chita useorah uful ve'adashim udochan vekhussemeth — wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and emmer wheat. This is siege-ration food — the mixture of available grains that a population under siege would bake when normal supplies were exhausted. The bread of distress (cf. Isa 30:20 'bread of distress and water of affliction') is the physical diet of the sign-act period, connecting the prophet's 390-day bodily experience to the lived experience of a besieged city.
4.10: Twenty shekels of food per day (esrim shekel, ʿeśrîm šeqel) — approximately 8 oz. / 225 grams, a starvation ration. The measured weight and set intervals (meit ad eit tochalennu, mēʿēt ʿad ʿēt tôʾkĕlennû) impose the discipline of famine proportioning on the prophet. The careful measurement dramatizes the systematic exhaustion of food supplies in a siege.
4.11: Water rationed at one-sixth of a hin (šiššît hahîn) — approximately 0.5 liters per day, severely inadequate for normal functioning. The combined food and water rations picture total deprivation: a person on these quantities would be visibly weakening, which is the visible sign. The prophet's deteriorating body is the living prophecy of Jerusalem's deterioration under siege.
4.12: Baked over gallelei tseat ha-adam (gĕlālê ṣēʾat hāʾādām): 'discs of human excrement.' The ultimate ritual defilement for a priest: cooking over human waste. The orderly, detail-oriented priest who has spent his life maintaining purity distinctions is commanded to violate the deepest levels of priestly cleanness. This enacts the defilement of the exile itself — Israel driven among the nations will eat defiled (tameh) bread, unable to maintain ritual purity in diaspora conditions.
4.13: 'This is how the Israelites will eat their defiled bread among the nations to which I will scatter them.' The sign-act explains its own meaning: the prophet's defilement in the sign = the nation's defilement in exile. The verb adam (ʾādām) in context: ṭāmēʾ (defiled/unclean) is the technical priestly term (Lev 11:43) — what Ezekiel enacts in the sign, the exiles will live in the nations where maintaining ritual purity is impossible.
4.14: Ezekiel protests (achah Adonai YHWH, ʾăhāh ʾădōnāy Yhwh): 'Ah, Lord GOD!' — the formula of prophetic protest (Jer 1:6; 4:10; 14:13). His protest centers on his priestly cleanness: from youth to the present (mineuray ve'ad atah) he has never eaten what died naturally or was mauled — never eaten nevelah (carrion) or terefa (torn flesh), both prohibited in Lev 11 and 17. The protest is not disobedience but priestly integrity — the covenant-keeper for whom defilement is not just distasteful but self-defining.
4.15: Divine concession: cattle dung (tsapat habaqar, ṣĕpieʿ habbāqār) instead of human dung. The divine accommodation to the prophet's priestly integrity allows a less ritually defiling fuel (cattle dung was commonly used as fuel in the ancient Near East). The concession does not annul the sign but modifies its medium — the defilement is still present (impure fuel for a priest) but not at the maximum level. God's responsiveness to the prophet's conscientious objection is itself a theological datum.
4.16: 'I will break the staff of bread (mateh lechem, maṭṭēh leḥem) in Jerusalem.' The staff of bread — bread as the support/staff that sustains life (Lev 26:26; Ps 105:16; Isa 3:1) — will be broken: the food supply will be cut off. The inhabitants will eat their rationed bread with dread (deagah, dĕʾāgâ) and drink their measured water in horror (shamemah, šāmĕmâ). The emotional states (dread, horror) as well as the physical deprivation will mark the siege-life of Jerusalem.
4.17: 'They will lack bread and water, look at each other in horror (nashammu ish veachiv, nāšammû ʾîš wĕʾāḥîw: they will be desolate, each man with his brother), and waste away (nemaqu, nāmaqû) because of their iniquity.' The final outcome: communal horror and individual wasting. Māqaq (to rot, waste away) is used for physical decay — the iniquity that caused the siege now causes the physical deterioration of the besieged. The connection between moral state and physical condition is explicit: they waste away because of their iniquity, not merely because of food shortage.

5.1: taar (taʿar): 'razor/sharp sword.' The command to use a sword as a barber's razor over head and beard is a multi-layered sign: (1) shaving the head and beard was a sign of extreme mourning and humiliation in the ancient Near East (Isa 15:2; 22:12; Jer 47:5); (2) the barber's razor over a prophet's head enacts the divine judgment 'cutting off' the people (cf. Isa 7:20: YHWH will shave with a hired razor — the king of Assyria — the head and the legs and the beard); (3) charba (ḥarbâ, sword) used as a razor: judgment-instrument as grooming-instrument. The scales (moznaim, mōznayim) to divide the hair into equal thirds introduces the tripartite judgment.
5.2: The three thirds: burn a third in fire in the middle of the city (when the siege days are complete); take a third and cut with the sword around it; scatter a third to the wind. The tripartite judgment pattern — fire (plague/famine death within the city), sword (those killed in the siege/battles), and scatter-to-wind (exile) — is the comprehensive instrument of covenant judgment (Lev 26:25, 33). The sword as a second pursuit after the scattered third: even in exile the judgment follows. The thirds structure becomes Ezekiel's characteristic judgment formula (5:12; 6:12).
5.3: A small number of hairs tucked into the fold of the cloak (kenafecha, kĕnāp̄êkā: 'your skirts/wings'). The remnant theme: a small quantity preserved within the judgment. Kānāp̄ (wing/skirt/corner) is the same word used for Boaz spreading his wing/cloak over Ruth (Ruth 3:9) — the covering of divine protection. The remnant tucked in the cloak is the protected minority, the preserved seed within the judgment.
5.4: 'Take some of those very hairs and throw them into the fire to burn; from that fire a flame will spread to all the house of Israel.' Even the preserved remnant does not escape entirely — some are thrown back into the fire, and from the burning remnant the judgment spreads to all Israel. This is the most severe element of the sign: no fully safe remnant. The flame spreading from the remnant's burning to 'all the house of Israel' anticipates the post-586 judgments that will affect even those not directly caught in the Jerusalem siege.
5.5: tabur ha-aretz (ṭabbûr hāʾāreṣ): 'navel/center of the earth.' Ṭabbûr literally means 'navel' (also used for the highest point of a mountain, Judg 9:37). Jerusalem placed in the center (navel) of the nations expresses both the divine intention for Jerusalem as the hub of the nations' instruction (Isa 2:2-4; Mic 4:1-3) and the special culpability that follows from privilege: the city placed at the center of divine dealings with the world has the greatest responsibility and therefore the greatest accountability for failure.
5.6: 'But she has rebelled against my laws more than the nations, and defied my statutes more than the surrounding countries.' The comparative: Jerusalem's wickedness exceeds the pagans'. The nations surrounding Jerusalem did not have the law; Jerusalem had it and defied it. This is the logic of Rom 2:17-24 (the Jew who has the law and breaks it dishonors God more than the Gentile who does not have it) and Jesus's woes on Chorazin and Bethsaida (Matt 11:21-22: worse than Tyre and Sidon in judgment because they had more light).
5.7: 'Because you have exceeded (hamonem, hamōnĕkem) the nations around you in rebellion — you have not followed my statutes, kept my ordinances, or even lived up to the standards of the nations surrounding you.' The triple failure: statutes, ordinances, even pagan ethical standards. The final point is most stinging: Israel has not even maintained the moral standards of pagans. The nations have natural law (Rom 2:14-15); Israel has natural law plus revealed law, and has violated both. Ḥuqqôt (statutes) and mišpāṭîm (ordinances) are the two primary covenant-law categories of Deuteronomy.
5.8: 'I — yes, I myself (ani ani, ʾănî ʾănî) — am against you' (hineni eleich, hinnĕnî ʾēlayik). The divine first-person double emphasis intensifies the direct confrontation: no intermediary, no proxy, YHWH himself is the adversary. This is the opposite of the covenant formula 'I will be your God' — when the covenant partner becomes the covenant-breaker, the God who was with them becomes the God who is against them. 'I will execute my judgments in your midst in the sight of the nations' — the public judgment that reverses the public blessing.
5.9: 'I will do to you what I have never done before and will never do again, on account of all your abominations.' The singularity of Jerusalem's judgment: unprecedented and unrepeatable. The hyperbole of singularity (also in Joel 2:2 and Matt 24:21 for the final tribulation) marks the watershed quality of the 586 BCE destruction. The claim that this has never been done before and will not be done again is not an absolute chronological assertion but a judgment of qualitative intensity — the closest analog in Israel's experience to the eschatological judgment.
5.10: 'Fathers will eat their sons within you, and sons will eat their fathers.' The covenant curse of cannibalism — the ultimate covenant curse of Lev 26:29 and Deut 28:53-57 (eating your own children in the siege). The physical act of siege-driven cannibalism is both the literal historical reality of siege warfare (Lamentations 4:10 records it happening in 586 BCE) and the covenant-curse vocabulary of Deuteronomy come to literal fulfillment. The survivors will be scattered to every wind — exile as the second stage of judgment after the internal destruction.
5.11: hay ani (ḥay ʾānî): 'as I live' — the divine oath formula using YHWH's own life as the guarantee. This is one of the most solemn forms of divine speech in the OT; it appears 16 times in Ezekiel (more than any other book). The divine oath that requires no external witness — YHWH swears by himself because there is no greater thing to swear by (Heb 6:13: since God had no one greater to swear by, he swore by himself). gara (gāraʿ): 'cut down/diminish' — 'my eye will not spare' (lōʾ tāḥûs ʿênî): the idiom for divine implacability (cf. 7:4, 9; 8:18; 9:5, 10).
5.12: The tripartite judgment statement in its fullest form: a third will die by plague and famine within you; a third will fall by the sword outside you; a third I will scatter to every wind, drawing a sword in pursuit. The comprehensive exhaustion of judgment: no exit left. The interior (plague/famine), the exterior (sword), and the diaspora (scatter) — the three spatial domains of the city (inside walls, outside walls, dispersed) — are all subject to divine judgment. No geographic location provides immunity.
5.13: 'Then my anger will be exhausted (yikhleh api, yikleh ʾappî), my fury will rest upon them (vahanachot chamati, wahanōḥōt ḥămātî) and find its end.' The divine anger has an end — it is not infinite or capricious but measured and teleological. Kālâ (to be completed, exhausted) indicates that the anger has a finite quantity matched to the sin. After the anger is spent, something new is possible. This restraint within divine judgment — even in the most severe oracle — preserves the possibility of the restoration that Ezekiel will prophesy in chapters 33-37.
5.14: 'I will make you a ruin and a disgrace (cherpah, ḥerpâ) among the surrounding nations, in the sight of all who pass by.' Ḥerpâh (reproach, disgrace) — the covenant curse of public shame before the nations (Deut 28:37). Jerusalem as a curse and a byword among the nations is the inversion of its calling to be a blessing (Gen 12:3). The public nature of the judgment ('in the sight of all who pass by') mirrors the public nature of Jerusalem's privilege: the city placed at the center of the nations falls publicly before the nations.
5.15: 'You will be a disgrace and a mockery (kelimah ugeduphah), a warning and a horror, to the surrounding nations.' The four terms for shame — disgrace, mockery, warning (musar, mûsār, also 'correction/discipline'), horror (shamamah, šammâ) — describe the reaction of the nations to Jerusalem's fall. The same terms will reverse when God restores Israel before the nations (36:30: no longer bring disgrace). The shame vocabulary is not merely descriptive but judicial: these are the formal covenantal penalties named in Deut 28:37 and Jer 24:9.
5.16: The deadly arrows of famine (chitsim hara'im hare'av, ḥiṣṣîm hārāʿîm hārāʿāb): 'the evil arrows of hunger/famine.' YHWH as archer, launching famine as arrows. The image of divine judgment as archery appears in Ps 38:2 (divine arrows sunk into the psalmist), Lam 3:12-13 (YHWH bent his bow and made the psalmist the target). The arrows aimed for destruction — lemashchit (lĕmašḥît): for destruction, for the Destroyer (the same root as the destroyer of the Passover, Exod 12:23).
5.17: The full catalog of judgment instruments: famine (raav), ferocious beasts (chayyah raah, ḥayyâ rāʿâ), childlessness from bereavement (shikhel, šiḵḵēl), pestilence (dever), bloodshed (dam), and the sword (charev). This is the complete covenant-curse arsenal of Leviticus 26: wild animals (26:22), pestilence (26:25), sword (26:25, 33), famine (26:26). All the covenant curses enumerated in Lev 26 are being sent upon Jerusalem simultaneously. 'I the LORD have spoken' (ʾănî Yhwh dibbartî) — the formula that seals the oracle and identifies its source as YHWH's own word.

18.1: Standard oracle formula: vayhiy devar-YHWH elai lemor — 'And the word of YHWH came to me saying.' The devar-YHWH (davar, H1697) formula occurs over 50 times in Ezekiel, more than any other book. It is both authorization and content — the word that comes is the word that creates reality.
18.2: bosér (H1155, 'sour/unripe grapes'): rare word appearing only 4× in the OT (Isa 18:5; Jer 31:29-30; Ezek 18:2). The proverb — fathers eat unripe grapes and the children's teeth are set on edge — asserts inherited guilt and transferred punishment. YHWH challenges this proverb directly, not because corporate identity is false but because it was being used to deny personal accountability. The same proverb appears in Jer 31:29-30 in the new-covenant context.
18.3: chay ani (ḥay ʾānî, 'as I live') — the divine oath formula. YHWH swears by his own life since there is no greater: the cessation of the proverb is guaranteed by divine self-commitment. This formula (unique to Ezekiel and Numbers) carries the force of the most solemn oath: YHWH's existence is pledged to the truth of what follows.
18.4: kol-hanephashoth li hen ('all souls/persons belong to me — both'): nephesh (H5315, dispute_level 3) here means 'person' rather than the interior soul. The dispute in translation centers on whether nephesh is the animating breath-life, the whole person, or the appetite/desire. In this legal-accountability context, it is the person as a moral agent. hanephesh hachot'et hi tamut ('the soul/person who sins, that one shall die'): the stark individual liability principle. tamut from mut (H4191, die) — covenant death, not merely biological.
18.5: tsaddiq (H6662, 'righteous/just one'): the righteous man is defined by what follows — a list of social, sexual, and cultic behaviors. Tsaddiq in Hebrew is primarily relational-covenantal: one who acts in accordance with the obligations of the covenant relationship (with YHWH and neighbor), not merely one with good inner character. The list that follows is a torah-like checklist of covenant fidelity.
18.6: lo achal al-heharim ('he has not eaten at the mountain shrines'): cultic separation from idolatry. lo nissa einav el-gillulei beit yisrael ('he has not lifted his eyes to the idols of the house of Israel'): gillulim (H1544, Ezekiel's characteristic word for idols) is a derogatory term related to galal (dung/dung pellets) — a deliberately contemptuous designation. Ezekiel uses gillulim 38 times (more than all other OT books combined). lo timme ('he has not defiled') — tame (H2930, be unclean/defiled): ritual-moral contamination vocabulary from Leviticus applied to sexual ethics.
18.7: chavlato ('his pledge/garment given as pledge'): chabol (H2254) means both 'to pledge' and 'to bind/destroy.' The returning of a pledge (especially a cloak taken as security for a debt, which Exod 22:25-26 and Deut 24:12-13 forbid keeping overnight) is the concretely poor person's protection. lachem nathen ('given food to the hungry') and erOM yechaseh ('covered the naked'): covenant welfare obligations derived from Deut 15 and Lev 19 — these are not works-of-supererogation but required covenant acts.
18.8: beneshech lo yiten ('he has not lent at interest/usury'): neshek (H5392, 'bite/usury') — the word's root is 'to bite,' vividly depicting interest as the creditor's bite into the debtor. Lending at interest to fellow Israelites was prohibited (Exod 22:25; Lev 25:36-37; Deut 23:19-20). mishpat emet asah ('he has rendered honest/true judgment'): emet (H571, truth/faithfulness) — judgment that corresponds to reality rather than being distorted by bribery or favoritism (Lev 19:15).
18.9: behuqqotai halach ('he has walked in my statutes'): halak (H1980, walk) as a covenantal metaphor for ongoing moral conduct — a Deuteronomic pattern (Deut 8:6; 10:12; 11:22). chayyoh yichyeh ('he shall surely live'): the infinitive absolute construction (chayoh yichyeh) emphasizes absolute certainty — this person will certainly, without qualification, live. Chayah (H2421, live) in this covenant context means covenantal life in YHWH's presence, not merely physical survival. Cf. Lev 18:5 ('the one who does them will live by them').
18.10: ben paritz ('a son who is violent/a bandit'): paritz (H6530, violent one, robber) — the son's character is established by a single descriptor before the list of offenses. The contrast with the righteous father creates the generational counter-case: righteousness does not automatically transmit. shofech dam ('who sheds blood'): shaphak dam (H8210 + H1818) — the most serious covenant violation; blood is YHWH's domain (Gen 9:6; Deut 19:10).
18.11: The wicked son reverses each covenant obligation the righteous father kept. The structure is antiphonal: father 'does not eat at the mountain shrines' — son 'eats at the mountain shrines'; father 'does not defile his neighbor's wife' — son 'defiles.' The moral catalogue of ch 18 is among the most systematic presentations of covenant ethics in the prophets, approaching the form of a legal code (cf. the Decalogue and Lev 19).
18.12: ani ve-evyon ('poor and needy'): the hendiadys pairing of ani (H6041, afflicted/humble/poor) and evyon (H34, needy/destitute) appears across the Psalms and prophets as the paradigmatic vulnerable person whom the covenant protects (Ps 72:12-13; Isa 41:17; Amos 8:4). Oppressing this pair defines the wicked man as the covenant's antithesis.
18.13: beneshech natan vetarbit laqach ('he has lent at interest and taken profit'): both neshek (H5392, interest/usury, lit. 'bite') and tarbit (H8636, increase/profit on a loan) appear together here — the legal doublet covers all forms of financial exploitation of the poor. The verdict: mot yumat — damo bo yihyeh ('he shall surely die — his blood is upon himself'). The death is judicial: YHWH holds the debt of blood for covenant violations.
18.14: The third-generation case: a son who observes his father's wickedness, vayar ('and he sees/considers') — the seeing is morally active, not passive observation. velo yaaseh kahen ('and does not do likewise'): the son's non-conformation to the father's pattern is itself a moral achievement, enabled by the seeing and considering. This counters fatalism about moral inheritance.
18.15: The third-generation righteous son's list mirrors the first-generation righteous father's list (vv.6-9) nearly verbatim — demonstrating that righteousness is a pattern that can recur across generations even when the intervening generation was wicked. Moral character is neither automatically inherited nor automatically blocked by parental failure.
18.16: lo hitziq ('he has not oppressed'): hitziq from yatzaq (H3238, oppress/wrong) or more likely atzaq — the not-taking of the pledge, not-robbing, giving food and clothing: the second-generation wicked son's exact opposites, the third generation now mirrors the first in covenant fidelity.
18.17: lo yamut beavon aviv ('he shall not die for the iniquity of his father'): avon (H5771, iniquity/guilt/punishment for iniquity) — the word covers both the act and its consequence. The son is not absolved of his father's avon on a technicality; rather, YHWH's justice is structured so that individual moral agency is the determining factor, not genealogical inheritance.
18.18: asher oshaq oshek ('because he practiced cruel oppression'): ashaq (H6231, oppress) doubled for emphasis — persistent, habitual oppression. gozal gazal ('robbed by violence') — gazal (H1497, rob/seize) similarly doubled. The father's death is entirely his own doing: beavono yamut ('in his iniquity he will die'). The covenant's moral economy is precise.
18.19: The people's objection (lama, 'why?') reflects cognitive dissonance with Deuteronomic theology: the tradition of Exod 20:5 (visiting iniquity on third and fourth generations) was understood by the exiles as explaining their suffering — but they were using it to escape personal accountability. YHWH's answer reframes the tradition: generational consequences operate at the level of community covenant identity, not individual forensic standing.
18.20: hanephesh hachot'et hi tamut ('the soul/person that sins — that one shall die'): the principle stated again for emphasis. tzidqat hatzaddiq alav tihyeh verishah harasha alav tihyeh ('the righteousness of the righteous belongs to him, and the wickedness of the wicked falls on him'): tzedaqah (H6666, righteousness/right-doing/right-standing) is not transferable; risha (H7564, wickedness/wrong-doing) is not inflicted on others. This is the bedrock principle of Ezekiel's moral theology.
18.21: vehu yashuv mikol chatotav ('and he turns from all his sins'): shuv (H7725, turn/return/repent) — the most important repentance verb in the OT. Appears 1,101× in the OT; Ezekiel uses it intensively. The turn is from (min) sins toward (el) YHWH's statutes. chayoh yichyeh lo yamut ('he shall surely live, he shall not die'): the double statement (positive then negative) is the strongest possible legal guarantee of life. The turning itself opens the forensic file of life.
18.22: kol-peshaav ('all his transgressions'): pesha (H6588, rebellion/transgression) — the most serious Hebrew sin term, denoting willful breach of a covenant relationship (as between vassal and suzerain). lo yizakeru lo ('will not be remembered against him'): zakar (H2142, remember) in a covenant context: YHWH's 'not remembering' is a covenantal act of amnesty, not mere forgetfulness. The forgiven pesha is sealed shut in the divine ledger — a promise with legal force.
18.23: hachapots achapots beMOT harasha ('do I really delight in the death of the wicked?'): the rhetorical question is divine self-revelation. chaphets (H2654, delight/desire/take pleasure in) — YHWH takes no pleasure in covenant death sentences; justice is not his goal, life is. im-lo veshuvo miderachav vechai ('rather that he should turn from his ways and live'): the divine will expressed in the im-lo ('is it not rather') construction. This verse is the theological heart of the chapter.
18.24: uveshuv hatzaddiq mitzidqato ('when the righteous person turns from their righteousness'): the counter-case: the righteous person who turns away forfeits their accumulated righteousness. kol-tzidqotav asher asah lo yizakeru ('none of their righteous deeds will be remembered'): the negative counterpart of v.22 — just as the forgiven sinner's record is cleared, the apostate's righteous record is wiped. The ledger is always current, not cumulative-permanent in either direction.
18.25: lo yitaken derech YHWH ('the way of the LORD is not fair/right'): the people's accusation against YHWH's justice. takan (H8505, to weigh out/measure/make right/be equitable) — used of accurate weights (Prov 16:2; 21:2; 24:12). The accusation is that YHWH's moral accounting is not properly balanced. YHWH reverses the charge: it is their ways that are not takan.
18.26: The principle restated: shuv from righteousness toward iniquity → death; the iniquity he commited (beavono asher asah) is the cause. YHWH's statement is not harsh but logical: if one chooses iniquity over the covenant relationship, one chooses the consequence.
18.27: uveshuv rasha merishtato ('when the wicked person turns from their wickedness'): risha (H7564, wickedness) → shuv (turn) → doing what is just and right (mishpat utzedaqah). hu yechayeh et-nafsho ('he will save his own life/soul'): literally 'he will keep his nephesh alive.' The turning person is the agent of their own life-keeping — YHWH's justice rewards the genuine turning.
18.28: vayyar vayashav ('because he has considered and turned'): the sequence of seeing-and-turning is the moral process Ezekiel envisions. ra'ah (H7200, see) here means moral reflection — taking stock of one's trajectory. The turning follows from genuine moral sight, which is why the wicked son of v.14 who 'sees and does not do likewise' is the positive counterpart.
18.29: The accusation repeated a second time: lo yitaken derech YHWH. YHWH's answer is consistent: he will judge each one by their own ways (ish kidrachav). The repetition (also in v.25) signals the depth of the dispute — this is not academic theology but the exiles' actual complaint against the justice of their situation.
18.30: shuvu vehashivu mikol-pisheichem ('repent and turn from all your transgressions'): the double imperative — shuv twice (repent! and turn!) — is the pastoral application of the theology. velo-yihyeh lachem lemichshol avon ('so that iniquity will not be your downfall'): michshol (H4383, stumbling-block/occasion of falling) — the accumulated iniquity that causes one to stumble. Repentance preempts the stumbling.
18.31: hashlichu mealeichem et-kol-pisheeichem ('cast away from you all your transgressions'): active imperative — the human agent must throw off the weight of accumulated rebellion. vaasu lachem lev chadash veruach chadasha ('make for yourselves a new heart and a new spirit'): the command that anticipates its own impossibility, resolved in 36:26-27 where YHWH promises to give what he here commands. The gap between command and ability is the pneumatological space that the new covenant fills.
18.32: ki lo achapots beMOT haMet ('for I have no pleasure in the death of anyone who dies'): haMet — 'the one who dies,' emphasizing that this is about actual deaths, not abstract principles. shuvu vechyu ('turn and live!'): the two-word summary of the entire chapter. Ezekiel's prophetic imperative at its briefest and most urgent. The entire chapter is a theological framework for the possibility and urgency of this simple command.

19.1: veath sa qinah ('raise up a lamentation/dirge'): qinah (H7015, formal dirge/elegy) — the distinctive Hebrew meter of 3+2 stresses associated with mourning (first systematically identified in Lamentations). Ezekiel's use of qinah-form for living kings is a prophetic death-announcement in literary form: to be the subject of a qinah is to be declared already dead in the prophetic vision. The form creates the reality it describes.
19.2: labiya (H3833, 'lioness'): the feminine form of lion, used for the mother who is also a figure of royal power. ben-ariot rabisat ('she lay down among lions'): ari (H738, lion) is the standard word for the mature male lion; labia is the mother. The royal house of Israel as a pride of lions draws on the Judah-lion blessing (Gen 49:9). gur (H1482, 'cub/whelp'): the young lion whose potential for royalty has not yet been tested.
19.3: vayyilmad litrop terep ('he learned to catch prey/to tear prey'): the education of the young king in the practice of power. tarap (H2963, to tear/rend) — predatory, violent acquisition of power. adam achal ('he devoured men'): the lioness/king who devours his own subjects — the parable indicts the predatory nature of Judah's late monarchy, contrasting with the Good Shepherd of ch 34.
19.4: vayishmehu goyim bashuachot nilkad ('the nations heard about him; he was caught in their pit'): shuachat (H7825, pit/trap/snare) — the pit that catches the lion is the Babylonian military trap. This refers to Jehoahaz (609 BCE), the first lion-cub, who was taken in chains to Egypt (2 Kgs 23:33-34). The nations that set the trap are Egypt in this first case, contrasting with Babylon in the second.
19.5: vatere ki nivchelah ('when she saw that her hope was deferred'): yachal (H3176, wait/hope) — the mother-lioness waiting for the return of the first cub, finally abandoning hope. tiqvatah aveda ('her hope was gone/lost'): tiqvah (H8615, hope/cord) — the same word as Rahab's scarlet cord (Josh 2:18): a thread of hope that can be cut. The mother's hope is extinguished by Jehoahaz's fate.
19.6: The second lion-cub (Jehoiachin or Zedekiah) learns the same predatory pattern. The repetition of the learning-sequence emphasizes the structural failure: not a one-off bad king but an institutional pattern of royal predation. vayyilmad litrop terep — 'he learned to catch prey': the same phrase as v.3, the educational cycle of violence repeating.
19.7: vayeda almanotav ('he ravaged their strongholds/widow-cities'): almanot can mean 'widows' or 'palaces/citadels' — the text may be deliberately ambiguous. The second interpretation (palaces/strongholds) fits the military context; the first (widows) accuses the king of preying on the most vulnerable. The ambiguity may be intentional: the king who destroys cities leaves behind widows.
19.8: vayitnu alav goyim miseviv mimdinot ('the nations gathered against him from the surrounding provinces'): the international coalition that closed in on the second cub. The spreading net (resheth, H7568) imagery: the lion is now prey, caught in what had previously been his hunting ground. The reversal of predator-to-prey is the poetic justice of the lamentation.
19.9: vayyitenuhu basugar bachachim ('they put him in a cage/enclosure in chains'): sugar (H5474, cage/enclosure for dangerous animals) — a royal lion caged is the ultimate humiliation. velo-nishma qolo od al-harei yisrael ('so that his voice would no longer be heard on the mountains of Israel'): the silencing of the royal voice is the defining loss. A king's authority is expressed through his voice commanding the realm; the cage ends the commands. Contrast: the voice of YHWH that speaks through the prophets cannot be caged.
19.10: imecha kagefen bedamecha ('your mother was like a vine in your vineyard/among the waters'): a second allegory begins: the lioness-mother is now reimagined as a vine. gefen (H1612, vine) — Israel as YHWH's vine is a fundamental OT metaphor (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7; Jer 2:21; John 15:1). meme-mayim ('beside/among the waters'): the well-watered vine as image of prosperity — Israel's earlier flourishing under the Davidic monarchy.
19.11: vayihyu-lo mattot azim ('she had strong/mighty branches/rods'): matteh (H4294, rod/branch/staff/tribe) — the strong branches that serve as rulers' scepters. The word simultaneously means the ruling rod (scepter of authority) and the branch of the vine (natural growth). This double meaning is the allegory's point: the branches that grow naturally also become the instruments of royal rule. shevet moshel ('scepter/rod of a ruler'): shevet (H7626) is both tribal affiliation and governing rod — Gen 49:10's 'the scepter shall not depart from Judah.'
19.12: vatitash bachema ('she was torn up in anger/fury'): amar (H2534, fury/heat of anger) — divine wrath as the active force behind the Babylonian destruction. veruach haqqadim hobish piryo ('the east wind dried up her fruit'): ruach qadim (east wind) is the consistent metaphor for Babylon in Ezekiel (17:10; 19:12; 27:26) — the wind from the Mesopotamian desert/steppe that scorches and kills vegetation. The Babylonian conquest is the east wind that dries up the Davidic vine.
19.13: veata shetulatbemdbar bearetz tziyah vetzama ('now she is planted in the wilderness — in a dry and thirsty land'): the transplanted vine in the wilderness is the exile itself. tziyah (H6723, dry ground/drought) and tzama (H6772, thirst) create a merism of waterless desolation, the opposite of the well-watered condition of v.10. The covenant land's fruitfulness is lost; the exile is drought.
19.14: vatetzeh esh mimmatteh badeha ('fire has spread from one of her branches'): the fire within the vine — internal destruction. This may refer to Zedekiah's rebellion against Babylon, which brought the final conflagration. ein-ba matteh-oz shevet lemoshel ('she has no strong branch left — no scepter/rod to rule with'): the Davidic monarchy's effective end. The lamentation is sealed with hi qinah vatehyi liqinah ('this is a lamentation and has been used as a lamentation'): the meta-statement about the genre — Ezekiel is aware he is composing liturgical mourning-poetry, and declares it will be used as such. The qinah outlives the events it mourns.

20.1: Precise dating: seventh year, fifth month, tenth day — about 591 BCE. anashim mizziqnei yisrael ('some of the elders of Israel'): zeqenim (H2205, elders) — the leadership delegation. lidrosh et-YHWH ('to inquire of/seek YHWH'): darash (H1875, inquire/seek/resort to) — the technical verb for prophetic consultation. YHWH refuses to be consulted by those who will not repent.
20.2: Standard messenger formula. The chapter that follows is Ezekiel's most extensive historical review — a covenant lawsuit (riv) tracing four generations of rebellion from Egypt to the present, in a structure that shows each generation receiving grace and abusing it, followed by divine restraint for the sake of the divine Name.
20.3: im-yiddaresh ani lachem ('I will not let myself be consulted by you'): the divine refusal to be 'inquired of' (darash) by those who continue in idolatry. This is not divine accessibility — it is divine integrity. YHWH does not provide oracular guidance while the inquirers maintain contradictory loyalties. The Niphal of darash here ('I will not be sought/consulted') is emphatic self-distancing.
20.4: hashophet otam ('will you judge them?'): YHWH asks Ezekiel to pronounce judgment by making known their fathers' abominations. The forensic vocabulary (shaphat, H8199, judge) positions the entire historical review as a legal indictment. hoda otam et-toavot avotam ('make known to them the abominations of their fathers'): toavah (H8441, abomination/detestable thing) — Ezekiel's other characteristic word for religious betrayal, alongside gillulim.
20.5: vaevachar beyisrael ('I chose Israel'): bachar (H977, choose/select) — the divine election vocabulary. vaessa yadi ('I raised my hand/swore'): the oath gesture — raising the hand is the physical accompaniment of the most solemn oath. YHWH's choosing of Israel is grounded in sovereign grace, not Israel's merit (Deut 7:6-8). lezara beit-yaaqov ('to the seed/offspring of the house of Jacob'): the election reaches back to the patriarchal promise.
20.6: eretz zavat chalab udvash ('a land flowing with milk and honey'): the Exodus-formula for the Promised Land (Exod 3:8,17; Lev 20:24; Num 14:8; Deut 6:3 etc.) — occurring 20× in the Pentateuch. In Ezekiel's retrospective it becomes evidence of YHWH's gracious intent: the gift was always the goal. tzvi hi lechol haaratzot ('the most glorious/the glory of all lands'): tzvi (H6643, glory/beauty/gazelle) — the land as the beautiful prize, the destination of YHWH's desire for his people.
20.7: ish et-shiqutzei einav hashlichu ('each of you must cast away the detestable things of his eyes'): shiqutzim (H8251, detestable things/abominations) — idols described by what they do to the senses: they are things the eyes desire (einav). vebegillulei mitzraim al-titammeu ('do not defile yourselves with Egypt's idols'): the idolatry Israel must leave behind in Egypt is already in their hearts. The Exodus is as much an inner detachment from Egypt as a geographic departure.
20.8: vayamru lo nishma elecha ('but they rebelled against me and would not listen'): marah (H4784, rebel/be contentious) — the first rebellion, in Egypt itself, before the Exodus. The failure to cast away Egyptian idols (v.7) was not ignorance but refusal. eshpoch chamati ('I said I would pour out my fury'): shaphak (H8210, pour out) + chemah (H2534, fury/heat) — the covenant anger that was consistently restrained throughout the history.
20.9: vaase lema'an shemi ('but I acted for the sake of my name'): the theological pivot that recurs four times in this chapter (vv.9,14,22,44). shem (H8034, name) — YHWH's name is his reputation, his identity-in-relationship, his covenant self-disclosure. To act 'for the sake of my name' is to maintain covenant integrity even when the covenant partner has forfeited it. lehallel ('to be profaned'): chalal (H2490, profane/defile/pierce) — the Niphal: YHWH's name would be profaned if he destroyed Israel before the watching nations, for they would conclude his power was limited.
20.10: vaotzi otam me-eretz mitzraim ('so I brought them out of Egypt'): yatsa (H3318, go out/bring out) in the Hiphil — the Exodus verb. The word appears throughout the chapter (vv.6,9,10,14,22,34,38,41,42) as the theological backbone: YHWH's primary redemptive act is the bringing-out, and this chapter recasts it as the beginning of a pattern that will be repeated in the new exodus from Babylon.
20.11: vaeten lahem et-chuqqotai ('I gave them my statutes'): choq (H2706, statute/engraving) — the word for law as something engraved or incised, fixed and permanent. Alongside mishpatim (H4941, ordinances/judgments — from shaphat, to judge). asher yaaseh otam haadam vachai bahem ('which if a person does them, he will live by them'): the Lev 18:5 citation — the principle that the law contains life within it; doing the law is the path to life. Paul cites this in Gal 3:12 and Rom 10:5 to contrast with the righteousness of faith.
20.12: vegam et-shabbetotai natati lahem ('I also gave them my sabbaths'): shabbat (H7676, sabbath/rest) — the gift vocabulary is striking: the sabbath is not a burden imposed but a grace bestowed. lihyot leot beni uveinam ('as a sign between me and them'): ot (H226, sign/token/signal/wonder) — the sabbath as the distinguishing mark of the covenant, the visible indicator of the YHWH-Israel relationship in time (Exod 31:13-17). ledet ki ani YHWH meqaddisham ('so they would know that I am YHWH who sanctifies them'): qadash (H6942, be holy/sanctify/set apart) — YHWH as the sanctifier; the sabbath is the temporal enactment of Israel's holiness.
20.13: vayimredu vi beit yisrael ('but the house of Israel rebelled against me'): the rebellion in the wilderness — the Sinai-to-Kadesh generation. veet-shabbetotai chilelu mead ('they profaned my sabbaths greatly'): chalal (H2490, profane) + meod (greatly/exceedingly) — the sabbath violation as particularly significant because the sabbath is the sign of the covenant; profaning it attacks the visible token of the relationship itself.
20.14: vaase lema'an shemi ('but I acted for the sake of my name'): second occurrence of the name-protection motif. The restraint of the justified divine wrath 'for the sake of the name' is the OT foundation for Paul's theology of divine forbearance in Rom 3:25-26 — God passing over former sins during the period before the cross.
20.15: vegam ani nasati yadi lahem bamidbar ('but I also swore in the wilderness'): the second oath, negative — not to bring them into the land. The land that YHWH described positively in v.6 (tzvi... zavat chalab udvash) is now withheld from the rebellious generation. The greatness of the gift makes the withholding more pointed.
20.16: ki bemishpatai maasu ('because they despised my ordinances'): maas (H3988, despise/reject/refuse) — the active rejection of the covenant's defining content. velibam holech acharei gilluleihem ('their hearts were set after/followed their idols'): lev (H3820, heart) — the directing of the inner person after gillulim (H1544, idols/dung-pellets). The heart going after idols is the covenant betrayal that explains every external violation.
20.17: vatachos eini alehem mishachet ('nevertheless my eye had pity on them, not destroying them'): chus (H2347, spare/have compassion on) — the unexpected mercy that interrupts the pattern of rebellion-judgment. YHWH's pity is not earned; it appears as a grace-moment within the judgment narrative. This recurs throughout the chapter as the divine counter-pattern to human unfaithfulness.
20.18: vaomar el-beneihem bamidbar ('and I said to their children in the wilderness'): the second generation receives direct instruction after the first generation is barred from the land. The instruction begins with negative commands: behuqqot avoteichem al-telechu ('do not follow your fathers' statutes') — the children are warned against inheriting their parents' patterns, not against the covenant itself.
20.19: ani YHWH eloheichem ('I am YHWH your God'): the Decalogue's opening formula (Exod 20:2; Deut 5:6) — the covenant identity declaration that grounds all the following commands. bechukotai telechu ('walk in my statutes'): halak (H1980, walk) as the metaphor for conduct — life as movement in a direction, with YHWH's statutes as the path.
20.20: et-shabbetotai qaddoshu ('keep my sabbaths holy'): qadash in Piel (H6942, to sanctify/treat as holy) — the human responsibility to treat the sabbath as the holy thing YHWH has made it. vehayo leot beni uveinechem ('they will be a sign between me and you'): the sabbath as the covenant-identity marker appears in this chapter's context as the single most important distinguishing practice — its observance or neglect becomes the synecdoche for covenant fidelity or betrayal.
20.21: The second generation rebels as the first did — the structural repetition shows the depth of the problem. Neither the punishment of the first generation nor the explicit instruction (vv.18-20) breaks the cycle. veshabbetotai chilelu ('they profaned my sabbaths'): the sabbath-profanation recurs. The pattern cannot be broken by law, instruction, or punishment — which sets up the new covenant promise of 36:26-27 where YHWH says he will act internally rather than externally.
20.22: vaasef et-yadi ('but I withdrew my hand'): the restraint of divine judgment — 'pulling back' the hand raised for punishment. Third occurrence of the name-protection motif. YHWH's consistency in holding back judgment 'for the sake of my name' across three generations reveals the patient divine pedagogy: the repeated offer of repentance, the repeated restraint of deserved judgment.
20.23: gam ani nasati et-yadi lahem bamidbar lehafitz otam bagoyim ('but I also swore to scatter them among the nations'): the counter-oath — the promise of judgment-through-scattering that Lev 26:33 and Deut 4:27; 28:64 established. The dispersion is not an accident of history but a covenant consequence. The exile is therefore not YHWH's abandonment of his people but the execution of a covenantal clause that Israel itself agreed to (Deut 27-28).
20.24: veyaen bemishpatai lo asu ('because they had not carried out my ordinances'): the three-part indictment — rejected mishpatim, despised huqqim, profaned shabbatot, and hearts after fathers' idols. The sabbath-profanation appears again as the lead indicator: to violate the sign is to dissolve the relationship it signifies.
20.25: vegam ani natati lahem huqqim lo tovim ('I also gave them statutes that were not good'): this troubling verse refers to YHWH giving over Israel to the consequences of their own rebellious choices — 'statutes not good' meaning the idolatrous practices they already followed, which YHWH 'gave' them by withdrawing his restraining hand. The 'statutes by which one cannot live' are the self-destructive practices of the nations (cf. Rom 1:24,26,28: God 'gave them over' to their desires).
20.26: vatimme otam bematnotam ('I made them unclean through their own offerings'): the defilement by one's own offerings — particularly child sacrifice (behaavir kol-peter rechem, 'in causing every firstborn to pass through the fire'). This is the bitter irony: what they intended as worship YHWH counted as self-contamination. lema'an ashem ('so that they might be desolate/appalled'): shamem (H8074, be desolate/appalled) — the horror of seeing one's own actions lead to desolation.
20.27: uven bigedu vi avoteichem ('your fathers committed treachery against me'): baged (H898, act treacherously/faithlessly) — the covenant betrayal vocabulary from the marriage metaphors of Hosea and Jeremiah. maalo maalu vi ('they committed unfaithfulness against me'): maal (H4603, act treacherously/commit trespass) — the doubling intensifies: persistent, habitual covenant unfaithfulness.
20.28: vaaviEM el-haaretz ('when I brought them into the land'): the transition from wilderness to settlement — the land-gift that should have ended the cycle. Instead: kol-givah ramah ('every high hill') and kol-etz avot ('every leafy tree') become sites of sacrifice. The land that was to be the locus of pure YHWH-worship becomes instead the expansion of Canaanite high-place practice.
20.29: mah-habamah asher atem habaim sham ('what is this high place to which you go?'): YHWH's question about the bamah — the word-play on bama (H1116, high place) may be connected to ba (H935, come/go) to suggest 'the place-you-go-to.' The name Bamah sticks ('its name has been called Bamah to this day') as an indictment: the illicit worship site has become a named landmark of disobedience.
20.30: hagedrekei avoteichem atem nitmaim ('are you making yourselves unclean in the same way as your fathers?'): the rhetorical question with a known answer. acharei shiqutzeihem atem zonim ('do you go whoring after their detestable things?'): zanah (H2181, whore/commit fornication/be unfaithful) — the marriage-covenant vocabulary: idolatry as adultery. This connects to Ezekiel's extended use of sexual-unfaithfulness imagery for religious betrayal (chs 16, 23).
20.31: uvehaniash mattanotechem ('when you present your gifts'): the child-sacrifice language recurs (behaavir benaichem baesh). im-yiddaresh ani lachem ('I will not be consulted by you'): the refusal to respond to hypocritical inquiry, as in v.3. The chapter's frame: the elders came to inquire; YHWH refuses; he instead reviews their history to explain why the consultation is invalid.
20.32: vehaaolah al-ruchechem hayoh lo tihyeh ('what you are thinking will not happen'): ruach (H7307, spirit/breath/wind) here used for 'thought/mind.' The people's desire to be like the nations (kegoyim) and worship wood and stone is a direct repudiation of Israel's covenant calling (Deut 4:5-8; 14:2). YHWH refuses this self-assimilation: the calling cannot be discarded.
20.33: chay ani — the divine oath. beyad chazaqah uvizroa netuyah ('with a mighty hand and outstretched arm'): the Exodus formula (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19 etc.) — the most recognizable shorthand for YHWH's redemptive power. The same mighty hand and outstretched arm that brought Israel out of Egypt will govern their return from exile. uvecheimah shefuchah ('and with outpoured fury'): the return will not be without judgment; the new exodus passes through a wilderness of accountability before reaching restoration.
20.34: vehozeti etchem min-haammim ('I will bring you out from the peoples'): yatsa (H3318) again — the bringing-out verb of the Exodus applied to the return from exile. The new exodus pattern is explicit: the same mighty hand, the same bringing-out, the same journey toward the covenant relationship — but after a severe discipline.
20.35: vahaveti etchem el-midbar haamim ('I will bring you into the wilderness of the peoples'): the new exodus will include a new wilderness — a place of divine confrontation before the land is entered. venispot etchem sham panim el-panim ('and there I will contend with you face to face'): panim el-panim — the direct personal confrontation (cf. Gen 32:30, Jacob wrestling with God face to face). The wilderness of peoples is a space for the covenant lawsuit to be prosecuted personally.
20.36: kaasher nispot et-avoteichem bemidbar eretz mitzraim ('as I contended with your fathers in the wilderness of Egypt'): the wilderness of the nations will replicate the Sinai wilderness experience — a second giving of the covenant. The new exodus recapitulates the first; the God who acted then will act again in the same pattern.
20.37: vehaaveti etchem tachat hashevet ('I will cause you to pass under the shepherd's rod'): shevet (H7626, rod/staff/tribe/scepter) — the shepherd's counting-rod. In ancient practice, sheep passed under the rod one by one for counting and inspection (Lev 27:32). uveheveti bemasoret habrit ('and I will bring you into the bond/chain of the covenant'): massoret (H4562, chain/bond) — the covenant as a binding obligation. The image is both protective (flock under the shepherd's care) and obligating (the covenant that binds both parties).
20.38: uvaroti mikem hamordim vehamopeshim bi ('I will purge out from among you the rebels and those who sin against me'): barar (H1305, purge/purify/select) — the purging of the dross from the refined metal; the same term appears in Mal 3:3 (the refiner's fire). The return is selective: the rebels will be removed before the restored community enters the land. The exodus-through-the-wilderness produces a purified remnant.
20.39: The bitter irony of YHWH's concession: if you will not listen, at least stop defiling his name with your gillulim. The minimum demand — if you cannot stop idolatry, at least do not mix it with YHWH-worship. This is not approval of idolatry but the final statement before the positive turn of vv.40-44: half-hearted worship is worse than straightforward defection because it pollutes the name.
20.40: bebhar qodshi ('on my holy mountain'): the eschatological gathering point. sham yaaveduni kol-beit yisrael kuloh ('there all the house of Israel — all of it — will serve me'): the restored worship is complete-Israel worship: no division, no partial remnant — kuloh ('all of it') — the healing of the division between north and south, the ingathering of all the dispersed. sham eratzehem ('there I will accept them'): ratzah (H7521, be pleased with/accept/satisfy a debt) — divine acceptance of the restored offering.
20.41: berei'ach nichoach ertzechem ('I will accept you as a pleasing aroma'): rei'ach nichoach (H7381 + H5207) — the 'pleasing aroma' of acceptable sacrifice (Gen 8:21; Exod 29:18,25,41; Lev 1:9,13,17 etc.) — the technical term for what makes an offering acceptable to YHWH. The restored people are themselves the pleasing sacrifice. venikdashti bachem leeinei hagoyim ('I will show my holiness through you before the nations'): the priestly-national vocation of Israel (Exod 19:5-6) is fulfilled in the restoration: YHWH is sanctified before the nations through the visible restoration of his people.
20.42: vidate'u ki ani YHWH ('you will know that I am YHWH'): the recognition formula that runs through Ezekiel — in exile the Israelites will know YHWH because of the judgment; in restoration they will know him because of the redemption. The same divine Name reveals itself through both modes of action.
20.43: ushantechem sham et-darkechem ('there you will remember your ways'): zakar (H2142, remember) — the memory of covenant failure is a gift, not a burden: it contextualizes the grace of restoration. veniqottem bifinechem ('you will loathe yourselves'): qot (H6962, loathe/feel disgust) — the repentance that comes not from fear of punishment but from seeing one's own past against the backdrop of received grace. This is the repentance the restored community will experience after the new exodus.
20.44: vide'etem ki ani YHWH baasoti itchem lema'an shemi ('you will know that I am YHWH when I have dealt with you for my name's sake'): the fourth occurrence of the name-protection motif. The chapter closes with the same divine logic that has governed it throughout: YHWH acts consistently for the sake of his name/character/covenant integrity. velo kederacheichem haraim ('and not according to your evil ways'): the restoration is entirely YHWH's initiative, not earned by the people's improved behavior. The new covenant operates from grace, not merit.
20.45: New oracle formula: the word of YHWH turns to a brief southern-fire oracle (vv.45-49 in English numbering). This short oracle serves as a transitional appendix to ch 20 and a prelude to the sword oracles of ch 21.
20.46: pane darkecha teiman ('set your face toward the south'): teiman (H8486, south/Negev) — the southern direction toward Jerusalem and Judah from Ezekiel's Babylonian location. hatteph el-derech negev ('speak/drip toward the south'): nataph (H5197, drip/drop/prophesy) — used for prophetic utterance that falls like rain-drops (Amos 7:16; Mic 2:6,11). The image of prophecy as dripping/dropping carries the sense of measured, accumulating utterance.
20.47: vehitzi baesh ('I will kindle a fire in you'): the forest-fire oracle — ya'ar hanegev (the forest of the Negev) is an unusual designation, since the Negev is characteristically treeless desert; the forest imagery may be allegorical for Jerusalem's population (cf. ch 21 where the 'forest' becomes the sword oracle against Judah). kol-panim mineggev tzaphona ('every face from south to north'): comprehensive — the fire leaves no face unscorched, corresponding to the comprehensive sword-judgment to follow.
20.48: verayu kol-basar ki ani YHWH bilati oto ('all flesh will see that I the LORD have lit this fire; it will not be quenched'): the universal witness formula. The unquenchable fire is the inescapable divine judgment; the prophet's word sets it; YHWH's name ensures it cannot be extinguished by human countermeasures.
20.49: va-aMaR ah adonai YHWH hemah omerim li ('then I said: Ah, Lord GOD! They are saying of me'): Ezekiel's complaint — his hearers dismiss the oracle as merely parabolic (moshel meshalim hu, 'he is a teller of parables'). The prophet is accused of speaking in riddles rather than plain prophecy. This reaction is itself a fulfillment of 33:31-32: hearing words but not acting on them, treating prophecy as entertainment. The chapter that began with YHWH refusing to be consulted ends with the prophet being dismissed as a parable-teller.

11.1: wayyissaʾeni ruach — "the Spirit lifted me up" — the verb nasaʾ (lift/carry) with ruach as subject is Ezekiel's standard transport-formula (also 3:12, 14; 8:3; 43:5). Ruach here cannot be translated "wind" as in natural contexts — the prophetic transport is the Spirit's agency. The gate facing east where Ezekiel lands is the same gate through which YHWH's glory will return in 43:1-4.
11.2: The two men and their twenty-five colleagues — likely military and civic leaders of Jerusalem — are singled out by name (Jaazaniah and Pelatiah). The naming in prophetic vision is the OT equivalent of the public accusation; these are not symbolic figures but real officials whose counsel is condemned as contrary to YHWH's purposes.
11.3: lo beqarov livnot batim — "it is not near to build houses" — the leaders' slogan denies the urgency of judgment. The implied meaning is: "we are safe in Jerusalem (the pot); the exile crowd is already gone." The pot metaphor (sir, cooking pot) is their image of security, which Ezekiel inverts: the pot is the place of cooking, not protection.
11.4: The divine command to prophesy (hinave, imperative piel of nava) against them is addressed to Ezekiel while still in the vision-state. The imperative is direct and immediate — piel aspect emphasizes the declared/prophetic nature of the speech act, distinguishing it from ordinary speech.
11.5: vatippol ʿalai ruach YHWH — "the Spirit of YHWH fell on me" — the verb nafal (fall) describes the sudden, overwhelming onset of prophetic inspiration. The same vocabulary appears for the Spirit falling on Saul (1 Sam 10:10) and Samson (Judg 14:6). The divine speech that follows ("thus says YHWH") is authorized by this Spirit-falling.
11.6: The accusation — you have multiplied your slain in this city — uses the perfect hirbatem (you have multiplied) as an accomplished fact. The fill-the-streets-with-corpses imagery (malletem ḥutsoteha ḥalal) is the judicial evidence that makes the leaders guilty of blood.
11.7: ʾasher natattem betokhah hem habasar vehi hasir vaʾatem hotsenu mitokhah — the wordplay reverses the leaders' slogan: yes, the city is a pot and you are in it — but the meat in the pot is the slain, not you; and you will be removed from it (the exile that is coming is not protection but expulsion). The grammatical stress on hem (they, emphatic) and vaʾatem (but you) creates a sharp contrast.
11.8: The fear they claim not to have (haḥerev yeratem) is what they will face: they feared the sword of enemies and so formed foreign alliances; YHWH will bring on them the very sword they tried to avoid. The irony of self-fulfilling judgment through policy choices is the structural point of vv7-8.
11.9: vehoseti etchem mitokha venatati etchem beyad zarim — the two-step judgment: extraction (hoseti, hiphil of yatsa, I will bring out) from the city and delivery (natati beyad zarim, I will give into the hand of strangers). The same verb pair (yatsa + natan beyad) appears throughout Judges and Kings for YHWH delivering Israel to enemies.
11.10: The border of Israel as the place of judgment — ʿal gevul Yisraʾel eshpet etchem — specifies the location: the frontier where Nebuchadnezzar caught and judged Zedekiah (2 Kgs 25:6-7; Jer 52:9-10). The prophecy is precise enough to be a prediction of an historical event, and precisely fulfilled.
11.11: The repeated declaration — hi lo tihyeh lachem lesir vaʾatem lo tihyu vasar betokhah — "this city will not be a pot for you and you will not be flesh within it" — is the definitive reversal of the leaders' slogan. The verb tihyeh/tihyu (imperfect of hayah) is a firm future declaration, not a possibility.
11.12: uveḥuqqot haggoyim asher sevivoteikhem ʿasitem — "you acted according to the statutes of the nations around you" — the word ḥuqqot (statutes/ordinances) used for pagan practices inverts the covenant formula: YHWH's ḥuqqim (statutes) were the law Israel was to follow; using ḥuqqot haggoyim condemns them for having a rival statutory code.
11.13: The death of Pelatiah (vayamat Pelatiah ven Benayahu) during the prophetic speech — while Ezekiel is still in the vision — is a sign-act death. Ezekiel's response (falling on his face, crying out) matches his response to the glory-vision in ch 1. The death within the vision corresponds to what will happen in history.
11.14: The transition to the consolation oracle — announced by the formula vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor (the word of YHWH came to me saying) — marks a new divine speech unit. The formula is the standard Ezekielian messenger-speech opener and signals a shift in tone.
11.15: The insulting slogan of the Jerusalem residents — riḥaqu meʿal YHWH lahem nittenah haʾarets lemorah — "they have gone far from YHWH; to us the land is given as a possession" — treats proximity to Jerusalem as proximity to YHWH. The perfect riḥaqu (they have gone far) is their assessment of the exiles; the divine response inverts it completely in what follows.
11.16: vehiʾi lahem lemigdash meʿat baʾaratsot asher baʾu sham — "I have been to them a little sanctuary in the countries where they have gone" — migdash meʿat (little/temporary sanctuary) is one of Ezekiel's most profound coinages. YHWH himself is the sanctuary in exile, replacing the temple as the place of divine presence. The term meʿat (a little, of minor extent) may be the divine humility of accompanying the exiles into Babylon.
11.17: The gathering-from-peoples promise — veqibbatsti etchem mehaʿamim veʾasafti etchem mehaʾaratsot — uses the same verb pair (qabats + ʾasaf, gather + collect) that Deuteronomy 30:3-4 uses for the eschatological regathering. The two verbs reinforce each other: qabats is the general term; ʾasaf (collect/harvest) adds the nuance of gathering what was scattered.
11.18: The promise of their cultic cleansing — vehesiru kol-shiqqutseyha ukhol-toʿavoteha mimennah — "they will remove all its detestable things and all its abominations from it" — uses shiqqutsim (detestable things, often idols) and toʿavot (abominations). Both terms are strong cultic-pollution vocabulary. The imperfect vehesiru is part of the sequential future narrative that follows the gathering promise.
11.19: venatati lahem lev ʾeḥad veruach ḥadashah etten beqirbam vehashirothi et-lev haʾeven mibesaram venatati lahem lev basar — the new-heart/new-spirit promise anticipates 36:26 almost word for word. Key terms: lev ʾeḥad (one heart — undivided loyalty, unlike the divided heart of v21); ruach ḥadashah (new spirit — not just reformed behavior but a new animating principle); lev haʾeven (heart of stone — the unresponsive covenant heart); lev basar (heart of flesh — responsive, feeling, alive to YHWH's word).
11.20: The covenant-formula conclusion — vehayu li leʿam vaʾani ʾehyeh lahem leʾlohim — "they shall be my people and I will be their God" — is the Sinaitic covenant formula in its simplest form (also Jer 31:33). The imperfects express ongoing covenant relationship. The formula occurs at the climax of restoration promises throughout the prophets.
11.21: The contrasting judgment clause — veʾel-lev shiqqutseyhem vetoʿavoteyhem libam holeikh — "as for those whose heart goes after their detestable things and their abominations" — the heart-going-after (holeikh, participle of halakh) the idols is the opposite of the one-heart promised in v19. The participial form describes an ongoing disposition.
11.22: The departure of the kavod (glory) — vayyissaʾu hakheruvim ʾet-kanfeyhem — the cherubim lifting their wings in preparation for departure is the signal that the kavod YHWH is about to move. The same verb nasaʾ (lift) used for Ezekiel being carried is used for the wings. The symmetry is deliberate: the prophet and the glory move together.
11.23: The kavod YHWH pausing ʿal-hahar ʾasher miqqedem laʿir — "on the mountain east of the city" — the Mount of Olives. This is the last resting place of the divine glory before it leaves for Babylon (implied in chs 10-11). The mountain east of the city is where the glory will return in 43:1-4 and where Zechariah 14:4 locates the eschatological theophany.
11.24: The Spirit-transport home — veruach nesaʾtni vateviʾeni khaldeʾyma — the same verb (nasaʾ) for the return trip. The whole vision journey is framed by Spirit-transport. The phrase bemar'eh beruach (in the vision by the Spirit) clarifies that the journey was visionary, not physical — an important marker in a book full of border-crossing between vision and reality.
11.25: The report to the exiles — vaʾadabber ʾel-haggolah ʾet kol-divrei YHWH — Ezekiel as messenger to the community. The term haggolah (the exile community/the deportees) is Ezekiel's standard designation for his audience. The act of speaking "all the words of YHWH" affirms the completeness of the prophetic report — nothing withheld from the community.

12.1: The standard word-event formula — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor — opens a new section. Ezekiel's book is organized around these divine-speech introductions; they mark the beginning of distinct oracular units. The formula occurs over 50 times in Ezekiel, more than any other prophetic book.
12.2: ben-ʾadam betokh beit-hameri ʾattah yoshev — "son of man, you are dwelling among a rebellious house" — beit hameri (house of rebellion) is Ezekiel's characteristic designation for Israel. Meri (rebellion) is a noun of action derived from marah (to be bitter/rebellious against authority). The phrase ʿeynayyim lahem lirʾot velo raʾu (they have eyes to see but do not see) echoes the Isaianic hardening formula (Isa 6:9-10) and is cited by Jesus in Mark 4:12 and John 12:40.
12.3: ʿaseh lekha kelei golah — "prepare for yourself exile-gear" — kelei (implements/gear) is a general term for personal equipment. The instruction to pack in front of them during the day and then move out at evening creates the visual narrative of deportation. The sign-act performs the coming exile before it happens.
12.4: The two stages of the baggage-carrying sign-act — during the day (yomam) and at dusk (beʿerev) — correspond to the stages of exile: the visible departure and the night-flight that Zedekiah will make (2 Kgs 25:4). The dusk/night timing is historically specific to Zedekiah's attempted escape.
12.5: beqir ḥatsor-lekha vehotseta vo — "dig through the wall before their eyes and go out through it" — the wall-digging sign-act represents the hole in the city wall through which Zedekiah's party escaped. The Hebrew ḥatsar means to dig/pierce; the action symbolizes the forced breach of the city's defenses.
12.6: bevalayl tatse — "go out at night" — the nighttime departure is the specific circumstance of Zedekiah's flight. The covering of the face (kaseh ʿal-paneikha) is interpreted in v12 as the prince covering his face so as not to see the land with his eyes — a reference to Zedekiah's blinding (2 Kgs 25:7). The sign-act precedes the event it symbolizes by months or years. The term mofet (wonder/portent) for Ezekiel himself is significant: he does not merely announce but embodies the prophetic word.
12.7: The faithful execution of the sign-act — vaʾaʿas keʾasher tsuvviti (I did as I was commanded) — is Ezekiel's standard response to divine commands. The Hebrew imperfect-consecutive narrative (vav-consecutive + imperfect) creates the sequential action report. The obedient execution is as much a part of the prophetic act as the verbal oracle.
12.8: Morning brings the word again — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai beboqer — the divine speech frames the sign-act. The night-departure is followed immediately by the morning-explanation, creating a close prophetic discourse unit.
12.9: The house of Israel's question — halo ʾamru ʾeleikha beit Yisraʾel beit hameri ma ʾattah ʿoseh — "has not the house of Israel said to you: what are you doing?" — the rhetorical question anticipates their puzzlement and sets up the explanation. The beit hameri (rebellious house) epithet attached to the questioners colors their question with the very attitude that makes the sign necessary.
12.10: hanasi hamassa hazze biYerushalaim — "this oracle concerns the prince in Jerusalem" — hanasi (the prince/leader) is Ezekiel's preferred term for the Judean king; he rarely calls the king melek (king) in chapters 1-24, possibly to avoid legitimizing Zedekiah's kingship.
