The term massa (oracle/burden) derives from nasaʾ (lift/carry) — the oracle is a weight carried.
12.11: Ezekiel as mofet — keʾasher ʿasiti ken yeʿaseh lahem bagolah yelikhu behasher — "as I have done, so it will be done to them; into exile, into captivity they will go" — the identification of the prophet with the people he represents is Ezekiel's distinctive feature. The sign-act has executive force: what Ezekiel mimes, the community will experience. Mofet (wonder) usually denotes a divine act; applying it to the prophet makes the prophet's enacted body a divine instrument.
12.12: The specific application to the prince — vehanasi ʾasher betokhah ʾel-khetefav yissaʾ baʾalafel veʾel-pi haqqir yiḥtoru lehotseti vo ʿeynayyiv yekhasseh yaʿan ʾasher loʾ yirʾe leʿayin ʾet-haʾarets — the covering of the eyes so as not to see the land with the eye is the predictive element: Zedekiah will have his eyes put out (2 Kgs 25:7) before being taken to Babylon. The sign-act's eye-covering turns out to be a precise prophecy of his blinding.
12.13: YHWH spreading his net over the prince — ufrassti et-resheti ʿalav venitpas bimetsudati — the hunter/net imagery for divine judgment. The verb paras (spread/stretch) + resheti (my net) + nitpas (captured) traces the divine entrapment. Zedekiah is caught while fleeing (2 Kgs 25:4-5); he will come to Babylon but not see it — the eyes put out before the arrival precisely fulfills "he will not see it."
12.14: The scattering of all who support the prince — vekhol-ʾagappayu ʾezroh lekhal-ruaḥ — "all who are around him I will scatter to every wind" — ruaḥ (wind/spirit) here means compass-directions ("to every wind" = in all directions). The four winds appear throughout Ezekiel as the scope of divine scattering and gathering (5:10; 37:9).
12.15: The recognition formula — veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH — "and they will know that I am YHWH" — is Ezekiel's most characteristic refrain (appearing ~72 times). The purpose of every judgment and every restoration is the acknowledgment of YHWH's identity and sovereignty. The formula uses the imperfect yadeʿu (they will know) — the knowing is future, when the events confirm the word.
12.16: The remnant that survives the sword, famine, and pestilence — vehishtarti mehem ʾanshei mispar — "I will leave of them a few men" — mispar (number/count) means "men who can be counted," i.e., a very small remnant. The remnant's purpose: lemaʿan yesapperu kol-toʿavoteyhem baggoyim — they will declare all their abominations among the nations. The scattered remnant becomes involuntary testimony to YHWH's justice.
12.17: Another word-event formula initiates the new sign-act. The double formula (vv1 and 17) marks chapter 12 as containing two distinct sign-act oracles.
12.18: benaʿara tokhal laḥmekha uviraʿash vetimhon tishte meimekha — "eat your bread with quaking and drink your water with trembling and apprehension" — the three nouns for fear/anxiety: naʿara (anxiety), raʿash (trembling), timhon (dismay/confusion). The escalating emotional vocabulary describes the physical experience of siege-anxiety. The sign-act mimes the psychology of siege more than its logistics.
12.19: The interpretation: the land and its fullness will be stripped from the city's inhabitants — letitom eret koʾah tiqqafrethah (she will be stripped of what fills her). The eret koʾah (fullness of the land) is everything the land produces. The expression "because of the violence of all who dwell in it" grounds the economic desolation in moral cause.
12.20: The ruined-city conclusion — vehaʿarim hanoshabot teḥarovna vehaʾarets shemamah tihyeh — follows the earlier pattern (6:14). The future tenses (teḥarovna, tihyeh) are imperfects of strong prediction. The verse summarizes the geographic scope: not just Jerusalem but all the inhabited cities.
12.21: A third oracle-unit opens with yet another word-event formula. The focus now is on a specific proverb that was circulating among the exiles and the Jerusalem community.
12.22: ma hammashal hazze lakhem ʿal-ʾadmat Yisraʾel leʾmor yaʾarikhu hayyamim veʾavad kol-ḥazon — "what is this proverb that you have in the land of Israel: the days grow long and every vision comes to nothing?" — the proverb challenges the credibility of unfulfilled prophecy. Mashal (proverb/byword) represents communal conventional wisdom that has become a form of resistance to the prophetic word. The rhetorical structure quotes the skeptical community directly.
12.23: YHWH's abolition of the proverb — vehishbatti et-hammashal hazze — the verb shavat (hiphil: cause to cease/abolish) is the same word used for the Sabbath rest. YHWH "Sabbaths" the false proverb — causes it to stop. The new formula to replace it: qarev hayyamim udvar kol-ḥazon — "the days are near and the word of every vision." The deferred fulfillment becomes imminent fulfillment.
12.24: The paired abolitions — kol-ḥazon shav vemiqsam ḥalaq — "every empty vision and flattering divination" — ḥazon shav (false/empty vision) is opposed to true prophecy; miqsam ḥalaq (flattering divination) refers to the false prophets' technique of smooth answers. Both will be abolished "in the midst of the house of Israel."
12.25: ki ʾani YHWH adabber ʾet ʾasher adabber davar veyyeʿaseh loʾ timmasheikh ʿod — "for I YHWH will speak the word that I speak and it will be performed; it will no longer be delayed" — the emphatic self-reference (ʾani YHWH, I myself am YHWH) and the repeated adabber... davar (I will speak... word) underscore the absolute reliability of divine speech. Timmasheikh (be drawn out/delayed) from mashakh — the delay is over.
12.26: A fourth oracle within ch 12 is introduced. The people's second proverb is quoted: haḥazon ʾasher zeh ḥoze liymim rabbim ulnitot shanim ʾaḥerot hu mitnabe — "the vision he sees is for many days; and he prophesies of times far off." This second proverb does not deny prophecy but safely defers it to the distant future, making it existentially irrelevant to the present generation.
12.27: The oracle that counters the second proverb — the specific content is in vv28. The structure of the chapter (three proverbs addressed in sequence: the vision-always-fails proverb, the imminence replacement, and the far-future safety proverb) represents a sophisticated engagement with the community's prophetic skepticism.
12.28: loʾ yimmasheikh ʿod kol-divray ki adabber davar veyyeʿaseh — "none of my words will be delayed any longer; the word I speak will be performed" — the chapter closes with the same imminence formula as v25, forming an inclusio around the two-proverb refutation. The word for "none of my words" (kol-divray) is emphatically comprehensive.

13.1: Standard prophetic-word formula opens the oracle against false prophets. The formal structure — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor — is followed immediately by the direct address ben-ʾadam (son of man), Ezekiel's consistent designation that marks his humanity before the divine commission.
13.2: hinave ʾel-neviʾei Yisraʾel hannivveʾim — "prophesy against the prophets of Israel who are prophesying" — the piel imperative hinave followed by the niphal participle hannivveʾim creates an ironic opposition: Ezekiel who truly prophesies (piel) is commanded to address those who claim to prophesy (niphal participial, merely performing the act). The phrase haholkhim ʾaḥar ruḥam — "who follow their own spirit" — names the source: not the divine ruaḥ but their own.
13.3: hoi ʾal-neviʾei hannavalim ʾasher holkhim ʾaḥar ruḥam — the hoi (woe) oracle begins. Navalim is the word for "foolish/wicked" (related to naval, the wicked man of 1 Sam 25 and Ps 14:1). The characterization emphasizes moral rather than merely intellectual failure. Umebal lo raʾu — "and have seen nothing" — the false prophets are blind to the divine reality they claim to disclose.
13.4: keshvaʿalim baḥaravot neviʾeikha Yisraʾel hayu — "your prophets, O Israel, have been like jackals among ruins" — the jackal-in-ruins image is a desolation metaphor: jackals inhabit abandoned places (14:15; Isa 34:13; Jer 9:11). The false prophets inhabit the ruin of the covenant community rather than building it up or standing in the breach (v5).
13.5: loʾ ʿalitem bifretsoyt vattivnu gader ʾal-beit Yisraʾel laʿamod bammiilḥama beyom YHWH — "you have not gone up into the breaches nor built up a wall for the house of Israel to stand in battle on the day of YHWH" — the wall/breach metaphor (paratsot, breaches; gader, wall) is the defining image of prophetic failure. True prophets stand in the breach (intercede in the gap between YHWH's anger and the people); false prophets have abandoned this intercessory function.
13.6: ḥazu shav veqesem kazav haʾomrim neʾum YHWH vaYHWH loʾ shelaḥam — "they have seen false vision and lying divination, saying "Thus says YHWH," when YHWH has not sent them" — the term shav (false/vain) is the same word used in the third commandment ("do not take the name of YHWH in vain"). Using the prophetic formula without divine commission is precisely the violation of that commandment.
13.7: The series of rhetorical questions — halo ḥazon shav ḥazitem umigesem kazav ʾamartem veʾomrim neʾum YHWH vaʾani loʾ dibbartim — "have you not seen a false vision and spoken a lying divination, when you say "Thus says YHWH," but I have not spoken?" — the ʾani loʾ dibbartim (but I have not spoken) is the decisive divine refutation. The first-person denial of authorship removes all legitimacy from their messages.
13.8: The result clause — lakhein koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH yaʾan dabberkem shav vaḥazitem kazav lakhein ḥinneni ʾaleikhem — "therefore thus says Lord YHWH: because you have spoken falsehood and seen lies, therefore I am against you" — the double lakhein (therefore... therefore) creates a formal legal structure: accusation → verdict. Hinneni ʾaleikhem (behold I am against you) is the opposite of ʾani ittekhem (I am with you) — the divine hostile-presence formula.
13.9: vehayetah yadi ʾel-hanneviʾim haḥozim shav vehaqqosemim kazav besod ʿammi loʾ yihyu — the three-part exclusion: (1) besod ʿammi loʾ yihyu (they will not be in the council/assembly of my people); (2) bikhethav beit Yisraʾel loʾ yikkathvu (in the register of the house of Israel they will not be written); (3) veʾel-ʾadmat Yisraʾel loʾ yavoʾu (to the land of Israel they will not come). The exclusions move from community to register to land — a comprehensive disinheritance.
13.10: yaʿan uveyaʿan hittu ʾet-ʿammi leʾmor shalom veʾein shalom vehu bone ḥayits vehinnam totim ʾoto tafeil — "because they have misled my people, saying peace when there is no peace; and when the people build a wall, they plaster it with whitewash" — the peace/no-peace (shalom veʾein shalom) contrast echoes Jeremiah 6:14 and 8:11. The whitewash word tafeil (plaster/smear) is the key technical term — applying a cosmetic covering to an inadequate structure.
13.11: The coming storm that will collapse the plastered wall — shotef geshem vehavi ʾavnei ʾelgavish tappol veruaḥ seʿarot tevaqeʿa — "the flooding rain will fall, hailstones will fall, and a stormy wind will break out" — the storm as YHWH's judgment uses meteorological imagery for historical events (the Babylonian invasion). The three agents — rain (geshem shotef), hail (ʾavnei ʾelgavish), wind (ruaḥ seʿarot) — are covenant-curse weapons (cf. Exod 9:22-26).
13.12: The rhetorical question that exposes the whitewash failure — heʾayye hattiḥ ʾasher taḥtem — "where is the plaster with which you plastered it?" — anticipates the community asking where the false prophets' message went when the wall collapsed. The question is directed by the survivors to the prophets who gave false assurance. The tafal/tiḥ terms (tiḥ is more general, tafal more specific) may represent dialectal variation or near-synonyms.
13.13: YHWH announces the storm personally — uvʾappe aʾaseh ruaḥ seʿarot (in my wrath I will make a stormy wind) — the divine anger (ʾap, nose/anger) is the source of the meteorological weapons. The personal agency (aʾaseh, I will make/cause) makes explicit what the false prophets denied: divine action is real and imminent.
13.14: The wall pulled down and its foundation laid bare — veḥarastiv ʾel-haʾarets venighla yesodo — uses the same verb haras (pull down/demolish) that appears in Ezekiel's commission (5:16: breaking the staff of bread). The foundation exposure is the decisive act: not just superficial damage but structural collapse.
13.15: The recognition formula follows (veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH) immediately after the destruction, as it does throughout Ezekiel. Here it is applied specifically to the false prophets: even they will know YHWH's identity when their message is falsified by events.
13.16: Summary statement restating the core accusation — the prophets of Israel who prophesied to Jerusalem and saw visions of peace for her (ḥozim lah ḥazon shalom veʾein shalom) — "when there is no peace." The repetition of shalom veʾein shalom brackets the entire oracle against false prophets (vv3-16), making the peace-lie the defining sin of false prophecy.
13.17: The second oracle unit addresses the prophetesses — ven-ʾadam sim panekha ʾel-benot ʿammekha hannivveʾot — "son of man, set your face against the daughters of your people who are prophesying" — the gendered shift is grammatically marked: benot (daughters) vs. neviʾei (male prophets). Their sin is outlined as involving specific objects and techniques not fully understood.
13.18: kesatot ʿal kol-mafrqot yadeyhem — "who sew magic bands on all wrists" — kesatot (plural of kesat) are unknown objects, possibly wristbands or cushions used in divination; the term occurs only here in the OT. The mispakhot (veils/coverings) are also uncertain — possibly used to cover faces in entrancement or as part of divination rituals. The exact practice is opaque but the condemnation of it as false prophecy is clear.
13.19: The prostituting of divine service for handfuls of barley and scraps of bread — vattekhallelenah ʾoti ʾel-ʿammi bishʾele shiʿorei seʿorim uvifitetei leḥem — the trivialization of the prophetic office for minimal payment is the economic dimension of their false prophecy. ḥillel (piel: profane/desecrate) is the same root as ḥalal (pierce/profane); the piel makes it intensive — utterly profaning the divine name among the people.
13.20: YHWH tearing the bands from the arms — hinneni ʾel-kesatoteikhem ʾasher ʾattem mefarshot sham ʾet-hannefattot — the hostile-presence formula (hinneni ʾel) applied to the implements of false prophecy. The liberation of the souls (implied: those who were ensnared by the false prophetesses) is the positive dimension of the judgment on the practitioners.
13.21: The veils torn — veqaraʿti ʾet-mispekhoteykem vehtsal ʾet-ʿammi miyyadekhem — the tearing verb qaraʿ (tear/rend) is used for the tearing of garments in mourning and for divine judgment (1 Sam 15:27-28; 1 Kgs 11:11). The imagery parallels the wall-plaster removal of vv11-14: false protective coverings torn away.
13.22: The moral indictment — bifashovtekhem lev-tsaddiq sheqer vaʾani loʾ hikhʾavtihu — "because you disheartened the righteous with lies when I had not grieved him" — the verb khiʾavtihu (caused him grief) is from kaaʾav (to be in pain). YHWH had not intended to pain the righteous; the false prophetesses did so independently. The counterpart is strengthening the wicked by their false assurances.
13.23: The future cessation — lakhein shav loʾ tḥazena uqesem loʾ teqsomna ʿod — imperfects of permanent refusal: "you will no longer see false visions nor practice divination." The definitive verb hitsalti ʾet-ʿammi miyyadekhem (I will rescue my people from your hand) uses the rescue vocabulary (natsal, hiphil) normally applied to deliverance from enemies. The false prophetesses are treating YHWH's people as prey.

14.1: Elders coming before Ezekiel — vayyavoʾu ʾelai ʾanashim miziqnei Yisraʾel vayyeshvu lifanai — "certain elders of Israel came to me and sat before me" — the sitting-before posture (yashav lifanai) indicates a formal consultation. The elders expect prophetic counsel; what they receive instead is a divine diagnosis of their interior idolatry.
14.2: Standard word-event formula marks the divine speech that interrupts or reframes the consultation. The elders may not know that YHWH will speak about them rather than to their agenda.
14.3: ben-ʾadam haʾanashim haʾelle heʿelu gilluleihem ʿal-libam umikshol ʿavonam natenu lineqed paneyhem — "son of man, these men have set up their idols in their hearts, and put the stumbling block of their iniquity before their faces" — gillulim (dung-idols, from gillul, related to gal, dung-heap) is Ezekiel's strongly pejorative term for idols; he uses it ~40 times. The expression gillulim ʿal-libam (idols on/in the heart) distinguishes interior from exterior idolatry: the problem is not necessarily that they have physical idols but that they have given the idol-allegiance of the heart.
14.4: The principle of the answering-oracle — kol-ish ish mibbet Yisraʾel ʾasher yaʿale ʾet-gillulav ʾal-libam... vavoʾ ʾel-hannavi ʾani YHWH neʿneiti lo vo baʾasher bo bemargoa gillulav — YHWH's declared policy: anyone who comes to a prophet while harboring idols in the heart will receive an answer that meets them in their idolatry ("through the very thing in which he has gone astray"). The ironic judgment mirrors the Pharaoh-hardening dynamic: the seeker's own idolatry becomes the medium of their self-condemnation.
14.5: The purpose of the answering-oracle — lemaʿan tafos ʾet-beit Yisraʾel belibam — "to lay hold of the house of Israel in their hearts" — tafas (seize/lay hold of) describes divine judicial arrest. The verb used for seizing the idolaters through their idolatry is a form of the lex talionis applied to interior religion: the idol in the heart becomes the means of divine apprehension.
14.6: The call to repentance — shuvu vehashivu meʿal gilulleikhem — "repent and turn away from your idols" — the double repentance verb: shuvu (turn/return, imperative qal) and hashivu (turn away, hiphil imperative — cause to turn). The two-part construction intensifies the urgency: simply repent + specifically turn away from idols. The hiphil suggests an active act of will distinct from the passive turning.
14.7: The general principle restated — kol-ish ish mibbet Yisraʾel umin-hagger asher yagur beYisraʾel — now expanded to include "the stranger who sojourns in Israel." The extension to the ger (resident alien) universalizes the principle beyond ethnic boundaries. The Deuteronomic legislation consistently included the ger in covenant obligations (Deut 5:14; 14:21; etc.).
14.8: The cutting-off (hikkratti) of the idolater from the people — venatattihu leʾot ulimshalim vekhirrattihu miqirb ʿammi — uses two distinct exclusion terms: ʾot (sign, he becomes a living warning-sign) and mashal (proverb/byword, his fate becomes a saying). To be made into a mashal is to have one's life turned into cautionary instruction for others (cf. 5:15; Deut 28:37).
14.9: The hardest verse in the oracle — vehanavi ki yefutteh vedibbar davar ʾani YHWH pittahti ʾet-hannavi hahu — "if the prophet is deceived and speaks a word, I YHWH have deceived that prophet" — the verb pittah (entice/deceive, piel) applied to YHWH is theologically loaded. The parallel in 1 Kings 22:19-22 (the lying spirit sent to deceive Ahab's prophets) clarifies the mechanism: YHWH permits or uses the prophet's own inclination to self-deception to produce prophetic misguidance. The grammar (ʾani YHWH pittahti, emphatic first-person) makes YHWH's secondary causality explicit without removing the prophet's primary responsibility.
14.10: Both prophet and inquirer bear their guilt equally — venasaʾu ʿavonam kikhʿavon hadoreshe ken ʿavon hannavi yihyeh — "they shall bear their punishment; the punishment of the inquirer and the punishment of the prophet shall be alike" — the nasa ʿavon formula (bear/carry iniquity) is the standard covenant-curse language for consequence-bearing. The equal distribution of guilt between the deceived inquirer and the deceiving prophet is consistent with the principle of individual accountability developed in ch 18.
14.11: The ultimate purpose of the prophetic judgment mechanism — lemaʿan loʾ-yitʿu ʿod beit Yisraʾel meʾaḥaray — "so that the house of Israel may no longer go astray from following me." The lemaʿan (in order that) clause grounds even the hardest aspect of ch 14 (YHWH enticing false prophets) in the larger purpose of restoring covenant faithfulness. The goal is restoration of the lev echad (one heart) promised in 11:19.
14.12: New oracle unit — the four-judgment schema begins. The formal opening — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor followed by a direct address — introduces the distinctive Noah-Daniel-Job speculation.
14.13: The conditional schema — eres ʾasher teḥeta li limʿol maʿal — "when a land sins against me by acting faithlessly" — maʿal (faithless/treacherous act, specifically covenant violation) is one of Ezekiel's favored terms for Israel's sin. The conditional pattern (four times in vv13, 17, 19, 21) and the response (YHWH sends a judgment agent) creates a systematic catalog of divine judgment tools.
14.14: vehayu shloshet haʾanashim haʾelle betokha Noaḥ Daniʾel veʾiyyov — the three righteous exemplars: Noah, Daniʾel (spelled differently from the prophet Daniel of the later book — probably the legendary hero of ANE tradition, as the spelling with yod is distinct), and Job. All three are men who were uniquely righteous in contexts of pervasive wickedness, and all were the only survivors in their contexts. The point: even the maximum of righteous intercession cannot avert the judgment once a land has crossed the threshold of covenant violation.
14.15: The second judgment scenario — lo ḥayya raʿah ʾaʿavir baʾarets — wild beasts as covenant curse (Lev 26:22; Deut 32:24). The desolation of the land (shemamah) is the result, a term that echoes through the book. The ʾaʿavir (hiphil imperfect of ʿavar, cause to pass through) frames the beasts as sent, not merely present.
14.16: The hypothetical righteous — hamma shloshet haʾanashim baʾtokha ḥay ʾani — YHWH swears by his life (ḥay ʾani, as I live, the divine oath formula) that even Noah, Daniel, and Job could save only themselves. The ḥay ʾani oath formula ("as I live") intensifies the declaration to maximum certainty; it is used when YHWH makes irrevocable pronouncements (also 5:11; 16:48; 17:16, 19, etc.).
14.17: Third scenario — the sword (ḥerev) as the third covenant-curse instrument. The paired judgments — sword/famine (vv17-18), wild beasts (v15-16), plague (v19-20) — are the "four sore acts of judgment" announced in v21 (shevu mishpatai raʿim).
14.18: The unchanging conclusion for each scenario — ʾim-benim veʾim-banot yatsillu hemmah levaddam yinnatselut — "they would deliver neither sons nor daughters, but they alone would be saved" — the repeated refrain in each judgment scenario (vv16, 18, 20) establishes the principle of individualized accountability that is fully developed in ch 18. Righteousness is non-transferable; it cannot serve as credit for another person's standing.
14.19: The fourth scenario — pestilence (dever) and blood (dam) — is the final covenant-curse instrument. The phrase leshafokh dami ʿaleha (to pour out my blood/fury on it) uses dam (blood) in the sense of bloody judgment (the poured-out blood = violent death). The quartet is now complete: famine, wild beasts, sword, plague — the four instruments of Lev 26:14-39.
14.20: The final application of the three-righteous formula — veNoaḥ Daniʾel veʾiyyov betokha ḥay ʾani... ʾim-ben veʾim-bat yatsillu hemmah levaddam yinnatselut — the ḥay ʾani oath and the hemmah levaddam (they alone) refrain are repeated for the third time. The repetition is not redundancy but legal reinforcement: the principle of non-transferable righteousness is established by the triple oath-formula.
14.21: ki koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH ʾaf ki ʾarbaat mishpatai haraʿim ḥerev veraʿav veḥayya raʿah vedever shilaḥti ʾel-Yerushalaim — "for thus says Lord YHWH: how much more when I send my four disastrous judgments — sword, famine, wild beasts, and plague — against Jerusalem" — the ʾaf ki (how much more) is the qal-waḥomer (light-to-heavy) argument: if even three hypothetical righteous cannot save anyone in a land under judgment, how much less can Jerusalem claim protection? The four judgments sent simultaneously represent maximum covenant curse.
14.22: The unexpected conclusion — a remnant survives: vehinneh noteret bo peletah mossiʾim banim uvanot — "behold, there will be survivors in it who will be brought out, sons and daughters" — the survival of survivors (and specifically sons and daughters, the very ones the three righteous of vv14-20 could not save) overturns the schema. The survivors are brought out not by the righteousness of Noah/Daniel/Job but by divine decision. Their purpose: when the watching community sees their behavior (darkeikhem veʾalileyhem, ways and deeds), they will be consoled.
14.23: The consolation paradox — veniqhamu etchem ki tirʾu ʾet-darkeikhem veʾet-ʾalileyhem viydeʾtem ki loʾ ḥinnami ʾasiti ʾet kol-ʾasher ʾasiti vah — "they will console you when you see their ways and their deeds, and you will know that not without cause have I done what I have done" — the consolation comes not from pleasant news but from the recognition of justice: the behavior of the remnant-survivors demonstrates why the judgment was necessary. The loʾ ḥinnami (not without cause) is YHWH's self-vindication and the community's acceptance of that vindication as their consolation.

15.1: Standard revelation formula (vayehi devar YHWH elai): 'The word of YHWH came to me.' This formula recurs ~50 times in Ezekiel, marking each oracle as direct prophetic reception. Unlike prophets who say 'the oracle of YHWH,' Ezekiel consistently uses the dabar (word) reception formula, emphasizing that what follows is verbal divine communication rather than visionary experience.
15.2: mah-yitron etz ha-gafen mikol-etz: 'What advantage does the wood of the vine have over any [other] wood?' Yitron (advantage, profit) is a Qoheleth term (Eccl 1:3) pressed into prophetic service here — the question is commercial/utilitarian. Etz ha-gafen (vine wood) was widely understood in Israel as Israel's symbol: Ps 80 calls Israel YHWH's vine, Isa 5 develops the vineyard parable, Hos 10:1 uses vine imagery. Ezekiel shocks by taking the symbol and asking: what is vine wood actually good for as a material? The answer is: nothing at all.
15.3: hayitkach mimenu etz lamelachah — im-yikchu mimenu yated litlot alav keli: 'Is wood taken from it for any work? Can one make a peg from it to hang a vessel on?' Melachah (work, craftsmanship, skilled labor) is the same word used in Exod 31:3 for the skilled craftsmanship of Bezalel. Vine wood cannot serve even the simplest carpentry purpose. The rhetorical double question (will it be used for work? can you even make a peg from it?) intensifies the uselessness. The vine's value lies entirely in its fruit; stripped of its fruit-bearing function, it is less than any other wood.
15.4: hineh la-esh nitana le-akal: 'Behold it is given to the fire for fuel.' Akal (eating, consuming) describes the fire's appetite — the vine wood is consumed completely. The verse adds that fire consumes both ends and chars the middle — even partially burned vine wood has no residual use. The image is complete consumption with no remainder, no recycling, no value retrieved from the waste.
15.5: ap ki hayo shlemah lo yeaseh lemalacha: 'Even when it was whole, it was not used for work — how much less, after the fire has consumed it and it is charred, can it be used for any work?' The rhetorical af ki (how much more/less) is a qal va-homer argument: if the vine was useless in its best condition (whole, unburned), the charred remnant is even more useless. This sets up the application in vv. 6-8: if Jerusalem was spiritually useless even before judgment, she will be completely useless after it.
15.6: lachem ken natati et-yoshevei Yerushalayim: 'Therefore thus I have given the inhabitants of Jerusalem.' The application of the parable is stark: the vine wood given to fire = Jerusalem given to judgment. The perfect tense (natati, I have given) treats the judgment as already certain — prophetic perfect. The transition from parable to application with lachem (therefore) is typical of Ezekiel's mashal-interpretation pattern (cf. ch. 17 and 24).
15.7: venathati et-panai bam: 'I will set my face against them.' The panim (face) of YHWH formula appears in blessings (Num 6:25, YHWH's face shining) and in judgments. Setting the face against someone is the opposite of the Aaronic blessing — it signals sustained divine opposition. The phrase implies the withdrawal of protective favor. The paradox that they escape one fire only to be consumed by another reflects the relentlessness of the judgment: there is no refuge from the fire that is YHWH's own judgment.
15.8: vahaarets tihyeh leshamamah: 'The land shall become a desolation.' Shamamah (desolation, waste) is Ezekiel's signature judgment word (cf. 6:14; 12:20; 33:28). The root shamam conveys both the physical waste of the land and the horror/astonishment it produces in those who see it. The closing formula (ne'um adonai YHWH, declares the Lord YHWH) seals the oracle — Ezekiel uses this double divine name throughout to emphasize YHWH's sovereign authority behind the judgment word.

16.1: Standard revelation formula opening the allegory of Jerusalem. Chapter 16 is the longest chapter in Ezekiel and one of the most extended and intense allegories in the OT. The chapter uses the marriage metaphor that appears throughout the prophets (Hosea, Jeremiah, Second Isaiah) but takes it further than any other text — both in the detail of YHWH's grace and in the explicitness of Jerusalem's unfaithfulness.
16.2: ben-adam hoda et-Yerushalayim et-toavotiha: 'Son of man, make known to Jerusalem her abominations.' Toavot (abominations, detestable things) is the strongest Hebrew term for covenant violations, particularly idolatry and sexual immorality. The imperative hoda (make known, cause to know) places the prophet in the role of covenant prosecutor — he must formally charge the city. The form is that of a legal rib (lawsuit), which will culminate in the covenant-making and judgment sections below.
16.3: mekhorotaich umoladetaich me-eretz hakena'ani avich ha-emori ve'imech Hittit: 'Your origin and birth are from the land of Canaan; your father was an Amorite and your mother a Hittite.' The genealogical charge is startling in an Israelite context. Theologically the point is that Jerusalem's religious and ethical formation was thoroughly Canaanite — YHWH's grace did not find a worthy recipient but an utterly unsuitable one, making election even more scandalous. The contrast with the covenant formulas to follow (I gave you this and that) makes grace all the more extravagant.
16.4: beyom hilledet otach lo-krat shorech: 'On the day you were born, your navel cord was not cut.' The withholding of standard birth care (cutting the cord, washing, salt-rubbing, wrapping in cloths) symbolizes complete rejection and abandonment. Each item in the list represents a stage of neonatal care that loving parents provide. The absence of all of them communicates that no one owned or valued this infant. The social abandonment of Jerusalem at her “birth” is the baseline from which YHWH's subsequent care will be measured.
16.5: vatushlikhei el-penei hasadeh: 'You were cast out into the open field.' Infant exposure (hashlacha = throwing away) was practiced in the ancient Near East for unwanted or deformed children. The phrase bego'al naphshech (in loathing of your life/soul) captures the complete contempt with which the infant was treated. This image of utter destitution and rejection is the exact opposite of Israel's later self-understanding as YHWH's beloved and chosen.
16.6: va'omar lach bedamaich chayi: 'I said to you in your blood, Live!' This is the theological crux of the chapter. Bedamaich (in your blood) is emphatic and repeated twice for weight. The infant is lying in birth blood, abandoned, dying — and YHWH passes by and speaks one word: chayi (live!). The divine word is performative: YHWH does not rescue and then command, but commands and the command enacts the rescue. This is creation-power applied to election: life spoken into non-life. The double repetition (“I said to you in your blood, Live — yes, in your blood I said to you, Live”) is unique in the chapter and emphasizes the extremity of the grace.
16.7: revavah ketzemach hasadeh netaticha: 'I made you thrive like a plant of the field.' Revavah (ten thousand, myriad) conveys abundant, explosive growth. The language shifts from infant to mature woman — breasts developed, hair grew — language of physical maturation marking the transition from childhood to marriageable age. Yet the phrase ve'at eriyah ve'eryah (but you were naked and bare) closes the verse with vulnerability: even at full physical development, she had no covering — no social status, no covenant protection.
16.8: va'ephros kenaph alayich va'akasse ervatach: 'I spread my corner over you and covered your nakedness.' Kanaph (wing, corner of garment) is the technical term for the marriage gesture: in Ruth 3:9 Ruth asks Boaz to spread his kanaph over her as a kinsman-redeemer. Here YHWH performs the same act — he claims Jerusalem as his wife by the covenant (va'avo veshevuat = I entered into a covenant/oath with you). The covenant formula follows: vatihyi li (you became mine). Divine election is cast as covenant marriage — the most intimate relational category available.
16.9: va'erchatzech bemayim: 'I washed you with water.' The washing that was withheld at birth (v. 4) YHWH now performs as a marriage preparation. The sequence (wash, anoint with oil, clothe) mirrors the preparation of a bride. Shemen (oil) for anointing was both cosmetic and symbolic of blessing and dignity. The reversal is total: the unbathed, unanointed, naked infant is now bathed, anointed, and adorned as a wife.
16.10: va'albishech riqmah: 'I clothed you in embroidery.' The clothing list escalates: riqmah (embroidered cloth), tahhash (fine leather, possibly dugong or dolphin skin — the same material used for the tabernacle coverings in Exod 26:14), shesh (fine linen/byssus), meshiy (silk or fine cloth). The pairing with tabernacle materials (tahhash) subtly links YHWH's adornment of Jerusalem to the construction of the sanctuary — the divine dwelling and the chosen city adorned with the same precious materials.
16.11: va'ede otach edi: 'I adorned you with adornments.' The jewelry list — bracelets, necklace, nose ring, earrings, crown — represents royal bridal adornment. Each item is a gift, transforming the abandoned infant into a queen. The accumulation of gifts communicates the extravagance of divine grace: not merely life but abundant flourishing, not merely covering but royal dignity.
16.12: va'eten netzer tiferet beroshech: 'I put a beautiful crown on your head.' Atara (crown) completes the royal status conferred. By v. 13 Jerusalem has become a queen (vatitzlehi limluchah, you prospered into royalty/queenship). The entire adornment sequence is a deliberate reversal of the birth scene: every deprivation of vv. 4-5 is superseded. The divine generosity is not partial restoration but complete transformation.
16.13: vatiphe me'od me'od vatitzlehi limluchah: 'You became exceedingly beautiful and prospered into royalty.' Tiphah (beauty) and tzlichah limluchah (prospered into queenship/royalty) mark the apex of the divine gift. The beauty is explicitly YHWH's own gift: yophyech is qualified by asher samti alayich (which I had placed upon you). This sets up the tragic irony of v. 15 — she trusts in a beauty that was never hers but only YHWH's gift through her.
16.14: vateze lecha shem bagoyim beyopheych: 'Your fame went out among the nations because of your beauty.' Shem (name, fame, reputation) going out among the nations recalls the reputation of Solomon's Jerusalem (1 Kgs 10), which was at its peak of beauty and wisdom. The beauty was kalil (complete, perfect) and behodi (in my [YHWH's] splendor/majesty) — language of theophanic glory applied to the city. YHWH's glory was meant to shine through Jerusalem to the nations; her subsequent defection is therefore not just a personal betrayal but a desecration of YHWH's glory-display.
16.15: vativti al-yiphyech vatizni al-shemech: 'But you trusted in your beauty and played the harlot because of your fame.' Vatavti (you trusted) is the decisive theological verb: batach (trust, rely on) in the OT is rightly directed to YHWH alone (Ps 115:9-11); misplaced trust is the root of idolatry and apostasy. She trusted in beauty that was YHWH's gift — making a gift into a god. Vatizni (you played the harlot) is the prophetic metaphor for idolatry that Hosea pioneered; here it encompasses both literal cult prostitution and the political alliances that treated the nations as Israel's true security rather than YHWH.
16.16: vatikchi mibigdei-otach vatasi lach bamot tilaot: 'You took from your garments and made for yourself colorful high places.' The misuse of YHWH's gifts (garments → shrines; jewelry → idols in vv. 17-18) is the structural logic of the indictment: the gifts of grace are the raw material for apostasy. Bamot (high places) were the localized shrines where illicit worship was offered. The phrase lo bau velo yihyeh (such things have not come and shall not be) is a condemnation formula indicating the unique monstrousness of the act.
16.17: vatikchi kli tiph'artecha mizahavi umikaspi: 'You took your beautiful jewels of gold and silver, which I had given you, and made male images.' Kli tiphartecha (articles of your splendor/beauty) — the same gifts from vv. 11-12 now repurposed. Tzalmei zachar (male images/figures) likely refers to phallic cult images associated with fertility worship. The irony is stark: YHWH the divine husband's gifts are fashioned into images that substitute for him as the object of desire and worship.
16.18: vatilbeshi otam riqmatecha: 'You clothed them with your embroidered garments.' The adorning of idols with YHWH's gifts of clothing and food (vv. 18-19) represents a total inversion: the things YHWH gave to adorn his bride are given to clothe the idols. Ve'qitorati ve'shemeni (my incense and my oil) — YHWH calls the offerings “mine” because they were his gifts first. The idol feast is made from YHWH's provisions, a culpable misappropriation.
16.19: velachemit asher natati lach: 'And my food which I gave you.' The catalogue of sacrificial food given to idols (fine flour, oil, honey) emphasizes that YHWH himself provided the materials for the apostasy. There is something profoundly offensive about using the benefactor's gifts to honor a rival — it is the behavior of a spouse using a wedding gift to buy presents for a lover.
16.20: vatikchi et-baniach ve'et-benotaich asher yaladti li: 'And you took your sons and daughters whom you bore to me, and you sacrificed them to be devoured.' This verse marks the climactic intensification of the indictment. Asher yaladti li (whom you bore to me) — YHWH claims the children as his own (they are the fruit of the divine-human covenant union). Child sacrifice (zivach lahem le'echol) is the ultimate desecration: covenant offspring given to the fire of Molech (cf. 2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31). Ezekiel returns to this charge repeatedly (20:25-26, 31) because it represents the covenant's complete inversion: the children given by YHWH's grace destroyed in service of foreign gods.
16.21: vatishchati et-banai vatitnenem beha'avir otam lahem: 'You slaughtered my children and delivered them up, passing them through the fire for them.' The repetition of the child sacrifice charge (v. 20 → v. 21) emphasizes horror through redundancy. Vatishchati (you slaughtered) uses the technical slaughter term, treating the children as sacrificial animals. Beha'avir otam (passing them through) is the technical phrase for the Molech rite (cf. Lev 18:21; Deut 18:10). The rhetorical question implied — could this have been in addition to all your other abominations? — sets up the litany of unfaithfulness still to come.
16.22: ve'et kol-toavotaich uzinutaich lo zakart et-yemei neurayich: 'In all your abominations and prostitutions you did not remember the days of your youth.' Lo zakart (you did not remember) is the covenant's antithetical response to YHWH's own remembering (he will “remember the covenant” in v. 60). Memory in covenant language means more than mental recall — it means active acknowledgment with the appropriate response. Forgetting the days of YHWH's tender care (the naked infant, the life-giving word, the bridal adornment) is the structural apostasy underlying all the specific acts of unfaithfulness.
16.23: vayehi acharei kol-raateki oy oy lach: 'After all your evil — woe, woe to you!' The double oy (woe, alas) is an exclamation of profound grief and horror — the prophet's own voice breaking through the divine speech. The exclamation interrupts the catalogue of sin to express the lament that both YHWH and the prophet feel at the magnitude of Jerusalem's unfaithfulness.
16.24: vatibni lach gav: 'You built yourself a platform/mound.' Gav (mound, platform) and ramah (high place) in v. 25 refer to the elevated shrines used for illicit worship. That Jerusalem built these structures throughout the city (“at every street corner” in v. 25) indicates the normalization and omnipresence of the cult prostitution metaphor — unfaithfulness was not occasional but systematic and public.
16.25: el kol-rosh derech banit ramatayich: 'At the head of every road you built your high places.' The public exposure of the prostitution — at the intersection of every major road — mirrors the publicity of Israel's covenant-breaking. Vatephtchi et-raglayich (you spread your legs) is the most explicit sexual language in the Hebrew Bible, used here not for pornographic effect but to capture the brazenness and public nature of Israel's apostasy. The phrase vatarbiy et-taznutaich (you multiplied your prostitutions) quantifies the extent of the unfaithfulness.
16.26: vatizni el-benei Mitzraim: 'You played the harlot with the sons of Egypt.' The first geopolitical alliance named as “prostitution” is Egypt — Israel's first and foundational political temptation. Egypt represents not just an ally but a parent-figure (Israel came from Egypt), making the political alliance a kind of return to slavery. Gedolei vasar (great of flesh) is anatomical language indicating the power/virility of Egypt as a military force. The attraction is size and power — the opposite of YHWH's way of choosing the weak to shame the strong (cf. Deut 7:7-8).
16.27: ve'hinei natati et-yadi alayich: 'Behold I stretched out my hand against you.' The divine hand (yad) stretched out in judgment is the Exodus formula inverted — YHWH's hand that delivered from Egypt (Exod 3:20; 6:6) now acts in judgment against Israel. YHWH diminished her food portion (va'agra chukateich) as a preliminary corrective judgment. That even the daughter of the Philistines was ashamed of Jerusalem's behavior measures how far she had fallen — nations without covenant shame Israel that has covenant.
16.28: vatizni el-benei Ashshur: 'You played the harlot with the sons of Assyria.' Assyria represents the next political temptation — the imperial power to the east that dominated the 8th century BCE. The iterative structure of the indictment (Egypt → Assyria → Babylonia) maps onto the historical sequence of Israel's political alliances. The phrase velo savat (you were not satisfied) captures the insatiability of idolatrous desire — each new alliance promises security that it cannot deliver, yet the appetite for the next grows.
16.29: vatarbiy et-taznutech el-eretz kena'an Kasdiymah: 'You multiplied your prostitutions to the land of the traders, Chaldea.' Kasdimah (Chaldea/Babylon) is the third and greatest of the alliance-prostitutions. The velo savat gam-zot (you were not satisfied with this either) is the final measure of insatiability. By listing Egypt, Assyria, and Babylon, Ezekiel has indicted every major empire of his day — Jerusalem had prostituted herself to all of them in turn, finding none of them able to satisfy the longing that only YHWH can fill.
16.30: mah amulah libatach: 'How sick is your heart!' or 'How enflamed your heart!' Amulah is a hapax legomenon (appearing only here) meaning weak/sick or intensely desirous; LXX rendered it as “enflamed.” The lev (heart) is the seat of decision and desire in Hebrew psychology. The adjective captures the disordered desire that lies behind all the acts of apostasy: a heart that is insatiably inflamed toward what cannot satisfy it.
16.31: binhot gavech bekhol-rosh derech: 'In building your platform at every street corner.' The detailed geography of the illicit shrines — at road junctions, in every square — conveys the total saturation of the city with idolatry. The climactic inversion: she was not like a harlot (zah) who receives payment — she was the opposite, giving payment to her lovers. This reversal is both economically and theologically significant: ordinary prostitution is at least transactional; Jerusalem's apostasy is gratuitous, giving away YHWH's resources to pursue empty substitutes.
16.32: ha'ishah hameno'efet: 'The adulterous woman.' The shift from prostitution metaphor to adultery metaphor (ni'uf = adultery) tightens the covenantal charge: adultery requires the prior bond of marriage. Prostitution with strangers may be reprehensible but adultery is specifically a covenant violation. By calling Jerusalem ha-ishah hameno'efet (the adulterous woman), YHWH identifies the real offense — it is not merely immorality but covenant-breaking against the divine husband.
16.33: el-kol-zonot yitenu nadan: 'To all prostitutes gifts are given.' The economic reversal is devastating: every prostitute receives payment for her services, but Jerusalem paid her lovers. Nadan (gift, bride-price, payment) is here the payment given to a prostitute. The observation that Jerusalem inverted the economic logic of prostitution compounds her shame — she did not even have the dignity of being a paid prostitute, but gave YHWH's gifts away to attract alliances that profited her nothing.
16.34: vatehiy bach hapech min-hanashim bitznutaich: 'So you were the opposite among women in your prostitution.' The summary of the economic reversal seals the indictment: she paid rather than was paid, so she was the hapech (reverse, opposite) of the normal prostitute. This is a legal summary before the sentencing. The peculiar monstrousness of Jerusalem's behavior is that she was not merely unfaithful but irrationally, wastefully unfaithful — spending covenant resources to pursue worthless substitutes.
16.35: lachem zonah shim'i devar YHWH: 'Therefore, O prostitute, hear the word of YHWH.' The judicial summons to hear (shim'i) initiates the sentence phase. The direct address as zonah (prostitute) is the formal judicial charge: you are convicted of what has been described. The transition from indictment (vv. 2-34) to sentencing (vv. 35-43) follows the pattern of the prophetic rib (covenant lawsuit).
16.36: ya'an hishafech nechushtech: 'Because your bronze/nakedness was poured out.' Nechoshtet is debated: LXX read it as “your bronze” (offering bowls?), but the context suggests it is a euphemism for the exposure of nakedness (erva). The sentence matches the crime: she exposed herself publicly in her prostitution, so she will be publicly exposed in judgment. This lex talionis logic (punishment mirrors offense) is characteristic of Ezekiel's jurisprudence.
16.37: lachem hineni mekabetz et-kol-me'ahavayich: 'Therefore I will gather all your lovers.' The judgment is that YHWH will convene the very nations she pursued and turn them against her. The lovers (Egypt, Assyria, Babylon) become the instruments of judgment — the objects of her misplaced trust become the agents of her destruction. This is the characteristic prophetic irony: the idols one trusts become the instruments of YHWH's punishment.
16.38: ushphattich mishpetei no'afot veshofechot dam: 'I will judge you as women who commit adultery and murder are judged.' The dual charge is adultery (covenant-breaking with YHWH) and murder (child sacrifice). Both capital offenses under Torah (Lev 20:10; 20:2). The judgment formulation ve'netaticha dam cheima vekina (I will make you blood of wrath and jealousy) invokes the divine jealousy (qin'ah) that is YHWH's response to covenant violation — his covenant love turned to wrath by infidelity.
16.39: venatati otach beyadam venatezu gavech: 'I will give you into their hands, and they shall tear down your platform.' The stripping and abandonment by the lovers enacts the exact reversal of vv. 10-14: the clothing, jewels, and structures given by YHWH that she gave to idols will be stripped from her. The lovers who received payment will take everything — another ironic reversal of the established economic logic.
16.40: vehe'elu alayich kahal vesaqelu otach be'even: 'They shall bring up a congregation against you and stone you.' Stoning was the Torah penalty for adultery (Deut 22:24) and for the daughter of a priest who became a prostitute (Lev 21:9). The assembly convened to execute judgment mirrors the assembly of lovers she gathered. Kahal (assembly, congregation) is the same word used for the assembly at Sinai — the covenant assembly becomes the judicial assembly executing the sentence for covenant violation.
16.41: vesarfu vateicha ba'esh: 'They shall burn your houses with fire.' The burning of the houses completes the stripping — buildings as well as clothing, jewels, and idols are destroyed. The phrase ve'asiti vach shefatim le'einei nashim rabbot (I will execute judgments on you in the sight of many women) indicates that the sentence will be public — a deterrent to other nations who might follow Jerusalem's example of covenant violation.
16.42: vahishbati chamatי uminaich: 'I will satisfy my wrath against you, and my jealousy will depart from you.' The exhaustion of divine hemat (wrath/burning anger) and qin'ah (jealousy/zeal) signals that the judgment, though terrible, is bounded: it is not permanent annihilation but a judicial sentence that has a conclusion. The phrase ve'shaqatty (I will be quiet/calm) indicates YHWH's own relief at the conclusion of judgment — divine wrath is not sadistic but corrective, with a goal and a terminus.
16.43: ya'an asher lo zakart et-yemei neurayich: 'Because you did not remember the days of your youth.' The judicial sentence closes with the ground of the charge: forgetting (v. 22). The inclusion of “forgetting the days of youth” at both the beginning (v. 22) and end (v. 43) of the central indictment section creates a frame — the entire catalogue of sins is organized under the rubric of forgetting YHWH's grace. Covenant memory (anamnesis) is the foundation of covenant loyalty; its absence produces the cascade of substitutes.
16.44: hineh kol-hamoshel alayich yimshol lamar ka'imah bitta: 'Everyone who uses proverbs will use this proverb about you: Like mother, like daughter.' Mashal (proverb, parable) here in the sense of a popular saying. The ka'imah bitta (like mother, like daughter) proverb is applied to Jerusalem: her Hittite-Amorite origins (v. 3) are now visible in her behavior. The genealogical charge of v. 3 finds its proof in the conduct — she has become what her Canaanite parentage was, despite YHWH's transformative grace.
16.45: bat-imech at: 'You are your mother's daughter.' The extended application: her mother (Hittite) loathed husband and children, and her sisters (Samaria and Sodom) loathed their husbands and children. The family analogy extends the indictment to include the northern kingdom (Samaria/Aholah in ch. 23) and Sodom as Jerusalem's “sisters” — all sharing the same pattern of received grace and covenant-breaking apostasy.
16.46: akhotach hagdolah Shomron: 'Your older sister is Samaria.' Samaria (the northern kingdom, which fell to Assyria in 722 BCE) is the older sister, Sodom the younger (pre-dating Israel's settlement). Jerusalem is placed between them, making the comparison devastating: you were meant to be better than both, but you proved worse than either. The geographic location (“to the left/north” = Samaria, “to the right/south” = Sodom) maps the covenant family onto the land.
16.47: velo bederkheihen halachte: 'But you did not even walk in their ways.' The comparative logic reaches its climax: Jerusalem's sins surpassed those of Samaria and Sodom. This is a prophetic scandal — Sodom is the OT's paradigm of wickedness (Gen 18-19), and to say Jerusalem exceeded Sodom is the greatest possible moral indictment. The excess is the central point: receiving greater grace demands greater response; greater grace spurned produces greater guilt.
16.48: chai ani ne'um adonai YHWH: 'As I live, declares the Lord YHWH.' The divine oath formula (chai ani) introduces the comparative analysis that follows. That YHWH swears by his own life (there being nothing higher to swear by, Heb 6:13) heightens the solemnity. The oath certifies what follows as not accusatory rhetoric but divine factual determination: Sodom and Samaria have not done what Jerusalem has done.
16.49: hineh zeh hayah avon Sodom achotaich: 'Behold, this was the iniquity of Sodom your sister.' Strikingly, Ezekiel's analysis of Sodom's sin focuses on social and economic sins: pride (gaon), fullness of food (sova lechem), prosperous ease (shelem hashqet), and failure to aid the poor and needy. The sexual offense of Gen 19 is subsumed under the broader category of covenantal unfaithfulness — exploitation and indifference to the vulnerable. Jerusalem's greater sin is that she had received the covenant, the temple, and the full revelation of YHWH's will, yet surpassed Sodom's record.
16.50: vatagevahnah vataa'seinah toevah lefanai: 'They were haughty and did abomination before me.' Gaavah (pride, haughtiness) is the ultimate root sin in the prophets — the elevation of the self above proper creaturely dependence. The toevah before YHWH likely refers to sexual offenses (Gen 19:5; Lev 18:22) but Ezekiel keeps the focus on pride as the root. When I saw [it] I removed them. The removal (va'asir otam) is YHWH's sovereign act — the destruction of Sodom is placed here as YHWH's direct judicial action.
16.51: veShomeron kachatzeit chatotaich lo chatah: 'And Samaria — she has not committed half your sins.' The quantitative comparison is meant to shock: Samaria's idolatry, which brought Assyrian destruction in 722 BCE and which Amos, Hosea, and Micah had excoriated, was less than half of Jerusalem's sins. Vatatzdiqi et-achotaich (you have made your sisters appear righteous) — Jerusalem's excess retroactively vindicates, by comparison, the sisters whose sins were considered paradigmatic. The prophetic reductio ad absurdum exposes the depth of Jerusalem's fall.
16.52: gam-at se'i kelimmatach asher pilla't laachotaich: 'Also, bear your shame, you who have made judgment favorable for your sisters.' Kelimmah (shame, disgrace, ignominy) is the covenant consequence of exposed unfaithfulness. The command to bear shame is not merely punitive but formative: shame is the emotional recognition that one's behavior has violated one's identity and obligations. Jerusalem's shame is deepened by the comparative analysis — she is shamed even by comparison with Sodom.
16.53: veshavti et-shevutehen et-shevut Sodom vebnoteiha: 'I will restore the fortunes of Sodom and her daughters.' The sudden turn to restoration (shavah et-shevut = restore the fortunes, a standard restoration formula) is startling: even Sodom's fortunes will be restored. This is eschatological inclusion — the most notorious pagan city of the OT tradition is not beyond YHWH's restorative reach. The inclusion of Sodom, Samaria, and Jerusalem in the restoration equally honors the universal scope of covenant grace while placing Jerusalem among sinners who have no claim except YHWH's unilateral mercy.
16.54: lema'an tisi et-kelimmatach: 'So that you may bear your shame.' The purpose clause connects the restoration of Sodom and Samaria to Jerusalem's shame: when even Sodom is restored, Jerusalem's position as the greatest sinner among them will be permanently visible. Shame here is not destructive but formative — the proper response to grace received and spurned, which itself becomes the context in which grace is re-received.
16.55: veSodom achotaich uvnoteiha tashovna lekadmatan: 'Sodom and her daughters shall return to their former state.' Kadmatan (former state, what they were before) signals a restoration to the original created order before catastrophic judgment. The triple restoration — Sodom, Samaria, Jerusalem — is the eschatological inclusivism that the prophets consistently announce: YHWH's redemptive purpose will encompass more, not fewer, than Israel's election had imagined.
16.56: velo hayeta Sodom achotaich leshemua befich: 'Sodom your sister was not even a byword in your mouth in the day of your pride.' In Jerusalem's days of prosperity and religious confidence, Sodom was looked down upon. Geona'tech (your pride) is the sin that prevented Jerusalem from recognizing her own condition by comparison. Pride is the mechanism of self-justification that makes one blind to one's own failures while remaining acutely aware of others'.
16.57: beterem tigaleh ra'atach: 'Before your own wickedness was uncovered.' The temporal clause is ironic: Jerusalem was comfortable looking down on Sodom before her own sins were exposed. The exposure (galah = uncover, reveal) is both the literal exposure of the city to its enemies and the theological exposure of the covenant violations that were always present but now publicly known through the prophet's indictment.
16.58: zimmtatech vetoe'avotaich at-nsatim ne'um YHWH: 'Your lewdness and your abominations, you have borne them, declares YHWH.' The forensic sentence formula closes the comparative section: Jerusalem has borne (and must bear) the consequences of her own lewdness (zimah = lewdness, scheme, wickedness) and abominations. The declaration ne'um YHWH seals the judicial verdict.
16.59: ki koh amar adonai YHWH ve'asiti otach ka'asher asit: 'For thus says the Lord YHWH: I will deal with you as you have done.' The principle of correspondence — judgment mirrors offense — establishes the justice of what follows. The specific offense cited is the despising of the oath (bazit alah) and breaking of the covenant (heper berit). The covenant language here shifts from the marriage metaphor back to the Sinai-covenant juridical frame: the offense is treaty-breaking, and the sentence is treaty-enforcement.
16.60: vezakarti ani et-beriti otach bimei neurayich vahakimoti lach berit olam: 'But I will remember my covenant with you in the days of your youth, and I will establish with you an everlasting covenant.' This is the theological heart of the chapter — the climactic reversal. Ani (I myself) is emphatic — YHWH takes the initiative unilaterally. Bezakarti (I will remember) is the covenant formula for faithful loyalty: not a mere mental act but an act of committed engagement. The berit olam (everlasting covenant) is the unconditional covenant of grace that exceeds and outlasts the Sinai covenant's conditionality. It echoes the unconditional promises to Noah (Gen 9:16), Abraham (Gen 17:7), and David (2 Sam 23:5).
16.61: vezakart et-derachayich vehikalamt: 'Then you will remember your ways and be ashamed.' The order is crucial: YHWH's remembering of the covenant (v. 60) precedes and enables Jerusalem's remembering of her ways (v. 61). The grace comes first; the shame is its fruit, not its prerequisite. Beten otach lach livnot (when I give them to you as daughters) — the restored sisters (Samaria, Sodom) will be given to Jerusalem, but not through the Sinai covenant. The qualifying phrase velo mibritech (but not from your covenant) is theologically decisive: the restoration is not the resumption of the conditioned Sinai relationship but something new.
16.62: vahakimoti ani et-beriti otach veyadata ki ani YHWH: 'I will establish my covenant with you, and you will know that I am YHWH.' The covenant formula and the recognition formula are joined: the establishment of the everlasting covenant produces the knowledge of YHWH. This is the goal of all of Ezekiel's judgment oracles — yadata ki ani YHWH (you will know that I am YHWH) is the book's signature phrase. The knowledge is not merely intellectual but relational acknowledgment born from covenant experience.
16.63: lema'an tizkheri vevoshet lach velo yihyeh lach od pitchon peh: 'So that you may remember and be ashamed, and never open your mouth again because of your shame.' The double purpose clause — remembering and being ashamed — frames the entire restoration as grace-induced humility. Lo yihyeh lach od pitchon peh (you will never open your mouth again) in the context of your shame captures the total surrender of self-justification that grace produces. The closed mouth of shame replaces the open mouth of boasting. This is not theological shame-based paralysis but the appropriate creature-response to extravagant covenant grace: speechless gratitude.

17.1: Standard revelation formula opening the parable of the two eagles. Chapter 17 presents Ezekiel's most elaborate mashal (riddle-parable): it requires two sections — the parable (vv. 1-10) and its interpretation (vv. 11-21) — followed by a messianic supplement (vv. 22-24) that transforms the parable's meaning from judgment to eschatological hope.
17.2: chud chidah umshol mashal: 'Pose a riddle and speak a parable.' Chidah (riddle, enigma) and mashal (parable, proverb, figurative speech) are paired — the oracle is deliberately opaque, requiring interpretation. This double generic label prepares the audience for indirect communication: the message about Babylon, Zedekiah, and Egypt will first be veiled in animal imagery and only later decoded. The rhetorical distancing may allow the audience to evaluate the situation objectively before recognizing their own culpability.
17.3: hannesher hagadol godol hakanapaim erech ha'ever: 'The great eagle with great wings, long of wing.' Nesher (eagle or vulture) is the Hebrew term for the largest birds of prey; Nebuchadnezzar is compared to an eagle throughout the prophets (Jer 48:40; 49:22; Hab 1:8). Mele ha'notzah (full of long feathers) indicates the eagle's majesty and power — an imperial figure. Lebanon represents Jerusalem and Judah (cedar = royal/Davidic dynasty); the eagle coming to Lebanon is the Babylonian invasion of Judah (605-597 BCE).
17.4: et-tzameret haaraz qataf: 'He cropped off the top of the cedar.' Tzameret (topmost branch, top, crown) of the cedar = Jehoiachin, the young king taken into Babylonian exile in 597 BCE (2 Kgs 24:12). The first eagle takes the top branch to the eretz kenaan (land of Canaan/merchants) and the ir rokhlin (city of merchants) = Babylon. The commercial language (kenaan as merchant, rochlim as traders) captures Babylon's status as the economic center of the ancient world.
17.5: vayikach mizera haarets: 'He also took from the seed of the land.' The second planting: after taking Jehoiachin, Nebuchadnezzar planted a new king from the royal line — Zedekiah (Mattaniah), Jehoiachin's uncle (2 Kgs 24:17). Bezera haarets (from the seed of the land) refers to planting a Judean vassal rather than a Babylonian governor. Al-mayim rabbim (beside many waters) = the Babylonian arrangements for Judah — political stability under Babylonian oversight.
17.6: vatitzomach vatehiy legefen seruchah shiphelet qomah: 'It grew and became a spreading vine of low stature.' The vine (gefen) images Zedekiah's vassalage: he prospered but remained deliberately low (shiphelet qomah = low of height), spreading but not rising up. Litphenot darnanim alav (its branches turned toward the eagle) shows the vine's dependence on the Babylonian eagle. The shorashav tachtav (roots under him) suggests Zedekiah's entire stability was grounded in the Babylonian treaty.
17.7: vehinei nesher acher echad gadol: 'But behold, there was another eagle, great.' The second eagle is Pharaoh Hophra/Apries of Egypt (cf. Jer 37:5-8; 44:30). The vine (Zedekiah) bent its roots toward this second eagle — seeking military alliance with Egypt against Babylon. Hishlacha shorasheiha alav (it stretched out its roots toward him) is the act of political treachery: breaking the sworn treaty with Babylon in favor of Egypt. The passive construction emphasizes the vine's own initiative — Zedekiah was not forced but chose the treacherous reorientation.
17.8: el-sadeh tovah al-mayim rabbim hi shetuah: 'It was planted in good soil beside many waters.' The description of Zedekiah's original planting (by Nebuchadnezzar, in Babylonian-protected Judah) as genuinely good and well-watered emphasizes that the problem was not the circumstance but the choice. Zedekiah had adequate resources and stability under Babylonian suzerainty; his reaching toward Egypt was gratuitous treachery, not desperate survival instinct.
17.9: emor hatzliach hatichlach: 'Say: Will it thrive? Will it not pull up its roots?' The rhetorical questions are ironic — any agricultural expert would know that a vine transplanted from good soil to a second location will not thrive. The withering of the vine despite the second eagle's protection is inevitable: Egypt will prove unable to protect Judah from Babylon (as Jer 37:7-10 confirms). The political analysis is also theological: alliances that involve oath-breaking with Babylon are also oath-breaking before YHWH (v. 19).
17.10: vehineh shetuah hatzlach: 'And behold — it is planted. Will it thrive?' The vine will wither even in its new location (al-arugot tzimchah tiyaves). The east wind (ruach hakadim = the sirocco, hot dry wind from the desert = Babylon) will complete the destruction. The entirely agricultural metaphor maintains the parabolic distance — the audience evaluates the botany before they realize they are evaluating their own king's political theology.
17.11: vayehi devar YHWH elai lemor: 'And the word of YHWH came to me, saying.' The formula marks the transition from parable to interpretation, which is characteristic of Ezekiel's extended parables (cf. 24:15-27). The interpretation section decodes the botanical imagery systematically — each image identified with its historical referent — making Ezekiel's hermeneutics transparent and modeling a method of reading figurative prophecy.
17.12: emor na leveit hameri: 'Say now to the rebellious house.' Beit hameri (house of rebellion) is Ezekiel's constant characterization of Israel/Jerusalem (2:3, 5, 6, 7, 8; 3:9, 26, 27). The interpretation is addressed directly to the exilic community already in Babylon — many of whom may have had hopes that Zedekiah's alliance with Egypt would produce a military reversal. The historical identification: the first eagle = Babylon's king; Lebanon's topmost branch = Jerusalem's king (Jehoiachin).
17.13: vayikach mizera hamemlucha: 'And he took from the royal seed.' The royal seed = Zedekiah (from the Davidic line). Vayikrot ito berit (he made a covenant with him) = the vassal treaty Nebuchadnezzar imposed on Zedekiah. The treaty is a formal berit (covenant, oath-bound agreement); its violation will carry the full weight of covenant-breaking. Vayavet oto be'alah (he brought him under oath) — the treaty was sworn before YHWH (the gods/YHWH being invoked as treaty witnesses).
17.14: liheyot mamlacha shephela: 'That it might be a lowly kingdom.' YHWH's actual design for Zedekiah was that of a lowly but stable vassal (shephela), not rising up but living quietly within the Babylonian framework. Lishmor et-berito (to keep his covenant) — the vassal covenant with Babylon is implicitly a covenant with YHWH's design for this season of judgment. Zedekiah's treaty-breaking with Babylon was therefore simultaneously a violation of the divinely-designed political arrangement.
17.15: vayimrod bo lishloach mal'achav Mitzraimah: 'But he rebelled against him by sending his messengers to Egypt.' The political treachery is described with the root marad (rebel) — the same word used for rebellion against YHWH. Sending to Egypt for horses (susim veam rav) was explicitly prohibited under the Deuteronomic law (Deut 17:16: “he shall not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt”). Zedekiah's Egyptian alliance violated both the Babylonian treaty and the Torah's prohibition of military dependence on Egypt.
17.16: chai ani ne'um adonai YHWH im-lo bemlachim hamemlich oto: 'As I live, declares the Lord YHWH: In the place of the king who made him king, in Babylon, he will die.' The divine oath formula seals the judicial sentence. The punishment fits the crime: Zedekiah who betrayed the king who made him king will die in Babylon. The irony is complete — his alliance with Egypt to escape Babylon results in his capture and death in Babylon (2 Kgs 25:6-7).
17.17: velo bechayil gadol uvkahal rav yaaseh oto Pharo: 'Neither with great army nor great company will Pharaoh make [anything] for him.' Egypt's military assistance will be useless (lo yaaseh oto) — the promised horses and troops will not materialize effectively against Babylon (Jer 37:7 records that Egypt briefly appeared and withdrew). The vine's fatal mistake was assuming Egypt could deliver what only YHWH can provide: security against the Babylonian instrument of divine judgment.
17.18: uvazah alah lahaper berit: 'He despised the oath by breaking the covenant.' Bazah alah (despised the oath/curse) — the alah (oath, curse) was the treaty-oath sworn before YHWH. To break it is not merely political perfidy but religious violation: treaty-oaths in the ancient Near East were sworn before the gods, and in Israel's case, before YHWH himself. Ezekiel's logic is that you cannot neutrally violate a Babylonian treaty if that treaty was sealed with YHWH as witness — to break the treaty is to despise YHWH's role as treaty-guarantor.
17.19: lachan koh amar adonai YHWH chai ani im-lo alati asher bazah ve'beriti asher hefer: 'Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: As I live, I will bring down upon his head the oath he despised and the covenant he broke.' This verse is theologically crucial: YHWH claims the violated treaty as his own (alati, my oath; beriti, my covenant). The Babylonian treaty became sacred through the swearing of an oath in YHWH's name. When Zedekiah broke it, he was not just politically treacherous but personally dishonoring YHWH, whose name was invoked in the covenant.
17.20: uferashti alav reshti venilkad bimtzudati: 'I will spread my net over him, and he will be caught in my snare.' YHWH's net is the characteristic image for inescapable divine judgment (cf. 12:13; Hos 7:12; Lam 1:13). The capture of Zedekiah is recounted in 2 Kgs 25:5-6 — he fled and was caught by Babylonian troops at Jericho. Ezekiel presents this military capture as YHWH's own action: the Babylonian army is the instrument; YHWH is the hunter. Vehaviotiv Bavel (I will bring him to Babylon) — judgment executed through historical agency is still YHWH's personal action.
17.21: umivchar migrachav bekhol-aguddav baherev yipolu: 'And all his best troops in all his squadrons will fall by the sword.' The military rout of Judah's army is included in the judgment sentence. Uvisharid yizru lekhol-ruach (the survivors will be scattered to every wind) — the scattering to the four winds is Ezekiel's characteristic image for exile (cf. 5:12; 12:14). The phrase veyadata ki ani YHWH dibarti (you will know that I, YHWH, have spoken) concludes the judgment section with the recognition formula.
17.22: koh amar adonai YHWH velakachti ani mitzameret haaraz haramah venatati: 'Thus says the Lord YHWH: I myself will take from the top of the cedar — from its high crown — and I will set it out.' The messianic supplement is introduced by the emphatic ani (I myself) — YHWH contrasts himself with the Babylonian eagle who took the previous top-branch (Jehoiachin). YHWH's own taking is a planting, not a deportation. Mi'tzameret roshey yeniqotav rach (from the topmost of its highest branches, a tender twig) = the messianic shoot from the Davidic line (cf. Isa 11:1; Jer 23:5; Zech 3:8). The tenderness and smallness of the twig emphasizes that this planting will be YHWH's miracle, not political prowess.
17.23: behar merom Yisrael eshtelenhu: 'On the high mountain of Israel I will plant it.' Behar merom Yisrael (on the high mountain of Israel) = Mount Zion/the eschatological mountain of YHWH (Isa 2:2-3; Ps 48:1-2). The planting on this mountain is the reversal of the Babylonian deportation to Babylon's “mountain of merchants.” The planted twig will become a noble cedar (erez addir) under which all birds will dwell — the language of the great world-tree (cf. Dan 4:10-12; Matt 13:32) signifying universal sovereignty. Every bird of every kind (kol tzipor kol-kanaf) will dwell in its shade — the messianic kingdom's universal reach.
17.24: veyadu kol-atsei hasadeh ki ani YHWH hishpalti etz gavoah: 'And all the trees of the field shall know that I, YHWH, have brought the high tree low and made the high tree low.' The recognition formula applied to the whole created order. The four reversals — high tree brought low, low tree raised high; green tree dried up, dry tree made to flourish — are the covenant's characteristic pattern (Hannah's prayer in 1 Sam 2:4-8; the Magnificat in Luke 1:51-53). YHWH is the sovereign who reverses human assessments of power and permanence. The final seal: ani YHWH dibarti ve'asiti (I YHWH have spoken and I will do it) — the absolute certainty of divine speech becoming act.

21.1: Standard oracle-reception formula. Chapter 21 opens a sustained sword-oracle that constitutes Ezekiel's most concentrated meditation on the divine instrument of judgment. The Hebrew chapter division places the pivot differently: MT 21:1 = English 20:45, making the 'south/forest' oracle the opening of this sword sequence. The English chapter numeration follows the LXX.
21.2: sim panecha derekh teman ('set your face toward the south'): sim panim (set the face) is Ezekiel's characteristic direction-formula for prophetic address — the prophet physically orients toward the target of the oracle. The 'forest' of the Negev is a figurative description, likely of Judah, since the Negev is not forested. The oracle establishes the direction before the target is named.
21.3: ya'ar haNegev ('forest of the south/Negev'): the allegory thin — the south-forest is Jerusalem-Judah. Fire devoured (achel) in the forest prepares for the explicit announcement that follows: 'I am about to draw my sword' (v. 4). Every tree — righteous and wicked — is consumed, establishing the totalistic nature of the coming judgment.
21.4: tzaddiq veresha ('righteous and wicked'): the sword indiscriminately cuts off both — a theological difficulty the exiles press (18:1-4; 33:10-20). YHWH's answer throughout chapters 18 and 33 is individual accountability, but here the sword oracle emphasizes the totalistic corporate judgment on the land as a whole. Both registers must be held: corporate judgment and individual accountability operate at different levels.
21.5: kol-basar yeda ki-ani YHWH heretzti et-charvi mita'rea ('all flesh will know that I, YHWH, have drawn my sword from its sheath'): the universal-recognition formula (yeda', they will know) applied to the sword-drawing. The sword in the sheath is a standard ancient Near Eastern idiom for restrained military power; drawing the sword means the restraint is now removed. YHWH himself is the one who draws — not merely permitting Babylonian aggression but actively initiating it.
21.6: ne'aneq beshivron motnayim ('sigh with the breaking/shattering of the loins'): the root n'q (to sigh, groan, moan) combined with shivron motnayim (collapsing/breaking of loins — the body's core giving way). Ezekiel is commanded to perform grief so physically intense that it signals structural collapse — not mere sadness but bodily breakdown. The sign-act makes the prophet's body the first casualty of the announced judgment.
21.7: lema ah atah ne'aneq ('why are you sighing?'): the audience query that triggers the public explanation — the sign-act requires interrogation to become proclamation. The answer frames the coming catastrophe as something that will make 'every heart melt, all hands hang limp, every spirit faint, every knee turn to water' — the full physiological register of paralytic terror (cf. 7:17).
21.8: Transition to the formal sword poem/oracle. The second oracle within the chapter sequence marks an escalation: from sign-act (sighing) to direct poetic speech about the sword. The sword oracle in vv. 8-17 is recognized as among the most intense martial poetry in the Hebrew Bible.
21.9: cherev cherev huchaddah vegam meruqah ('a sword, a sword — sharpened and polished'): the doubled cherev intensifies urgency (cf. Jer 4:10 and other doubled words of alarm). Huchaddah (sharpened, Hophal of chadd) and meruqah (polished, Pual of maraq): the sword is combat-ready — the double preparation suggests both effectiveness (sharpened) and terror (gleaming). The war-readiness of the divine instrument is total.
21.10: lematbeach tivchah ('sharpened to make a slaughter'): the sword's purpose is stated baldly — slaughter (tevach). The second half of the verse is notoriously difficult: o nasis shevet beni — 'or shall we rejoice/dismiss the rod of my son?' may be a shocked question rejecting comfort-seeking when the scepter of Israel has itself become the target. The shevet (rod/scepter) that should represent Davidic authority is now itself being swept away.
21.11: vetitten ota limrut lamatbeach lachuf ('it is given to be polished, to be handled, to make a slaughter'): three infinitives of purpose — the sword exists to be polished, handled, and used for slaughter. The passive 'it is given' (nittenah) signals divine agency without explicitly naming YHWH as the giver. The sword has been commissioned and prepared; all that remains is its deployment.
21.12: za'aq vehaveil ('cry out and wail'): direct commands to Ezekiel to perform lamentation. hak-yerekha ('strike your thigh'): a gesture of distress — literally striking the upper thigh as a sign of horror and grief. Compare Jer 31:19 where the same gesture marks repentance. Ki-hi haytah vichal-nesi'ei Yisrael ('for it will be against all the princes of Israel') — the royal class is named as the sword's target.
21.13: bachan ('test/proof'): a brief, cryptic verse — 'it is tested' or 'because it is a test.' The verb bachan (to test, examine, prove) suggests the sword-judgment is also a divine probation — YHWH's action will prove the truth of his word and the nature of Israel's condition. Vema tihyeh im-gam mateh bozit ('what then if even the despising rod will be no more?') — the scepter of authority has been judged useless.
21.14: hak-yad el-yad ('clap hand to hand'): Ezekiel is commanded to clap hands together — a sign of either grief or bitter irony (cf. 6:11; 22:13). Cherev shalishit ('the sword doubled/tripled'): the sword is to strike again and again — doubled, then tripled — against the slain. The intensification through repetition drives home the completeness of the judgment.
21.15: lema'an lamug lev ('in order that hearts may melt'): the sword's psychological as well as physical purpose — not merely killing but creating the paralytic terror in all who witness.
