Every gate is targeted (achad sha'ar mamgurut cherev''am — the sword positioned at every gate), ensuring there is no exit. The sword threatens from every direction.
21.16: hitchaddi yemini hasmili le'anaph panayich ('be sharp, go right; direct yourself, go left — wherever your face is directed'): the command to the sword to be versatile, omnidirectional. The sword's freedom of movement in any direction mirrors the chariot's omnidirectional movement from chapter 1 — both are divine instruments of perfect responsiveness to the divine will.
21.17: vegam ani acha el-kappi ('and I also will clap my hands'): YHWH himself joins the clapping — the divine gesture that Ezekiel performed (v.14) is now performed by YHWH. vahinnichoti chemati ('and I will satisfy/rest my fury'): nuach (Hiphil, 'to cause to rest') — the fury that has been building is brought to completion. The word nuach is also the word for 'Noah' (Noach), though the pun is not in view here. The fury rests when it is fully enacted.
21.18: New oracle: sim lecha shetei derakhim ('set for yourself two ways/roads'): the road-sign oracle. Ezekiel is to draw a map showing two roads from Babylon — one to Jerusalem, one to Rabbah of Ammon. The dual-target establishes that Babylon's judgment is not specific to Judah but applies to the whole region. The 'sword of the king of Babylon' serves YHWH's purposes in both directions.
21.19: veber'i bara' ('and engrave an engraving'): the carved road-marker at the crossroads points both ways. Rosh derekh ir ('the head of the road to a city') — the fork in the road. The military intelligence Babylon actually used (divination at crossroads) is described here from the perspective of YHWH's sovereign direction of Babylonian decision-making: their divination will happen to point toward Jerusalem because YHWH has determined that outcome.
21.20: lesim qesem qesem cherev el-Yerushalayim velo el-Rabbah Bnei-Ammon ('to set divination — the sword against Jerusalem, and against Rabbah of Ammon'): two targets of the same Babylonian sword. The oracle holds both in view simultaneously — Israel is not specially targeted; rather, the sword reaches all who stand under divine judgment. Ammon will receive its oracle in 21:28-32.
21.21: ki-amad melech Bavel el-em haderekh bereshit shetei hadderakhim liqsom qasem ('for the king of Babylon stands at the fork of the two roads, at the head of the two ways, to use divination'): qesem qesamim (divination/divination) — an intensive or generalized form. Three divination methods listed: herak bacheitzim (shaking arrows/belomancy), consulting teraphim (shal baterafim), and examining a liver (ra'ah bakabed). All three are condemned in Deuteronomy, yet here YHWH uses even Babylonian divination to accomplish his purpose.
21.22: beyad-yemino hayah haqesem Yerushalayim ('in his right hand was the divination — Jerusalem'): the lot falls on Jerusalem. Whatever the Babylonian diviner intended, the outcome was divinely predetermined. Latum krim (to direct battering rams), lif'toch peh beretach (to open the mouth in slaughter/with a shout), leharim qol biteruah (to raise the voice with a battle cry) — the military operations described in technical detail: siege equipment, shouting, and war-cry.
21.23: uhem kiqsam shav be'eineihem ('and it will be to them like a false/empty divination in their eyes'): the inhabitants of Jerusalem will dismiss the Babylonian divination as meaningless — their false prophets have told them they are safe. The sworn oaths (shevuot shavu'ot) with which they have been reassured will create a false confidence that makes the judgment more devastating when it falls. The same dynamic recurs in 13:10: 'peace when there is no peace.'
21.24: laken koh amar Adonai YHWH ya'an hizkarekhem avonekhem ('therefore, because you have caused your iniquity to be remembered'): the Babylonian divination that Jerusalem dismisses as empty is precisely the means by which YHWH 'remembers' Israel's iniquity — that is, brings it into the sphere of active judgment. Avon (iniquity) remembered by YHWH is iniquity being brought to account.
21.25: ve'atah chalal rasha nesi Yisrael ('and you, O profaned wicked prince of Israel'): direct address to Zedekiah (or the Davidic monarchy generally). Chalal (profaned/pierced) — the prince has been desacralized, stripped of the protective holiness of the anointed office. Asher-ba yomo be'et avon qetz ('whose day has come, at the time when iniquity reaches its end') — the convergence of the prince's failure and the end of the age of iniquity.
21.26: haser hamitznefet veharim haketer ('remove the turban and lift off the crown'): two objects removed. Mitznefet (turban) is the high priestly turban (Ex 28:4; 39:28) — here worn by the prince, blurring royal and priestly roles. Keter (crown) is the royal diadem. The removal of both signals the simultaneous dissolution of both offices. Zot lo-zot ('this shall not be this'): the divine declaration that the present order of things is reversed — the high brought low and the low raised.
21.27: avvah avvah avvah asimennah ('overturned, overturned, overturned — I will make it'): the triple repetition of avvah (ruin/overturning) is unparalleled in Hebrew prose — three successive destructions of the Davidic throne (Jehoahaz, Jehoiakim, Zedekiah — each deported or killed). gam-zot lo haytah ('also this shall not be') — the throne ceases. Then: ad-bo asher-lo hamishpat venatattihu ('until he comes to whom the right/judgment belongs, and to him I will give it'): the suspension formula — the throne is overturned and held in eschatological suspension until the one to whom it belongs by right arrives. This echoes Genesis 49:10 (ad ki-yavo Shiloh) — the scepter withheld until the one to whom it belongs claims it.
21.28: uven-adam hinave el-Bnei Ammon ('and son of man, prophesy to the Ammonites'): the sword that was directed toward Jerusalem now pivots to Ammon. The oracle shows that YHWH's judgment is not partialistic: all nations under divine indictment receive the sword. Ammon mocked Israel's downfall (25:3-7); their turn is coming. The Ammonite oracle is brief compared to the Jerusalem oracle — the sustained focus on Israel reflects the depth of the covenant relationship.
21.29: cherev cherev petuyah latatbeach meruqah ('a sword, a sword unsheathed for slaughter, polished'): the sword poem that opened in v.9 is reprised for Ammon — the same sharpened, polished sword turns toward Ammon. Chazot shav ('while they see false visions') — the Ammonite prophets who offered false comfort will be proved wrong as surely as Israel's false prophets were proved wrong. The sword does not spare those whose prophets promised peace.
21.30: hashev el-ta'areh ('return it to its sheath'): a shift — the command to return the sword to its sheath. This is not the end of judgment but a temporary withdrawal from the Ammonite oracle, anticipating the fuller judgment oracle against Ammon in chapter 25. Bimqom asher nivrate sham tishafet ('in the place where you were created, there you will be judged') — the land of origin is also the land of judgment.
21.31: shafachti alecha za'mi ('I will pour out my indignation upon you'): za'am (indignation, burning anger) — a term used for YHWH's most intense categorical anger. Nishaft'ti alecha be'esh evrati ('I will blow on you with the fire of my wrath') — the breath/blowing of divine wrath as fire. The same terms used for judgment on Jerusalem (7:8; 22:22) are applied to Ammon.
21.32: uchol-Yerushalayim umikol-ha'aretz ('to all Jerusalem and all the land'): the Ammonite oracle ends with the declaration that Ammon will be 'fuel for the fire' — burned as fuel rather than smelted as metal (the distinction between destruction and refining). Lo tizakher ki ani YHWH dibarti ('you will not be remembered, for I, YHWH, have spoken'): the finality formula — Ammon will be forgotten, not preserved as a witness. The covenant nation Israel receives judgment but is preserved through it; Ammon receives judgment and is obliterated. The distinction between covenant and non-covenant judgment is stark.

22.1: Another oracle-reception formula opening a distinct unit. Chapter 22 is a sustained judicial proceeding — YHWH as prosecuting attorney presenting the case against Jerusalem in legal form (hatishpot, 'will you judge?' — the interrogative implies Ezekiel is being commissioned as a judge). The catalogue of specific offenses (vv. 6-12) is the most comprehensive indictment list in Ezekiel.
22.2: ha'ir haddamim ('the city of blood'): a compound construct — Jerusalem named by its defining characteristic. Damim (bloods, plural) suggests multiple acts of bloodshed, accumulated guilt. The city's identity is fixed by what it has done with blood — the violation of Genesis 9:6 and Leviticus 17:11 ('the blood is the life') on a systemic scale. Hoda'at et kol-to'evoteiha ('make known all her abominations'): the prophet as prosecutorial witness.
22.3: ir shofechet dam betocha ('a city that sheds blood within herself'): the blood is internal — Jerusalem's bloodguilt is not from external violence against foreigners but from internal exploitation and injustice. Labo ittah ('to bring her time'): the blood-shedding hastens YHWH's judgment — each act of injustice moves the eschatological clock. The idols made within the city add ritual defilement to moral bloodguilt.
22.4: asha'mt bedam asher shafacht ('you have incurred guilt by the blood you shed'): asham (guilt/guilt offering) — the legal term for offense requiring restitution/atonement. The verb form here places the guilt as already incurred, not merely potential. Tameh biglulaich ('defiled by your idols'): tame' (ritually/morally unclean) — the same root used in Leviticus for the pollution that must be addressed through purification rituals, here applied to the systemic state of the city.
22.5: qerovah vesheloha mimecha tiqalasena ('those near and those far from you will mock you'): the universal mockery that falls on the humiliated city. Tamei hashem rabbat hamehumah ('defiled in name, abounding in turmoil'): name-defilement — the city whose name should mean 'city of peace' (Yerushalayim) is now defined by its defiled reputation. Turmoil (mehumah) as the defining atmosphere of the fallen city.
22.6: hinneh nesi'ei Yisrael ish lizro'o hayu vach ('the princes of Israel in you, each according to his arm/power, have shed blood'): ish lizro'o ('each by his arm') — each prince acting on his own strength/power, not under Torah constraint. The rulers are the first indicted in the catalogue. When leadership fails the covenant, every subsequent level of society follows. Hayu vach lema'an shefoch dam ('they were in you in order to shed blood') — the purpose-clause makes the bloodshed deliberate, not incidental.
22.7: av ve'em haqelu vach ('father and mother they have dishonored in you'): the first specific offense — violation of the fifth commandment (Ex 20:12; Deut 5:16). The catalogue that follows closely mirrors the Holiness Code (Lev 17-26) and the Decalogue: parent-honor, treatment of strangers, orphans, and widows. Lager asu ve'oshek bethochech ('the resident alien they have oppressed'): Leviticus 19:33-34 specifically commands fair treatment of the ger (resident alien).
22.8: miqdashi bazit ('my sanctuary you have despised'): the verb baz (to despise, treat with contempt) — active disregard for the sacred. Et-shabbatotai chillalt ('my sabbaths you have profaned'): challal (profane/desecrate) — the sabbath as covenant sign (Ex 31:13-17) is violated by treating it as an ordinary day. The pairing of sanctuary-contempt and sabbath-profanation brackets the whole cultic system of covenant order.
22.9: anshei rachil hayu vach lema'an shefoch dam ('slanderers were in you for the purpose of shedding blood'): rachil (tale-bearer/slanderer) — from the root meaning to go about/peddle; the one who circulates damaging reports. The legal prohibition in Lev 19:16 explicitly connects slanderous tale-bearing with endangering lives. Zimmah (lewdness/shameful conduct) — a strong term for sexual and moral perversion.
22.10: erva av gilla vach ('the nakedness of a father they have uncovered in you'): incest-language drawn directly from Leviticus 18 — 'uncovering nakedness' as the standard euphemism for prohibited sexual relations. The specific listing of violations — father's wife, women in their impurity — follows the Levitical list almost verbatim, demonstrating that the indictment is based on specific Torah legislation already in force.
22.11: ish et-eshet re'ehu asah to'evah ('each man did abomination with his neighbor's wife'): to'evah (abomination) — the term used throughout Leviticus 18 for the sexual violations that 'defile the land' (Lev 18:27). The list includes adultery, incest with daughter-in-law, and violation of a half-sister. The word to'evah links these sexual offenses to the idolatry described earlier — both are categorized as covenant-defiling abominations.
22.12: shochad lachu vach lema'an shefoch dam ('bribes they have taken in you for the purpose of shedding blood'): the economic/judicial corruption — bribery corrupts the justice system, leading to wrongful deaths. Neshech vetarbith laqachta ('interest and increase you have taken'): neshech (biting interest, from nashak, to bite) and tarbith (increase/profit) — the two prohibited forms of usury from Leviticus 25:36-37. Both exploit the economically vulnerable and violate the covenant economic order. Oti shachacht ('me you have forgotten') — the summary of all the offenses: the root cause is forgetting YHWH.
22.13: hinneh hikiti kapi el-bitzek'ach asher asit ('I have struck my hands together over your unjust gain'): YHWH performs the hand-clapping gesture (cf. 21:14, 17) — here as a sign of judgmental finality, possibly mimicking a legal sealing-of-verdict action. The gesture marks the decisive moment when the divine judge has heard enough evidence and pronounces the verdict.
22.14: hayya'amod libech ('will your heart endure?'): the interrogative challenges the city's imagined resilience. Im-techazaknah yadayich ('or will your hands be strong?'): the rhetorical questions expect 'no' — the heart that tolerated all the abominations will not endure when YHWH acts. Bayamim asher ani oseh otach ('in the days when I deal with you') — the divine dealing is certain; only the timing is unspecified.
22.15: vehifitzotich bagoyim vezereticha ba'aratsot ('I will scatter you among the nations and disperse you among the lands'): the covenant-curse of dispersion (Lev 26:33; Deut 28:64) explicitly enacted. Vatimachti timatchich mimmech ('and I will consume your filth from you'): the dispersion serves a purifying function — the removal of the impurity from the land, even through the harsh means of exile. The land vomits out its inhabitants (Lev 18:28).
22.16: venicchalit bach le'einei goyim ('you will be profaned in yourself before the eyes of the nations'): challal (profaned) — the city that profaned YHWH's sanctuary will itself be profaned before the watching nations. The public nature of the humiliation mirrors the public nature of the abominations: what was done visibly in the temple courtyards will be answered by visible public judgment. Yada'ta ki-ani YHWH: recognition-formula closing the indictment section.
22.17: Transition to the furnace/smelting allegory (vv. 17-22). The oracle now shifts from a legal catalogue of offenses to a metallurgical metaphor for the purifying action of judgment. This unit is complete in itself: diagnosis (vv. 17-19), process (vv. 20-21), and result (v. 22).
22.18: hayu-li beit Yisrael le-sigim ('the house of Israel has become dross to me'): sigim (dross, slag) — the worthless residue left after refining metal. All that Israel has become is listed: dross of silver, bronze, tin, iron, and lead. The precious metal (silver) has become slag; the base metals that contaminate it are now all that remains. The metallurgical vocabulary was familiar in the ancient Near East: Mesopotamian texts speak of gods refining their people as a smith refines silver.
22.19: lachen koh amar Adonai YHWH ya'an heyot kulchem le-sigim ('because all of you have become dross'): the lachen (therefore) marks the consequence that follows from the metallurgical diagnosis. The gathering of Israel into the furnace-city (kur) is explicitly compared to gathering metal for smelting: qibbetz etchem el-toch Yerushalayim ('I will gather you into the midst of Jerusalem').
22.20: kiqibbutz kesef unchoshet uvarzel ve'oferet ve'bdil el-toch kur ('as silver and copper and iron and lead and tin are gathered into a furnace'): the complete list of smelted metals gathered for the furnace. Lenafuach alav esh ('to blow fire upon it'): the bellows of the smelter that intensifies the heat. YHWH is both the metallurgist who has diagnosed the dross and the one who works the bellows: apen bi'api ushilchti etchem ('I will blow with my wrath and I will put you in and melt you').
22.21: vekanavasti etchem vefachati aleichem be'esh evrati venitu'sem betochatah ('I will gather you and blow upon you with the fire of my wrath, and you will be melted in its midst'): the extended metaphor reaches its climax — YHWH as the divine smelter working the furnace with his own breath of wrath. Evrati (my wrath/overflow) — the same word as 'outburst' — the uncontained energy that drives the smelting heat. The city is the furnace and the people are the metal to be melted.
22.22: kehintoch kesef betoch kur ken tinatchu betochah ('as silver is melted in the midst of a furnace, so you will be melted in its midst'): the simile made explicit — the metaphor's equation stated plainly. Veyeda'tem ki-ani YHWH shafachti chamati aleichem ('and you will know that I, YHWH, have poured out my fury upon you'): the recognition-formula closes the smelting oracle. The knowledge gained through the furnace-judgment is the goal of the whole process.
22.23: A new word-reception formula introduces the oracle against the land's classes (vv. 23-31). The land is diagnosed as lo ge'ushma beyom za'am ('not rained upon in the day of indignation') — a land that should have received the cleansing rain of YHWH's blessing has instead received only the drought of judgment. The social analysis that follows moves class by class through the failure of every sector of leadership.
22.24: lo ge'ushma beyom za'am ('not cleansed/rained upon in the day of wrath'): ge'ushma (a hapax legomenon, likely from gasham, to rain) — the land that has not received purifying rain is parched and unclean. The 'day of wrath' (yom za'am) echoes the 'day of YHWH' language of chapter 7. The land's condition as unwatered/uncleansed is the environmental correlate of its moral condition.
22.25: qesher nevi'eiha bequirbah ('a conspiracy of her prophets in her midst'): the prophets are indicted first — as the primary source of false guidance. Ke'aryeh sho'eg toreh taref ('like a roaring lion tearing the prey'): the prophets who should protect and guide the flock have become predators. Nefashot lachu ('they devour lives/souls') — the false prophets consume the people they claim to serve. Choshen nachalah lavku ('they have taken treasure and wealth') — the prophets profit materially from their falsehoods.
22.26: kohaneiha chamesu torati veyechalelu qodoshai ('her priests have done violence to my Torah and profaned my holy things'): chamas (violence/wrong) applied to Torah-treatment — the priests have violated the Torah they were commissioned to teach. Bein qodesh lechol lo hivdilu ('between holy and common they have not distinguished'): the priestly function is precisely the making of distinctions (Lev 10:10-11; Deut 33:10) — between holy and common, clean and unclean. The priests have blurred the very boundaries they exist to maintain. Shabbetotai he'elemu eineihem meyhem ('and from my Sabbaths they have hidden their eyes').
22.27: sareiha bequirbah ke'ze'evim torfei taref ('her officials in her midst are like wolves tearing the prey'): the officials/rulers are wolves — predators on the very community they govern. Lishpoch dam le'abed nefashot lema'an beroa batza' ('to shed blood, to destroy lives, for the sake of dishonest gain'): the officials' specific failure — they use their power not for justice but for extraction. The three classes — prophets (v25), priests (v26), and officials (v27) — are each indicted in order of expected covenant faithfulness.
22.28: venevi'eiha tachu lahem tafal ('and her prophets have plastered over them with whitewash'): the prophets' second indictment — they cover up the officials' crimes with false reassurance. Tafal (whitewash/plaster of no value) — cheap, cosmetic covering over structural defects. Chazim shav velohesim lahem kazav ('seeing false visions and divining lies for them'): the prophets serve the corrupt officials by providing theological cover — false visions that validate what the officials want to do. The same image as 13:10-16.
22.29: am ha'aretz asheq asheq vegazu gazel ('the people of the land have practiced oppression and committed robbery'): the final class — the common people themselves are indicted. Ani ve'evyon honu ('the poor and needy they have wronged'): hona (to oppress/wrong, often economically) — the most vulnerable members of society exploited by those who should protect them. The covenant's particular concern for the poor (Ex 22:21-27; Lev 19:9-10; Deut 24:10-22) is comprehensively violated at every social level.
22.30: avakesh me'ahem ish godar geder ve'omed baperetz lefanai ('and I sought from them a man building a wall and standing in the breach before me'): godar geder (one who builds a wall/fence) — the intercessor as structural restorer. Ve'omed baperetz ('standing in the breach'): the military image of a defender standing in the gap made in a city wall during siege — the last defender who could prevent the collapse. Moses was the archetypal breach-stander (Ps 106:23). YHWH searched for one human being among all the prophets, priests, officials, and people — and found none. Velo matza'ti: three words that carry the weight of Israel's total failure of intercessory representation.
22.31: vashpoch aleichem za'mi be'esh evrati killitihem ('I poured out my fury upon them; with the fire of my wrath I consumed them'): the result of finding no intercessor — judgment proceeds without any mediating figure to stay it. Derekham berosham natati ('their way I repaid on their own heads'): the covenant principle of lex talionis elevated to the national level — what Israel has done returns to Israel as its own judgment. The final word of the chapter is ne'um Adonai YHWH ('declares the Lord YHWH'), the sealing oracle-formula, affirming that the divine sentence is irrevocable.

26.1: The date formula — vayehi bishtei ʿesre shanah beʾeḥad laḥodesh — "in the eleventh year, on the first of the month" — is unusual in omitting the month. Either the month is implied from context or there is a textual gap. The precision of Ezekiel's other date-formulas (1:1; 8:1; 20:1; 24:1; 29:1; 30:20; 31:1; 32:1, 17; 33:21; 40:1) suggests a month is missing here. The absence has attracted scribal notice since antiquity.
26.2: yaʿan ʾasher ʾamrah Tsur ʿal-Yerushalaim heʾaḥ nishberah delet haʿammim nasavvu ʾelai ʾemmalleʾ hoḥarebah — "because Tyre said of Jerusalem: Aha! the gate of the peoples is broken; it has turned to me; I shall be replenished, now that she is laid waste" — delet haʿammim (gate of the peoples) refers to Jerusalem's strategic commercial role; Tyre's gloating (heʾaḥ, the exclamation of malicious delight) is the same that condemns Ammon in 25:3. The verb nasavvu ʾelai (turned to me) reflects Tyre's expectation of absorbing Jerusalem's trade.
26.3: YHWH's hostile-presence formula — hinneni ʿaleikha Tsur — introduces the oracle. The metaphor of waves (kammayyim ʿolim goyim, nations rising like the sea) applied to the attacking nations is appropriate given Tyre's maritime identity. The sea that is Tyre's strength becomes the vehicle of its destruction.
26.4: The stripping of Tyre's walls and towers — venatats ḥomot Tsur uheharset migdaleha — uses the verbs natas (pull down) and haras (demolish), the standard terminology for city-destruction throughout the prophets. The result — veḥisoti ʿafarah mimennah venatati ʾotah letsuaḥ sela (I will scrape her dust from her and make her a bare rock) — introduces the key pun: tsur (rock) is both the Hebrew name for Tyre and the common noun for rock. Tyre will become what her name means.
26.5: The bare-rock image is developed — mishtoaḥ ḥaramim tihyeh betokh hayam — "she will be a place for spreading nets in the midst of the sea." The image of a fishing net-drying rock in the sea emphasizes Tyre's reduction from an empire to a substrate. The declaration ʾani dibbartim neʾum ʾAdanai YHWH (I have spoken, declares Lord YHWH) invokes divine authority as the guarantee of fulfillment.
26.6: The satellite settlements on the mainland — benoteyha ʾasher bassadeh (her daughters in the field) — are the mainland towns around Tyre proper. They will fall by the sword (baḥerev tiharagna) — the same fate as the island city but by an earthly army rather than the sea-imagery used for Tyre herself.
26.7: melek hamelakhim — "king of kings" — is Nebuchadnezzar's title in Ezekiel (also Dan 2:37; Ezra 7:12). The Aramaic and Hebrew titles translate Akkadian šar šarrāni, the imperial honorific of the Babylonian and Assyrian kings. The listing of his military assets (sus verekev vefarashim, horses, chariots, horsemen + infantry with shields, helmets, and spears) is the standard battle-array formula. He comes from the north (mittsfon) — the compass direction of Babylon's approach to Canaan via the Fertile Crescent.
26.8: The siege procedure — bnot shefelekha baḥerev yaharog venadhan ʿaleikha dayeq ushapal ʿaleikha solelah — siege-ramp (solelah), siege-wall (dayeq), shield-roof (tsinnah) — is the full technical vocabulary of ancient siege warfare. The same sequence appears in siege descriptions in 4:2 and 17:17.
26.9: The battering ram against the walls — umḥi qabbelet yittein beḥomotayikh vemigreynotav yittots migdaleikha — qabbelet (battering engine) from qabal (to be opposite/facing); migreynotav (his axes/picks) or possibly "battle-axes." Both terms are military technical vocabulary that preserves the specific ancient engineering of siege assault.
26.10: The abundance of Nebuchadnezzar's horses (merov susav yekhasseikh ʿafaram) emphasizes the numerical superiority of the invaders — their dust alone will cover the city. The noise of horsemen and wagon-wheels and chariots entering the gates (bebôʾô bivaʿayikh kevaʾu befaret ʿir meforatsah) gives the imagery of breach: he enters through the shattered gates as through a breached city.
26.11: befarsat susav yirmessek — "with the hooves of his horses he will trample all your streets" — the personalization of the horses' hooves on Tyre's streets intensifies the violence. The killing of the people (yotsatsekha ʾet-ʿammekha baḥerev yaharog) and the toppling of the steles (vematsebot ʿuzzekha laʾarets tirad) — the memorial pillars of Tyre — represent both demographic and religious destruction.
26.12: The plundering sequence — veshalelu ḥeileikh (they will plunder your wealth), webazezu rekhullateikh (loot your merchandise), veharshu ḥomotayikh (pull down your walls), venyitstsetshu batei ḥemudateikh (demolish your fine houses) — and the disposal of the rubble and timber into the sea (vehishlikhuh ʾel-tokh hayam) is the specific prediction that later commentators noted as pointing to Alexander's causeway-construction from the rubble.
26.13: The silencing of Tyre's music — vehishbatti hamon shirayikh veqol kinnoroteikh loʾ yishamaʿ ʿod — "I will put an end to the noise of your songs and the sound of your lyres shall be heard no more" — uses the same silencing vocabulary as the anti-Babylon oracles in Revelation 18:22. The music of the commercial city is a marker of its prosperity; its silence marks desolation.
26.14: The bare-rock conclusion returns — venatattikh letsuaḥ sela mishtoaḥ ḥaramim tihyi loʾ tibbane ʿod — "I will make you a bare rock; you will be a place for spreading nets; you will never be rebuilt" — the loʾ tibbane ʿod (never be rebuilt) is the absolute form of the desolation oracle, used also for Babylon (Isa 13:20) and Nineveh (Nah 1:14). The divine self-declaration follows: ki ʾani YHWH dibbartim.
26.15: The reaction of the coastlands to Tyre's fall — hakoh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH leTs veharaʿash mishmia ḥallaleikh beʾanqot ḥalal beharag herag betokhakh — the trembling of the coastlands at the sound of Tyre's fall (raʿash) anticipates the extended mourning-lament of ch 27. The structural pattern of ch 26 is: announcement (vv1-6) → battle description (vv7-14) → cosmic reaction (vv15-18) → divine verdict (vv19-21).
26.16: The princes of the sea (kol nesiʾei hayam) descending from their thrones, removing their robes, and sitting on the ground in mourning garments (ʿal-haʾarets yeshvu uḥaradu leragatim veshamehu ʿaleikh) — the gestures of mourning (sitting on the ground, trembling, horror) are the standard mourning postures of the ANE. Their response is the emotional correlate of Tyre's fall's economic-political significance.
26.17: The qinah (lament) the maritime princes compose — venasʾu ʿaleikh qinah veʾamru lakh — begins here and continues through v18. The rhythmic 3+2 qinah meter (the standard Hebrew lament meter of Lamentations) shapes the verse. The content — eikh ʾabaddt nashevet miyammim haʿir hahulala (how you have perished, you who were inhabited from the seas, the praised city) — uses the rhetorical eikh (how!) that opens Lamentations 1:1.
26.18: The fear of the coastlands on the day of Tyre's fall — ʿattah yeḥardetu ha-iyyim beyom mappaltek venivhalu hayyim ʾasher bayam mitsʾeteikh — "now the islands will tremble on the day of your fall; the islands in the sea are terrified by your passing" — the two verbs yiḥradu (tremble) and nivhalu (are terrified) express the psychic shock of the whole maritime world at the removal of Tyre from it.
26.19: The deep-water imagery of the divine judgment — ki koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH betiiti ʾoteikh kʿarim neʾeshamot kahaʿalot aleikh ʾet-tehom umikhasseikh hayyim harabim ʿaleikh — YHWH bringing up the deep (tehom) over Tyre returns to the creation-chaos vocabulary. Tehom is the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2; covering a city with it inverts creation and reduces a human settlement to primordial chaos.
26.20: Descent to the pit — vehoredetikh ʾet-yordei vor ʾel-ʿam ʿolam — "I will bring you down with those who go to the pit, to the ancient people" — bor (pit/cistern) is one of the Hebrew terms for Sheol; ʿam ʿolam (ancient people) refers to the long-dead of previous generations. The final state of Tyre is existence among the dead, not among the living, in the land (betsiyot taḥtiyot kaḥarevot ʿolam, the desolate places of the deep, as in ancient ruins).
26.21: The terror-term conclusion — ballahot ʾettenek veʾeynek loʾ tihyeh uvuvuqesh uloʾ timmatsʾi leʿolam ʿod — "I will make you a terror and you will cease to be; though you are sought, you will never be found again." Ballahot (terrors/sudden destruction) is a term used in Job (18:11, 14) for the terrors that pursue the wicked. The complete cessation — never to be found — is the eschatological form of the desolation oracle.

27.1: Standard word-event formula introduces the qinah (lament) over Tyre. The chapter is entirely composed as a funeral song in the second person, addressed to Tyre directly.
27.2: veʾattah ben-ʾadam seʾa ʿal-Tsur qinah — "and you, son of man, take up a lament over Tyre" — the command to raise a qinah is the standard introduction to the dirge genre (also 19:1; 26:17; 28:12; 32:2). The qinah meter (3+2 beats) creates the limping, mournful rhythm associated with death throughout the Hebrew poetic tradition.
27.3: Tyre's self-description — ʾani kelilat yofi — "I am perfect in beauty" — echoes the pride-language of the Eden-cherub in 28:12. The pride of Tyre is her maritime position: yoshevet ʿal-mevoʾot yam (you who sit at the gateway to the sea) and her role as merchant to many peoples (rohelet haʿammim). The term rohelet (female merchant/trader, from rahal, to trade) personifies the city as a woman engaged in commerce.
27.4: The ship metaphor begins — bigvul yammim bonayikh kallelu yofeikh — "your builders perfected your beauty in the midst of the seas" — bigvul yammim (in the heart/boundary of the seas) is Tyre's geographic location on an island. The builders (bonayikh) who perfected her beauty are those who built the physical city; the beauty metaphor applied to a city's architecture is common in wisdom poetry.
27.5: The shipbuilding details begin with the timber — ʿatsei shenim mizLevanon laʾsu lakh leʾoren lehashpilat kanafeikh — "they made all your planks of fir trees from Lebanon; they took a cedar from Lebanon to make a mast for you." The sourcing of cedar and fir from Lebanon is historically accurate — the Phoenicians were the primary timber traders of the ancient world, using Lebanese cedar for their own shipbuilding and export.
27.6: The oars from oaks of Bashan — allonim miBashan ʿasu mishshoteikh qarsheikh ʿasu shen-bat-ʾashshurim meʾiyyei Kittim — "of oaks of Bashan they made your oars; your deck they made of ivory inlaid in boxwood from the coasts of Cyprus." Bat-ʾashshurim is unclear — possibly "daughter of Asshur-trees" (a species of cypress) or a corrupt text. Kittim consistently refers to Cyprus in the OT.
27.7: The sail of Egypt — shesh-beriqmah miMitsrayim hayah mifrasek lihyot lakh nes — "fine embroidered linen from Egypt was your sail, serving as your banner" — shesh (fine linen) is the high-quality Egyptian linen used for priestly garments and for luxury sails. The awning materials from Elishah (probably Alashiya = Cyprus) complete the luxury ship-fitting.
27.8: The professional crew — yoshevei Tsidon veʾArvad hayu shotatayikh ḥakhameikh Tsur hayu vakh yoshevei ḥavveleikh — sailors from Sidon and Arvad (Arwad, an island city north of Byblos), skilled craftsmen and pilots from Tyre's own population. The ḥakhamim (wise men/skilled craftsmen) of Tyre are the same class referenced in 28:3-5 — the expertise of the city is built into both the trade-ship lament and the king-of-Tyre oracle.
27.9: The elders of Gebal (= Byblos) as caulkers — ziqnei Geval vaḥakhameyhah hayu vakh maḥazeqei bidzqeikh kol-ʾaniyot hayam veshallehem hayu vakh laʿarov markolteikh — Gebal/Byblos was a Phoenician city whose elders were skilled shipwrights (ḥazaq = to strengthen/caulk). The phrase "all the ships of the sea and their mariners were in you to trade your merchandise" summarizes Tyre's function as the trading hub of the ancient world.
27.10: Persia, Lud (Lydia), and Put in Tyre's army — Paras veLud vaPhut hayu veḥeileikh ʾanshei milḥamateikh — "Persia and Lud and Put were in your army, your men of war" — the foreign military professionals who served as mercenaries. Phut (Libya/Somalia) and Lud (Lydia in western Anatolia) indicate the geographic breadth of Tyre's military recruitment. The hanging of shields and helmets on walls (hem tallu ʿaleikh tsinnah vekhovaʿ hem natenu hoddekha) is the decorative military display typical of Phoenician cities.
27.11: The Arvadites and Gammadites on the towers — uvenei Arvad veḥeileikh ʿal-ḥomotayikh saviv vegammadim bemigdolotayikh hayu ʾashperoteyhem tillu ʿal-ḥomotayikh saviv hem killu yofeikh — the Arvadites as garrison troops on the walls, Gammadim (origin uncertain — possibly a place-name or a term for elite warriors) on the towers. The decoration of the city with military hardware ("they completed/perfected your beauty") connects back to the kelilat yofi (perfect in beauty) claim of v3.
27.12: The trade catalog begins with Tarshish (Tarshish sokhartekh merov kol-hon bekhesef barzel bedia uvol natan ʿizaveikh) — Tarshish (probably Tartessus in Spain) trading silver, iron, tin, and lead for Tyre's wares. The verb ʿizzevikh (supplied you) from ʿazav (leave/leave for, here in the sense of leaving goods in payment) will recur as the structural verb of the trade catalog throughout the chapter.
27.13: Greece (Javan), Tubal, and Meshech — Yavan Tuval uMeshekh hemmah rohaleikh benefshet ʾadam ukhle neḥoshet natenu ʿizbeikh — trading in human slaves (nefesh ʾadam, lit. human soul/person — the OT term for slave trade) and bronze vessels. Javan (Ionia/Greece), Tubal and Meshech (regions in Anatolia/Caucasus) are grouped together as they are in the Table of Nations (Gen 10:2) and in the Gog oracle (Ezek 38-39).
27.14: Beth-togarmah (mivet Togarmah susim uperashim uperedim natenu ʿizbeikh) — trading horses, war-horses, and mules. Togarmah (probably Armenia/southeastern Anatolia) was known in antiquity for its horse-breeding; the connection is confirmed by Assyrian annals that record tribute in horses from that region.
27.15: The sons of Dedan — benei Dedan rohalaikh iyyim rabbim sekullat yadeikh shenhabbim vehobnin heshivu ʿeshbeikh — trading from "many coastlands" in ivory (shen-ʾabbim, lit. elephant-tooth) and ebony (hobnin, the word is borrowed from an Egyptian or Semitic root for hard dark wood). Dedan (northwest Arabia) served as a caravan hub connecting Arabian and African trade goods to Mediterranean markets.
27.16: Aram (Syria) — Aram sokhartekh merov maʿaseikh benovfeikh veʾargavan veriqmah uvuts veraʾmot venokhet natenu ʿizbeikh — trading turquoise (nofekh), purple (ʾargaman), embroidered work (riqmah), fine linen (buts), coral (raʾmot), and rubies/red coral (kadkod — the identification is uncertain). The luxuries are consistent with what Aram controlled through caravan routes.
27.17: Judah and Israel — Yehudah veʾerets Yisraʾel hemmah rohaleikh beḥitey Minnit vepannat udvaish vashemen utsori natenu maʿarveikh — trading wheat of Minnith (a place in Ammon, Judg 11:33), confections (pannat — uncertain, possibly pastry/cakes), honey, oil, and balm (tsori, the resin product associated with Gilead). The inclusion of Judah and Israel in the trade catalog is significant: even YHWH's covenant people are commercial partners of Tyre.
27.18: Damascus — Dammesek sokhartekh berov maʿaseikh merov kol-hon beyein Ḥelbon uvetsemer Tsaḥar — trading Helbon wine (a premium wine region near Damascus) and white wool (tsemer Tsaḥar or wool of Zahar). The wine of Helbon was noted in Assyrian records as a luxury item sent to the royal court.
27.19: Dan and Javan and Uzal — vedan veYavan meʾuzzal beʾizbeikh natanu barzel ʿashut qinnamon uqane hayah bemaʿarveikh — Dan and Javan (possibly Aden or a Yemenite location named Uzal/Azal; Gen 10:27) trading wrought iron, cassia, and cane. The aromatic spices (cassia, cane) were luxury goods from the East.
27.20: Dedan — Dedan rohaltekh bebigdei ḥofshy lerikbah — trading in saddle blankets/riding cloths (bigdei ḥofshi lerikbah). Dedan's second appearance in the catalog (also v15) reflects its dual role as both an ivory/ebony importer and a textile supplier.
27.21: Arabia and all the princes of Kedar — Araviʾah vekhol neisiʾei Qedar hemmah sokherei yadeikh bekevashim uʾeilim vaʿattudim bam sokhartekh — trading in lambs (kevasim), rams (ʾeilim), and goats (ʿattudim). The pastoral/livestock trade of the Arabian peninsula rounds out the catalog's commodity diversity.
27.22: The merchants of Sheba and Raamah — rohallei Shevaʾ veRaʿmah hemmah rohalaikh beroshe kol-boshem uvekhal-ʾeven yeqarah uvezahav natanu ʿizbeikh — trading spices (beshem) and precious stones and gold. Sheba (southwest Arabia/Ethiopia) was associated with gold, spices, and precious stones in the OT (1 Kgs 10:2; Ps 72:10; Isa 60:6).
27.23: Haran, Canneh, Eden, the merchants of Sheba, Asshur, and Chilmad — Ḥaran veKhanneh veʿEden rohallei Shevaʾ Ashshur veKhilmad rohaltekh — the northern Mesopotamian trade centers complete the circle. Haran (the city of Abraham's sojourn) and Asshur (Assyria) appear as commercial partners alongside the south Arabian Sheba, indicating the full compass range of Tyre's trade network.
27.24: These traders bring maklulim — "complete/choice things" — including luxurious garments (gelomei tekhelet verigmah), treasures of fine cloths (geneizei brumim), and bound and bound-and-twisted cords (vaḥavlim veʾarozim bemarkolloteikh). The vocabulary here is highly specialized and several terms are hapax legomena (words occurring only here). The textile and cord trades may reflect particular Phoenician specializations.
27.25: ʾaniyot Tarshish sharat suḥrateikh vatimmaʾli vatikked meʾod belev yammim — "the ships of Tarshish were carriers for your merchandise, and you were filled and made glorious in the heart of the seas." The trade catalog concludes with the aniyyot Tarshish (Tarshish-ships — a class of large ocean-going cargo vessels rather than necessarily ships bound for Tarshish specifically). Their "singing" (sharat) Tyre's trade is a personification of commercial activity.
27.26: The east-wind destruction — beʿaddeikh heshiʾahu shotatayikh ʾet-yam hamereḥet ruaḥ haqqadim shevaratikh belev yammim — "your rowers have brought you into deep waters; the east wind has wrecked you in the heart of the seas." The ruaḥ haqqadim (east wind) is the hot, destructive wind from the Arabah/desert that destroys ships and crops throughout the OT (Jonah 4:8; Hos 13:15; Ps 48:7). The east wind from YHWH is the agent of Tyre's destruction after the elaborate building of the ship in vv4-25.
27.27: The sinking inventory — honayikh veʾozaroteikh maʿaraveikh ḥalatayikh veʾanshei milḥamateikh umisraʿim rabbim bekol-qahal betokhekh yamafilu belev yammim beyom mappaltek — every element of Tyre's wealth and personnel enumerated in the trade catalog now sinks: riches, merchandise, exchange goods, mariners, pilots, repairers, traders, warriors, all the assembly. The inverted echo of the catalog is the literary conclusion of the ship-metaphor.
27.28: The sailors on the land shake at the sound of the cry of the pilots — liqol zeʿaqat ḥavvalaikh yirʿashu migrashot — the trembling coastal communities (migrashot, lit. open spaces/suburbs outside a city) echo ch 26's description of the coastlands trembling at Tyre's fall.
27.29: The abandonment of the ships — veyardu meʾaniyyoteyhem kol tofssei mishet ḥalatayim vekhol-ʾanshei hayam el-haʾarets yaʿamodu — everyone who works the sea (ships' crews) comes ashore. The movement from ship to land in response to Tyre's fall inverts Tyre's perpetual habitation of the sea.
27.30: The mourning postures — vehishmiʿu ʿaleikh beqolam vayitsʿaqu mara veyaallu ʿafar ʿal-rosheiyhem beʾefer yitpalashu — raising the voice in bitter crying (mara), putting dust on the head (ʿafar ʿal-rosheiyhem), rolling in ashes (beʾefer yitpalashu) — three mourning gestures. Hitpalesh (roll in ashes/dust) appears also in Jer 6:26 and Mic 1:10 for intense mourning.
27.31: Shaving the head and girding with sackcloth — vehiqriḥu ʾaleyikh qorḥah veḥagru saqim uvaḥu ʾeleyikh bemarrat nefesh mourning bitter of soul — the combination of shaved head (qorḥah), sackcloth (saq), and bitter weeping (marat nefesh) is the standard mourning-triad of the OT. The mourning is performed specifically ʿaleikh (over/for you, Tyre).
27.32: The qinah (dirge) composed by the mourners — unesʾu ʿaleikh benihem qinah veqanenu ʿalaykh mi keTsur kedomemah betokh hayam — "and in their wailing they raise a lamentation for you and lament over you: Who is like Tyre, like her who is stilled in the midst of the sea?" — the rhetorical mi-k (who is like?) formula is the OT incomparability formula applied here to commercial greatness. The same form appears in the Exodus songs (Exod 15:11, mi-khamokha) where YHWH's incomparability is declared. Here the incomparability is Tyre's commercial power — which makes its loss all the more devastating.
27.33: The goods flowing out from Tyre satisfying many peoples — betset ʿizbeikh miyyammim hisbaʿta ʿammim rabbim verov honayikh umaʿaraveikh hivʿarta malkei ʾerets — "when your wares came from the seas you satisfied many peoples; with your abundance of wealth and merchandise you enriched the kings of the earth." The verb hisbaʿta (you satisfied/filled) and hivʿarta (you enriched, lit. made rich) are the economic vocabulary of Tyre's commercial power at its height — now mourned as past.
27.34: The time of breaking — ʿet nishbaret miyyammim bemaʿaqqei mayim maʿaraveikh ukhol-qahalekh betokh nafalu — "now you are wrecked by the seas, in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and all your crew have sunk with you." The temporal shift from past prosperity (v33) to present destruction (ʿet, now/at the time) creates the rhetorical pathos of the lament.
27.35: The horror of the coastlands — kol yoshevei haʾiyyim nashemu ʿaleikh umalkeyhem saʿaru saʿar raʿu phanim — the same aghast-reaction vocabulary of 26:15-18 is repeated here to frame the lament from the mourner's perspective. Saʿaru saʿar (their hair bristles in terror — cognate with seʿar, hair) is an idiomatic expression for extreme fear.
27.36: The hissing conclusion — sokhrim baʿammim sherequ ʿalaykh ballahot hayit veʾeynekh leʿolam ʿad — "the merchants among the peoples hiss at you; you have come to a dreadful end and shall be no more forever." Sharequ (hissing) is both the sound of shock and the ritual gesture of contempt/dismissal for a destroyed city (Jer 19:8; 49:17; Lam 2:15). Ballahot hayit (you have become terrors) echoes the ballahot conclusion of 26:21 — the same word closing both the oracle and the lament.

28.1: Standard word-event formula opens the two-part oracle against the prince of Tyre (vv1-10) and the king of Tyre (vv11-19). The distinction between nagid (prince/leader) and melek (king) may reflect political reality — Ithobalos II of Tyre used the title nagid — or may be a deliberate prophetic choice to deny Tyre's ruler the full royal dignity.
28.2: ben-ʾadam ʾemor lenagid Tsur koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH yaʿan gabaḥ libbekha vattoʾmer ʾel ʾani moʾshav ʾelohim yashavti belev yammim — the first sin named: gabaḥ libbekha (your heart was lifted up/proud). The prince's claim is ʾel ʾani (I am a god) — not merely king-like or divinely appointed, but divine identity claimed as his own. The phrase moʾshav ʾelohim (seat/dwelling of God) locates the claim geographically: the prince sits in the divine dwelling-place (the sea-citadel of Tyre = the mythological sea-abode of the divine assembly). The contrast: veʾattah ʾadam velo-ʾel — "but you are a man and not God."
28.3: ḥakham ʾattah miDaniʾel kol-setum loʾ ʿamam ʿaddekha — "you are wiser than Daniel; no secret is hidden from you." The ironic comparison to Daniel — almost certainly the legendary Daniʾel of ANE wisdom tradition (not the biblical Daniel, whose book was written later) — is the same figure referenced in 14:14, 20. Tyre's king claims a wisdom (ḥakham) surpassing this proverbially wise man. The pride is compounded: he claims both divine identity and surpassing wisdom.
28.4: The wisdom used for commercial gain — bekhokhmateikha uvivunateikha ʿasita lekha ḥayil — "by your wisdom and your understanding you have made wealth for yourself" — the connection between ḥokhmah (wisdom) and commercial success is a theme of Proverbs (8:18-19; 3:16). What distinguishes Tyre's case is that the wisdom-wealth is self-generated and self-attributed.
28.5: The causal chain — wisdom → gold → pride: berov ḥokhmateikha birekhulatekha hirbeita ḥeileikh vayyigbaḥ levaveikha beḥeileikha — "by your great wisdom in your trade you have increased your wealth and your heart has become proud because of your wealth." The wayhi-consecutive (vayyigbaḥ levaveikha) marks the pride as a consequence of the prosperity.
28.6: The divine response to the divine-claim — lakhein koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH yaʿan sitteikha libbekha klevav ʾelohim — "therefore thus says Lord YHWH: because you have compared your heart to the heart of God" — the verb shat (set/compare) in sitteikha libbekha (you have set/compared your heart) uses the same root as ʾani YHWH sham (I YHWH have set it there) in other oracular contexts. The setting/comparing of the heart to the divine heart is the specific theological offense.
28.7: veheveʾeti ʿaleikha zarim ʿaritsim goyim — "therefore I am about to bring strangers against you, the most ruthless of nations." Aritsim (ruthless/terrible ones) from arits (to be ruthless, to cause fear) — the same word used for the Babylonians in Ps 54:3 and Isa 29:5. YHWH deploying the most ruthless nations against the self-styled divine ruler is the counter-force.
28.8: Descent to the pit — lashaḥat yoridukha vamata ḥallelei ḥalal belev yammim — "they will thrust you down into the pit and you will die the death of those who are slain in the heart of the seas." The irony: the divine dwelling in the heart of the sea (belev yammim in v2) becomes the death-location in v8. The claim to inhabit the divine seat is overturned by dying in the same sea.
28.9: The deathbed non-deity — haʾamor toʾmar ʾel ʾani lifnei horegekha veʾattah ʾadam velo-ʾel beyad meḥallelekha — "will you still say 'I am God' in the presence of the one who slays you? You are a man and not God in the hand of the one who wounds you." The repetition of ʾadam velo-ʾel (a man and not God) from v2 is the literary inclusio of the first oracle.
28.10: mot ʿarelim tamut beyad zarim — "you will die the death of the uncircumcised by the hand of strangers." Death by the hand of the uncircumcised (ʿarelim) is the most dishonorable death for a Semitic ruler. The term ʿarel (uncircumcised) carries the full weight of covenant-outside status — to die at the hands of those outside the covenant community is to die without honor, the antithesis of the divine status the prince claimed.
28.11: The second oracle — seʾa qinah ʾal-melek Tsur — shifts the address from nagid (prince) to melek (king) and changes the genre from accusation-oracle to qinah (lament). The shift in title has been interpreted as: (1) a second historical ruler; (2) a shift from the human ruler to his demonic counterpart; (3) a rhetorical elevation of the addressee to heighten the contrast of fall. The lament form applies the highest possible status claims to Tyre's ruler precisely to make the fall most devastating.
28.12: attah ḥotem toknit male ḥokhmah vekhilil yofi — "you were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty." Ḥotem toknit (seal/pattern of perfection) — toknit from takan (to measure/construct, be established); the "seal of the measuring pattern" describes the king as the perfect exemplar of what a being should be. The same cluster of terms (ḥokhmah, yofi) appears in ch 27's description of Tyre-the-ship.
28.13: The Eden setting — beʿEden gan-haʾElohim hayita — "you were in Eden, the garden of God." The twelve precious stones that covered him (even yequrah mekhaseka) correspond approximately to the twelve stones of the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28:17-20), though the lists differ and the text is partially corrupt in both Hebrew and LXX traditions. The king-in-Eden parallel to Adam is the template for the next verses.
28.14: ʾatt-kerub mimshakh hasokek venatatikha — "you were an anointed guardian cherub, and I placed you." Kerub mimshakh (anointed cherub) — the mimshakh (anointed/spread out) could modify either the cherub (as anointed) or the position (as covering/spanning); the LXX renders it differently. Hasokek (covering/screening) from sakhakh (to cover, used of the cherubim over the ark in Exod 25:20) — the covering function identifies this cherub with the role of the ark-cherubim. The holy mountain of God (har qodesh ʾElohim) is a mythological term for the divine assembly's location (also Isa 14:13; Ps 48:2).
28.15: tamim ʾattah biderakhekha miyom hibborekha ʿad-nimtsaʾ ʿavelah vakh — "you were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until unrighteousness was found in you." The term tamim (blameless, complete) is the character-descriptor of Noah (Gen 6:9) and Abraham (Gen 17:1), and Job (Job 1:1). Its use here establishes that the king's primordial state was genuine moral integrity, not just surface perfection — making the subsequent fall all the more tragic.
28.16: The sin that caused the fall — berob rekhullatekha mallu tokhekha ḥamas vatetaʾ — "in the abundance of your trade you were filled with violence and you sinned." The trade-pride (rekhullah) that filled him with hamas (violence/injustice) is the commercial corruption that corrupted the primordial wisdom. The verb vatteḥeʾ (and you sinned, qal imperfect consecutive) narrates the moral causation simply and directly.
28.17: The pride of beauty — gabaḥ libbekha beyofeikh shihhateta ḥokhmateikha ʿal-yifʾeikha — "your heart was proud because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor." The three corruptions: pride (gabaḥ) because of beauty (yofi), corruption (shihhat) of wisdom because of splendor (yifʾeikha). The sequence — wisdom → beauty → pride → corruption — is Tyre's tragedy in compressed form.
28.18: The profaning of the sanctuaries — merov ʿavoneikha beʿevel rekhullatekha ḥillalta miqdashekha — "by the multitude of your iniquities, in the unrighteousness of your trade, you profaned your sanctuaries." Ḥillel (piel: profane/defile) applied to one's own sanctuaries (miqdashekha) — the commercial corruption extended into the sacred sphere. YHWH's response: vaʾotsaʾ ʾesh mittokekha — "I brought forth fire from your midst" — the fire from within Tyre itself as the agent of self-destruction.
28.19: The universal horror conclusion — kol yodeʿeikh baʿammim shamemuʾ ʿaleikha ballahot hayita veʾeynnekha leʿolam ʾad — "all who know you among the peoples are appalled at you; you have become a terror and you shall be no more forever." The ballahot (terrors) conclusion is the third time this word closes an Ezekiel oracle in chs 26-28 (also 26:21; 27:36), creating a formal refrain of terror and non-existence across the anti-Tyre oracle complex.
28.20: Standard word-event formula opens the brief oracle against Sidon (vv20-26). Sidon was Tyre's neighbor and the older Phoenician city; the pair Tyre-and-Sidon appears frequently in prophetic literature (Isa 23; Jer 27:3; Joel 3:4).
28.21: The address to Sidon — sim panekha ʾel-Tsidon vehinave ʿaleha — uses the set-face formula (sim panekha ʾel) that Ezekiel uses for Jerusalem (4:3), the mountains of Israel (6:2), the south (20:46), and Ammon (25:2). The formula marks the prophetic oracle as a directed, intentional divine word.
28.22: YHWH's hostile-presence formula — hinneni ʿaleikh Tsidon venikkabdeti betokhekh — "I am against you, Sidon, and I will be glorified in your midst." The divine self-glorification (nikkabed, niphal of kavad, be honored/glorified) through judgment is a recurring theme: divine honor is established when divine words are vindicated by events. The recognition formula — veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH — follows immediately.
28.23: Plague and sword in Sidon's streets — veshillaḥti vah dever vedam beḥutsoteha venafall ḥalal betokhah baḥerev ʿaleha missaviv — the sword-from-outside and plague-from-within complement each other as siege-weapons. The encircling sword (ʿaleha missaviv) is standard siege-attack language.
28.24: The removal of the thorn from Israel's side — veloʾ yihyeh ʿod lebeit Yisraʾel sillon mamʾir vequts makʾiv mikkol sevivotam habbazim ʾotam veyadeʿu ki ʾani ʾAdanai YHWH — "and for the house of Israel there shall be no more a brier to prick or a thorn to cause pain from all their neighbors who have treated them with contempt." Sillon mamʾir (pricking brier) and quts makʾiv (painful thorn) are the images for the surrounding hostile nations that perpetually irritate/wound Israel. Their removal is the positive aspect of the OAN judgments.
28.25: The gathering and restoration oracle — koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH beqabbetstsi ʾet-beit Yisraʾel min-haʿammim ʾasher nafotsu vam veniqddashti vam leʾeinei haggoyim — "when I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they are scattered, I will manifest my holiness in them before the eyes of the nations." The gathering-from-the-nations promise echoes 11:17 and 20:34. Veniqddashti vam (I will be sanctified among them) — divine sanctification through the gathered community: the nations' acknowledgment of YHWH comes through his restoration of Israel.
28.26: The security of dwelling in the land — veyashvu ʿaleha lavetaḥ uvanu batim venataʿu keramim veyashvu lavetaḥ beʿasoti shefatim bekol-hazim otam missevivotam veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH ʾeloheyhem — "they will dwell securely, building houses and planting vineyards; they will dwell securely when I execute judgments against all their neighbors who have treated them with contempt, and they will know that I am YHWH their God." The covenant-security formula (yashav lavetaḥ, dwell in safety; benot batim, build houses; nataʿ keramim, plant vineyards) is the Deuteronomic covenant-blessing formula. The final veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH ʾeloheyhem (they will know that I am YHWH their God) is the only instance in the OAN section where the recognition formula includes ʾeloheyhem (their God) — marking the Sidon oracle as the passage back toward Israel's restoration.

33.1: New oracle-reception formula. Chapter 33 is structurally pivotal: it reopens the watchman commission of chapter 3 (vv. 1-9), revisits the individual accountability principles of chapter 18 (vv. 12-20), and is anchored by the news of Jerusalem's fall (v. 21). Chapters 1-32 are primarily judgment; from chapter 33 onward, restoration becomes the dominant register.
33.2: ben-adam daber el-bnei amecha ('son of man, speak to your people'): the shift from beit Yisrael (house of Israel) to amecha (your people) is subtle but significant — the prophet has a personal covenant relationship with the community to whom he speaks. The watchman (tzofeh) analogy returns from 3:17: a man from among a people, posted at the walls, responsible for alarm. The responsibility is communal but the appointment is individual.
33.3: ura'ah et-hacherev ba'ah al-ha'aretz ('and he sees the sword coming upon the land'): the watchman's function is perception followed by communication — taqe'a beshofar (blow the trumpet). The shofar as the alarm instrument carries the weight of covenant-calling throughout the Hebrew Bible: the same instrument used for Sinai (Ex 19:16-19), jubilee (Lev 25:9), and Joel's eschatological alarm (Joel 2:1). The watchman's trumpet is a prophetic voice.
33.4: damav be'rosho yihyeh ('his blood will be on his own head'): the legal principle of transferred responsibility — when the alarm is sounded and the person fails to act, the watchman's duty is discharged and the unresponsive person bears their own consequence. The 'blood on the head' idiom (cf. Josh 2:19; 2 Sam 1:16; Acts 18:6) is a standard Ancient Near Eastern legal formula for placing responsibility.
33.5: hu shama et-qol hashofar velo nizhar ('he heard the sound of the trumpet and was not warned'): the paradox of hearing-without-heeding — the ear received the signal but the will did not respond. Damav bo yihyeh ('his blood will be on himself'): the legal accountability is total. The watchman who warns has done everything required; the hearer who ignores bears all responsibility. This creates the framework for Ezekiel's ministry: he sounds the alarm; the response belongs to each individual.
33.6: vedamav midayav edrosh ('and his blood I will require from the watchman's hand'): the reverse case — the watchman who fails to warn is held responsible for the deaths that result. Midam is here plural (bloods) — the accumulated deaths resulting from the failure to warn. The legal language makes Ezekiel's prophetic ministry a matter of life and death not merely for his audience but for himself: silence is complicity.
33.7: uven-adam tzofeh natati lecha levet Yisrael ('and you, son of man, I have made you a watchman for the house of Israel'): the application of the general watchman-principle to Ezekiel personally. The repetition of the watchman commission from 3:17 in nearly identical terms at the book's structural midpoint reminds the reader (and Ezekiel) that the initial commission has not expired. With Jerusalem fallen, the prophetic watchman-role continues into the restoration era.
33.8: be'omri lerasha YHWH mot tamut ('when I say to the wicked man, "Wicked one, you shall surely die"') — the legal formula mot tamut (dying you shall die, a doubled infinitive absolute for certainty). The watchman's failure to speak this warning makes him complicit in the death that results. Damav midayav edrosh: YHWH holds Ezekiel responsible. The weight of the prophetic office is that silence kills.
33.9: ve'atah ki-hizaharta rasha midarkav leshuv mimenna velo-shav ('but if you warn the wicked man to turn from his way and he does not turn'): the watchman's discharge: if the warning is given and refused, the watchman is free. Bedamav yamut ('in his blood he will die') — his refusal to turn seals his fate; the blood is entirely his own. Ve'atah nafshecha hitzalta ('but you — your life you will have saved'): Ezekiel's personal liberation from legal responsibility when he faithfully warns.
33.10: ben-adam emor el-beit Yisrael ('son of man, say to the house of Israel'): the exiles' complaint — avoneinu va'chata'teinu aleinu ('our transgressions and our sins are upon us'). The weight metaphor: sin and iniquity as a burden pressing down. Umah niqeh ('how can we live?'): the question of despair — if the covenant curses have fully fallen and there is no reversal, survival is impossible. The question prepares for the stunning divine answer of v. 11.
33.11: chai-ani ne'um Adonai YHWH im-echpatz bemot harasha ('as I live, declares the Lord YHWH, I do not desire/take pleasure in the death of the wicked'): the most theologically important verse in the chapter — perhaps in the book. The divine oath by YHWH's own life (chai-ani) guarantees its absolute truth. Echpatz (to desire, delight in) — YHWH's chefetz (delight/pleasure) is not judgment but restoration. Ki im-beshuv rasha midarkav vachayah ('but rather in the wicked man turning from his way and living'): the teshuvah-formula — turning (shuv) as the path to life. Shuvu shuvu midarkechem hara'im ('turn! turn from your evil ways!'): the doubled imperative of urgency.
33.12: tzidqat hatzaddiq lo tatzilenu ('the righteousness of the righteous will not deliver him'): the individual accountability principle (cf. ch 18) — accumulated prior righteousness does not create a permanent credit balance. Veresha' harasha lo yikashel bo ('and the wickedness of the wicked will not cause him to stumble when he turns from it'): accumulated wickedness does not permanently disqualify. The moral condition at the moment of action determines destiny — not the accumulated record.
33.13: be'omri latzaddiq chayoh yichyeh ('when I say to the righteous man, "You shall surely live"') — chayoh yichyeh, the doubled infinitive absolute of certainty. The danger named: if the righteous person trusts in the promise and then commits evil — kol-tzidqotav lo tizakarnah ('none of his righteous acts will be remembered'). The divine accounting system is not cumulative but present-tense: the dominant direction of the will at any moment determines covenantal standing.
33.14: uvea'omri larasha mot tamut veshuv mechata'to ('when I say to the wicked man, "You shall surely die," but he turns from his sin'): the pivot case — the condemned man who turns. What the legal verdict declares (death) is reversed by the turn. Ve'asah mishpat utzedaqah ('and does justice and righteousness'): two terms for the same thing from different angles — mishpat (right legal action) and tzedaqah (righteous moral standing). The doublet covers both legal and relational dimensions of covenant faithfulness.
33.15: chavol yashuv rasha yashiv ('if the wicked man restores the pledge'): specific acts of restitution — gezelah yashlem (repay what was stolen), bechuqot hachayim halach (walk in the statutes of life). The abstract principle of 'turning' is grounded in specific acts of restitution. Without concrete behavioral change, 'repentance' is merely verbal. The statutes of life (chuqqot hachayim) — Torah as the revealed path to life, not merely rules to obey.
33.16: kol-chata'av asher chata lo yizakru lo ('all the sins he sinned will not be remembered against him'): the divine forgetting of sin upon genuine repentance. The theological weight here is extraordinary: YHWH actively does not remember — not merely forgives but ceases to count. This anticipates the new covenant formula of Jeremiah 31:34 (velo-ezkor od, 'and I will remember no more'). Mishpat utzedaqah asah chayoh yichyeh: the doublet of justice-righteousness again.
33.17: va'omru bnei amecha lo yitaken derekh Adonai ('and your people say, "The way of the Lord is not fair/straight"') — the accusation against YHWH's justice. Tiqen (to be right/level/fair): the same accusation from 18:25 and 18:29 — YHWH's ways are said to be unequal (lo-yitqanu). The accusation presupposes a human standard of justice by which YHWH's actions can be judged. Ezekiel's response is to reverse the accusation: it is Israel's ways that are unequal.
33.18: beshov tzaddiq mitzidqato ve'asah aval vameth bahem ('when a righteous man turns from his righteousness and does wrong, he will die by it'): the principle stated again in its application. The verb meth bahem (dies by them) — the sins become the mechanism of death. The righteous person who turns to evil does not merely face future punishment; the evil itself is lethal, carrying death within it.
33.19: uveshov rasha miresha'o va'asah mishpat utzedaqah ('and when the wicked man turns from his wickedness and does justice and righteousness'): the symmetrical case. Hu yachieh alehem ('he will live by/because of them'): the righteous acts become the mechanism of life. The verb yichyeh (he will live) answers the desperate question of v. 10: 'how can we live?' — by turning and doing justice and righteousness. The answer is simple, costly, and completely accessible to every individual.
33.20: va'omarten lo yitaken derekh Adonai ('you say, "The way of the Lord is not equal"') — final repetition of the accusation, now answered definitively. Ish kedrakav ishpot etchem ('I will judge each of you according to his ways'): the individual judgment principle as the seal. YHWH's equality is not abstract impartiality but personal accountability — each person judged by their own path. The defense of divine justice is the affirmation of individual moral responsibility.
33.21: bayom chamishi le'asarah lachodesh bishnat shtem-esreh ('in the fifth day of the tenth month, in the twelfth year'): the precise date marks the most dramatic moment in the book — the arrival of the survivor from Jerusalem. Ba-elai happalit ('the fugitive/survivor came to me'): palit (one who has escaped) — the technical term for a survivor of catastrophe. Hukkatah Yerushalayim ('Jerusalem has been struck'): the single sentence that is the axis of the entire book. The judgment oracles (1-24) have culminated; the restoration oracles (25-48) now begin their context.
33.22: veyad YHWH haytah elai ba'erev lifnei bo happalit ('and the hand of YHWH was upon me in the evening before the arrival of the survivor'): YHWH prepares Ezekiel supernaturally the evening before the news arrives — the divine communication precedes the human messenger. Uftach et-pi ('and he opened my mouth'): Ezekiel's mouth had been sealed since 3:26 — the dumbness that marked his prophetic restraint during the judgment period. Now it is opened. The new era of speech begins before the news arrives, signaling that YHWH orchestrates both the silence and the speaking.
33.23: New oracle following the arrival of the news. The people who remained in the land (not exiled) are addressed — they have appropriated the Abrahamic promise to justify possessing the land while the exiles are gone.
33.24: yoshvei hecharvot ha'el al-admat Yisrael ('those who live in those ruins on the land of Israel'): the remaining population in the land. Their theological argument: Avraham echad hayah vayyiresh et-ha'aretz ('Abraham was one and he possessed the land'): if the solitary Abraham inherited, how much more do we who are many. The use of Abraham as a precedent is a specific misapplication of the promise — the Abrahamic covenant promised the land to Abraham's descendants, not to those who remain after the faithful are exiled.
33.25: al-hadam ta'achelu ('you eat on/over blood'): the specific violations of the remaining population — eating with blood (prohibited in Gen 9:4; Lev 17:10-14), idolatry (ve'eineihem ta'su el-gilluleichem), murder (vedam tishpeku), all while claiming the land by inheritance. The combination of covenant violations disqualifies them from the very inheritance they claim. The Abrahamic promise is conditional on covenant faithfulness.
33.26: amadtem al-charbechem asitem to'evah ('you stand on your sword, you have done abomination'): standing on the sword — living by violence — as a defining characteristic. The acts of violence (murder, sexual violence implied by ish et-eshet re'ehu timme'u), idolatry, and bloodshed are the grounds for disqualification from the inheritance. Possession of land without Torah-faithfulness is illegitimate in the covenant framework.
33.27: koh tomar lahem ('thus you shall say to them'): the specific judgment on the remnant — those in the ruins will fall by sword, those in the open field to animals, those in strongholds will die by pestilence. The full triad of judgment (sword, pestilence, famine/wild animals) from chapters 5-6 is now applied to the remnant. No category escapes; the completeness of judgment matches the completeness of apostasy.
33.28: venatatiti et-ha'aretz shemamah umeshimamah ('I will make the land a desolation and a waste'): shemamah umeshimamah — the doubled desolation (cognate words for emphasis). The ga'on uzza Israel ('pride of her strength') will cease — the same phrase used in Lev 26:19 in the list of covenant curses. The land itself becomes uninhabited, the ultimate covenant curse (Lev 26:33).
33.29: veyad'u ki-ani YHWH betiteni et-ha'aretz shemamah ('and they will know that I am YHWH when I make the land a desolation'): the refrain-formula again, but now pointing toward future recognition. Even the desolation serves YHWH's revelatory purpose — not merely punishment but the establishment of covenant truth for those who survive to comprehend it.
33.30: ve'atah ben-adam bnei amecha hamitdabrim becha etzel hakirot ('and you, son of man, your people who talk about you by the walls'): the problem of performative piety — the exiles gathering communally to hear Ezekiel as a kind of entertainment. Ve'omer ish el-achiv ('and each one says to his brother'): word about Ezekiel spreads through the community. Bo-na ushemu ma-haddavar hayotze me'et YHWH ('come and hear what the word is that goes out from YHWH'): the intellectual curiosity about prophetic content without obedient response.
33.31: uvau elecha kimevo am ('they come to you as a people comes'): the formal gathering of a religious audience. Veyashvu lefanecha ami ('my people sit before you'): they identify as 'my people' — the covenant language is used. Veshamu et-devarecha ('and they hear your words'): hearing (shama') without the covenantal dimension of obedience. Ve'otam lo ya'asu ('but they do not do them'): the hearing-doing gap — what James 1:22-25 calls being 'hearers only.' Ki agavim befithem ('for they are like love-songs on their lips'): Ezekiel's words are aesthetically appreciated as shir agavim (love song) — beautiful language without moral force.
33.32: vehineka lahem keshir agavim ('and you are to them like a love song'): shir agavim — erotic/romantic song, beautiful but not binding. Yefeh qol umentiv naggen ('beautiful of voice and skilled in playing'): Ezekiel is experienced as an aesthetic performance — appreciated for craft and beauty, not transformed by content. The passage is a devastating analysis of religious consumerism: the audience receives the word as entertainment while rejecting it as command. Ushe'amu et-devarecha ('they hear your words') but ve'otam einam osim ('and they do not do them').
33.33: uvevoa'ah uvevoa'ah uhinenah ba'ah ('and when it comes — and it will come — then they will know'): the eschatological guarantee — when Ezekiel's prophesied events materialize, the entertainment-hearers will finally know that a prophet was among them. Ki navi hayah betocham ('that a prophet had been in their midst'): the past tense in the prediction — when it comes true, they will say 'there was a prophet among us.' The recognition arrives after, not before, the fulfillment. Truth often convinces only retrospectively.

34.1: New oracle. Chapter 34 is the great shepherd passage of Ezekiel — the indictment of Israel's failed shepherds (kings, priests, and leaders) followed by YHWH's declaration that he himself will shepherd Israel, culminating in the promise of David my servant as the eschatological shepherd-prince.
34.2: hinneh anochi el-haro'im ('I am against the shepherds'): the prophetic confrontation formula hinneni elekha (I am against you) — used for Gog (38:3), Tyre (26:3), Egypt (29:3), and here for Israel's shepherds. The same formula for Israel's leaders as for pagan nations: they have failed so completely as to be indistinguishable, in YHWH's covenantal reckoning, from the enemies of Israel. Ra'u et-ha'eder ('they shepherd the flock'): the charge — shepherds who only fed themselves.
34.3: et-hacheleiv tocheilu ve'et-hatzemer tilbashu ('the fat you eat and the wool you wear'): the specific economic exploitation — taking the produce of the flock (fat/milk, wool) without caring for the animals. Ha'berirah tizbachu ('the sleek ones you slaughter'): the best of the flock consumed for the shepherds' own tables. Et-hatzon lo tiru'u ('the flock you do not shepherd'): the inversion of the shepherd's role — consuming rather than serving.
34.4: et-hachallot lo chizaqtem ve'et-hachola lo-ripe'item ('the weak you have not strengthened, the sick you have not healed'): the four-fold failure — not strengthening the weak, not healing the sick, not binding the broken, not returning the strayed. Et-hanidachat lo hashevotm ('the driven-away you have not brought back'): nidachat (the driven/banished) — those scattered by force, including the exiles themselves. Et-ha'avudat lo dirashem ('the lost you have not sought'): the seeking function of the shepherd — to actively pursue the lost, not merely care for those who remain.
34.5: vatifutzena mibeli ro'eh ('and they scattered because there was no shepherd'): the consequence of pastoral failure is directly the scattering of the flock. Vatihyena le'oclah lechol-chayat hassadeh ('they became food for every wild animal'): when the shepherd withdraws protection, predators move in. The sheep's vulnerability is not their fault but the shepherd's failure. The political meaning is transparent: Israel's exile is the direct result of corrupt leadership.
34.6: yittu tzoni bechol-heharim ('my flock wanders on all the mountains'): YHWH calls Israel tzoni (my flock) — possessive, covenantal. The wandering on mountains and hills echoes the bamot apostasy — the high places where false worship happened are also the hills where the scattered flock grazes without direction. Ve'ein dorresh ve'ein mevaqesh ('and there is no one who searches and no one who seeks'): the failed shepherds are indicted by their absence from the two defining activities of pastoral care — searching and seeking.
34.7: Direct address to the shepherds. The second person masculine plural shifts to the indicted leaders specifically. The formula lachen ro'im shim'u devar YHWH ('therefore, O shepherds, hear the word of YHWH') frames what follows as legal testimony — they are called to hear the divine verdict on their conduct.
34.8: chai-ani ('as I live'): the divine oath sealing the following statement with ultimate authority. Im-lo ya'an heyot tzoni levaz ('if it is not because my flock has become plunder'): the swearing formula of divine resolve. Vatihyena tzoni le'ochla lechol-chayat hassadeh mei'ayin ro'eh ('my flock became food for all the wild animals, without a shepherd'): the accusation against the shepherds is grounded in the documented evidence — YHWH has observed his flock's condition.
34.9: The repetition of the summons to 'hear the word of YHWH' signals the formal legal proceeding. The shephalim (shepherds/leaders) are being tried in the divine court. The pattern of accusation — you have not sought, you have not strengthened, you have not returned — is the evidence presented at the trial.
34.10: hinneni el-haro'im vedrashhti et-tzoni miyadam ('I am against the shepherds and I will require my flock from their hand'): darash (to seek, require, hold accountable) — the same verb used for blood-accountability (9:1; 33:6). YHWH 'requires' the flock from the shepherds' hands: they were entrusted with the flock; now the owner demands an accounting. Vehishbatti et-haro'im mir'ot tzon ('I will cause the shepherds to cease from shepherding the flock'): the Davidic/royal line of leadership is terminated — the shepherds' commission is revoked.
34.11: ki koh amar Adonai YHWH hinneni ani-udarashti et-tzoni uviqashtim ('for thus says the Lord YHWH: I myself will search for my flock and seek them out'): the dramatic pivot — the shift from 'I am against the shepherds' to 'I myself will shepherd.' The emphatic ani-udarashti ('I myself will seek') makes YHWH the direct replacement of the failed human shepherds. No intermediate human shepherd is mentioned here — YHWH takes the task personally. This is one of the most direct statements of divine pastoral action in the entire Hebrew Bible.
34.12: keiro'eh edro beyom heyoto veton-tzone nifzarot ('as a shepherd inspects his flock on a day when he is among his scattered sheep'): nifzarot (scattered/dispersed) — the diaspora condition. YHWH will seek out the scattered ones (ken avaqesh et-tzoni) from all the places they have been scattered — with the specific mention of yom anan va'arafel ('the cloudy and dark day'): the day of the exile, defined by divine hiddenness, is the specific context of YHWH's seeking activity.
34.13: vehiotzetim min-ha'amim ('I will bring them out from the peoples'): the gathering-from-exile as a second Exodus. Uqibbatzttim min-ha'aratsot ('and gather them from the lands'): a universal ingathering — not just from Babylon but from all nations. The list of terrains — mountains, valleys, watercourses, inhabited places — mirrors the comprehensive scattering described earlier: the seeking is as comprehensive as the dispersal was total.
34.14: bimra'eh tov ir'em ('in a good pasture I will shepherd them'): mera'eh tov (good pasture/feeding place) — the pastoral ideal of rest and abundance. Vehar marom Yisrael yihyeh ne'vehem ('and on the high mountains of Israel their fold will be'): ne'veh (dwelling place, fold) — the settled, secure community. The same mountains where the scattered flock wandered without a shepherd (v.6) become the home pasture. Sham tirbatznah ('there they will lie down'): rest in abundance as the sign of restored wellbeing.
34.15: ani er'eh tzoni ve'ani arbitzem ne'um Adonai YHWH ('I myself will shepherd my flock and I myself will make them lie down, declares the Lord YHWH'): the doubly emphatic ani... ani (I... I) — YHWH as the personal shepherd who does both the feeding and the settling. The resting of the flock (arbitzem) is the image of complete security — the posture of animals that have no fear of predators. Compare Psalm 23:2 and John 10:11-16 where the same vocabulary structures the good shepherd imagery.
