34.16: et-ha'ovedet avaqesh ve'et-hanidachat ashiv ('the lost I will seek and the driven away I will bring back'): the fourfold restoration program — seeking the lost, returning the strayed, binding the broken (lashevurah echavesh), strengthening the sick (vachalah achazzeq). These are the precise pastoral activities the failed shepherds of v.4 did not perform. YHWH does what the human shepherds refused to do. Ve'et-hashmenah ve'et-hachazaqah ashmed ('but the fat and the strong I will destroy'): the judgment element within the restoration — the powerful exploiters within the flock are not preserved in the restoration.
34.17: uve'atten tzoni koh amar Adonai YHWH hinneni shofet ('and as for you, my flock, thus says the Lord YHWH: I am judging'): the judicial address shifts to the flock itself. The judgment is now between members of the community — not just between the shepherds and YHWH. Bein seh laseh laelim velatayishim ('between sheep and sheep, between rams and he-goats'): distinctions within the flock — the powerful members who exploited the weak. The shepherd who protects also judges within the flock.
34.18: hame'at miqem mira'eh tov tiruu ('is it too little for you that you graze on the good pasture'): the indictment against the strong within the flock — they consume the best pasture, then trample what remains; they drink the clear water, then muddy the rest with their feet. The image is of resource hoarding and deliberate degradation of communal goods. What the powerful don't use themselves, they destroy for others.
34.19: vetzon mira'ai mirmesteihem tir'ena ('and my flock eats what your feet have trampled'): the weak flock suffers from the strong's waste. The pastoral imagery captures the dynamics of social inequality: the poor eat what the rich leave after trampling, drink the muddy water after the powerful have drunk their fill. This is the flock-level equivalent of the shepherd-level exploitation described in vv. 3-4.
34.20: lachen koh amar Adonai YHWH eleichem ('therefore thus says the Lord YHWH to you'): the judicial verdict begins. Hinneni ani shafatti bein seh biriyah uvein seh razah ('I myself will judge between the fat sheep and the lean sheep'): YHWH takes the role of divine judge within the restored community — not merely restoring the flock from outside enemies but adjudicating internal inequities.
34.21: ya'an betzad uvechatel tidehu ('because with flank and shoulder you have pushed'): the physical aggression of the strong against the weak — pushing with flank and shoulder, butting with horns. Ad asher pitzartem et-kol-hachallot ('until you scattered all the sick ones'): the weak members of the community have been driven out by the aggressive behavior of the strong. The pastoral metaphor captures real economic and social dynamics within the exilic community.
34.22: vehoshati le'tzoni velo-tihyena od lavaz ('I will save my flock so they will no longer be plunder'): the divine rescue declared — the condition of being prey ends. Veshafatti bein seh laseh ('and I will judge between sheep and sheep'): the intra-community justice that guarantees the weak are protected from the strong within the restored flock. The good shepherd both protects from external predators and maintains internal justice.
34.23: vahaqimoti aleihem ro'eh echad ('and I will raise up over them a single shepherd'): ro'eh echad — one shepherd, singular. Ve'ro'ah ethen ('and he will shepherd them'): the subject unexplained — then named: avdi David ('my servant David'). The Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:12-13) here made eschatological — not David returned but a David-figure yet to come. Hu yir'em vehu yihyeh lahem lero'eh ('he will shepherd them and he will be their shepherd'): the doubled role — doing the shepherding and being the shepherd — emphasizes identity, not merely function.
34.24: va'ani YHWH ehyeh lahem le'Elohim ve'avdi David nasi betocham ('and I, YHWH, will be their God and my servant David will be a prince among them'): the covenant formula completed — YHWH le'Elohim (YHWH as God) and am (people, implied from context). The Davidic figure is named nasi (prince/leader) rather than melek (king) — a distinction Ezekiel maintains throughout (chs 44-46), perhaps to distinguish the eschatological Davidic ruler from the discredited kings of Israel's history. Ani YHWH dibarti ('I, YHWH, have spoken'): the inviolable divine word sealing the promise.
34.25: vekarati lahem brit shalom ('and I will make with them a covenant of peace'): brit shalom — the covenant of peace, appearing here and in 37:26 in Ezekiel (also Num 25:12; Isa 54:10; Mal 2:5). Shalom in the covenant context is not merely 'peace' but the complete well-being of the covenant relationship restored. Vehishbatti chayah ra'ah min-ha'aretz ('I will eliminate dangerous animals from the land'): the restoration of safety in the land — the predators that plagued the flock when the shepherd was absent are removed.
34.26: unatatti otam usevivot giv'ati berakhah ('and I will make them and the places around my hill a blessing'): berakhah (blessing) as the defining characteristic of the restored land. Vehora'deti haggeshem be'itto ('and I will send down rain in its season'): the seasonal rain as covenant blessing (Lev 26:4; Deut 11:14) restored. Gishme verakhah yihyu ('rains of blessing there will be'): the Hebrew plays on the sound — gashm (rain) connected to berakhah (blessing) — the material blessing is itself a form of divine speech.
34.27: veyitan etz hassadeh et-piryou ('and the tree of the field will give its fruit'): the restoration of agricultural fertility — the reversal of the covenant curses of Leviticus 26:20 ('your trees will give no fruit') and Deuteronomy 28:40-42. Veyad'u ki-ani YHWH beshivri et-motot ullam ('and they will know that I am YHWH when I break the bars of their yoke'): the broken yoke as liberation from Babylonian domination — the servitude symbolized by the yoke Ezekiel wore as a sign act (ch 4).
34.28: velo-yihyu od bevaz lagoyim ('and they will no longer be plunder for the nations'): the reversal of the condition described in v.5 and v.22. Uvat-ha'aretz lo tochlem od ('and the wild animals of the land will not devour them any more'): both human predators (nations) and environmental predators (wild animals) are eliminated from the picture of restoration. Veyashvu lavetach ve'ein macharid ('and they will dwell securely with none to make them afraid'): the lavetach (security) formula from Lev 26:5 — the covenant blessing of unafraid habitation restored.
34.29: vehaqimoti lahem matta leshm ('and I will raise up for them a planting for a name/renown'): matta (planting, cutting, shoot) — the agricultural image of divine planting. Leshm can mean 'for a name' or 'for renown' — the planted community that will be the glory of the restored land. Velo-yihyu od asufei ra'av ba'aretz ('and they will no longer be gathered by famine in the land'): the famine that drove scattered Israel is ended. The planted people (matta) deliberately echoes the agricultural restoration throughout — YHWH as the divine gardener.
34.30: veyad'u ki-ani YHWH Eloheihem ittam ('and they will know that I, YHWH their God, am with them'): the recognition formula takes its fullest covenantal form — not merely yeda'u ki-ani YHWH (they will know I am YHWH) but Eloheihem ittam (their God, with them). The divine presence is the content of the covenant formula. Vehem ami beit Yisrael ('and they are my people, the house of Israel'): the covenant formula completed — ani Eloheihem vehem ami (I am their God and they are my people). The full bilateral covenant restored.
34.31: ve'atten tzoni tzon mar'iti adam atem ('and you are my flock, the flock of my pasture — you are human beings'): the chapter closes with the affirmation that the restored Israel is both tzoni (my flock — covenant possession) and adam (human beings — dignity). The pastoral metaphor has a limit: the sheep are people. The divine-shepherd relationship does not reduce humanity to mere livestock but uses the pastoral image to convey intimate care and protection. Va'ani Eloheichem ne'um Adonai YHWH ('and I am your God, declares the Lord YHWH'): the covenant seal on the chapter.

35.1: Oracle-reception formula opening the Edom oracle. Chapter 35 is structurally paired with chapter 36 (the oracle about the mountains of Israel): chapter 35 addresses Mount Seir/Edom and its fate; chapter 36 addresses Israel's mountains and their restoration. The pair forms a chiasm with 6:1-7 (oracle against Israel's mountains for apostasy) and 35 (oracle against Edom for opposing restoration).
35.2: sim panecha el-har Se'ir ('set your face against Mount Seir'): the prophetic direction-formula. Har Se'ir (Mount Seir) is the Edomite heartland — the mountainous territory south and east of the Dead Sea inhabited by Esau's descendants. The directional formula matches the same formula used for the oracles against Israel's mountains (6:2) and foreign nations. Edom is treated as a full theological subject, not merely a historical enemy.
35.3: hinneni elecha har Se'ir ('I am against you, Mount Seir'): the confrontation formula. Unatiti yadecha shedu umshamah ('I will make you a desolation and a waste'): the same terms used for Jerusalem's judgment (5:14-15; 6:14). Edom receives the same category of judgment as Israel — the equality of divine justice before covenant and non-covenant nation is maintained. The terms of desolation applied to Edom will be reversed for Israel in chapter 36.
35.4: arecha choreiv eten ('your cities I will make ruins'): the cities-to-ruins formula applied to Edom. Ve'atah tihyeh shemamah ('and you yourself will be a desolation'): the judgment is comprehensive — land and people. Veyada'ta ki-ani YHWH: the recognition formula applied to Edom — even a foreign nation will come to know YHWH's identity through the enacted judgment on them. The refrain-formula is not reserved for Israel alone.
35.5: ya'an heyot lecha eivat olam ('because you have had an ancient/eternal enmity'): eivat olam (ancient/perpetual enmity/hatred) — Edom's hostility toward Israel is not merely political but goes back to the Esau-Jacob conflict (Gen 27-28; Num 20:14-21; Obad 10-14). Vatagger et-bnei Yisrael al-yedei charev ('you handed over the Israelites to the sword'): the crime of treacherous complicity with Babylon during Jerusalem's fall — Edom aided the enemy rather than helping their kin. Obadiah 10-14 details the same accusation.
35.6: lachen chai-ani ('therefore, as I live'): the divine oath sealing the judgment. Vedam ered'ka ('I will make blood pursue you'): dam (blood) as the substance of the divine judgment — blood-guilt pursued by its own reckoning. Im-lo dam sane'ta udam yirdefecha ('if you did not hate blood, blood will pursue you'): a play on Edom's double relationship with blood — if they hate blood (guilt), blood (judgment) still pursues. The inescapability of bloodguilt is the main point.
35.7: venatati et-har Se'ir leshimamah ushemamah ('I will make Mount Seir a desolation and waste'): the doubled desolation again (cf. v.3 and 33:28). Vehikhrati mimmenu over vashav ('and I will cut off from it both the one going and the one coming back'): the elimination of traffic — human movement ceasing entirely. A land where no one walks is a land utterly emptied of life. The thoroughgoing nature of Edom's judgment mirrors the totality of Edom's enmity.
35.8: umaleti et-harav chelalaiv ('I will fill his mountains with his slain'): the mountains of Seir, which were Edom's geographic identity, become the mass grave of the Edomites. Begeva'otecha uvaga'yotecha uvechol-afiqecha ('on your hills, in your valleys, and in all your ravines'): the complete geography of Mount Seir saturated with the fallen. The same specificity of 'hills and mountains and valleys' used for Israel's scattering (6:3) is applied to Edom's devastation.
35.9: shemamot olam ettencha ('eternal desolations I will make you'): the eschatological dimension of Edom's judgment — not merely temporary destruction but permanent emptiness. Ve'arecha lo tushavnah ('and your cities will not be reinhabited'): the curse of permanent uninhabitedness. The contrast with Israel's restoration (the old ruins will be rebuilt — 36:10, 33) is deliberate: what Edom receives permanently, Israel receives temporarily and has reversed.
35.10: ya'an amrecha shetei hagoyim ushetei ha'aratsot li tihyena ('because you said: "The two nations and the two lands will be mine"') — the specific claim that Edom made during Jerusalem's fall: with both Israel and Judah defeated, the entire land could be claimed by Edom. Uvey YHWH sham hayah ('but YHWH was there'): the stunning counter — despite the desolation, YHWH's presence in the land persists. The land is not vacant of divine ownership even when it appears abandoned.
35.11: lachen chai-ani ('therefore, as I live'): second divine oath. Ve'asiti ke'apcha ukeqin'atecha asher asita missin'atecha lahem ('I will act according to your anger and according to your jealousy which you showed in your hatred of them'): the lex talionis principle at the national level — Edom's anger, jealousy, and hatred toward Israel are returned to Edom by YHWH in kind. Venoda'ti bahem ka'asher eshpotem ('and I will make myself known among them when I judge you'): the recognition-formula applied to Israel as witness of Edom's judgment.
35.12: veyada'ta ki-ani YHWH shama'ti et-kol-niv'otecha ('and you will know that I, YHWH, have heard all your insults'): YHWH as the one who overhears everything spoken against Israel — asher dibbarta al-harei Yisrael ('which you spoke against the mountains of Israel'). The mountains of Israel are again personified — the same mountains that in chapter 6 receive the divine oracle of judgment now receive divine defense against Edom's insults. Lemor shamemu lanu nitenu le'oclah ('saying, "They are desolate, they are given to us to devour"').
35.13: vatag'du alai befichem ('you spoke against me with your mouths and multiplied your words against me'): the Edomite boasting over Israel's fall is directly identified as speech against YHWH himself. The equation is significant: to insult YHWH's people in their humiliation is to insult YHWH. The covenant solidarity between YHWH and Israel means that their enemies' contempt of Israel's defeated state is contempt of YHWH's faithfulness.
35.14: koh amar Adonai YHWH ('thus says the Lord YHWH'): the oracle formula resets the pronouncement. Besamoach kol-ha'aretz shemamah e'eseh lach ('when all the earth rejoices I will make you desolate'): the timing of Edom's desolation — when the earth-wide restoration comes (for Israel), Edom is excluded. The joy of universal restoration is the backdrop against which Edom's permanent desolation is seen.
35.15: ka'asher samachta al-nachalat beit Yisrael ('as you rejoiced over the inheritance of the house of Israel because it was desolate, so I will deal with you'): the final indictment — rejoicing over Israel's humiliation. The principle of lex talionis closes the chapter: what Edom did to Israel in Edom's hour of perceived superiority, YHWH does to Edom in YHWH's hour of restoration. Shemat tihyeh har Se'ir vekhol-Edom kullah ('desolate you will be, O Mount Seir, and all Edom, all of it'): the comprehensive desolation as the final word. Veyad'u ki-ani YHWH: the refrain, now sealing Edom's recognition of the God they mocked in Israel's defeat.

29.1: Precise date: tenth year, tenth month, twelfth day — January 587 BCE, during the final siege of Jerusalem. The oracle against Egypt is contemporary with the most intense phase of the Babylonian siege, when Israel's hope in Egyptian intervention was at its highest. Ezekiel speaks against the very power his people are counting on.
29.2: sim panecha al-paro melek mitzraim ('set your face against Pharaoh king of Egypt'): the sim panim el formula is Ezekiel's distinctive confrontational stance — the prophet's embodied posture of opposition (cf. 6:2; 13:17; 25:2; 28:21). The whole land of Egypt is judged through its king; Pharaoh embodies the national claim to divine status.
29.3: hatannim haggadol harobetz betoch yeorah ('the great sea dragon/crocodile lying in the midst of his rivers'): tannin (H8577, sea monster/dragon/large reptile) — the chaos-monster vocabulary of Gen 1:21 (God created the great sea creatures); Ps 74:13-14 (you crushed the heads of the sea monster; you crushed the heads of Leviathan); Isa 27:1. Pharaoh as the embodiment of chaos lying in the Nile. li yeori vaani asitini ('my Nile is mine — I made it'): the creature claiming creator-rights, the ultimate act of creature-hubris. The Nile was treated as divine in Egyptian religion (Hapy, the Nile flood god). YHWH attributes this self-deification directly to Pharaoh as the arrogance that warrants judgment.
29.4: venatatti chchachim bilichyeycha ('I will put hooks in your jaws'): the great fish hooked and hauled out — a reversal of the dragon's dominion over the waters. The same imagery of divine sovereignty over sea-monsters appears in Job 40:24-25 (can you put a hook in Leviathan's jaw?); YHWH's ability to hook the chaos-dragon is the claim to ultimate sovereignty. qasqaset (H7193, scales/armor-plating of fish/reptile) — the dragon's natural armor is no protection against YHWH.
29.5: unetattikha el-hamidbar otcha vekhol-degat yeorecha ('I will throw you into the wilderness, you and all the fish of your Nile'): the Egypt-dragon thrown into the desert — the reversal of Egypt's entire civilization which depended on the Nile. al-penei hassadeh tipol ('you will fall in the open fields, not gathered or collected'): proper burial was enormously important in Egyptian culture (the entire elaborate mortuary industry). To lie unburied in the open field, exposed to scavengers, was the ultimate disgrace. lechayyat haaretz ulelof hashamayim ('food to the beasts of the field and birds of the air'): the exact reversal of Egyptian animal veneration — the sacred ibis and crocodile become consumers of Egypt's corpse.
29.6: veyadefu kol-yoshevei mitzraim ki ani YHWH ('all the inhabitants of Egypt will know that I am the LORD'): the recognition formula applied to a foreign nation — YHWH's self-disclosure extends beyond Israel. yaan heyotam lemishenet qaneh ('because they have been a staff of reed'): mishenet (H4938, staff/support) + qaneh (H7070, reed/cane) — the hollow reed that looks like a staff but splinters when leaned on. The reed staff is the central image of the oracle: Egypt's military alliance is structurally unreliable.
29.7: beachozam becha vekhappecha ('when they grasped you with their hand'): the literal image of clutching a reed staff — it shatters and pierces the hand that grips it. ushavarta lahem kol-katef ('you shattered/broke for them every shoulder'): the staff that breaks and twists the shoulder of the one leaning on it. The political theology: alliances with Egypt consistently injured Israel rather than supporting it (Isa 30:3-7; 31:1-3). The reed staff oracle interprets a consistent historical pattern.
29.8: The sword oracle against Egypt — heveiti alayich cherev ('I will bring a sword against you'): the Babylonian sword as YHWH's instrument. vechikhrati mimech adam uvehemah ('I will cut off both human and animal'): comprehensive desolation — not just military defeat but demographic and agricultural collapse.
29.9: vehaytah eretz mitzraim lishemamah uvechorvah ('the land of Egypt will become a desolation and a waste'): shemamah (H8077, desolation/devastation) + chorbah (H2723, ruin/waste) — the covenant-curse pair appearing throughout the OAN sections and in the Jerusalem oracles. The cause: yaan amar yeori li vaani asitiv — 'because he said the Nile is mine and I made it.' The theological arrogance of v.3 is the explicit grounds of the judgment.
29.10: mimmigdol seveneh vead gevul kush ('from Migdol to Syene, as far as the border of Cush'): the geographical formula for all of Egypt — from the northeastern delta (Migdol, on the Sinai border) to the southern extreme (Syene = Aswan, at the first cataract). The judgment covers the entire land without exception.
29.11: arbaim shanah ('forty years'): the wilderness-generation period applied to Egypt — forty years of desolation mirrors Israel's forty years in the wilderness as a generation of judgment. This is a round number for a complete period of divine discipline. Historical records suggest a Babylonian campaign against Egypt under Nebuchadnezzar (568-567 BCE) but the land was not uninhabited for forty literal years; the number carries symbolic weight.
29.12: vehapitzoti et-mitzraim bagoyim ('I will scatter the Egyptians among the nations'): the same diaspora-language used of Israel's punishment applied to Egypt. The judgment of the nations mirrors the judgment of Israel, demonstrating YHWH's universal sovereignty. No nation is exempt from the same covenant-logic that governs Israel's history.
29.13: veqibatzti et-mitzraim min-haamim asher-naphutzu shamah ('I will gather the Egyptians from the peoples'): the remarkable restoration promise — even Egypt receives a measure of the return-from-exile that Israel will receive. YHWH's purposes for Egypt include eventual restoration, though not to their former greatness.
29.14: veshavti et-shevut mitzraim ('I will restore the fortunes of Egypt'): the shuv shevut (H7725 + H7622, 'restore fortunes/restore captivity') formula — the same idiom used for Israel's restoration (Jer 30:3,18; 31:23; Ezek 39:25). Egypt will return to Pathros (Pathros = Upper Egypt, the traditional homeland of Egyptian civilization). mamlakhah shephalah tihyeh ('there they will be a lowly/humble kingdom'): shephel (H8217, lowly/humble) — Egypt will survive but will never again be a great power claiming divine status for its river and kings.
29.15: min-hamamelechet hishphal tihyeh ('it will be the humblest of kingdoms'): the permanent diminishment of Egypt as the eschatological outcome. The proud civilization that claimed self-creation will be preserved in a humble form, no longer posing a spiritual or political threat to Israel through unreliable alliances. This is the 'lowering the proud, lifting the humble' principle (Ezek 21:26-27; Luke 1:52-53).
29.16: velo yihyeh od liveit yisrael lemivtach ('Egypt will no longer be a source of confidence/trust for the house of Israel'): mivtach (H4009, security/confidence/object of trust) — derived from batach (H982, to trust/rely on). The root of Israel's Egyptian alliance-problem was misplaced bitachon — trusting Egypt instead of YHWH (Isa 30:1-5; 31:1; Jer 17:5-8). The oracle resolves the problem at its source: Egypt's reduction eliminates the temptation.
29.17: Latest-dated oracle in Ezekiel: twenty-seventh year, first month, first day — April 571 BCE, some sixteen years after the fall of Jerusalem (586 BCE). The Tyre oracle (ch 26-28) promised Nebuchadnezzar the plunder of Tyre; the historical Tyre campaign (585-572 BCE) ended without the promised plunder, so YHWH redirects the payment to Egypt.
29.18: nebuchadretstsar melech bavel he'evid et-cheilo avodah gedolah el-tzor ('Nebuchadnezzar made his army labor strenuously against Tyre'): avodah gedolah ('great labor/service') — the thirteen-year siege of Tyre was one of the most exhausting campaigns in ancient Near Eastern history. kol-rosh muqrach vekhol-kateph murad ('every head was bald and every shoulder was rubbed raw'): vivid imagery of the physical toll — helmets wearing away hair, equipment chafing shoulders. Despite the effort, Tyre's island position meant much of the city's wealth had been evacuated by sea.
29.19: noten et-eretz mitzraim linebuchadretstsar melech bavel ('I am giving the land of Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar'): YHWH as the sovereign who assigns nations as payment for services rendered to his purposes. The theological point: even pagan military campaigns against other pagan powers serve YHWH's overarching plan. Nebuchadnezzar is 'my servant' (Jer 27:6) — given Egypt as wages.
29.20: ucheret lo et-eretz mitzraim bescharoh asher-avad bi ('I have given him the land of Egypt as his wage for what he did, because he worked for me'): the astonishing claim — a pagan king 'working for YHWH' without knowing it (cf. Isa 44:28-45:1 on Cyrus; Acts 4:27-28 on Herod and Pilate). The divine economy compensates its instruments; even Nebuchadnezzar receives his wage.
29.21: bayyom hahu atzmichai qeren leveit yisrael ('on that day I will cause a horn to sprout for the house of Israel'): qeren (H7161, horn) — the symbol of royal power and strength (Ps 92:10; 132:17 'I will cause David's horn to bud'; Lk 1:69 'raised up a horn of salvation in the house of David'). The Egypt oracle, after all its prophecy of judgment, closes with a restoration promise for Israel: the same day Egypt is humbled, Israel's royal power is renewed. eftach-picha betovchem ('I will open your mouth among them'): the recommissioning of Ezekiel's prophetic speech (cf. 3:26-27; 33:22).

30.1: Oracle formula without date — the undated position suggests this oracle extends the Egypt judgment section as a poetic complement to the dated prose oracles.
30.2: heylilu al-hayyom ('wail! alas for the day!'): the opening lament-cry — heylel/yalal (H3213, wail/howl) — onomatopoeic mourning. hayyom ('the day') — the ominous definite article: a specific day of reckoning is coming, not a general future.
30.3: ki qarov yom veqarov yom laYHWH ('for the day is near — the day of the LORD is near'): the only explicit yom YHWH in the Ezekiel OAN section. The Day of the LORD against Egypt — the concept developed in Amos 5:18-20 (for Israel); Isa 13 (against Babylon); Joel; now applied to Egypt. et goyim ('time/appointed time of the nations') — the Day is not Israel-specific but universal: it applies to all nations that have set themselves against YHWH's purposes.
30.4: ubaa cherev bemitzraim ('the sword will come against Egypt'): the Babylonian sword as divine instrument. chavlah cush ('anguish will grip Ethiopia'): Egypt's southern ally suffers collateral damage — the 'anguish' (chil, H2427, writhing pain/anguish, as in labor pangs) is the shock of seeing the dominant regional power fall.
30.5: The coalition list: Cush, Put, Lud, mixed peoples (erev, H6153/6154), Cub — the nations allied with or dependent on Egypt. erev ('mixed peoples') may be the same as 'mixed multitude' (Exod 12:38 — non-Israelites who joined the Exodus). The Egyptian coalition represents the multiplicity of nations that have organized their security around Egyptian power rather than YHWH.
30.6: ga'on uzza tarda ('her proud strength will collapse'): gaon (H1347, pride/majesty/excellence) applied to Egypt's power — the same word Ezekiel uses for Israel's pride (7:24) and Assyria's (32:12). ga'on is what YHWH consistently opposes: 'every proud one is an abomination to YHWH' (Prov 16:5). Egypt's gaon — built on the claim to divine self-creation of the Nile — is precisely what the judgment dismantles.
30.7: Desolation formula — the same geographical sweep as 29:10 (Migdol to Syene). The repetition across multiple oracles reinforces the comprehensiveness of the judgment and establishes the established fact through multiple witnesses (Deut 19:15: a matter is established by two or three witnesses).
30.8: veyadefu ki ani YHWH beshtoni esh bemitzraim ('they will know that I am the LORD when I set fire to Egypt'): the recognition formula again — but for Egypt's helpers, not only Egypt. The circle of YHWH's self-revelation through judgment extends to all who supported Egypt, including those nations who thought their security lay in Egyptian alliance.
30.9: biqqayot yetzeyu miti lepahad kush batach ('messengers will go out by ship to terrify complacent Cush'): batach (H982, secure/complacent/at ease) — Ethiopia's false security. The same root as mivtach (v.16 in ch 29): trust/security placed in human power rather than YHWH. The fast-moving messengers by boat (along the Nile) carrying news of Egypt's fall: the speed of news transmission in antiquity was along waterways.
30.10: vehishbati et-hamon mitzraim beyad nebuchadretzar ('I will put an end to the multitude of Egypt by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar'): hamon (H1995, multitude/crowd/noise/wealth) — Egypt's vast population and resources. The hamon that makes Egypt impressive is what YHWH will terminate. Nebuchadnezzar is explicitly named as YHWH's instrument — the Babylonian king who acts without knowing that he serves the divine purpose.
30.11: araritzei goyim ('the most ruthless of nations'): Ezekiel's characteristic description of Babylon (28:7; 30:11; 31:12; 32:12) — aritz (H6184, ruthless/violent/terrifying). The ruthlessness that is morally problematic in itself is used as YHWH's instrument. Divine sovereignty working through imperfect instruments is a constant in Ezekiel's theology of history.
30.12: venatatti yeoroth charavah ('I will make the Nile channels run dry'): the drying of the Nile — the reversal of Egypt's foundational water-source, directly answering Pharaoh's claim 'My Nile is mine.' umakarti et-haaretz beyad-reim ('I will sell the land into the hand of evil men'): makar (H4376, sell) — YHWH selling Egypt, suggesting Egypt's own subordinate status to divine sovereignty; ra (H7451, evil/bad) applied to the Babylonian conquerors.
30.13: vehishbati gillulim ('I will destroy the idols'): gillulim in Memphis (Noph) — the demolition of Egypt's cultic statuary. Memphis was the ancient capital and religious center, home to the Ptah temple and the Apis bull cult. venassi mieretz mitzraim lo yihyeh od ('there will no longer be a prince in the land of Egypt'): the political and cultic structures collapse simultaneously — religious and political power are co-terminal.
30.14: Egyptian geography: Pathros = Upper Egypt; Zoan = Tanis in the eastern delta (where the Israelites labored, Ps 78:43); No = Thebes (No-Amon in Nah 3:8), the great temple city of Amon-Ra. The judgment reaches the entire Nile valley from south to north, targeting each major religious center.
30.15: veshafachti chamati al-sin ('I will pour out my wrath on Pelusium'): sin/sewen = Pelusium, the northeastern fortress-gateway to Egypt from Sinai — the military key to Egypt's eastern defense. uveratii et-hamon No ('and cut off the multitude of Thebes'): Thebes, the wealthiest city in the ancient world (Homer, Iliad 9.381: 'Egyptian Thebes where the treasure-houses are fullest'), the religious heart of Egypt.
30.16: The cascade of Egyptian cities — Pelusium, Thebes, Memphis — three strategic centers of power facing destruction simultaneously. The three cities represent Egypt's military gateway (Pelusium), religious heart (Thebes/Amon), and administrative capital (Memphis/Ptah).
30.17: On = Heliopolis, the center of the Ra/sun cult (Gen 41:45 — Potiphera, Joseph's father-in-law, was priest of On). Pi-beseth = Bubastis, center of the Bastet (cat-goddess) cult. Two of Egypt's primary religious cities face military destruction — the judgment targets the cultic infrastructure specifically.
30.18: vetachpanches chasakh hayyom ('at Tahpanhes the day will grow dark'): Tahpanhes = Daphne, the garrison city in the eastern delta where Jeremiah was taken after the assassination of Gedaliah (Jer 43:7-9). beshivri sham et-mattot mitzraim ('when I break the yokes of Egypt there'): matteh (H4294, rod/yoke/tribe) — the breaking of Egypt's dominion-rods, the power that Egypt exercised over neighboring peoples.
30.19: veasiti shephath bivmitzraim ('thus I will execute judgments in Egypt'): the chapter concludes with the recognition formula — veyadu ki-ani YHWH. The entire oracle is structured as a theodicy: these judgments are not arbitrary displays of power but the execution of covenant justice that will result in the knowledge of YHWH among the nations.
30.20: New date: eleventh year, first month, seventh day — April 587 BCE, one year after the ch 29 oracle, still during the Jerusalem siege. The arm-breaking oracle interprets the failure of Pharaoh Hophra's attempted intervention against Babylon (Jer 37:5-8) as YHWH's action.
30.21: et-zero'a paro ('the arm of Pharaoh king of Egypt'): zero'a (H2220, arm/strength/power) — the arm as the symbol of military power (Ps 44:3; 77:15; Isa 51:5,9). lo-chatash lashum chatvulot ('it has not been bound up to heal it, no bandage applied'): the wound that is not treated — the military debacle of Hophra's failure to relieve Jerusalem. The army that failed to help Israel has no healing; its defeat is permanent.
30.22: veshivarti et-zero'otav et-hachazaqah veeth-hanishbaret ('I will break his arms, both the strong and already-broken one'): both arms broken — total military incapacitation. The complete disabling of Egyptian military power means no help can come from that quarter. vehipalti et-hacharev miyado ('I will make the sword fall from his hand'): the disarmed Pharaoh, unable even to hold a weapon, much less wield it in Israel's defense.
30.23: vahapitzoti et-mitzraim bagoyim ('I will scatter the Egyptians among the nations'): the diaspora-formula for Egypt — the same verb (putz, H6327, scatter/disperse) used for Israel's exile. In the divine economy, all nations are subject to the same covenant-logic: pride leads to scattering; humility enables gathering.
30.24: vehizzaqti et-zero'ot melek bavel ('I will strengthen the arms of the king of Babylon'): the contrast with broken Pharaoh-arms — YHWH breaks Egyptian arms and strengthens Babylonian arms. zero'a melek bavel versus zero'a paro: the same word (zero'a) used for both, with YHWH controlling which is strong and which is broken. venatatif et-charvi beyado ('I will put my sword in his hand'): Nebuchadnezzar wielding YHWH's sword — the pagan king as the divine executor of judgment.
30.25: veyadu ki-ani YHWH benitni charevi beyad melek bavel ('they will know that I am the LORD when I put my sword in the hand of the king of Babylon'): the recognition formula is attached to the action through a pagan king — YHWH's self-disclosure happens through the unexpected agent. The Babylonian king is the instrument; YHWH is the sovereign. This is the theological key to understanding geopolitical history in Ezekiel.
30.26: The concluding formula of the arm-oracle restates the scattering of Egypt (vahapitzoti et-mitzraim) and the recognition formula (veyadu ki-ani YHWH). The chapter ends as it began: YHWH is the subject; Egypt's humiliation is the means of universal knowledge of the LORD.

31.1: Date: eleventh year, third month, first day — June 587 BCE, still before Jerusalem's fall. The cedar of Lebanon allegory responds to Pharaoh's implied claim to greatness comparable to Assyria's.
31.2: el mi damita vegadlecha ('whom are you like in your greatness?'): the rhetorical question that opens the comparison — Ezekiel will answer it with the Assyrian cedar, then with the Eden trees. The question's form presupposes that Pharaoh has compared himself to great powers; YHWH will use that comparison to bring him down.
31.3: hineh ashur erez balevanon ('consider: Assyria was a cedar in Lebanon'): erez (H730, cedar) — the royal tree of the ancient Near East, used for palace and temple construction throughout the region (Solomon's temple used Phoenician cedar; Sennacherib's annals boast of cutting cedars). The cedar in Lebanon as the paradigm of greatness: the tallest trees in the world, fragrant, prized, associated with divinity. vemareh yophi ('beautiful in appearance'): yapheh (H3303, beautiful/fair) — the same word used for the tree of the knowledge of good and evil in Gen 3:6. Beauty that leads to pride and fall is a consistent biblical pattern.
31.4: mayim gidluhu ('the waters made it great'): tehom (H8415, the deep/primordial waters) raising the cedar — the cosmic deep waters that appear in Gen 1:2 as the primordial chaos now serve a fertilizing function. The deep waters irrigating the cosmic tree connect this vision to the Eden imagery: the river in Eden (Gen 2:10) watered the garden. The cedar fed by tehom is sustained by the same waters that the creation narrative assigns to YHWH's sovereignty.
31.5: al-ken gavhah qomato mikol atzei hassadeh ('therefore it grew taller than all the trees of the field'): the height as the result of the deep-water nourishment. The vertical axis — height — is the spatial metaphor for power and pride. What grows tallest is most visible, most admired, and most exposed to the wind. The anatomy of pride: nourished by resource; expressed in height; exposed in fall.
31.6: bise'ipotav qinenu kol-of hashamayim ('in its boughs all the birds of the sky nested'): the cosmic-tree motif — the great tree as the dwelling of all creatures. This imagery appears in Daniel 4:12 (Nebuchadnezzar's dream of the cosmic tree under which all beasts sheltered) and in Jesus's parable of the mustard seed (Matt 13:32: 'birds of the air come and perch in its branches'). The tree that shelters all living things is both imperial symbol and creation-order image.
31.7: yapheh gadlo ve-orech daliyotav ('beautiful in its greatness, magnificent in the length of its branches'): yapheh again — beauty that will become the grounds of the comparison with Eden (v.8-9) and therefore the grounds of the fall. What is most beautiful and most admired is closest to the pride that triggers judgment.
31.8: aratzim lo adaduhu began elohim ('the cedars in the garden of God could not match him'): the explicit Eden-garden comparison. gan elohim (H1588 + H430, 'garden of God') = the garden of Eden (cf. Ezek 28:13; Gen 2-3). The cedar has surpassed even the Eden trees — the ultimate cosmic comparison. This connects to the Eden-fall pattern of ch 28 (the prince of Tyre): the most beautiful creature in the divine garden is the one most prone to pride and fall.
31.9: yafe asitiv berob daliytav ('I made him beautiful with his many branches'): YHWH claims the beauty as his own gift — the creator made the cedar beautiful, which means the cedar's beauty belongs to the creator, not to the cedar. vayekanehu kol-atzei eden asher began haelohim ('so that all the trees of Eden in the garden of God envied him'): the Eden-trees envious of the supremely beautiful cedar. The divine garden as the standard of cosmic beauty, now surpassed by Assyria (= Egypt). The claim to be greater than Eden is the ultimate hubris.
31.10: tachath asher gavah qomato ('because it grew tall'): the exact mechanism of the fall — the height itself is the problem. vayyarem libbov bego'vo ('its heart was proud of its height'): rum lev (H7311 + H3820, heart lifted up/proud) — the proud heart that Deut 8:14 and 17:20 warned against. The tree's pride is described as an interior condition (a 'heart') — Ezekiel consistently anatomizes pride as an inward movement before it manifests in outward action.
31.11: vaettnenu beyad el-goyim aasor aaseh lo ('I gave him over to the hand of the mightiest of nations'): el-goyim ('the mightiest of nations') = Babylon, YHWH's designated executor. The Niphal aasor — 'to deal with/act against' — the conquering nation as the agent of the divine judgment against the proud tree. berish'ato gerashtihu ('I drove him out for his wickedness'): garash (H1644, drive out/expel) — the same word for Adam and Eve's expulsion from Eden (Gen 3:24), and for the Canaanites' expulsion before Israel (Exod 23:28-31). The pattern: beauty, pride, expulsion from the garden/realm.
31.12: vayikretahu zarim araritzei goyim ('foreigners — the most ruthless of nations — cut him down'): the great cedar felled by the most ruthless peoples. The branches on the mountains, ravines, valleys — the scattered branches of a fallen empire. yazavu tzilo kol-ammei aretz ('all the peoples of the earth departed from his shade'): the shelter that the tree provided (v.6) is gone; the nations that dwelt in its shade now abandon it. Political alliances evaporate with power.
31.13: After the fall, the same birds and beasts that nested in the cedar (v.6) now inhabit the fallen trunk — not as sheltered guests but as scavengers on the corpse. The fallen empire's infrastructure is occupied by its former dependencies. The reversal of the living tree's hospitality into the dead tree's exploitation by creatures is a powerful image of imperial collapse.
31.14: lema'an asher lo yigbehu kol-atzei hamayim ('so that no tree beside the waters may exalt itself'): the fall of the great cedar is a lesson for all great trees — no water-nourished power should lift itself in pride. netenum el-mot el-eretz tachetit ('for all who drink water are given over to death, to the depths of the earth'): the universality of mortality even for the greatest: mot (death) and eretz tachetit (the land beneath/Sheol) await all who grow tall. The phrase betouch benei adam el-yordei bor ('among the children of men, with those who go down to the Pit') — all human greatness shares the mortality that the Pit/Sheol represents.
31.15: beyom ridto sheolah ('on the day he went down to Sheol'): Sheol (H7585, the underworld/realm of the dead) — Ezekiel's abundant use of Sheol vocabulary in these Egypt chapters (31:14-17; 32:17-32) is distinctive. kissiyt alav et-tehom ('I covered the deep over him'): the same tehom that nourished the cedar (v.4) now becomes its burial shroud — the waters that supported its life become the waters of death. vatit'abbal alav levanon ('I made Lebanon mourn for him'): Lebanon personified, mourning the loss of its signature tree/empire — the landscape itself grieves.
31.16: miqqol mappelo hirgashti goyim ('I made the nations shake at the sound of his fall'): the resonance of a great empire's fall — nations trembling at the sound. vayinnachamu begei eretz tachetit kol atzei eden mivchar vetov levanon ('and all the trees of Eden... were comforted in the realm below'): the remarkable 'comfort' of the Eden-trees in Sheol at seeing the great cedar join them. The underworld receives the most exalted as equals; death is the great leveler.
31.17: vegam-hem itav yaredu sheolah ('they too went down to Sheol with him'): the tree's zeroa (H2220, arm/strength — those allied with Assyria) share its fate. The empire takes its dependents with it into death. el-chalalei-cherev ('to those slain by the sword') — military death as the path to Sheol, contrasted with the honorable burial of those who die at peace.
31.18: el-mi damita kachah bichavod uvegodel batzei eden ('to whom are you like in glory and greatness among the trees of Eden?'): the rhetorical question of v.2 is answered: Pharaoh is like all the great trees of Eden, all of whom have gone down. vetushav veshachavta bein arelim ('you too will be brought down... and lie among the uncircumcised'): arel (H6189, uncircumcised) — in the OT, to lie with the uncircumcised in Sheol is to share the shame of those outside the covenant (cf. 32:19-32). The honored burial of the circumcised versus the shameful mass grave of the uncircumcised: Pharaoh's final address places him outside the dignity of covenant membership in death.

32.1: Date: twelfth year, twelfth month, first day — March 585 BCE, eight months after Jerusalem's fall. The second lamentation over Pharaoh comes after the catastrophe Ezekiel had prophesied — now the fall of Egypt can be seen in light of the confirmed fall of Jerusalem.
32.2: sa qinah al-paro ('raise a lamentation over Pharaoh'): the second qinah (H7015, dirge/elegy) for Pharaoh — ch 19's lion/vine dirge for Israel's kings is now applied to Egypt's king. kelefir goyim domita veatta katannim bayammim ('you were like a young lion among the nations, and like a monster in the seas'): tannin (H8577) again — the sea-dragon of ch 29. But the comparison is complex: Pharaoh thought he was a young lion (kephir, H3715, the prime young lion) but was actually the sea-monster that roils the waters — a chaos creature rather than a royal beast.
32.3: ufarsetti alecha et-reshet biqhal ammim rabbim ('I will throw my net over you with a host of many peoples'): YHWH as fisherman casting a net — the same fishing/netting imagery as 29:4, but now applied to the sea-monster context. The chaos creature caught in the divine net. veheeluka becharemi ('they will haul you up in my net'): the nations as YHWH's fishing tools, hauling up the tannin.
32.4: venitashticha al-haaretz ('I will throw you on dry land'): the sea-monster removed from its element — the fish out of water. The birds and beasts consuming Pharaoh (v.4) reverse the images of Egyptian religious power: the sacred ibis, vulture, and crocodile, all representations of Egyptian deities, will feed on the corpse of the divine king.
32.5: venatatti et-besarecha al-heharim ('I will lay your flesh on the mountains'): the empire's vast body (basar, H1320, flesh/body) scattered over mountains and valleys — the geographic distribution of the Egyptian corpse across the landscape. The image of an empire's remains spread across a broad terrain captures both the scale of the defeat and its permanence.
32.6: vehishqeiti eretz tzafecha midamecha ('I will drench the land with your flowing blood'): the blood that flows from the corpse as a flood — the blood-drenching reverses the Nile flood that made Egypt's agriculture possible. The Nile that gave life through flooding becomes the blood-flood of death. el-heharim umilleu hayaqot ('even to the mountains, the ravines will be filled'): the blood reaches everywhere, the inverse of the Nile's life-giving overflow.
32.7: uvchasoti bekhavotecha et-shamayim ('when I blot you out I will cover the heavens'): cosmic darkness accompanying Egypt's fall — the darkening of celestial bodies. vehishchakti et-shimsha beanav uyare'ach lo-ya'ir oro ('I will cover the sun with a cloud and the moon will not give its light'): solar and lunar eclipse as divine signs of judgment. This imagery appears in the Day of the LORD texts (Joel 2:10,31; 3:15; Amos 8:9) and in NT eschatological contexts (Matt 24:29; Rev 6:12-13). The darkening of Egypt's lights is both literal (smoke and darkness of conquest) and metaphorical (the end of Egyptian civilization's 'light' to the world).
32.8: kol-meoroth hameeor bashamayim achasheet alecha ('all the luminous lights of the sky I will darken over you'): the comprehensive darkening — sun, moon, stars, all lights extinguished. Egypt was the civilization of Ra/sun worship; to darken the sun over Egypt is to defeat the deity Egypt trusted. The theological polemic is precise: the god Egypt worshiped cannot protect it; the God Egypt ignored is the one who controls all lights.
32.9: vekaastu lev ammim rabbim ('I will trouble the hearts of many peoples'): kaas (H3707, provoke/trouble/grieve) — the psychological ripple effect of Egypt's fall. Nations that organized their world around Egyptian power as the regional anchor will be destabilized. el-aratzot asher lo yedatum ('to countries you have not known'): the scope of the trouble extends beyond Egypt's immediate neighbors to distant lands — a universal shock.
32.10: vehashimoti alecha ammim rabbim ('I will appall many peoples over you'): shamem (H8074, be desolate/appalled) — the same word for the land's desolation applied here to the nations' psychological response. vemalkeihem yisaaru alecha sareer ('and their kings will shudder with horror when I brandish my sword before them'): saar (H8175, shudder/shiver/tremble) — visceral fear in the rulers when they see the sword of judgment fall on the greatest empire of the age.
32.11: cherev melek bavel tevoaka ('the sword of the king of Babylon will come against you'): the explicit naming of the human instrument after the extended cosmic imagery (vv.7-10). The celestial phenomena of darkness and the political reality of the Babylonian military are two descriptions of the same event: YHWH's judgment executed through history.
32.12: vehippilti hamonech bechorvot gibborim araritzei goyim culam ('I will cause your multitude to fall by the swords of the mighty — the most ruthless of nations, all of them'): gibborim (H1368, mighty warriors/heroes) — the elite warriors; araritzei goyim again. vegadevu et-gon mitzraim ('they will destroy the pride of Egypt'): gaon mitzraim — Egypt's pride, the word used in 30:6. The pride that characterized Egypt from v.3 ('my Nile — I made it') is the specific target of the judgment's destruction.
32.13: vehibadeti et-kol-behemtah mealy mayim rabbim ('I will destroy all its livestock from beside the great waters'): the pastureland fed by the Nile — Egypt's agricultural wealth dependent on the river's overflow. velo tedolel maregel adam od ('no human foot will trouble them anymore'): the silence after destruction — no more footsteps in the devastated land, no more cattle-hooves disturbing the river. The peace of desolation.
32.14: az ashiq meimehem veneharehem kashesmen azilem ('then I will let their waters settle and cause their rivers to flow like oil'): the paradoxical peace of the post-judgment Nile — waters no longer muddied by Pharaoh's thrashing (cf. 29:3), running smooth as oil. The oracle that began with Pharaoh muddying the waters (32:2) ends with the waters cleared of his disturbance.
32.15: beten et-eretz mitzraim shemamah ('when I make the land of Egypt desolate'): the recognition formula anchors the chapter's conclusion — the cosmic darkness, the military defeat, the silenced waters: all serve the epistemological purpose of YHWH's self-disclosure. The end of Egypt as a great power is the means by which the nations will know the LORD.
32.16: qinah hi veqonenuha ('this is the lamentation they will sing over her'): the meta-statement about the oracle's genre, identical in structure to 19:14 ('this is a lamentation and has been used as a lamentation'). The daughters of the nations (benot goyim) sing the lamentation — the mourning women of antiquity who formalized communal grief. Egypt receives the same funeral rite that a great city deserves, pronounced by the nations over whom it once exercised power.
32.17: A third date within the Egypt section: twelfth year, fifteenth of the month (month unspecified — likely the same twelfth month, two weeks after v.1). The descent-to-Sheol oracle (vv.17-32) is the most extended underworld passage in the OT outside of Isaiah 14.
32.18: ben-adam naleh el-hamon mitzraim ('son of man, wail for the multitude of Egypt'): naleh (H5098, wail/growl/roar) — a different and stronger word than standard mourning verbs; the prophet's wailing drags Egypt down. vehored otah ve-et-benot goyim adamot ('bring them down — both Egypt and the daughters of the majestic nations'): the command to bring nations down to Sheol is prophetic speech-act — the oracle performs what it describes. The mourning song of the prophet is simultaneously the vehicle of descent.
32.19: mimi naamt redi vehashshkab et-arelim ('whom do you surpass in beauty? Go down and lie with the uncircumcised'): the contempt of the invitation — Egypt's beauty (naom, H5278, pleasantness/beauty) is dismissed; the invitation to lie with the uncircumcised is ironic hospitality. arel (H6189, uncircumcised) — in the Sheol roll-call that follows, 'uncircumcised' is the mark of those who died in disgrace outside the covenant community. For Egypt to be uncircumcised is also ironic: Egyptian males were circumcised (Herodotus 2.36-37; Gen 34:9-10), yet the theological uncircumcision of their hearts (Jer 9:25-26; Ezek 44:7-9) places them with the covenant-excluded.
32.20: betoch chalalei-cherev yipolu ('they will fall among those slain by the sword'): the military death as the entry point to Sheol. cherev nitanah meshochahu vekol-hamoneiha ('Egypt is delivered to the sword — draw her down with all her multitude'): the sword as the agent of the descent, delivering Egypt to Sheol along with its entire hamon (multitude/wealth).
32.21: yedaberu-lo elei gibborim mitoch sheol ('the mightiest warriors will speak to him from the midst of Sheol'): the underworld as a place of ongoing speech — the shades of mighty warriors addressing the newly-arrived Pharaoh. This is the most developed OT picture of Sheol as a realm with inhabitants (cf. Isa 14:9-11 where Sheol's rulers rise to greet the king of Babylon). mehelai-cherev yeredu shachvu arelim ('slain by the sword, they have gone down, lying uncircumcised'): the entrance formula for Sheol in this passage.
32.22: sham ashur vechal-qahalah ('Assyria is there with all its company'): the great empire catalog begins — Assyria, which fell to Babylon (612 BCE), is already in Sheol. The Assyrian army's graves surround it — the dead empire retains its military formation in death, but now it is a formation of graves, not fighting units. The parade of fallen empires makes the theological point: no nation that has lorded its power over others escapes the common fate.
32.23: qivroteiha beyrketei-bor ('its graves are in the depths of the Pit'): bor (H953, pit/cistern/dungeon) — the underworld pit. The Assyrian army's graves in the depths, 'gathered around its tomb' — even in death, the military structure is maintained as a grotesque formation of corpses. asher-nateenu chittit bearetz chayyim ('who had spread terror in the land of the living'): the refrain that appears for each nation — their power was measured by the terror they induced; in Sheol, that terror is silenced.
32.24: sham elam vechal-hamonah ('Elam is there with all its multitude'): Elam, the ancient Mesopotamian kingdom east of Babylon — its bowmen were famous in antiquity (Isa 22:6; Jer 49:34-39). arelim yoredei eretz tachetit ('uncircumcised, gone down to the depths of the earth'): the shame-formula — lying in Sheol among the uncircumcised, despite having spread terror. The equation of military might with shame in death is the chapter's central theological move.
32.25: The description of Elam's grave-bed betoch chalalim ('among the slain') with graves surrounding it. veyutenat mishkavah vetoch chalalim — the ignominious grave 'among the slain' is the opposite of honorable burial (kingly tombs, family burial sites). Despite the terror spread among the living, the powerful end up in a common pit with the violently slain.
32.26: sham meshech tuval vechal-hamonah ('Meshech and Tubal are there with all their multitude'): the northern peoples of Gen 10:2 (sons of Japheth) — often identified with areas in Asia Minor (Phrygia/Cappadocia). Their terror too is silenced in Sheol. These are the same peoples of Gog (Ezek 38-39), completing the catalog of northern, eastern, and western powers.
32.27: velo yishkevu et-gibborim nophlim mearelim ('do they not lie with the fallen warriors of old who went down to Sheol with their weapons?'): the 'warriors of old' (nophlim me-arelim or gibborim meolam) — possibly a reference to the ancient heroes/Nephilim of Gen 6:4. The ancient warriors went to Sheol with their weapons, weapons placed under their heads and shields covering their bones — honorable warrior burial. But Meshech and Tubal lie differently: not as honored warriors but as those who spread terror without receiving the honor of proper warrior burial.
32.28: veattah betoch arelim tishaver ('you too will be shattered among the uncircumcised'): Pharaoh, addressed directly, joins the roll-call. The catalog moves from third-person descriptions (Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom) to second-person address: you will be shattered among them. The roll-call is not a historical survey but an address to the living king — this is what awaits you.
32.29: sham edom melacheha vechal-nesieiha ('Edom is there — its kings and all its princes'): Edom, despite being south of Israel rather than north, is included. The catalog is arranged by symbolic weight rather than geography. Edom as the perpetual enemy of Jacob/Israel (from Gen 25 onward, through the Esau-Jacob conflict) receives no more special treatment in Sheol than any other nation. bechol-geburtam ('despite their might'): the great leveling — might does not exempt from Sheol's common shame.
32.30: sham nasichei tzaphon kulam vesidoni ('there are all the princes of the north and all the Sidonians'): the 'princes of the north' = Aramean kingdoms; Sidon = Phoenicia. The fear they inspired (chetit mig'vurtam, 'terror from their might') is gone; they lie in shame with the slain. The geography of the roll-call — Assyria (northeast), Elam (east), Meshech-Tubal (north), Edom (south), northern princes and Sidon (northwest) — encompasses the entire known world around Egypt. Pharaoh will be joined by every regional power.
32.31: otam yireh paro venecham ('when Pharaoh sees them he will be comforted over all his multitude'): dark comfort — Pharaoh will find consolation in seeing that all the great powers share his fate. The comfort of shared shame is the only comfort Sheol offers. nacham (H5162, comfort/console) — the word used for genuine divine consolation (Isa 40:1; 51:12) here inverted: the comfort of the underworld is merely that misery has company.
32.32: ki-natati chitti bearetz chayyim ('for I spread his terror in the land of the living'): YHWH acknowledges that Pharaoh's terror (chitti, from chat, H2844, terror) was real — but it was YHWH who gave it to him, and YHWH who now withdraws it. The final clause veyushkav et-paro vekhol-hamono bein arelim ('Pharaoh and all his multitude will be laid among the uncircumcised') closes the entire four-chapter Egypt section with the verdict: despite greatness, power, beauty, and terror, Egypt's end is the common grave of the uncircumcised. The claim 'my Nile — I made it' (29:3) ends in a grave among those outside the covenant.

39.1: The resumption formula ve'atah ben-adam hinabu al-Gog (and you, son of man, prophesy against Gog) reopens the oracle of ch. 38. The two-chapter unit (38-39) forms the great Gog oracle — Ezekiel's eschatological battle text. Chapter 38 announced the invasion; chapter 39 describes YHWH's destruction of Gog and its theological aftermath. Rosh nasi Meshech veTuval (chief prince of Meshech and Tubal) — rosh here is a title (chief/head), not the country Russia (a common anachronistic misreading; the connection to Russia comes from 19th-century exegetes reading Rosh as a proper name).
39.2: veshivvathicha veshishethicha: 'I will turn you back and lead you on.' The Hebrew roots here are debated: shavah can mean 'to turn back' or 'to lead,' and shashah can mean 'to lead' or 'to pull along by a hook' (cf. 38:4 where YHWH puts hooks in Gog's jaws). The MKT renders both verbs actively — YHWH is the agent who drives Gog forward to Israel's mountains. The crucial theological point is that Gog's invasion is not successful enemy initiative but YHWH's own deployment of an enemy instrument, as with Assyria in Isa 10:5-6.
39.3: vehikeithi qashtech miyad semolecha: 'I will strike your bow from your left hand.' Military disarmament by divine action — the bow (qashet) from the left hand and arrows (chitzim) from the right hand. Archery was the dominant long-range weapon of ancient Near Eastern warfare. YHWH's direct action against Gog's weapons mirrors the exodus tradition: he fights for Israel, not Israel fighting for itself. The stripping of weapons prefigures the subsequent burning of those weapons by Israel over seven years (v. 9-10).
39.4: al-harei Yisrael tipol atah vechal-agapeicha: 'On the mountains of Israel you will fall, you and all your troops.' The mountains of Israel (harei Yisrael) is a recurring Ezekielian location that appears in both judgment oracles against Israel (ch. 6) and salvation oracles for Israel (ch. 36). Gog falls in Israel's own land — judgment executed where the enemy had anticipated victory. Lefzit qin'ah (to the birds of prey of every kind) — the feeding of enemies to birds and beasts is the reversal of proper burial, a form of judgment that denies the defeated the dignity of covenant burial (cf. Deut 28:26).
39.5: al-penei hasadeh tipol: 'You will fall in the open field.' The sadeh (open field, countryside) burial-denial formula is sealed with ki ani dibarti ne'um adonai YHWH (for I have spoken, declares the Lord YHWH). Ezekiel's characteristic concluding formula makes the divine speech itself the guarantee of the outcome — not military analysis or political prediction but the performative word of YHWH that accomplishes what it announces.
39.6: veshilachti esh beMagog: 'I will send fire on Magog.' The judgment extends beyond Gog's army to Magog (the homeland) and the coastlands (iyyim) that “dwell in security.” Esh (fire) as the instrument of judgment is YHWH's characteristic weapon (Isa 30:30; Amos 1-2; Rev 20:9). The recognition formula veyad'u ki ani YHWH (they will know that I am YHWH) extends to the nations: the Gog defeat is not just Israel's salvation but a universal theophany that makes YHWH known among all peoples.
39.7: ve'et-shem qodshi odiya bekerev ami Yisrael: 'I will make my holy name known in the midst of my people Israel.' Shem qodshi (my holy name) is the theological center of the Gog oracle and of Ezekiel's entire theology: YHWH's purpose is the vindication of his name among nations (cf. 36:20-23, where Israel's exile had profaned YHWH's name among the nations). The Gog defeat is the eschatological event that restores YHWH's reputation — the nations will know that Israel's exile was not YHWH's defeat but his judgment, and Israel's restoration is his power. Lo akhallel et-shem qodshi od (I will not let my holy name be profaned anymore) — the permanent securing of the divine honor.
39.8: hineh baat umayom asher dibarti: 'Behold it is coming and it will happen, declares the Lord YHWH. This is the day of which I have spoken.' Ba'ah (it is coming) as perfect tense — prophetic certainty. The phrase zeh hayom asher dibarti (this is the day of which I have spoken) connects the Gog oracle to YHWH's prior speech — possibly to the restoration oracles of chs. 34-37 or to the foundational threat oracles of chs. 4-24. The day is singular — hayom — but it encompasses the eschatological event Ezekiel has been building toward.
39.9: veyatze'u yoshevei arei Yisrael uviy'aru vehissiqu bensheq: 'Those who dwell in the cities of Israel will go out and make fires of the weapons.' The burning of weapons for seven years (sheva shanim) is the practical consequence of total military victory. Magen vetzinnah qeshet vachitzim umes madanot varomach — the weapon list (shields, bucklers, bow, arrows, war clubs, spears) represents the complete arsenal of ancient Near Eastern warfare. Using enemy weapons as fuel rather than importing wood from the forest is a sign of the complete sufficiency of the victory — the defeated enemy's equipment meets all of Israel's energy needs.
39.10: lo yis'u etzim min-hasadeh: 'They shall not carry wood from the field.' The fuel self-sufficiency of vv. 9-10 has a symbolic dimension beyond practicality: Israel does not need to go out to the territory (sadeh) where enemies dwell to gather their sustenance. The plundering of those who plundered them and the spoiling of those who spoiled them (veshaleloo et-sholeheihem ve'vezzoo et-bozezehem) is the classic prophetic reversal — exact recompense through divine governance of history.
39.11: venathati leGog makom sham qever beYisrael: 'I will give Gog a burial place there in Israel.' Gei ha-avarim (Valley of the Travelers/Passers-by) — the name is debated: avarim can mean “those who pass through” (common travelers) or is related to the Abarim mountains in Transjordan (Num 27:12; Deut 32:49). The MKT renders it 'travelers' following the contextual use. The renamed valley Gei Hamon-Gog (Valley of the Multitude of Gog) commemorates permanently the scale of YHWH's victory. The nose-blocking of the passers-by (ve'gosemeth et-avar ha-avarim) indicates an enormous stench from the mass of corpses.
39.12: uvekabram otam beit Yisrael: 'The house of Israel will be burying them.' Seven months (shiva chodeshim) of burial is the temporal counterpart to the seven years of weapon-burning (v. 9) — the number seven in both cases signals completeness. The extended burial process communicates the scale of Gog's defeat but also the thoroughness of the land's cleansing: Israel must actively participate in removing all traces of the hostile army from the land of covenant blessing.
39.13: vekabru kol-am haarets: 'All the people of the land will bury them.' The communal burial is characterized as a source of honor (ve'hayah lahem leshem = it will be to them a name/renown) in the day YHWH glorifies himself (beyom hikavedi). The burying of the enemy is not merely hygienic but theological — it is participation in YHWH's great act of vindication. The phrase beyom hikavedi (on the day I am glorified/honored) connects the burial directly to the kavod (glory) theme that runs through Ezekiel's theology.
39.14: va'anshei tamid yavdilu oveerei baaretz: 'Men shall be set apart, regularly employed to pass through the land.' Anshei tamid (men of continualness/regularity) — a specialized burial detail (mekabrim) whose continual task is to search the land for remaining bones. The thoroughness of the land-cleansing operation emphasizes that even scattered and forgotten bones must be located. The passage of seven months was not sufficient — a dedicated professional corps is needed for the final purification.
39.15: verau etzem adam: 'And when any one sees a human bone.' The wayfarer who sees a bone sets up a marker (tziyyun = a road marker, sign) beside it so that the burial detail can find it. This is the ancient equivalent of a danger marker and collection protocol. The systematic nature of the cleanup — specialized workers, markers, final collection — communicates that land defilement by unburied corpses (cf. Num 19:16) has cosmic importance: the land must be ritually pure for the divine presence to return (chs. 40-48).
39.16: vegam shem-ir Hamonah: 'And the name of the city will also be Hamonah.' Hamonah (multitude, horde) is the city near the burial valley that will bear the name as a permanent memorial of YHWH's victory. The naming of both the valley (Hamon-gog, v. 11) and the city (Hamonah) permanently commemorates the defeat — the landscape itself becomes memorial testimony. This anticipates the eschatological transformation of place-names in Ezekiel's vision (44:35: Jerusalem named “YHWH is There”).
39.17: ve'atah ben-adam koh amar adonai YHWH emor letzipor kol-kanaf: 'And you, son of man, thus says the Lord YHWH: Speak to every winged bird.' The summoning of birds and beasts to the sacrificial feast on Gog's corpses is deliberately described as a zevach (sacrifice) and a mishteh (feast/drinking party). The feast on the mighty and their horses (v. 18-20) is the inverse of the covenant sacrificial feast — instead of Israel feasting on covenant blessing, the creation feasts on covenant-enemy. Revelation 19:17-18 quotes this passage for the eschatological battle's aftermath.
39.18: bashar gibborim tochelu vedam nesiayei haaretz tishteh: 'You will eat the flesh of mighty men and drink the blood of princes.' The specifics — alim (rams), karim (lambs), attudim (he-goats), parim (bulls) — use sacrificial animal terminology metaphorically for human rulers and warriors. The princes (nesiyim), mighty men (gibborim), and their forces are the “animals” at this inverted feast. The drinking of blood — normally the ultimate dietary prohibition in Israel (Lev 17:14) — here applied to birds and beasts signals that this feast is outside all normal categories: it is YHWH's own slaughter-feast.
39.19: va'akaltem chelev lesova: 'You will eat fat until you are satisfied.' The three words — chelev (fat, the richest portions), lesova (to satiety/fullness), ve'shatem dam lishkarah (and drink blood to drunkenness) — are the language of excessive feasting. The irony is that the fat and blood — both reserved for YHWH in the sacrificial system (Lev 3:16-17) — are here given to creation's scavengers, because this feast is YHWH's own judgment-sacrifice in which the enemy army is the animal offering.
39.20: usevitem al-shulchani: 'You will be filled at my table.' YHWH's table (shulchani) at which birds and beasts feast is the judgment-altar's counterpart to the sanctuary table (the table of the Presence). The complete military taxonomy — horses, riders, mighty men, warriors — confirms the universality of the judgment: no component of Gog's force escapes. The recognition formula follows: venathati et-kevodi bagoyim (I will set my glory among the nations).
39.21: venathati et-kevodi bagoyim: 'I will set my glory among the nations.' The kavod (glory) statement is the theological climax of the Gog narrative. YHWH's purpose throughout the oracle has been the universal recognition of his sovereign power — not merely Israelite vindication but cosmic theophany. Vereyu kol-hagoyim et-mishpati (all the nations will see my judgment) — the Gog battle is a public event visible to all peoples, establishing beyond doubt that YHWH governs history and judges those who challenge his covenant purposes.
39.22: veyad'u beit Yisrael ki ani YHWH elohehem: 'And the house of Israel will know that I am YHWH their God.' The recognition formula is applied to Israel specifically here: elohehem (their God) adds the covenant dimension — not merely that YHWH exists or is powerful but that he is Israel's own covenant God who has acted on their behalf. The phrase min-hayom hahu vema'alah (from that day onward) signals that the Gog defeat produces a permanent change in Israel's covenant knowledge — no more forgetting, no more idol substitution.
39.23: veyad'u hagoyim ki ba'avonam galu beit Yisrael: 'The nations will know that the house of Israel went into exile for their iniquity.' The theological explanation of the exile for the benefit of the nations: YHWH hid his face (hisstarti panai) not because he was weak but because Israel sinned (be'avonam). YHWH's hiding of his face was a judicial act, not a defeat. The nations' misunderstanding of the exile (assuming YHWH was powerless to prevent it) is now corrected by the restoration — they learn both why Israel was exiled and why YHWH is now acting for her.
39.24: ketumatam uchefishehem asiti otam: 'According to their impurity and according to their transgressions I acted against them.' The theological vindication is precise: YHWH's action in exile was perfectly calibrated to Israel's sin. Tuma (impurity, ritual/ethical defilement) and pasha (transgression, covenant violation) are the two categories of Israel's failure. YHWH's judicial response was measured, not arbitrary or excessive. The nations can now see that the exile was proportionate divine justice, not random military misfortune.
39.25: lachan koh amar adonai YHWH atah ashiv et-shevut Yaakov: 'Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Now I will restore the fortunes of Jacob.' The lachan (therefore) of restoration is the climactic reversal. Shevut Yaakov (the fortunes of Jacob) — the formula shav shevut signals the turning of captivity/fortune (it can mean both restoring from exile and reversing misfortune). The jealousy for YHWH's holy name (qinati leshemi qodshi) is the motivation — the same jealousy that motivated judgment (36:5-6) now motivates restoration. Divine jealousy is not petty but the covenant passion that drives both justice and redemption.
39.26: venessu et-kelimmatam vekhol-ma'alam asher ma'alu vi: 'They will bear their shame and all their treachery with which they have been unfaithful to me.' The restored Israel will not be returned to complacency — they will bear (nasa = to carry, to forgive, to bear responsibility for) the memory of their shame. This is neither condemnation nor forgotten grace: the shame is borne in the context of security (beshivtam al-admatam labetach = dwelling securely on their land). Held shame in the context of security is the formative posture of those who have experienced grace they did not deserve.
39.27: beshofeti otam beshivti et-shevutam: 'When I have gathered them from the peoples and gathered them from their enemies' lands.' The public kiddush hashem (sanctification of the divine name) executed before many nations — venikdashti bam le'einei goyim rabbim (I will manifest my holiness through them before many nations). The return of the exiles is itself a theophanic event: it declares to watching nations that YHWH is who he claimed to be. The restoration is not merely humanitarian but doxological — its purpose is the universal proclamation of YHWH's holiness.
39.28: veyad'u ki ani YHWH elohehem begaloti otam el-hagoyim: 'They will know that I am YHWH their God — I who exiled them among the nations.' The theological conclusion states explicitly what the narrative has implied throughout: YHWH was the agent of both exile and restoration. The same God who exiled (galoti otam = I exiled them) now gathers (vikibatzti otam al-admatam = I gathered them to their land). Divine sovereignty governs both movements — the scattering and the ingathering are both YHWH's actions, which is the basis of hope. Velo-otir od mehם (and I left none of them behind) — complete restoration, not a remnant of a remnant.
39.29: velo-astir od panai mehem: 'I will no longer hide my face from them.' The reversal of the judicial face-hiding (hisstarti panai of v. 23-24) is the ultimate restoration promise. Asher shafachti et-ruachi al-beit Yisrael (when I have poured out my Spirit on the house of Israel) — the Spirit-pouring is both the means and the sign of the restoration. The poured-out Spirit is the eschatological fulfillment that Joel 2:28-32 develops and Acts 2:17-21 applies to Pentecost. The final seal: ne'um adonai YHWH — the divine declaration that closes the great Gog oracle.

40.1: bechameish ve'esrim shanah legalutenu berosh hashanah be'asor lachodesh: 'In the twenty-fifth year of our exile, at the beginning of the year, on the tenth of the month.' The precise date anchors the vision in history: 25th year of exile = approximately 573 BCE, 14 years after Jerusalem's fall in 587 BCE. The tenth of the first month (Nisan/Abib) is Yom Kippur's preparation date — the same date on which the Passover lamb was selected (Exod 12:3). The coincidence is theologically weighted: a vision of the restored sanctuary comes on the day of paschal selection. Yad YHWH hayah alai (the hand of YHWH was upon me) — the prophetic inspiration formula indicating Ezekiel's transport into the vision.
40.2: bemareh Elohim heviani el-eretz Yisrael: 'In divine visions he brought me to the land of Israel.' Mareh Elohim (divine visions, visions of God) — the phrase used for the inaugural vision in 1:1. Ezekiel is brought to har gavoah meod (a very high mountain) on whose south side (negbah) was something like a city structure (kimivneh ir). The high mountain of divine dwelling is the universal religious imagination realized in Israelite form: Sinai (Exod 19), Zion (Ps 48; Isa 2:2), Eden-mountain (Ezek 28:14). The approach from the south/negev is distinctive and may indicate the eschatological rebuilding of Jerusalem proper.
40.3: vayavi otam shamah vehineh ish: 'He brought me there, and behold a man.' The guide-figure — whose appearance is kemar'eh nehoshet (like the appearance of bronze) — holds a pethil pishta (linen measuring cord) and a qane hammidah (measuring reed). The bronze appearance links this figure to the cherubim and the divine throne-chariot vision (1:7). The measuring instruments establish the vision's architecture as precise and divinely-specified — not symbolic approximation but exact divine plans for the restored sanctuary. John borrows this measuring-angel figure for Rev 21:15.
40.4: ben-adam ree be'einekha ushema be'oznekha: 'Son of man, see with your eyes and hear with your ears.' The instruction to observe carefully (sim libecha = set your heart/attention to everything being shown) establishes the visionary program: Ezekiel is not merely a passive recipient but an active documenting witness. Lema'an areh otcha hovaati henna (for the purpose of showing you, I brought you here) — the vision has a communicative purpose. What Ezekiel sees and records becomes the specification for the future sanctuary — the architectural equivalent of Moses being shown the pattern on the mountain (Exod 25:9, 40).
40.5: vehineh choma michutz labayit saviv saviv: 'And there was a wall all around on the outside of the temple complex.' The outer wall (choma) establishing the sacred precinct is measured at six cubits both in height and width — and crucially, the cubit used is amah ve'topach (a standard cubit plus a handbreadth), making it approximately 21 inches rather than the standard 18-inch cubit. The measurement standard is divinely specified, not inherited from prior human architectural practice. This establishes from the start that the sanctuary's dimensions are revelatory rather than conventional.
40.6: vayavo el-sha'ar asher panav derech haqadim: 'He came to the gate that faces east.' The east-facing outer gate is the first structure measured. The east gate has particular significance: the glory of YHWH will re-enter through the east gate (43:1-4), and the gate will be shut after the glory enters (44:1-2) because YHWH has entered by it. The gate complex is measured systematically: the steps, the threshold, the guardrooms (taha), the gate posts, and the vestibule.
40.7: vetaha echad amah achat aroch ve'amah achat rochav: 'Each guardroom was one cubit long and one cubit wide.' The tahot (guardrooms, niches, side chambers in the gatehouse) are each measured individually. The number three on each side (v. 10) creates a total of six guardrooms per gate — symmetrical design. The standardization of measurements throughout (each guardroom identical, each threshold the same) communicates divine order and perfection in the architectural plan: this is not a human builder's practical compromise but YHWH's ideal specification.
40.8: vayamad et-ulam hasha'ar mibayit: 'He measured the vestibule of the gate from inside.' The inner vestibule (ulam) completes the gate complex measurement. The precision of the measurement vocabulary — threshold, jamb, vestibule, guardroom — reflects Ezekiel's priestly background: he knows temple architecture as a specialist and records the visionary temple in the technical language of his professional formation.
40.9: vayamad et-ulam hasha'ar shmonah amot: 'He measured the vestibule of the gate: eight cubits.' The vestibule is eight cubits with pillars (ayil) of two cubits — the pillars flanking the vestibule entrance. The eight-cubit vestibule width versus the six-cubit guardrooms creates a progression: the entrance chambers become progressively larger as one moves deeper into the gate complex, corresponding to the increasing holiness of the inner zones. The architectural progression from common to holy is built into the spatial design.
40.10: vetahot hasha'ar derech haqadim: 'The guardrooms of the east gate were three on each side.' The symmetric three-plus-three arrangement (six guardrooms total) with identical dimensions establishes the symmetrical perfection of the divine design. The uniformity of the guardrooms — mida achat leshloshtam (the same measure for all three) — communicates that the sanctuary is not the product of human aesthetic variation but the exact replication of a heavenly template (cf. the tabernacle pattern, Exod 25:40).
40.11: vayamad et-rochav pethach hasha'ar: 'He measured the width of the gate passage.' The gate passage (pethach hasha'ar) is ten cubits wide; the total gate length is thirteen cubits. These proportions determine what kind of traffic (processions, individual worshipers) can pass through and the spatial experience of those entering. The precision of the measurement commentary — width, length, and specific elements — reflects the architectural vision's character: it is detailed enough to function as a building specification.
