41.10: The space between the chambers — twenty cubits around the temple. This free space (the gizrah of v12) created a buffer between the sanctuary and the outer courts. The structural principle of graduated separation between zones of holiness is built into the architecture of the vision.
41.11: The doorways of the side chambers facing the free space — petaḥ hatsela ʾel-hammanuaḥ — one door to the north and one to the south. The orientation of the entrances toward the manuaḥ (free space) rather than toward the outer court is a deliberate boundary-maintaining feature: access to the side chambers is from the sanctuary-side, not from the public court-side.
41.12: habbinyan ʾasher lifnei haggizrah — the building (binyan) before the separated yard (gizrah) on the west side: seventy cubits wide, ninety cubits long, with five-cubit walls. Gizrah (separated area/restricted zone) is a technical architectural term derived from gazar (to cut off/separate). The western building with its separated yard completes the overall plan of the sanctuary compound.
41.13: The overall temple measurements: one hundred cubits long and one hundred cubits wide — the perfect square of the outer boundary. The overall compound is thus a perfect square, with the inner most room (20 × 20) as its core. The numerical symbolism of 100 × 100 (= 10² × 10²) encodes divine perfection at the macroscopic level of the entire complex.
41.14: The width of the east face of the temple — one hundred cubits — and the separated area: the breadth of the face toward the east is established as the final measurement completing the outer boundary. The repeated use of the word phanim (face/front) for the east side marks the temple's orientation: the east is the phanim (front/face) because YHWH's glory entered from the east (43:1-4).
41.15: The building behind the temple including its galleries: one hundred cubits. The term ʾattiq (gallery/colonnade) is a technical architectural term of uncertain etymology; it appears to refer to the covered colonnades running along the sides of the inner court. The measurement confirms the overall square footprint of the complex.
41.16: The thresholds (sippim), latticed windows (ḥallonot ʾatumat), and galleries (ʾatiqot) round about on three sides — paneled with wood from the floor to the windows. Ḥallonot ʾatumat (narrow/closed windows) is the same term used for Solomon's temple windows (1 Kgs 6:4). The covering (paneling) with wood from floor to ceiling is the sanctuary's interior finish. Saf (threshold/floor-level) establishes the starting point for the wood-paneling measurement.
41.17: The space above the door and to the inner chamber and to the outside — measurements given. The spatial description moves carefully from the measured threshold up to the carved surfaces. The wood-paneling program is the context for the carvings described in vv18-20.
41.18: ʿasuyi keruvim vetimmorot — made with cherubim (keruvim) and palm trees (timmorot). The alternating cherub-palm decorative motif is the same as Solomon's temple interior (1 Kgs 6:29, 32, 35). Each cherub has two faces: penei ʾadam ʾel-hattimorat mizzeh upenei kefir ʾel-hattimorat mizzeh — the face of a man and the face of a young lion alternating on each side of the palm. This is a simplified version of the four-faced cherubim of ch 1 and 10.
41.19: The two faces of the wall carvings — human face to the palm on one side, lion face to the palm on the other. The reduction from four faces (ch 1) to two faces on the wall carvings represents the adaptation of the vision-cherubim for architectural use. The facing-pairs (human/palm, lion/palm) suggest bilateral symmetry and the integration of creation-order (human authority and leonine power) with the garden-axis of the sanctuary (the palm as stylized tree-of-life).
41.20: The cherubim and palm trees carved from the floor to above the doorway — mehaʾaretz ʿad-meʿal hapetaḥ — on the entire wall of the nave. The continuous carved program from floor to lintel transforms the nave interior into a visual garden-sanctuary, continuous with the garden-imagery of Eden. The same motif decorates both the nave (heikhal) and the Most Holy Place entrance, linking the two zones visually.
41.21: hamezonah mezuzot hamishkan — the doorposts of the nave are square: mezuzat (doorpost, the structural element that receives the mezuzah-scroll in Deut 6:9) combined with mishkan (tabernacle/dwelling-place). The specific term for the nave's doorposts is architecturally precise; the square doorpost form distinguishes the nave from the curved/rounded entryways of ordinary buildings.
41.22: hamizbeaḥ ʿets shalosh ʾammot ghovah... veqirot mimmenu ʿatsayim — the wooden altar: three cubits high, two cubits long — veqirot mimmenu ʿatsayim (its walls of wood). The angel identifies it: zeh hashulḥan ʾasher lifnei YHWH — "this is the table that is before YHWH." The wooden altar is explicitly identified as a shulḥan (table) rather than an altar for burnt offerings. This is the incense altar/table of presence, not a sacrificial altar. The three terms — altar dimensions, wooden construction, identification as table before YHWH — distinguish it clearly from the great bronze altar of v13.
41.23: The two doors of the nave and of the Most Holy Place: ushetayim daletot lahekhal veladevir. The term devir (inner oracle/Most Holy Place) appears here — it is the term used in 1 Kgs 6:5, 16, 19-23 for the innermost chamber. The use of devir here (not just "inner room") anchors the visionary temple to the Solomonic temple vocabulary.
41.24: The double doors (delatayim — a dual of delet, door) with two swinging/folding leaves (tsalmot) on each door: two leaves on one door and two leaves on the other. Tsalmot (folding leaves, from tselem or possibly related to tsalal, to fold/collapse) allows the doors to swing open to either side. The precision of the architectural vocabulary shows the visionary guidance takes the temple engineering seriously.
41.25: Cherubim and palm trees carved on the nave doors as on the walls — the decorative program is continuous between walls and doors. The wooden canopy (ʿets ʿav) before the entrance of the nave: ʿab (thick/dense) applied to wood suggests a dense carved wooden screen or a carved lintel. The threshold (saf) at the entrance completes the architectural frame.
41.26: The narrow windows and palm trees on either side of the porch, including the side chambers — the carved program extends from the inner spaces outward to the porch. The consistency of the palm-tree motif throughout the temple complex (nave walls, doors, portico, side chambers) creates a unified visual theology: the sanctuary is the garden, and the garden is the place of divine presence.

42.1: The guide takes Ezekiel northward into the outer court to the chamber complex opposite the separated yard — hammenuḥah and the building. The spatial movement in the vision narrative is always purposeful: each new zone is accessible only after the prior zone is fully measured, enacting the principle that entry into holiness requires preparation and sequence.
42.2: The north-facing structure — a hundred cubits long and fifty cubits wide. The explicit measurement of the chamber complex to the north of the sanctuary follows the same convention as the measurements of the sanctuary itself. The north/south pairing of priestly chamber complexes (v11 describes the south equivalent) creates the bilateral symmetry of the priestly precinct.
42.3: neged haʿesrim — opposite the twenty cubits of the inner court and opposite the pavement of the outer court: galleries (ʾatiqim) in three stories over against each other. The three-tiered gallery structure (ʾatiq ʾel-mul ʾatiq shelosh peʿamim) echoes the three-tiered side chambers of ch 41. The repetition of the three-tiered structure in both the sanctuary side chambers and the priestly chambers emphasizes the architectural principle of ascending order.
42.4: A walkway (mahalakh) in front of the chambers — ten cubits wide and a hundred cubits long: upetiḥam latsafon — their openings facing north. Mahalakh (walkway/passage, from halakh, to walk) is the corridor that gives access to the chambers. The north-facing orientation is the priests' access side — approaching from the outer court rather than from the sanctuary proper.
42.5: The upper chambers are shorter than the lower and middle ones because the galleries (ʾatiqim) take up more of their space — ki yiʾkhlelu ha-ʾatiqim mimmennah min-hattaḥtonot umin-hattikhonot mibbenavyan. This describes a structural feature of the gallery system: each level's floor space is reduced by the gallery width, so the uppermost rooms are shortest. The engineering precision of this description suggests the vision is not symbolic schematic but architectural reality.
42.6: The three-story chambers have no pillars (ki eyn lahem ʿammudim kaʿammudei haḥatserot) like the pillars of the courts. The absence of the free-standing columns that support colonnaded courts distinguishes the priestly chambers from public colonnaded spaces. The structural support comes from the walls themselves rather than columns, making the chambers more enclosed and private.
42.7: The outer wall (gadar) — a single wall of fifty cubits along the outer court in front of the chambers. Gadar (wall built of stones, as distinct from ḥomah, a city/boundary wall) is used for a structural dividing wall. The fifty-cubit wall creates the outer boundary of the priestly chamber precinct relative to the outer court.
42.8: The length before the outer chambers is fifty cubits — ve-ʿal-penei hallishakhot ʾasher larabbim ʾorek ḥamishim ʾammah — while the nave length is one hundred cubits. The fifty/one hundred ratio of the outer chambers to the full nave maintains the proportional system of the entire complex.
42.9: Below these chambers — mitaḥat hallishakhot haʾelle — the entrance from the east side as one enters from the outer court. The lower entrance (mevoh lahen miqqedem bevo lahen miḥatser haḥitsonah) connects the outer court to the priestly chamber precinct. The access pattern — entering from the east side at the lower level — parallels YHWH's glory entering from the east (43:1-4).
42.10: In the wall of the court toward the east — chambers facing south (derom) before the separated yard and before the building. The south-side chambers are the mirror of the north-side chambers, creating the bilateral symmetry of the priestly precinct. The directional description makes explicit the four-sided enclosure of the sanctuary zone.
42.11: The walkway before them (derekh lifnehem) — like the appearance of the north chambers — same length, same width, same exits and arrangements (kemishpateyhen vekhifteḥeyhen). The exact parallelism between north and south priestly chambers is stated explicitly: kekhol toʾarotam vekhifteḥeyhen ukhematkhnotam (all their forms and all their exits and all their arrangements). The principle of mirror-symmetry in the priestly precinct is architectural theology: the bilateral structure encodes the completeness of priestly service.
42.12: The entrance to the south chambers — lifnei hagadar hadarek ʾasher qedem bevoʾam — "before the wall, the way as one enters from the east." The access pattern from the east is repeated for the south chambers as for the north. The east-entry convention for both priestly chamber complexes connects to the east-facing orientation of the entire sanctuary.
42.13: lishkhot hatstasfon velishtot hadarom ʾasher lifnei haggizrah — the north and south chambers before the separated yard are the holy chambers (lishkhot haqqodesh) where priests who approach YHWH eat the most holy offerings (qodshei haqqodashim — a double plural of the superlative, "the holiest holy things"). The specific activities for these chambers: eating the grain offering (minḥah), sin offering (ḥattat), and guilt offering (ʾasham). The three sacrifice types that require eating in the holy precinct are precisely the three types eaten only within the sanctuary per Lev 6-7.
42.14: uvevaʾam hakohanim veloʾ yetsʾu min-haqqodesh ʾel-heḥatser haḥitsonah — when the priests enter the holy precinct they shall not go out from the holy place to the outer court without leaving there their vestments in which they minister. The consecration principle: priestly garments yeniḥu vigdeyhem ʾasher yesharteru vahem (they shall lay aside the garments in which they serve) before entering the outer court, because these garments are holy (qodesh hem) and must not carry holiness into the common space. The levushot aḥerot (other garments) worn in the outer court are the boundary between sacred and common space made textile.
42.15: The outer court measurement upon completion — ukhilah ʾet-midot habbayit hapenimi (having finished measuring the inner temple) — the guide takes Ezekiel out through the east gate to measure the outer boundary. The east gate as exit parallels the east gate as entry: the east is both the entrance and the exit of the sanctuary vision, framing the entire measurement sequence.
42.16: The east side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod — ḥamesh meʾot ḥamesh meʾot qane hamiddah. The measurement is by the full measuring rod (six long cubits each rod, per 40:5), not by individual cubits. The outer boundary at five hundred reeds × four sides creates the enormous perimeter of the holy district.
42.17: The north side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod. The bilateral symmetry of north and south, east and west at the outer boundary confirms the perfect square of the entire holy precinct at the macro-scale.
42.18: The south side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod. The systematic measurement of all four sides in sequence — east, north, south, west (reversing to west in v19) — follows the orientation convention that places east first (the face/front of the sanctuary).
42.19: The west side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod. The four-directional completion of the outer boundary measurement corresponds to the four-directional completeness of the sanctuary's holiness. The five-hundred-reed measurement on each side creates a perfect square of 500 × 500 reeds.
42.20: lehavchal ven-haqqodesh uven-haḥol — the wall had a measurement of five hundred cubits on each side to make a separation between the holy and the common: lehavchal ven-haqqodesh uven-haḥol. The double terms qodesh (holy/sacred) and ḥol (common/profane) express the fundamental binary of the Israelite cult. Lehavchal (to divide/separate, hiphil of badal, the same root as mavdil, divider) — the wall's ultimate purpose is to embody the distinction that is foundational to Israel's entire theological and ritual system. This ḥomah (boundary wall) is YHWH's architectural encoding of the holy/common distinction that governs all of life.

43.1: vayolickheni ʾel-hashaʿar shaʿar ʾasher pone derekh haqqadim — the guide leads Ezekiel to the east gate. The east gate is the climax and destination of the entire architectural tour: all the measurements have been orientation-framework for the event that now occurs. Pone derekh haqqadim (facing the way of the east) repeats the phrase from 40:6 where the tour began at the east gate.
43.2: vehinneh kevod ʾElohei Yisraʾel ba midderekh haqqadim — "and behold the glory of the God of Israel came from the way of the east." The return of the kavod (glory) from the east is the inverse of its departure eastward to the Mount of Olives in 11:23. The kavod vocabulary is drawn from chs 1-3 and 8-11: qol (voice/sound) + mayim rabbim (many waters) is the theophanic sound-descriptor of 1:24; the earth shining with his glory (vehaʾarets hiʾirah mikkevodo) recalls the initial throne-chariot vision.
43.3: The vision Ezekiel now sees is like the vision at the time of the city's destruction (kimmarʾeh hammarʾeh ʾasher raʾiti bivoʾi leshaḥet ʾet-haʿir) and like the vision by the river Khebar. The divine self-consistency is the point: the kavod that judged Jerusalem is identical to the kavod that now returns to redeem it. YHWH does not change; the change is in the circumstances of encounter. Ezekiel falls on his face (vayyipol ʾal-panay) — the standard response to the theophany (1:28; 3:23).
43.4: ukhevod YHWH ba ʾel-habbayit derekh shaʿar ʾasher panayw derekh haqqadim — the glory of YHWH entered the house by the east-facing gate. The precision is theologically loaded: the same gate through which YHWH's glory departed (the Mount of Olives, 11:23) is the gate of return. The dwelling in the temple is not automatic but deliberate and directional: YHWH chooses the east gate as both the gate of departure and the gate of return.
43.5: vattiśśaʾeni ruach vatteviʾeni ʾel-heḥatser hapenimi — the Spirit (ruach) lifts and brings Ezekiel into the inner court. The Spirit-transport formula recurs from 2:2; 3:12, 14; 8:3; 11:1, 24 — each time Ezekiel is carried to a position of prophetic witness. The inner court is the closest a non-priest approaches the sanctuary; the Spirit brings Ezekiel there to witness the glory filling the house.
43.6: veʾeshmaʿ meiddabber ʾelai min-habbayit — Ezekiel hears the divine voice speaking from within the house. The shift from the angel-guide as speaker (throughout chs 40-42) to YHWH speaking directly (meiddabber ʾelai) marks the theological climax of the vision. The divine speech replaces the guided measurement tour — the sanctuary is now interpreted by its owner/inhabitant.
43.7: ben-ʾadam ʾet-meqom khisʾi veʾet-meqom kapot raglay — "son of man, this is the place of my throne and the place of the soles of my feet." The meqom khisʾi (place of my throne) combines the ark-throne and the footstool images from Ps 99:5; 132:7. Kapot raglay (soles of my feet) — YHWH's feet resting on the earth/temple echoes Isa 66:1. The forever-dwelling declaration — ʾasher eshkon sham benei Yisraʾel leʿolam — "where I will dwell among the children of Israel forever" — is the covenant-promise formula in its spatial form: the sanctuary as the locus of the mishkan (dwelling) relationship.
43.8: The specific sins that defiled the former temple — besimam sipam ʾet-sippi umezuzatam ʾetsel mezuzati — "by setting their threshold by my threshold and their doorpost beside my doorpost." The complaint: the royal palace shared a wall with the temple, symbolically treating the divine and human dwelling as co-equal. The royal construction that abutted the temple violated the qodesh/ḥol (holy/common) boundary that the entire vision of chs 40-42 has been establishing architecturally. Their toʿavot (abominations) defiled YHWH's holy name.
43.9: The removal of their harlotry and the carcasses of their kings — veʿattah yerraḥiqu ʾet-znutam veʾet-pigrê malkheyhem mimmenni — the condition for permanent divine dwelling: the ritual and political pollution must be removed. Pigrê malkheyhem (corpses/carcasses of their kings) may refer to royal burials adjacent to or within the temple precincts, a practice confirmed by archaeology. The promise: veshakhantti vetokhkham leʿolam (I will dwell among them forever).
43.10: veʾattah ven-ʾadam hagged ʾet-beit Yisraʾel ʾet-habbayit — "and you, son of man, describe the temple to the house of Israel." The purpose of the entire architectural vision is now stated: it is not only for Ezekiel to see but for him to describe to the exilic community. The act of description (hagged, hiphil of nagad, to declare/tell) carries the same weight as the act of measurement. Ezekiel's verbal declaration of the visionary temple shapes the community's hope.
43.11: The condition for the temple plan's disclosure to Israel — veʾim nikhlemu mikol ʾasher ʿasu tsurat habbayit — "if they are ashamed of all they have done, make known the form of the house." The nikhlem (ashamed, niphal of kalam, to be ashamed/humiliated) is the prerequisite for hearing the temple vision. The disclosure is conditional: the vision is given in anticipation of repentance, not despite its absence. Once ashamed, they are to know kol-tsurato (all its form/plan) and kol-ḥuqqotav (all its ordinances).
43.12: zot torat habbayit — "this is the law of the temple." The programmatic phrase torat habbayit (law/instruction of the house) is the title for the entire architectural-cultic revelation of chs 40-48. Torah here means not just "law" but "instruction/directive" — the blueprint for restored covenant life. The content of the law: ʿal-roʾsh hahar kol-gevulo saviv saviv qodesh qodashim — "on the top of the mountain, all its surrounding area shall be most holy." The entire mountain summit is declared qodesh qodashim (most holy/holy of holies) — the same superlative applied to the inner sanctuary's offerings (42:13). The mountain-top geography encodes graduated holiness at the landscape scale.
43.13: The altar measurements begin: veʾelleh middot hamizbeaḥ beʾammot — the altar in cubits. The base (ḥeq, literally bosom/hollow) one cubit high and one cubit wide with a rim (geshen) of a handbreadth — this is the lowest ledge of the altar. The technical term ḥeq hamizbeaḥ (the bosom of the altar) is unique here; it describes the base channel or drain of the altar that collects blood and liquids.
43.14: From the base (ḥeq haʾarets) to the lower ledge (ʾazarah haqqetanah): two cubits, one cubit wide; from the smaller ledge to the larger ledge (ʾazarah haggadolah): four cubits, one cubit wide. The stepped-ledge design of the altar — base, small ledge, large ledge, hearth — mirrors the stepped approach of the temple precincts: each step higher is more restricted, more holy.
43.15: vehaharʾel ʾarbaʿ ʾammot — the harʾel (hearth of God / altar-hearth, a hapax; possibly related to ʾariel of Isa 29:1, "hearth of God") is four cubits; from the ʾariel upward, the four horns (qarnot). The four horns of the altar are the most theologically charged feature: they are the objects to which the blood of sin offerings is applied (Exod 27:2; 29:12; Lev 4:7, 18, 25, 30) and to which one could flee for asylum (1 Kgs 1:50-51).
43.16: The harʾel (altar hearth) is twelve cubits square — ʾarbaʿ ʿasar ʾorek beʾarbaʿ ʿasar roḥav — actually fourteen cubits by fourteen, not twelve: the fourteen-by-fourteen measurement is the ʾariel upper surface. The square dimension with equal length and breadth (ʾarbaʿah tsedadim, on four sides) mirrors the Most Holy Place's 20 × 20 perfect square.
43.17: The larger ledge (ʾazarah haggadolah) fourteen cubits square — steps (maʿalot) facing east. Maʿalot (steps/ascent) from ʿalah (to go up/ascend) — the same root as the ʿolah (burnt offering, lit. that which goes up). The priest ascends by steps from the east to approach the altar top. The east-orientation of the altar steps parallels the east-orientation of the entire sanctuary.
43.18: veyyoʾmer ʾelai ben-ʾadam koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH ʾelleh ḥuqqot hamizbeaḥ — "he said to me: son of man, thus says Lord YHWH: these are the ordinances of the altar." The transition from measurement (middot) to ordinances (ḥuqqot) marks the altar section's movement from architecture to ritual. Ḥuqqot (statutes/ordinances) are divinely established ritual procedures — not arbitrary rules but the structuring principles of holy engagement.
43.19: The instructions for the altar's consecration day: give to the Levitical priests of the seed of Zadok (kizeraʿ Tsadoq) who approach YHWH a bull (par ben-baqar) as a sin offering (ḥattat). The Zadokite priesthood (bene Tsadoq) is Ezekiel's consistently preferred priestly line (40:46; 43:19; 44:15; 48:11) — the line that remained faithful when the Levites went astray. The specificity of the priestly lineage for the altar consecration is the cultic application of the general principle that holiness requires designated mediators.
43.20: The blood of the sin offering applied to the four horns (qarnot), the four corners of the ledge (ʾazarah ʾarbaʿ pinot), and the surrounding rim — vekhiparta ʿalav (and you shall make atonement for it). The verb kippar (piel: atone/purge) is the central cultic action: the altar must be atoned for before it can be used to make atonement. The seven-day altar consecration purges the altar from all defilement before regular worship begins.
43.21: The sin offering bull — velaqaḥta ʾet-happar haḥattat veserafto bamifqad habbayit miḥuts lammiqdash — burned at the appointed place outside the sanctuary (mifqad habbayit, the designated/appointed place of the house). The burning outside the sanctuary (not on the altar) follows the Levitical rule for sin offerings whose blood is brought into the holy place (Lev 4:12; 6:11; cf. Heb 13:11-13).
43.22: On the second day: a male goat without blemish (seʿir ʿizzim tamim) as a sin offering. The Levitical principle of tamim (without blemish, whole/complete) for sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3, 10; 3:1, 6; 4:3, etc.) is maintained. The goat's blood purges the altar as the bull's did: the two-day sin-offering consecration doubles the purification.
43.23: After completion of the purification — vekhaleta ḥatteʾ — a bull and a ram from the flock without blemish (pen-baqar tamim veʾayil min-hatsson tamim). The transition from sin offering (ḥattat) to burnt offering (ʿolah) marks the completion of the purification phase and the beginning of the consecration/dedication phase. The burnt offering is entirely consumed on the altar, expressing total dedication.
43.24: vehiqravtem lifnei YHWH — "and you shall present them before YHWH." The priests (kohanim) throw salt (melaḥ) on the offerings. The salt covenant (berit melaḥ, Num 18:19; Lev 2:13) — salt symbolizes incorruptibility and the endurance of the covenant relationship. Every offering is salted because every offering is covenant-ratifying.
43.25: Seven days — shivʿat yamim — a goat for a sin offering each day, and a bull and ram from the flock. The seven-day duration of the altar consecration parallels the seven-day ordination of the Aaronic priests (Lev 8:33-35) and the seven-day dedication of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:65). Seven days constitutes a complete time-unit for sacred transitions.
43.26: Seven days the priests make atonement (yekapperu) for the altar and cleanse it (vetiharu ʾoto) and consecrate it (umiluhu yado — lit. and fill its hand). The phrase millo yad (fill the hand) is the technical term for priestly ordination/installation, used throughout Exodus-Leviticus (Exod 28:41; 29:9, 29, 33, 35; Lev 8:33; etc.). Applying it to the altar is striking: the altar undergoes ordination as the priests do, because it is the primary locus of priestly service.
43.27: umimleʾot hayyamim vehaya vayom hashemiʾni vahalʾaah yaʿasu hakohanim ʾal-hamizbeaḥ — "and when the days are completed, it shall be that from the eighth day and onward the priests shall offer your burnt offerings and your peace offerings on the altar." The eighth day (yom hashemiʾni) is the day of new beginning — the day after the seven-day completion, the day of resurrection/new creation (also Lev 9:1; 12:3; the circumcision of the eighth day). Veratsti etchem — "and I will accept you." The declarative divine acceptance of the community through the altar service is the culminating point of the altar consecration: the restored sanctuary enables restored worship which enables YHWH's acceptance of his people.

37.1: haytah alai yad-YHWH vayotzieni veruach YHWH ('the hand of the LORD was on me; the Spirit of the LORD carried me out'): the inaugural vision formula (cf. 1:1; 3:14,22; 8:1) — the characteristic language of Ezekiel's visionary transport. ruach YHWH (H7307, Spirit of the LORD) — the Spirit appears 9 times in vv.1-14, bracketing the entire vision. vayenicheni betouch habiq'a ('and set me down in the middle of the valley'): the same biq'ah (H1237, valley/plain) as 3:22 — the Chebar plain. The vision is located in the same space as Ezekiel's original commissioning vision.
37.2: vehi melea atzamot ('it was full of bones'): the valley of dry bones — Israel as the dead scattered across a battlefield. vaavir oti aleichem saviv saviv ('he led me around them on all sides'): the prophetic tour of the battlefield — a complete survey of the extent of the death before the resurrection is proclaimed. The careful observation of the death prepares the prophet and the reader for the magnitude of what follows.
37.3: vayomer elai ben-adam hatichyena haatzamot haeleh ('he said to me: son of man, can these bones live?'): the central question of the vision — posed as a genuine question to the prophet, not as a rhetorical certainty. vaomar adonai YHWH atah yadatah ('I said: Lord GOD, you know'): Ezekiel's response is the perfectly calibrated prophetic humility — he does not claim to know what is outside his sight. The answer to the question 'can these bones live?' is known only by YHWH. The prophetic posture of epistemic dependence is the right posture before the divine possibility of resurrection.
37.4: vayomer elai hinabbe al-haatzamot haeleh ('he said to me: prophesy over these bones'): the command to prophesy to the dead — an act that demonstrates that prophetic speech participates in the divine creative act. The prophet speaks into non-existence and calls it into existence. shim'u devar YHWH ('hear the word of the LORD'): even the dead bones are commanded to hear — the word of YHWH has power that bypasses the apparent impossibility.
37.5: koh amar adonai YHWH laatzamot haeleh ('thus says the Lord GOD to these bones'): YHWH speaking directly to dead bones — the oracle addressed to the non-living is the most radical deployment of prophetic speech in the OT. hineh ani mebi bachem ruach vicheeytem ('I will cause breath to enter you and you will live'): ruach (H7307, breath/wind/spirit) — the same word as in Gen 2:7 where YHWH breathes into Adam's nostrils. The valley of dry bones is a second creation, recapitulating the first.
37.6: venatatti aleichem gidin vehaaleti aleichem basar ('I will put sinews on you and bring flesh on you'): the anatomical sequence — sinews (H1517, gidin), flesh (H1320, basar), skin (H5785, or). The physiological reconstitution of a human being in precise order: the structural framework (bones) → sinews (tendons binding bones) → flesh (muscle) → skin (covering). The reversal of decomposition. venatatif lachem ruach vicheeytem: the breath/spirit given last — structure without spirit is still a corpse; the order matters.
37.7: venibbeti kaasher tzuviti vatashav ruach vetiqqerenah atzamot atzem el-atzmo ('as I prophesied there was a noise, and a rattling, and the bones came together, bone to bone'): the great noise (qol, sound) and the rattling (rash, H7494, earthquake/rattling/commotion) of bones assembling — the auditory experience of resurrection. atzem el-atzmo ('bone to its bone') — each bone finding its anatomical counterpart: precise, purposeful assembly. The chaos of the battlefield is reorganized into living bodies.
37.8: veraiti vehinneh aleichem gidin uvasar alah vatiqra aleichem or ('I looked, and sinews and flesh appeared on them and skin covered them over'): the visible sequence of reconstitution — the prophet watches it happen layer by layer. veruach ayin bahem ('but there was no breath/spirit in them'): the corpses are anatomically complete but not yet living — a crucial observation. Physical reconstitution alone does not constitute life; the Spirit must be separately given. The two-stage resurrection emphasizes the independence of the Spirit's gift.
37.9: vayomer elai hinabbe el-haruach ('he said to me: prophesy to the breath'): the second prophetic command — now to the ruach itself. koh amar adonai YHWH merarba ruchot boi haruach ('thus says the Lord GOD: come from the four winds, O breath'): me-arba ruchot ('from the four winds') — north, south, east, west — the breath/spirit gathered from the entire cosmos to fill the reconstituted bodies. The cosmic gathering of the Spirit parallels the cosmic gathering of the exiles in v.21. ufchi baharugim haeleh viyichyu ('breathe into these slain that they may live'): the exact parallel to Gen 2:7 (vayipach beapav nishmat chayyim).
37.10: vatavo bahem haruach vayyichyu vayyiqumu al-ragleichem ('the breath entered them and they lived and stood on their feet'): the sequence of resurrection: breath enters → they lived (chayyah, H2421) → stood on their feet. chayil gadol meod meod ('a very, very great army'): from dead bones scattered on the battlefield to a vast living army. The hyperbolic meod meod ('exceedingly very great') emphasizes the completeness of the reversal — not merely survival but abundant vitality.
37.11: ben-adam haatzamot haeleh kol-beit yisrael hemah ('son of man, these bones are the whole house of Israel'): the divine interpretation of the vision — the bones are not a future military event but present-tense Israel in exile. hinnam omrim yaveshu atzmoteinu veaveda tikvatenu ('they are saying: our bones are dried up and our hope is perished'): the national lament: tikvah (H8615, hope/cord/expectation) — the same word as in Job 17:15 (where can my hope be found?) and Jer 29:11 (plans for a future and a hope). The exiles' despair is that hope itself has been destroyed — not merely delayed but eliminated.
37.12: lachen hinabbe veamarta aleichem koh amar adonai YHWH hineh ani potei'ach et-qivrotechem ('therefore prophesy and say to them: thus says the Lord GOD: I will open your graves'): the direct application of the vision to the national situation — YHWH will open graves and bring out (alah, H5927, cause to come up) the people from their graves. qever (H6913, grave/tomb) — exile as a kind of death-state. The return from exile is analogized to resurrection from the grave. vahaveti etchem el-admat yisrael: the land of Israel as the destination of the resurrection.
37.13: veyidattem ki-ani YHWH befotchi et-qivrotechem ('you will know that I am the LORD when I open your graves'): the recognition formula attached to the resurrection act — the knowledge of YHWH is the purpose of the resurrection. The resurrection event is epistemological: it teaches Israel who YHWH is in a way that nothing else could. uvehaaloti etchem miqivrotechem ami ('and when I bring you up from your graves, my people'): the covenant designation ami ('my people') — restored at the moment of resurrection. The resurrection constitutes the covenantal relationship.
37.14: venatatti ruchi bachem vicheeytem ('I will put my Spirit in you and you will live'): the synthesis of ch 36:27 and the valley vision — the Spirit given internally (36:27) is the breath that animates the dry bones (37:5-10). The two oracles are the same promise from different angles: the outward vision (dry bones to living army) and the inward promise (new heart, Spirit within). vehinnachti etchem al-admatechem ('I will put you down on your own land'): the final and ultimate settling — not temporary residence but permanent establishment (nuach, H5117, rest/settle permanently).
37.15: The new unit: two-sticks oracle (vv.15-28). The visual demonstration is different from the valley vision: instead of a landscape transformation, it is a hand-held enacted sign. Ezekiel holds two sticks, one for Judah and one for Ephraim/Joseph, and the two become one in his hand.
37.16: et le-Yehudah ('for Judah'): etz (H6086, tree/stick/wood) — a stick of writing material (a writing tablet or rod). ulveit Yisrael chaveirav ('for the house of Israel his companions') — the northern kingdom's associated tribes. veet-et Yosef asher beyad Efrayim ('the stick of Joseph which is in Ephraim's hand'): the dominant northern tribe gives its name to the Northern Kingdom. The two sticks represent the 931 BCE division of the united monarchy (1 Kgs 12) which had become permanent national separation.
37.17: veqarev otam echad el-echad lecha leetz echad vehayu leachad beyadechu ('join them together into one stick, and they will become one in your hand'): the enacted prophecy — the physical joining of two sticks in the prophet's hand demonstrates the political reunification of the divided kingdom. leetz echad ('to become one stick') — the oneness is specified: not merely cooperation but unity. The acted sign communicates what Israel's political history said was impossible.
37.18: The disciples' question: 'Will you not tell us what you mean by these?' — the acted prophecy requires interpretation, and the disciples recognize its interpretive demand. Ezekiel's sign-acts consistently create interpretive questions that provide the occasion for the divine explanation (cf. 12:9; 24:19). The question is part of the pedagogical design.
37.19: hineni loqei'ach et-etz Yosef asher beyad Efrayim ('I am taking the stick of Joseph which is in Ephraim's hand'): YHWH's own description of the sign-act, identifying with what the prophet performs. vesamti otam alav et-etz Yehudah vaasitim leetz echad ('and I will put them together — the stick of Judah — and they will become one stick'): the political reunification is explicitly YHWH's action. The political possibility of the sign is grounded in divine sovereign ability, not human political process.
37.20: The sticks held visibly in the prophet's hand — the public enactment of the oracle. The acted prophecy is communal: the sign is performed before the eyes of the exilic community, making the promise concrete and memorable. Even in Babylon, the exiles are watching a visual demonstration of their promised future.
37.21: velaqachti et-benei yisrael mibbein haggoyim asher halchu sham ('I will take the Israelites from among the nations where they have gone'): the gathering formula — laqach (take/gather) from among the nations. The second Exodus pattern: just as YHWH took Israel out of Egypt, he will take the scattered exiles from among the nations. veqibatzti otam missaviv ('I will gather them from all around'): the comprehensive gathering of diaspora from every direction.
37.22: vaasiti otam legoy echad baaretz beharei yisrael ulemelech echad yihyeh lahem lemelech lekulam ('I will make them one nation in the land on the mountains of Israel, and one king will be king over them all'): the political outcome of the restoration — goy echad (one nation) with melech echad (one king). The division of the Solomonic monarchy is permanently reversed. velo yihu od lishnei goyim ('they will no longer be two nations'): the permanent end of the north-south division. The two-kingdoms situation, which had persisted for four centuries, will not recur.
37.23: velo yitamme'u od begilluleihem ('they will no longer defile themselves with their idols'): the spiritual component of the restoration — freedom from idolatry. vehoshi'a otam miqqol moshevoteihem asher chatu bahem ('I will save them from all their apostasies'): yasha (H3467, save) — salvation from the specific sin of apostasy, not merely from external enemies. vetihartem vehayu li le'am ('I will cleanse them and they will be my people'): the covenant formula applied to the spiritually restored community.
37.24: veabdi David melech aleichem ('my servant David will be king over them'): the Davidic covenant renewed — the promised Davidic king who will shepherd the unified nation. David here is not the historical David (who died c.970 BCE) but the eschatological Davidic descendant, the anointed king promised in 2 Sam 7 and Ps 2; 89; 110. veroeh echad yihyeh lechulam ('one shepherd will be for all of them'): the shepherd-king of ch 34 is the Davidic descendant. uvechuqqotai yelekhu ('they will walk in my statutes'): the Spirit-enabled Torah-observance of 36:27 finds its political correlate in the Davidic ruler.
37.25: veyashvu al-haaretz asher natati leabdi leyaaqov asher yashvu vah avoteichem ('they will live in the land I gave to my servant Jacob — the land where your fathers lived'): the patriarchal land-grant renewed through Davidic-covenant language. The triple covenant connection: the land given to Jacob (patriarchal), the king from David (Davidic), and the Spirit-given Torah obedience (new covenant). venasi lahem le'olam ('a prince will be over them forever'): nasi le'olam — the eternal prince. The Davidic ruler's permanence answers the revolving-door kingship of Israel's historical kings.
37.26: vekarati lahem berit shalom ('I will make a covenant of peace with them'): berit shalom (H1285 + H7965, covenant of peace) — the fullest form of the new covenant promise (also Num 25:12; Isa 54:10). berit olam yihyeh otam ('it will be an eternal covenant with them'): berit olam — the permanent covenant that cannot be broken. The covenant of peace combines political security, agricultural abundance, and divine presence. untetim vehirbeitem venatatiti et-miqdashi betocham le'olam ('I will increase them and put my sanctuary in their midst forever'): the sanctuary — YHWH's permanent dwelling in the midst of the restored unified nation.
37.27: vehayah mishkani aleichem ('my dwelling will be over them'): mishkan (H4908, dwelling/tabernacle) — the tabernacle term, the mobile sanctuary of the wilderness period now made permanent. vehayiti lahem le'elohim vehem yihyu li le'am ('I will be their God and they will be my people'): the covenant formula for the last time in ch 37. The covenant formula appears at the end of the restoration section as the ultimate statement of what YHWH's restoration accomplishes: the covenant relationship fully reconstituted.
37.28: veyadu haggoyim ki ani YHWH meqaddesh et-yisrael ('the nations will know that I am the LORD who sanctifies Israel'): the recognition formula in the chapter's closing verse — but with a new epithet added: meqaddesh et-yisrael ('the one who sanctifies Israel'). YHWH's sanctifying of Israel is the means of his self-disclosure to the nations. The nations learn who YHWH is by watching what he does to Israel: restores, reunifies, gives a Davidic king, places his sanctuary, sanctifies. bihyot miqdashi betocham le'olam ('when my sanctuary is in their midst forever'): the sanctuary's permanent presence is the sign that the nations read.

38.1: The Gog oracle opens with the standard word-event formula — and then addresses the most mysterious figure in Ezekiel's prophecy. The oracle's placement after chs 36-37 (restoration promises) and before ch 40-48 (the temple vision) situates it as the climactic threat before the final rest: the restored Israel will face a last assault before the permanent sanctuary is established.
38.2: ben-adam sim panecha el-Gog eretz Magog nesi rosh Meshech veTuval ('son of man, set your face toward Gog, the land of Magog, the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal'): Gog (H1463) — the personal name of the eschatological adversary; Magog — the land/people (Gen 10:2, son of Japheth). The genealogical connection to Japheth's descendants locates Gog among the northern peoples. nesi rosh ('chief prince' or 'prince of Rosh') — the title that identifies Gog as the paramount political-military ruler of the northern peoples. Meshech and Tubal reappear from 27:13 and 32:26 (peoples associated with Asia Minor).
38.3: hineni elecha Gog ('I am against you, Gog'): the divine confrontation formula — only YHWH can address the eschatological adversary in the second person. The same formula used against Pharaoh (29:3; 30:22) and the other OAN figures is here directed at the mysterious Gog. The hineni elecha ('I am against you') is the most direct expression of divine opposition in Ezekiel's vocabulary.
38.4: veshavti otach venatethi chchachim bilichyeycha ('I will turn you around and put hooks in your jaws'): the hooks-in-jaws image again — used for Pharaoh in 29:4. YHWH driving the great adversary into position is the theological claim that the Gog invasion is itself part of the divine plan. vetzotzeti otach ('I will lead you out') — YHWH as the one who brings Gog against Israel, making the attack itself the vehicle of divine judgment. vechal-cheylecha susim uphashim ('all your army, horses and horsemen'): the great military coalition Gog assembles.
38.5: The coalition: Paras (Persia/Iran), Cush (Ethiopia), Put (Libya/North Africa). The same African coalition as appeared in the Egypt oracles (30:5) now assembled under Gog. The eschatological army draws on the same imperial geography as Ezekiel's historical oracles — all the great powers of the known world assembled against restored Israel.
38.6: Gomer and Beit-Togarma from the northern quarters — the peoples from the far north. Gomer (H1586, Cimmerians in Greek; Gen 10:2-3, son of Japheth); Beth-Togarmah (north of the Euphrates, probably Armenia). The comprehensive directional coverage: south (Persia, Cush, Put) and north (Gomer, Beth-Togarmah) — a global coalition against the tiny restored Israel.
38.7: hikkon vehachen lecha atah vekhol-qahalcha haniqhalim alecha ('be prepared and ready — you and all the company assembled around you'): YHWH addressing Gog as if giving military orders — the ironic military briefing of the adversary whom YHWH is actually positioning for destruction. vehayta lahem lemishmar ('be their guard'): Gog as the guardian/commander of the coalition assembled against Israel.
38.8: meyamim rabbim tipaqed ('after many days you will be called up'): paqad (H6485, attend to/visit/muster — often used for divine inspection or military muster). beacharit hashanim ('in the latter years'): acharit hashanim (H319 + H8141, end of years/latter years) — the eschatological time-marker. tavo el-aretz meshovevet mecherevin niqubbetzet al-harei yisrael ('you will come against a land restored from the sword, gathered from many peoples, on the mountains of Israel'): the attack is explicitly against the restored Israel of chs 36-37 — the returned exiles in their homeland are the target of the eschatological assault.
38.9: vaalita kashoah tavo ('you will come up like a storm'): shoah (H7722, devastating storm/catastrophe/tempest) — the overwhelming force of the eschatological assault. keanav lechassot haaretz tihyeh atah vekhol-agafecha ('like a cloud covering the land you and all your troops'): the storm-cloud imagery for a vast army covering the land. The darkness of the cloud-army against the land of restored Israel is the visible manifestation of the eschatological threat.
38.10: veaalah devarim al-levavecha vechashabta machashevet raah ('thoughts will arise in your mind and you will devise an evil plan'): the interior motivation of Gog is exposed — evil machashevet (H4284, thoughts/devices/plans). The evil plan arises from Gog's own heart, yet it is simultaneously within YHWH's sovereign orchestration (v.4: YHWH puts hooks in Gog's jaws). The dual agency of evil instruments serving divine purposes is sustained throughout these oracles.
38.11: veamarta e'eleh el-eretz perazot ('I will go up against the land of unwalled villages'): perazot (H6519, unwalled/open settlements) — Israel dwelling in security without walls or bars or gates. The vulnerability of the restored Israel is its openness: a people dwelling in safety, no walls, no defenses. The attack on the open villages is the attack on trust in YHWH's protection rather than in military fortification.
38.12: livzoz baz ulishlol shalal ('to plunder and loot'): the double alliteration in Hebrew — baz (H957, plunder) + shalal (H7998, spoil/booty). The economic motivation of the attack: the accumulated wealth of the restored Israel is the target. al-am mesuphaf mitoch haaratzot ('on a people gathered from the nations'): the exiles-returned, now wealthy from agricultural restoration (36:29-30), are the economic target. qinyanim veqonyeh ('goods and possessions') — the tangible prosperity of the covenant restoration becomes the bait that draws Gog.
38.13: The trading nations — Sheba, Dedan, merchants of Tarshish — question whether Gog is coming to plunder (showing commercial awareness of the attack). These are the same trading nations of 27:20-22 (Ezekiel's Tyre oracle). Their question is not opposition but commercial interest: halasheset baz attah ba? ('have you come to plunder?'). The merchants of the world are watching the eschatological assault with commercial calculation rather than moral horror.
38.14: lachen hinabbe ben-adam veamarta leGog ('therefore prophesy, son of man, and say to Gog'): Ezekiel is commissioned to prophesy directly to Gog — the eschatological adversary as the object of prophetic address, just as historical nations received the OAN oracles. beyom shevet ami yisrael lavetach halo teda ('on the day my people Israel dwell in safety, will you not take notice?'): YHWH asking Gog rhetorically — the secure settlement of Israel is both the trigger for Gog's attack and the sign of YHWH's restoration.
38.15: ubata mimqomcha miyrektei tzaphon ('you will come from your place out of the remote parts of the north'): yarktei tzaphon (H3411 + H6828, remote parts/recesses of the north) — the north as the direction of mythic threat (cf. Jer 1:14 'out of the north evil will break out'; Isa 14:13 'mountain of assembly in the far north'). The cosmic north as the direction of the final assault. ammim rabbim itach ('many peoples with you'): the vast multinational coalition of vv.5-6.
38.16: vaalita al-ami yisrael ke'anan lechassot haaretz ('you will go up against my people Israel like a cloud covering the land'): the cloud imagery of v.9 repeated — the storm-cloud assault against restored Israel. beacharit hayamim yihyeh ('in the latter days it will be'): the eschatological time-marker (acharit hayamim, H319 + H3117) — the time when the present age reaches its completion. veheveti otcha al-artzi ('I will bring you against my land'): YHWH explicitly orchestrating Gog's attack against his own land — the assault serves YHWH's purpose of displaying his holiness to the nations. lemaan deathi haggoyim bi lehiqqdashi vecha Gog leeyneichem ('so that the nations may know me when I show myself holy through you, Gog').
38.17: hata attah hu asher dibberi biyamim qadmonim beyad avadai neviiei yisrael ('are you the one about whom I spoke in earlier days through my servants the prophets of Israel?'): YHWH's self-referential query — connecting the Gog oracle to earlier prophetic traditions (Isa 14:25-27; Jer 4:5-13; Zeph 3:8). The eschatological adversary was anticipated by earlier prophecy; Ezekiel's oracle is the explicit fulfillment of those earlier references. This verse places the Gog material within the canonical tradition of eschatological judgment.
38.18: vehayah bayyom hahu beyyom bo Gog al-admat yisrael ('on that day, when Gog comes against the land of Israel'): bayyom hahu — the eschatological 'on that day' formula. yaale chamati vaappi ('my wrath will flare up'): chemah (H2534, wrath/heat/fury) + ap (H639, nose/anger) — the classic pair of divine anger vocabulary. beqin'ati vebe'esh evrati ('in my jealousy and in the fire of my wrath'): three anger words — qin'ah (jealousy/zeal), chemah (wrath), evrah (H5678, rage/fury) — piled up to convey the intensity of the divine response to the final assault on restored Israel.
38.19: vebiqqin'ati vebe'esh evrati dibbari ('in my jealousy and blazing wrath I declare'): the formal declaration of the eschatological judgment. im-lo bayyom hahu yihyeh ra'ash gadol al-admat yisrael ('there will be a great earthquake in the land of Israel on that day'): ra'ash (H7494, earthquake/shaking/commotion) — the cosmic shaking that accompanies YHWH's intervention against Gog. The earthquake is not merely geological but theophanic — YHWH's physical presence disturbing the created order.
38.20: vera'ashu mipenai degei hayam veoph hashamayim vechayat hassadeh ('the fish of the sea, the birds of the sky, and the beasts of the field will tremble before me'): the cosmic trembling extends to the entire animal kingdom — the earthquake disrupts the entire created order. vekhol-adam asher al-penei haaretz ('and all the people on the face of the earth'): universal human trembling at the theophany. veniharsu heharim venaphelu hamaqdshot ('the mountains will be thrown down and the cliffs will fall'): the topographical disruption — the mountains of Israel that received the restoration promises now shake at the divine self-disclosure.
38.21: veqarati alav lekol-harai cherev ('I will call for a sword against him throughout all my mountains'): YHWH summoning the sword throughout his own land — the mountains of Israel where Gog's forces are deployed become the site of their destruction. cherev ish beachiv tihyeh ('every man's sword will be against his brother'): the coalition's internal collapse through friendly fire — the multinational army turns on itself, the confusion of the divine judgment causing fratricide among the allied forces.
38.22: venishpatti itto bedever uvdad vegeshem shotef veavaney elgavish ('I will execute judgment on him with pestilence and bloodshed; I will rain down torrents of rain, hailstones, fire and brimstone'): the five instruments of divine judgment against Gog — pestilence, blood, rain, hail, fire and brimstone. The last two (fire and brimstone) recall Sodom's destruction (Gen 19:24) — the primordial judgment city-destroying event applied to the eschatological adversary. elgavish (H68 + H1378, brimstone/sulfur hailstones) — the specific word for the sulfurous hail of divine judgment.
38.23: vehitgaddilti vehitqaddishti venodaati le-eynei goyim rabbim ('I will show my greatness and my holiness and make myself known in the eyes of many nations'): the recognition formula at maximum scope — not just one nation but goyim rabbim (many nations) will know YHWH through the Gog judgment. veyadu ki-ani YHWH: the Ezekielian recognition formula closes the chapter, as it closes every major unit. The entire Gog oracle, like all of Ezekiel's oracles, serves this one epistemological purpose: YHWH's self-disclosure.

44.1: The visionary guide brings Ezekiel to the outer east gate of the sanctuary. This is the same east gate through which the glory of YHWH departed (10:19; 11:23) and returned (43:1-4). The gate's future status is now revealed — it will remain sealed permanently, its closing serving as a permanent memorial to the divine entry.
44.2: sagar yihyeh lo yippatach ('it shall be shut; it shall not be opened'): the absolute permanence of the closure established through doubled negative construction. Ve'ish lo-yavo vo ki YHWH Elohei Yisrael ba bo ('and no one shall enter by it, because YHWH the God of Israel entered by it'): the gate sealed by divine entry is thereby permanently reserved for YHWH. The logic is that of holiness by contact — the gateway touched by the divine presence becomes permanently sacred and off-limits to ordinary use.
44.3: hanasi hanasi hu yeshev-bo ('the prince, the prince — he shall sit in it'): the emphatic double title. Nasi (prince) — Ezekiel's deliberate choice throughout chapters 40-48 to avoid melek (king). The kings had profaned YHWH's sanctuary (43:7-9); in the restored order the human leader has a different title and a different relationship to the sacred space. The prince may enter by the vestibule and eat a covenantal meal in the gateway — a unique privilege marking his mediating role.
44.4: vayavi'eni derekh sha'ar hatzafon el-pnei habayit ('and he brought me by the way of the north gate to the front of the house'): Ezekiel is repositioned for a new view. va'ereh vehinneh male kavod-YHWH et-beit YHWH ('I looked and behold, the glory of YHWH filled the house of YHWH'): the kavod (glory) that departed in chapters 10-11 is now fully resident in the restored temple. Ezekiel's face-down response mirrors his response to the initial glory vision (1:28; 3:23).
44.5: sim libecha ('set your heart/mind'): sim lev (to direct attention, take note) — the same idiom used in 40:4 at the beginning of the temple tour. YHWH instructs Ezekiel to pay careful attention to all the ordinances and laws of the house — both the exits and entrances (kol-motza'ei hammiqdash) and all the regulations (kol-Torah hammiqdash). The emphasis on careful observation prepares for the detailed priestly legislation that follows.
44.6: ve'amarta el-meri el-beit Yisrael ('and say to the rebellious house of Israel'): the oracle is directed to the rebellious house — the term meri (rebellious) was Ezekiel's epithet for Israel from the beginning (2:3, 5-8; 3:26-27). Even in the context of the restoration vision, the audience is reminded of the identity that made the departure of the glory necessary. The restoration is not earned but gracious.
44.7: behavi'achem bnei-neichar arelei-lev va'aralei basar ('when you brought foreigners, uncircumcised of heart and uncircumcised of flesh'): the double uncircumcision — of heart and body. Ben-neichar (foreigner) — specifically a foreign national, not the ger (resident alien) who could be incorporated into the covenant. Aral-lev (uncircumcised of heart): the spiritual-moral category; aral-basar (uncircumcised of flesh): the physical-covenant mark. Deuteronomy 10:16 calls for heart-circumcision alongside physical circumcision; Ezekiel requires both for sanctuary access.
44.8: velo shemartem mishmeret qodoshai ('and you have not kept the charge of my holy things'): shameret (charge/keeping/guard-duty) — the specific custodial responsibility of sanctuary service. Israel appointed others to perform the duties they should have done themselves. The specific failure named: outsiders in the sanctuary. The result is the profaning of YHWH's covenant — lehafar et-briti.
44.9: koh amar Adonai YHWH kol-ben-neichar aral-lev va'aral-basar lo-yavo el-miqdashi ('thus says the Lord YHWH: no foreigner, uncircumcised of heart and uncircumcised of flesh, shall enter my sanctuary'): the absolute exclusion rule for the new temple. The doubling of the condition (heart AND flesh) distinguishes the foreigner from those who might have physical circumcision without covenantal allegiance, or covenantal allegiance without the covenant sign.
44.10: ki im-haleviyim asher-rachaqu me'alai ('but the Levites who went far from me'): the Levites who participated in Israel's apostasy — who served at the gillulim (idols) when Israel went astray — receive a demoted status in the restored order. Venasa'u avontam ('they will bear their iniquity'): their punishment is the loss of priestly status. They retain temple service but not altar ministry — a permanent consequence of their historical infidelity.
44.11: meshartim babayit ('serving in the house'): the Levites' reduced role — gatekeepers (she'arim hasha'ar), temple attendants (meshartim et-habayit), slaughtering the burnt offering and sacrifice for the people. They serve the people but not directly at the altar of YHWH. Hem ya'amdu lifneihem leshartam ('they shall stand before them to serve them'): the preposition lifneihem (before them = the people) rather than lifnei YHWH (before YHWH) marks the demotion.
44.12: ya'an asher sher'tu otam lifnei gilluleihem ('because they served them before their idols'): the gillulim again — the contemptuous dung-word for idols. The Levites ministered to Israel in the idolatrous context, facilitating rather than opposing the apostasy. Vehayah leVet Yisrael lemikshol avon ('and became a stumbling block of iniquity to the house of Israel'): the Levites who were supposed to prevent stumbling became the stumbling block.
44.13: velo yigshu elai lechahanen li ('and they shall not draw near to me to minister as priests to me'): the nagash (draw near) vocabulary of priestly approach is denied to the apostate Levites. Likol-qodshai ('to any of my holy things'): exclusion from the entire realm of direct sacred ministry. They retain some temple function but not the constitutive function — the approach to YHWH in priestly mediation. Venasu klimatam uveto'avotam asher asu ('they shall bear their shame and their abominations which they committed').
44.14: unatati otam shomrei mishmeret habayit ('and I will make them keepers of the charge of the house'): the specific role assigned to the demoted Levites — custodial maintenance. Lekhol-avodat ('for all the service'): the full range of support and maintenance tasks, but not the altar service. The Levitical demotion is not expulsion but reassignment — they remain in the covenant community but with different responsibilities that reflect the permanent mark of their historical infidelity.
44.15: uhakohanim haleviyim bnei Tzadok ('but the priests, the Levites, the sons of Zadok'): the contrast clause — asher shamru et-mishmeret miqdashi ('who kept the charge of my sanctuary'). The sons of Zadok maintained their priestly faithfulness during Israel's apostasy. Hem yiqrevu elai lesharteni ('they shall draw near to me to serve me'): the same nagash (draw near) that was denied to the apostate Levites is now granted to the faithful line. Access to the divine presence is the mark and reward of fidelity.
44.16: hem yavo'u el-miqdashi ('they shall enter my sanctuary'): the sons of Zadok as the full priests of the restored temple. Ve'hemah yiqrevu el-shulchani lesharteni ('and they shall approach my table to serve me'): the 'table of YHWH' — the altar as dining table. The metaphor of divine hospitality expressed in the altar-table imagery appears in Ezekiel 41:22 and 44:16; YHWH as host, the priests as servants of the table, Israel as the guests. Veshemru et-mishmarti ('they shall keep my charge').
44.17: uvevo'am el-sha'are hechatzer hapnimit bigdei pishtim yilbashu ('when they enter the gates of the inner court, linen garments they shall wear'): pishtim (linen/flax) — the priestly material prescribed since Exodus 28:39-42. Wool is explicitly forbidden in v.17b: lo ya'aleh alehem tzemer ('wool shall not come up on them while serving'). The reason given in v.18 — 'so that they do not sweat' — reflects the purity concern about bodily secretion in the sacred space.
44.18: pea'rei pishtim yihyu al-rosham ('linen headcoverings shall be on their heads'): pe'er (turban/headcovering) — the priestly head covering. Umiknesei-pishtim yihyu al-motneihem ('linen breeches/undergarments shall be on their waists'): complete linen from head to waist. Lo yachgeru bayya' ('they shall not gird themselves with anything causing sweat'): the bodily integrity requirement — the priestly body in sacred service must not produce the secretion associated with labor and physical exertion.
44.19: ubetzetam el-hechatzer hachitzonah ('when they go out to the outer court'): the transition from holy to common space requires a change of garments. Ufishtenu bigdeihem asher-yeshartam bam ('they shall put off the linen garments in which they have been serving'): two sets of garments — one for the inner court (holy), one for the outer court (common). Levelo-yequaddesh et-ha'am bevigdeihem ('lest they consecrate/sanctify the people with their garments'): holiness is contagious — the sacred garments can transfer sanctity to ordinary people who contact them, requiring a buffer between the holy and common realms.
44.20: verosham lo yegale'chu ('and their heads they shall not shave'): the prohibition on priestly shaving of the head (cf. Lev 21:5). Vefara lo yeshallachu ('and they shall not let their hair grow wild/loose'): the priest must not adopt the unkempt hair of the mourner or the Nazirite-like long growth. Kasom yiksemu et-ra'sheihem ('trimming they shall trim their heads'): the middle path — neither shaved nor uncontrolled, regularly trimmed. The priest's body itself is regulated as part of sacred service.
44.21: veyayin lo yishteh kol-kohen bevo'am el-hechatzer hapnimit ('and no priest shall drink wine when they enter the inner court'): the alcohol prohibition in sacred service from Leviticus 10:9 (where the context is Nadab and Abihu's disastrous unauthorized fire — perhaps connected to intoxication). The inner court is the highest zone of sacred proximity; full cognitive clarity is required for service there.
44.22: ve'almana ugershusha lo-yiqchu lahem lenashim ('and a widow or a divorcée they shall not take as wives for themselves'): the stricter priestly marriage code. Ki im-betulot mizera beit Yisrael ('but only virgins from the seed of the house of Israel'): and widows of priests (ve'ha'almana asher tihyeh almana mikohen yiqachu). The high priest's restriction from Leviticus 21:14 is applied more broadly to all priests in the new temple order — the priestly family's purity reflecting the sanctuary's higher holiness.
44.23: ve'et-ami yoruu bein qodesh lechol ('and my people they shall teach between holy and common'): the core priestly teaching function — havdalah (distinction, separation), the ability to distinguish and communicate the difference between sacred and ordinary. Uvein-tame utahor yori'um ('and between clean and unclean they shall teach them'): the full range of purity distinctions that structure Levitical religion. The priests as teachers of the sacred/common and clean/unclean distinctions is the foundational purpose of the priestly class (cf. Lev 10:10-11; Deut 33:10; Mal 2:7).
44.24: val-riv hemah ya'amdu lishpot ('and in a dispute they shall stand to judge'): the priests as civil judges in cases of dispute. Bemishpatai yishpetahu ('according to my ordinances they shall judge'): YHWH's Torah as the legal code for the priestly court. Ve'et-torotai vechukotai bechol-mo'adai yishmoru ('and my instruction and statutes at all my festivals they shall observe'): the priestly role of legal, liturgical, and educational mediation fully described.
44.25: ve'el-met adam lo yavo litame' ('and for a dead person he shall not come to be defiled'): corpse impurity — the most severe level of uncleanness in Levitical law (Num 19). Only for the closest relatives — father, mother, son, daughter, brother, or unmarried sister — may a priest contract corpse impurity. Ve'im-lev lav yitamma''u ('but for these they may defile themselves'): the family exception to the general priestly prohibition on corpse contact.
44.26: ve'achar taharo shiv'at yamim yisperu-lo ('and after his purification, seven days they shall count for him'): the seven-day purification period after corpse defilement (Num 19:11-14). The counting begins the purification process — time as the healing mechanism within the purity system. Veyom tiharto ('on the day of his cleansing'): a fixed point after which priestly service can resume.
44.27: uveyom bo'o el-haQodesh ('and on the day he enters the holy place'): the reintegration offering. El-hechatzer hapnimit lesharet baQodesh ('to the inner court to minister in the holy place'): on the day the purified priest returns to full duty, he must offer a chatat (sin offering). The purification process is not merely the passage of time but requires a priestly sacrifice to restore full sacred access.
44.28: uhayetah lahem lenachala ani nachalatam ('and it shall be their inheritance — I am their inheritance'): the most theologically charged verse in the priestly legislation. Ani nachalatam ('I am their inheritance') echoes Numbers 18:20 ('I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites') and the Levitical principle from Joshua 13:14 that YHWH himself is the Levitical inheritance. Ve'achuzah lo-titenu lahem beYisrael ('and possession you shall not give them among Israel'): the priests receive no land — YHWH is their land.
44.29: hamincha vehacha'tat veha'asham hemah yocheluhu ('the grain offering, the sin offering, and the guilt offering — they shall eat them'): the priestly portion from the sacrificial system. The three offerings eaten by priests at the sanctuary represent YHWH's provision for their physical needs. Vechol-cherem beYisrael lahem yihyeh ('and every dedicated/devoted thing in Israel shall belong to them'): cherem (dedicated thing) — items vowed or devoted to YHWH become priestly property.
44.30: vere'shit kol-bikkurei kol ukhol-terumah mikol ('and the best of all the first-fruits of all, and every offering of all'): the first-fruits (bikkurim) and contributions (terumah) belong to the priests. Re'shit arissoteichem titenu lakohen ('the first portion of your dough you shall give to the priest'): even the dough-offering (arisah = dough, perhaps the first bread from the millstone) is priestly. Lehaniach berakhah el-beitecha ('to bring blessing upon your house'): the connection between giving first-fruits and receiving blessing — the economic order of worship sustains both priests and worshippers.
44.31: kol-nevela uterefah min-ha'of umin-habehemah lo yochlu hakohanim ('all carcass or torn animal — whether bird or beast — the priests shall not eat'): the standard Levitical prohibition on eating animals that die naturally or are killed by predators (Lev 22:8). The priests must not eat from death; their food must come through proper slaughter. This closes the priestly code of chapter 44 — the priest's complete separation from the unclean, the common, and death itself, sustained by YHWH's own provision as their inheritance.

45.1: uvehapilchem et-ha'aretz benachala tarimu terumah laYHWH ('and when you divide the land by lot as an inheritance, you shall set apart an offering for YHWH'): terumah (offering/contribution/portion set apart) — literally 'that which is lifted up' (rum, to rise). The sacred district is conceptually 'lifted' from the surrounding land as the part dedicated to YHWH. Qodesh hi ('it is holy'): the designation of the entire district as sacred space, not merely the inner sanctuary.
45.2: yihyeh min-zeh lamiqdash ('this shall be for the sanctuary'): the precise allocation of the sacred area. Chamesh me'ot b'chamesh me'ot ravia' ('five hundred by five hundred — a square'): the perfect square of the inner sanctuary precinct, already noted in 40:47. Vachamishim amah migrash lo saviv ('and fifty cubits of open space around it'): a buffer zone of unoccupied land surrounding the sanctuary compound — both for visual separation and practical protective distance.
45.3: The measured sacred district contains both the sanctuary and the priestly quarters (lishkat hakohanim). The sanctuary is embedded within the priestly territory — the priests literally dwell around their liturgical center. The spatial arrangement makes theological sense: those who serve the sanctuary should live in proximity to it, physically expressing the covenant of sacred service.
45.4: qodesh hu min-ha'aretz ('it is holy — from/of the land'): the land itself is categorized as holy by allocation. Likohanim mesharte hammiqdash ('for the priests who minister at the sanctuary'): the allocation is functional — the land serves those who serve the house. Vemakom levatehem ('and a place for their houses'): the priests' domestic life is embedded within the sacred district — no separation between priestly home and priestly work.
45.5: vachamisha esrim elef orech vachamisha asar elef rochav ('twenty-five thousand long and fifteen thousand wide'): the Levitical portion, somewhat smaller than the priestly portion. Laleviyim meshartei habayit ('to the Levites, the servants of the house'): the Levites' district adjacent to the priestly district — the same spatial theology: those who serve the sanctuary live near it. Achuzzat arim lahem ('possession of cities for them'): the Levitical cities tradition from Numbers 35 continued in the new order.
45.6: va'achuzzat ha'ir titenu ('and the possession of the city you shall give'): the city district — distinct from both the priestly/Levitical sacred section and the prince's section. Chamesh elef rochav vachamisha ve'esrim elef orech: the city's dimensions. Kol beit Yisrael ('for all the house of Israel'): the city is communal property belonging to the whole covenant community, not to a tribe or the priestly class. The egalitarian city-portion is a structural covenant provision for all Israel.
45.7: velanasi mize umize lterumah haQodesh ulachuzzat ha'ir ('and for the prince — on both sides of the sacred offering and the city property'): the prince's land flanks the sacred and city districts on both sides (east and west). The spatial arrangement is theologically significant: the prince does not own the sacred center but flanks it as its guardian. Umefaat yam yama umipaat qedma qedma ('from the western border to the sea, and from the eastern border to the east'): the prince's territory extends to the actual borders of the land.
45.8: ba'aretz yihyeh-lo achuzzah beYisrael ('in the land he shall have as property in Israel'): the prince is guaranteed a specific territorial portion — he is not to be landless like the priests (44:28). Velo-yonu od nasai et-ami ('and my princes shall no longer oppress my people'): the specific prohibition on the kind of land-confiscation that characterized the Israelite monarchy (1 Kgs 21, Naboth's vineyard; cf. Isa 5:8; Mic 2:2). Ve'et-ha'aretz yitenu leBeit Yisrael leshivteihem ('and the land they shall give to the house of Israel according to their tribes').
45.9: rav-lachem nesi'ei Yisrael ('enough, O princes of Israel!'): a direct, urgent address — rav-lachem is the strong rebuke used by Moses to the Levites in Numbers 16:3. Chamas va'shod hasiru ('remove violence and oppression!'): the same charge against the rulers from 22:27 and 34:2-4. Even within the restoration vision, the priestly legislation addresses the princes with the covenant demand for justice. Ve'asitem mishpat utzedaqah ('and do justice and righteousness'): the standard prophetic doublet for comprehensive covenant faithfulness.
45.10: mozenei tzedeq ve'efat tzedeq uvat tzedeq yihyeh lachem ('honest scales and an honest ephah and an honest bath shall be yours'): the three instruments of honest commerce: moznayim (scales, for weighing), ephah (dry measure), and bat (liquid measure). Tzedeq (honest/just) — the adjective that attaches to all three: the covenant requirement for commercial integrity drawn directly from Leviticus 19:35-36.
45.11: ha'efah ve'habat tochen echad yihyeh lahem ('the ephah and the bath — one standard they shall have'): the standardization of the dry and liquid measures at the same volume. Liset ma'aser ha'chomer ('to contain a tenth of a homer'): the homer (chomer, a donkey-load) as the base unit, with the ephah and bath each being a tenth of it. The standardization eliminates the possibility of using different measures for buying and selling — a common form of ancient economic fraud.
45.12: vehasheqel esrim gerah ('and the shekel — twenty gerahs'): the standard shekel weight defined. Esrim sheqalim chamisha ve'esrim sheqalim asarah vacha'mishah sheqalim hamaneh yihyeh lachem ('twenty shekels, twenty-five shekels, fifteen shekels — the maneh shall be yours'): the maneh = 60 shekels total (20+25+15). The standardization of weights is a covenant-justice requirement: dishonest weights are explicitly condemned in Leviticus 19:35-36 and Proverbs 11:1; 16:11; 20:23.
45.13: zot haterumah asher tarimu ('this is the offering that you shall contribute'): the specified contribution rates. Shishit ha'eifah mikhomer hachittim ('a sixth of an ephah from a homer of wheat'): one-sixtieth — a tithe on a tithe. The mathematical precision of the sacred contributions reflects the careful accounting of covenant obligations. The people's contribution to the prince funds the public worship that the prince is obligated to provide for the whole community.
45.14: vechoq hashemen habat hashemen ('and the ordained amount of oil — the bath of oil'): the oil contribution rates. Ma'aser habat min-hakkor ('a tenth of a bath from the kor'): the kor = 10 baths, so the tithe is one bath per kor. The careful layering of measurements (kor, bath, homer, ephah) establishes a consistent system of sacred contributions based on standardized measure.
45.15: vesheh-achat min-hatzon ('and one sheep from the flock'): the animal contribution — one sheep per two hundred from the watered pastures of Israel. Leminchah ulelolah ulshelamim ('for grain offering and burnt offering and peace offering'): the three primary categories of sacrifice funded by these contributions. Lechapper alehem ('to make atonement for them'): the whole sacrificial system serves the atonement function — maintaining the community's covenant access to YHWH.
45.16: kol-ha'am ha'aretz yihyu el-haterumah hazot ('all the people of the land shall be involved in this contribution'): the communal nature of the sacred contribution — it is not merely an individual religious duty but a collective provision for the national worship system. El-hanasi beYisrael ('for the prince in Israel'): the people fund the prince's obligatory offerings, and the prince provides the national worship. The economic triangle — people → prince → sanctuary → people — structures the covenant community's liturgical life.
45.17: ve'al-hanasi yihyeh ha'olot vehamminchah vehanasekh ('and upon the prince shall be the burnt offerings and the grain offerings and the drink offerings'): the prince's specific liturgical obligations — funding all the burnt offerings, grain offerings, drink offerings, sin offerings, and peace offerings for national festivals, Sabbaths, new moons, and set times. Lechapper bead beit Yisrael ('to make atonement for the house of Israel'): the prince's role is ultimately intercessory — his funding of worship maintains the national relationship with YHWH.
45.18: koh amar Adonai YHWH barishon be'echad lachodesh ('thus says the Lord YHWH: in the first month, on the first day of the month'): the priestly calendar begins. The year-opening purification offering — taking a bull without blemish (par ben-baqar tamim) for the sin offering. Vechitta et-hammiqdash ('and you shall purify the sanctuary'): chitta (Piel of chata', to purify/decontaminate) — the sanctuary itself requires regular purification, not because YHWH is polluted but because the accumulated impurities of the worshipping community need periodic liturgical cleansing.
45.19: velaqach hakohen middam hachatta't ('and the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering'): the blood application ritual — applying the sin-offering blood to the doorposts, the four corners of the altar ledge, and the doorposts of the inner court gate. The blood marks the sacred threshold as under the protection of the atonement sacrifice, extending the cleansing function beyond the altar itself to the entire sanctuary boundary.
45.20: veken ta'aseh beshiv'ah lachodesh me'ish shogeg ume'peti ('and so you shall do on the seventh of the month for those who sin unintentionally and for those who are naive'): the seventh-day purification ritual. Shogeg (one who sins inadvertently/unintentionally) — the category of non-deliberate sin covered by the Levitical purification system (Num 15:27-29). The twice-yearly purification (first and seventh days of the first month) covers the accumulated unintentional sins of the community.
45.21: barishon bearba'ah asar yom lachodesh yihyeh lachem haPesach ('in the first month on the fourteenth day shall be the Passover for you'): the restoration-temple Passover — on the same date as the original (Ex 12:6). Chametz (leaven) absent from the festival — shiv'at yamim matzot ye'achel ('seven days of unleavened bread shall be eaten'). The Passover as the foundational covenant-memorial is maintained unchanged in the new temple order — the Exodus remains the paradigmatic salvation event even in the eschatological restoration.
45.22: ve'asah hanasi bayom hahu ('and the prince shall prepare on that day'): the prince's Passover role — providing a bull as a sin offering for himself and all the people. The prince's intercession on behalf of the whole community is explicit: he atones for himself (bado) and for all the people (bead kol-am ha'aretz). The priestly-intercessory dimension of the nasi function is most visible at the great festivals.
45.23: ushiv'at yamim ha'chag ya'aseh olah laYHWH ('and for seven days of the feast he shall prepare a burnt offering for YHWH'): the Passover week offerings — seven bulls and seven rams daily, without blemish (tamim). Lechatta't sa'ir izim echad layom ('and for a sin offering a male goat daily'): the goat sin offering maintained throughout the festival week.
