The magnitude of the Passover offering — seven perfect bulls and seven perfect rams daily for seven days plus a goat — reflects the weight of the atonement the festival is accomplishing for the whole community.
45.24: uminchah efah lapar ve'efah la'ayil ('and as a grain offering an ephah for the bull and an ephah for the ram'): the proportionate grain accompaniment to the animal sacrifices. Vehin shemen la'efah ('and a hin of oil per ephah'): the oil portion. The precision of the measurements reflects the covenant exactitude — the liturgical calendar is not improvised but ordered according to divine specification.
45.25: beshivi'i bechamisha asar yom lachodesh bechag ('in the seventh month, on the fifteenth day of the month, at the feast'): the Feast of Booths (Sukkot), though not named as such. Ke'eleh ya'aseh shiv'at hayamim ('according to these he shall do for seven days'): the symmetry between Passover (first month, 14th) and Booths (seventh month, 15th) is maintained — two seven-day festivals with identical offerings, creating a liturgical year structured around the two great redemptive memories (Exodus from Egypt / wilderness wandering). Kachatta't ke'olah ukhaminchah vekhasshamen: all offerings identical to Passover.

46.1: koh amar Adonai YHWH sha'ar hechatzer hapnimit ('thus says the Lord YHWH: the gate of the inner court'): the gate regulations for the east gate of the inner court (not the outer east gate of 44:1-2). The six working days/two exceptional days principle: sheshet yamei hama'aseh yisager ('on the six days of work it shall be shut') and veyom hashabbat yippatach ('but on the day of Sabbath it shall be opened') — the Sabbath and New Moon as the days of open access to the inner court.
46.2: uva hanasi derekh ulam hasha'ar mibachutz ('and the prince shall enter by the vestibule of the gate from outside'): the prince's specific entry route. Ve'amad al-mezuzat hasha'ar ('he shall stand at the doorpost of the gate'): the prince as a liminal figure — standing at the threshold between common and sacred space. He does not enter the sanctuary but stands at its gate. The priests prepare his offerings; he oversees from the threshold. His Sabbath and New Moon worship is described in detail (vv. 4-7).
46.3: vehishtachavu am ha'aretz petach hasha'ar hazeh ('and the people of the land shall worship at the entrance of that gate'): the congregation worships at the outer gate while the prince stands at the inner gate. The concentric model of sacred access: prince closer to the inner court than the people, but neither entering the sanctuary itself. The spatial theology of access matches covenantal proximity: the people gather at the outer perimeter of sacred space.
46.4: vehha'olah asher-yaqrib hanasi laYHWH beyom hashabbat ('and the burnt offering that the prince shall bring to YHWH on the Sabbath day'): six lambs and a ram without blemish — the Sabbath offering in the new temple order. The Sabbath offering increases dramatically from the Torah's two lambs (Num 28:9-10) to six lambs plus a ram — the eschatological intensification of Sabbath worship. Tamim (without blemish) governs all the animals.
46.5: vehamminchah la'ayil ('and the grain offering for the ram'): the grain accompaniment. Ve'likhvasim minchah matnat yado ('and for the lambs, a grain offering as his hand gives freely'): matnat yado (the gift of his hand) — a freewill element in the specified quantity. The fixed animal offerings have a flexible grain component. Vehin shemen la'efah: the oil portion consistently specified.
46.6: uveyom hachodesh par ben-baqar tamim ('and on the day of the new moon, a young bull without blemish'): the New Moon offerings — a bull, six lambs, and a ram. The New Moon (Rosh Chodesh) is elevated to an occasion of substantial sacrifice alongside the Sabbath. Sheishet kevasim ve'ayil tamim yihyu: the animal list. The New Moon as a covenant calendar marker is given structural importance in the new temple order.
46.7: uminchah efah lapar ('and a grain offering — an ephah for the bull'): the proportionate grain offerings. The flexibility noted in v.5 — matnat yado for the lambs — reappears here. The prescribed quantities for bulls and rams are fixed; for lambs the grain offering is proportionate to what the prince can give. The freewill element within the regulated system balances obligation and devotion.
46.8: uvebo hanasi derekh ulam hasha'ar yavo ('and when the prince enters, he shall enter by the vestibule of the gate'): the specific path for the prince's entry and exit — always through the vestibule (ulam). Uvedarko yetze' ('and by the same way he shall go out'): unlike the general worshippers (vv. 9-10) who enter one gate and exit another, the prince enters and exits by the same route — perhaps indicating his distinctive mediating role between the people and the sanctuary.
46.9: uvevo am ha'aretz lifnei YHWH bammo'adim ('when the people of the land come before YHWH at the appointed feasts'): the one-directional flow rule for the people at festivals — entry through the north gate means exit through the south gate, and vice versa. Yotze derekh-sha'ar nagevu ('shall go out by the way of the south gate'). The traffic management of worshippers prevents congestion and ensures orderly, reverent movement through sacred space. The directional regulation also has symbolic weight: forward movement through the sanctuary, not retreat.
46.10: vehannasi betocham bevo'am yavo uvetzetam yetze'u ('and the prince, among them — when they enter, he enters, and when they go out, he goes out'): the prince as the representative member of the worshipping community. He does not precede or follow the people as a separate dignitary but moves with them (betocham, among them). The communal nature of the prince's worship is emphasized — he is the first among worshippers, not a separate liturgical class.
46.11: uvachagim uva'mo'adim tihyeh hamincha efah lapar ('and at festivals and at appointed feasts, the grain offering shall be an ephah for the bull'): the festival grain-offerings specification repeated for the general festival context. The grain offerings follow the same structure as the specific festival prescriptions in chapter 45 — a unified system across all sacred occasions. Matnat yado for the lambs again — the freewill element in the lamb-grain offering preserved.
46.12: uchi ya'aseh hanasi nedavah ('and when the prince makes a freewill offering'): the freewill offering (nedavah — from nadav, to volunteer/give spontaneously) is specified separately from the required offerings. The east inner gate is opened specifically for the prince's freewill offering; after he exits the gate is closed again. The opening and closing of the gate for the freewill offering signals that spontaneous devotion, not merely calendar obligation, has its own access to the sacred space.
46.13: keves ben-shnato tamim ta'aseh olah laYHWH laboqer boqer ('a lamb a year old without blemish you shall prepare as a burnt offering to YHWH morning by morning'): the daily burnt offering — one lamb each morning, not the two lambs of Numbers 28:3-4. The evening offering (tamid) is omitted in Ezekiel's vision — only the morning daily. This has generated discussion: perhaps the vision is selective, emphasizing a continuous morning orientation toward YHWH rather than prescribing the complete Levitical daily rhythm.
46.14: uminchah ta'aseh alav baboqer ('and a grain offering you shall prepare with it in the morning'): the accompanying grain offering — a sixth of an ephah of fine flour (solet) with a third of a hin of oil. Mincha tamid olat tamid laYHWH ('a continual grain offering, a continual burnt offering for YHWH'): the tamid (continual) characterizes both offerings — the daily morning worship is perpetual, not occasional. The rhythm of boqer boqer (morning by morning) establishes the tempo of the new temple's liturgical life.
46.15: ya'asu et-hakeves ve'et-hamminchah ve'et-hashemen baboqer boqer ('they shall prepare the lamb and the grain offering and the oil — morning by morning'): the triple repetition of the morning-orientation confirms the continuous daily worship at the heart of the new temple order. The eschatological temple has a daily rhythm that is simple (one lamb), perpetual (tamid), and consistently oriented toward the morning — the beginning of each new day acknowledged as YHWH's gift.
46.16: koh amar Adonai YHWH ki-yitten hanasi mattana la'echad mivanav ('thus says the Lord YHWH: if the prince gives a gift to any of his sons'): the inheritance principle for the prince's land. Gifts to sons become their permanent inheritance (achuztam hi birushasha lahem). But gifts to servants can only be held until the jubilee year (shnat haderut), after which they revert. The jubilee principle from Leviticus 25 is maintained in the new order: land cannot be permanently alienated through servitude.
46.17: ve'im-yitten mattana me'nachalato le'echad me'avadav ('but if he gives a gift from his inheritance to one of his servants'): the servant case. Vehayetah lo ad-shnat haderut veshavah lanasi ('it shall be his until the year of release/jubilee and shall return to the prince'): the jubilee reversion — the servant does not permanently acquire the prince's land. Ach nachalato lava'nav tihyeh lahem ('only his inheritance shall be for his sons to them'): the prince's land is held in trust for his descendants, not permanently dispersible through gift to servants. This provision prevents the prince from accumulating or dispersing land in ways that destabilize the tribal land inheritance.
46.18: velo-yiqach hanasi min-nachala ha'am ('and the prince shall not take from the people's inheritance'): the direct prohibition on the Naboth's-vineyard type of royal confiscation. Lehona'am me'achuzatam ('to oppress them out of their possession'): hona (to oppress, especially economically) — the specific verb for the economic exploitation the Israelite kings regularly practiced. Me'achuztam ('from their possession'): each family's ancestral land holding is protected from royal appropriation. The prince's portion is specified (45:7-8) precisely so he will not need to take from others.
46.19: vayvi'eni bemavo asher al-ketef hasha'ar el-leshkot haQodesh ('and he brought me through the entrance that was at the side of the gate into the priestly chambers'): the vision guide brings Ezekiel to the temple kitchens. The two sets of kitchen facilities — for the priests (vv. 19-20) and for the Levitical servants (vv. 21-24) — reflect the two-tier sacred service structure of chapters 44-45.
46.20: zeh hamaqom asher yevashelu sham hakohanim et-ha'asham ve'et-hachatta't ('this is the place where the priests shall boil the guilt offering and the sin offering'): the sacred kitchen where the priests' portion of the guilt and sin offerings is cooked. Veyafeu et-hammincha leBelti hotzi el-hechatzer hachitzona ('and where they shall bake the grain offering, in order not to bring them out to the outer court'): the isolation principle — the offerings that belong to the priests must be cooked and eaten within the priestly zone. The holiness of the sacrificial food must not be allowed to 'sanctify the people' by contact — the same principle as the garment-holiness of 44:19.
46.21: vatze'eni el-hechatzer hachitzona ('and he brought me out to the outer court'): transition from the priestly inner area to the outer court, where the Levitical servants' kitchen facilities are located. Four corner areas in the outer court, each with a small enclosed court. Bechot yot hechatzer chatzerot qetanot ('in the corners of the court were small courts'): the four enclosed areas symmetrically placed.
46.22: arba machzurot bearbaat pinnot hechatzer ('four joined/enclosed courts in the four corners of the court'): machzurot (enclosed/walled courts) — the small kitchen areas. Arba'im orech ushEloshim rochav midda achat learba'tam ('forty long and thirty wide, one measure for all four'): uniform size, symmetrical placement. The architectural regularity of the sacred space is a consistent feature of Ezekiel's temple vision — perfect symmetry expressing divine order.
46.23: turo sav lahem saviv learba'tam ('a course of stone was around them for all four'): the construction detail of the kitchen enclosures. Umevushillot asuyot tachat haturim saviv ('and cooking-hearths made under the courses/ledges around'): the cooking facilities built into the stone structure. The detail suggests functional kitchens fully integrated into the architectural plan — worship includes the physical preparation of food, and that preparation has its proper designated space.
46.24: vayomer elai eleh beit hamevashllim asher yevashelu sham meshartei habayit et-zevach ha'am ('and he said to me: these are the houses of boiling where the servants of the house shall boil the sacrifice of the people'): the Levitical servants cook the people's portions of the peace offerings here. The chapter — and the liturgical regulation section of the temple vision — closes with a picture of shared communal meal: priests eating their portions of sin and guilt offerings in the inner priestly kitchen, people's peace offerings cooked in the outer Levitical kitchen, the entire community sustained by the sacrificial system that YHWH has ordained. The temple is not merely a ritual performance space but a place of communal feasting in the presence of YHWH.

47.1: vayeshiveni el-petach habayit vehineh mayim yotzeim mitachat miftan habayit qadimah: 'He brought me back to the entrance of the house, and behold, water was flowing out from below the threshold of the house toward the east.' The temple river (nahal) originates beneath the temple threshold — specifically the south side of the threshold at the south side of the altar. The source is the sanctuary itself, not a natural spring. This is the theological crucial point: the life-giving water does not arise from the land or any natural source but from YHWH's own dwelling place. The direction — east (qadimah) — is eschatologically significant: it flows toward the Dead Sea (the Arabah), the most desolate region of the land, and toward the sunrise, the direction from which the divine glory returned (43:1-4).
47.2: vayotzieni derech sha'ar tzafon: 'He brought me out by way of the north gate.' The guide leads Ezekiel out through the north gate and around the outside to the outer east gate, where he sees the water trickling (mephakkim) from the south side. The detail that Ezekiel must exit through the north gate and go around — rather than passing through the closed east gate — is consistent with 44:1-2 (the east gate is shut because YHWH has entered by it). The river flows past the east gate and away.
47.3: beyetzet ha'ish qadim uqav biyado vayamad elef amot: 'As the man went out eastward with a measuring line in his hand, he measured a thousand cubits.' The four measurements of 1,000 cubits each (vv. 3-5) create a fourfold progressive deepening: echel-hamaim (ankle-water), maim birkayim (knee-water), maim motnayim (hip/waist-water), then impassable swimming depth. The 1,000-cubit intervals — approximately 500 meters — mark a journey of 4 kilometers from the temple, with each stage representing an exponential increase in the river's volume with no upstream tributaries visible. The quantitative impossibility signals the river's supernatural, divine character.
47.4: vayamad elef vaya'avereni bamayim maim birkayim: 'He measured another thousand, and led me through the water — it was knee-deep.' The progression from ankle (echel) to knee (birkayim) continues. The personal nature of the journey — being led (va'avereni = he brought me through) — means Ezekiel experiences each stage physically, wading deeper. The incremental wading enacts the reader's increasing immersion in the vision's blessing: each step deeper is a step into greater divine abundance.
47.5: vayamad elef nahal lo-uchal la'avor: 'He measured another thousand, and it was a river I could not cross.' The climax: what began as a trickle is now an impassable river (nahal lo-uchal la'avor = a river that cannot be crossed/forded). Gavehu hamayim maie schia (the waters had risen to swimming level) — the water that was trickling under the south threshold is now a great river requiring swimming. The quantitative impossibility is the point: no natural spring multiplies 1000-fold every 500 meters. This is creation-power applied to land renewal — YHWH's own life flowing out through his temple to heal the most cursed landscape on earth.
47.6: vayomer elai hen-raita ben-adam: 'He said to me: Son of man, have you seen?' The rhetorical question invites Ezekiel — and through him the exilic audience — to register the wonder. The guide's question is not information-seeking but attention-directing: have you seen this? The pause at the riverbank before the explanation forces a moment of pure wonder before the theological articulation of what has been seen.
47.7: beshuveni vehineh el-sefat hanachal etzim rabbim me'od: 'When I returned, I saw on the banks of the river very many trees on both sides.' The trees on both banks of the river are identified in v. 12 as fruit trees whose leaves do not wither and that bear fruit every month. Before the explanation comes the visual impression — an abundance of lush trees lining both banks of the river flowing through the Arabah's barren landscape. The me'od (very much/exceedingly) emphasizes the profusion: not a few trees but a forest of them.
47.8: vayomer elai hamayim haeleh yotzeim el-gelilah haqadmonit veyardu el-haAravah: 'He said to me: These waters flow toward the eastern region and go down into the Arabah and enter the sea.' The destination is precise: the Aravah (the Jordan Valley rift below sea level, site of the Dead Sea) and hayyam hammutzaim (the sea that is brought out = the Dead Sea, so called because it is the stagnant terminal basin). Umayim yeraphe'u (and the waters will be healed/made fresh) — raphe (to heal) is the medical term for restoration to health. The healing of the Dead Sea is the ultimate geographical reversal: the most salt-saturated, lifeless water body on earth made fresh and teeming with life by the temple river.
47.9: vehaya kol-nefesh chaya asher yishrots el kol asher yavo sham hanachalaim yichyeh: 'Every living creature that swarms, wherever the river goes, will live.' Nefesh chaya (living creature) is the same phrase used in Gen 1:20-21 and 2:7 for the living creatures of creation. The river creates new bara-level life wherever it flows — it is a creation event, not merely irrigation. Vehayah ha-dagah rabbah meod (the fish will be very many) — the Dead Sea, currently devoid of fish due to extreme salinity, will teem with fish in every variety (ke-dagat hayam hagadol harbeh meod = like the fish of the Great [Mediterranean] Sea, very many).
47.10: ve'amdu alav daugim me-En-Gedi ve'ad En-Eglaim mishtoach lachemotayim: 'Fishermen will stand beside it from En-gedi to En-eglaim; it will be a place for spreading nets.' En-gedi (a spring-oasis on the western Dead Sea shore) and En-eglaim (probably at the northern end) mark the entire length of the Dead Sea's western shore. The Dead Sea — currently having no fishermen because it has no fish — will support commercial fishing operations along its entire length. Leminehah tiheye (each kind in its kind) — the taxonomic completeness of the fish population emphasizes the total renewal: every category of fish, not merely a few hardy species.
47.11: biztzetav uvge'avav lo' yeraphe'u lamelach nittanu: 'But its swamps and marshes will not become fresh; they will be given over to salt.' This exception is theologically interesting: not everything is healed. The swamps and marshes at the Dead Sea's margins remain salty and will be used for salt production. Salt has covenant significance (Num 18:19; Lev 2:13: “covenant of salt”) and practical necessity. The exception preserves salt resources while still transforming the main sea body. It also introduces an element of eschatological realism: the new creation preserves functional continuity with present creation rather than annihilating it.
47.12: ve'al hanachal ya'aleh el-sifato mizeh umizeh kol-etz-mazon: 'On the banks of the river, on both sides, will grow all kinds of food trees.' Etz-mazon (food/nourishment trees) — not ornamental but edible trees, trees that feed. The three characteristics (lo yinbol alehav velo yitom piryov = leaves will not wither, fruit will not fail) reverse the curse on creation (Gen 3:17-19: thorns, thistles, painful toil). Lechadashehem yivkeh ki meimaiv min-hamiqdash hemah yotz'im (they will bear fresh fruit each month, because their water flows from the sanctuary) — monthly fruit (not seasonal) and evergreen leaves are the marks of the eschatological Eden-restoration. Revelation 22:2 quotes this verse directly for the new creation.
47.13: koh amar adonai YHWH ze gevul asher titnahaluh et-haarets: 'Thus says the Lord YHWH: This is the boundary by which you shall apportion the land as inheritance.' The transition from the visionary river to the land-allotment legislation (nachalah = inheritance, ancestral possession) is abrupt but theologically coherent: the river vision establishes the life-giving character of the land to which the tribes will return. Yosef chavalim (Joseph shall have two portions/lots) — this gives Ephraim and Manasseh each their own portion in the land, fulfilling Jacob's adoption of Joseph's sons in Gen 48:5.
47.14: unechaltem oto ish kechiv: 'You shall divide it equally, each the same as his brother.' The equal distribution (ish kechiv = one like his neighbor, equally) removes the tribal inequalities of the historical settlement (where Judah received disproportionately large territory and some tribes received inadequate portions). Nasati et-yadi latheta otah laavoteichem (I raised my hand to give it to your ancestors) — the divine oath formula authenticates the allotment: this land distribution is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic covenant promise, not a new political arrangement.
47.15: vezeh gevul haaretz ligvul tzafon: 'This shall be the northern boundary of the land.' The northern boundary runs from the Mediterranean Sea (hayam hagadol) by the road of Hethlon toward Lebo-hamath and Zedad. This boundary is similar to but not identical with the historical land boundaries — it is a divinely specified ideal land, not a historical reconstruction. Lebo-Hamath (the entrance of Hamath) is the traditional northern marker of the Promised Land (Num 34:8; 1 Kgs 8:65).
47.16: Chamat Berota Sivrayim: 'Hamath, Berothah, Sibraim.' The precise place names — Berothah, Sibraim, Hazer-hatticon, Hauran — trace the northern boundary through the Damascus-Hamath region. The precision of the geographic specification (even if some locations are uncertain to modern scholars) communicates that the eschatological land is a real geographic space, not a purely spiritual abstraction. The vision's realism — actual place names, measurable dimensions, fishermen and farmers — is Ezekiel's insistence that YHWH's promises are materially concrete.
47.17: vehayah gevul min-hayam Chatzar-Einan: 'The boundary shall run from the sea to Hazar-enan.' The northern boundary terminates at Hazar-enan, on the northern edge of Damascus's border territory. Gevul Chamat tzafonah (with Hamath's border to the north) — the boundary is carefully calibrated to the east-west extent of the northern region. The land described is larger than any historically realized Israelite territory, corresponding to the ideal land promised to Abraham (Gen 15:18: from the Nile to the Euphrates) without being identical to it.
47.18: vepeat qadim mibeyn Hauran umibeyn Dammesheq: 'The east side: between Hauran and Damascus.' The Jordan River (hayarden) forms the eastern boundary from the north (Hauran/Damascus region) down to the eastern sea (the Dead Sea or Gulf of Aqaba). The use of the Jordan as eastern boundary reflects the historical land's eastern limit, though Transjordan is now included in the tribal allotments (in contrast to the historical settlement where the eastern tribes held Transjordanian territory as a secondary arrangement).
47.19: vepeat negev teiman mitamar ad-mei Merivat-Qadesh: 'The south side: from Tamar to the waters of Meribah-kadesh.' The southern boundary runs from Tamar (near En-gedi) through Meribah-kadesh (near Kadesh-barnea, the wilderness staging ground) to the Brook of Egypt (nachal Mitzraim = the Wadi el-Arish). This follows the traditional southern boundary of the Promised Land (Num 34:3-5). The inclusion of Meribah-kadesh (the place of Israel's rebellion: Num 20:13; Ps 95:8) in the restored land's boundary marks the redemption of a place associated with faithlessness.
47.20: vepeat yam hayam hagadol miggevul ad-nokhach levo-Chamat: 'The west side: the Great Sea [Mediterranean] from the southern boundary to the entrance of Hamath.' The western boundary is the simplest: the Mediterranean coastline from south to north. The land's four boundaries — north (Hamath region), east (Jordan), south (Negev/Sinai), west (Mediterranean) — form a comprehensive and generous land grant that exceeds historical Israel's settled territory and fulfills the Abrahamic promise of a great land from the river to the sea.
47.21: uchalqetem et-haarets hazot lachem leshivtei Yisrael: 'You shall divide this land among yourselves according to the tribes of Israel.' The distribution is tribal — the twelve-tribe structure of Israel is preserved in the eschatological land allotment, connecting the restoration to the foundational covenant structure (the twelve sons of Jacob, cf. Gen 49). The land is for the tribes of Israel, but vv. 22-23 immediately extend this.
47.22: vehaya tachelqu otah benahalah lachem ulgerim hagarim betochechem: 'You shall apportion it as an inheritance for yourselves and for the resident aliens who live among you.' The inclusion of the ger (resident alien, sojourner) in the tribal inheritance is a significant extension of the historical Torah's treatment of aliens. While the Torah protected aliens and required equal justice for them, it did not give them tribal land inheritance. Here, aliens who have had children in Israel (asher holedu vanim betochechem) receive land like native Israelites (vegeru batochem yihyu lachem ke'ezrach). This is the eschatological fulfillment of the universal reach of covenant blessing.
47.23: vehaya bashevet asher gar hasham haager titenu nachalato: 'In whatever tribe the sojourner resides, there you shall give him his inheritance.' The alien's inheritance is located with the specific tribe where he lives — not a separate zone but integrated into tribal territory. Ne'um adonai YHWH — the divine declaration seals the unprecedented provision. This prophetic extension of covenant inheritance to resident aliens anticipates Paul's argument in Galatians and Ephesians that Gentiles in Christ are fellow-heirs and members of the covenant household.

48.1: ve'eleh shemot hashvatim miqtzei tzafon: 'These are the names of the tribes: beginning from the north.' The tribal allotments are described from north to south in strips running the full width of the land from the eastern to the western border (mipeat qadimah el-yam = from the east side to the sea). The northernmost tribes (Dan, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Reuben, Judah) are placed north of the sacred central zone; the southern tribes (Benjamin, Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun, Gad) are placed south of it. The arrangement differs significantly from the historical tribal territory allocation in Joshua.
48.2: ve'al gevul Dan mipeat qadimah ad-peat yamah Asher echad: 'Adjoining Dan's territory, from east to west: Asher, one portion.' The formula mipeat qadimah ad-peat yamah (from the east side to the sea side) applies to every tribal allotment — each tribe receives a horizontal strip spanning the full width of the land. This equal-width distribution replaces the historical irregular allotments with geometrically equal portions, expressing the divine equity that characterizes the eschatological order.
48.3: ve'al gevul Asher Naphtali echad: 'Adjoining Asher: Naphtali, one portion.' The formulaic repetition of the allotment descriptions — each tribe receiving one portion, each described in the same brief formula — emphasizes the equality of the distribution. The tribal order (Dan, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Reuben, Judah from north to south) is not the birth order of the sons of Jacob but a divinely specified arrangement that places Judah immediately adjacent to the sacred central zone.
48.4: ve'al gevul Naphtali Menasheh echad: 'Adjoining Naphtali: Manasseh, one portion.' Manasseh is placed above Ephraim, reversing the birth-order blessing of Gen 48:14-19 where Jacob crossed his hands to give Ephraim (the younger) the greater blessing. In Ezekiel's eschatological arrangement, the historical reversals are resolved: Manasseh occupies a portion slightly north of Ephraim (his younger brother), and both receive equal single portions rather than the double portion implied by Joseph's double-inheritance.
48.5: ve'al gevul Menasheh Ephraim echad: 'Adjoining Manasseh: Ephraim, one portion.' Ephraim — the northern kingdom's leading tribe after the split, associated with the Israelite apostasy Ezekiel had condemned — receives a portion in the restored land alongside Judah. The inclusion of the northern tribes in the eschatological allotment is the fulfillment of the reunification promise of 37:15-22 (the two sticks joined into one in Ezekiel's hand).
48.6: ve'al gevul Ephraim Reuven echad: 'Adjoining Ephraim: Reuben, one portion.' Reuben, the firstborn who lost his preeminence (Gen 49:3-4; Deut 33:6), receives a full portion equal to all other tribes. The historical diminishment of Reuben's significance (the tribe became numerically small and historically marginal) is reversed in the eschatological allotment — primogeniture curses are dissolved in the new order.
48.7: ve'al gevul Reuven Yehudah echad: 'Adjoining Reuben: Judah, one portion.' Judah is placed immediately north of the sacred central zone (the terumah). This is the most theologically significant placement: Judah as the royal-messianic tribe (Gen 49:10) flanks the sacred precinct on its north side, as Benjamin (the tribe of the Jerusalem temple's historical location) flanks it on the south. The arrangement places the two most significant covenant tribes on either side of YHWH's dwelling place.
48.8: ve'al gevul Yehudah mipeat qadimah ad-peat yamah tihyeh haterumah: 'Adjoining Judah, from east to west, shall be the contribution [sacred portion].' Terumah (contribution, elevated/lifted offering) is the term for the sacred central strip. The same width as the tribal allotments (east to west) but of specified length (25,000 cubits wide). The sanctuary (hamiqdash) is at the center of this sacred zone. The word terumah for land — normally used for grain offerings lifted to YHWH — applies the concept of the elevated gift to sacred territory: this central strip is Israel's land-offering to YHWH.
48.9: haterumah asher tarimun laYHWH orech esrim ve'chamesh elef: 'The offering you shall set apart for YHWH shall be twenty-five thousand cubits in length.' The sacred portion measures 25,000 × 20,000 cubits (approximately 12.5 × 10 km). Within this zone the priests' territory (25,000 × 10,000), the Levites' territory (25,000 × 10,000), and the city territory (25,000 × 5,000) are arranged in horizontal strips. The sacred central zone is the architectural heart of the entire land distribution — everything else is organized around it.
48.10: uleileh tihyeh terumah haqodesh lakohanim: 'For the priests shall be the holy contribution.' The priestly allocation — 25,000 × 10,000 cubits — has YHWH's sanctuary (miqdash YHWH) at its center (betoch). The Zadokite priests receive the central strip closest to the sanctuary. The physical geography of the sacred zone mirrors the holiness hierarchy: the sanctuary at the center, surrounded by the priests' territory, surrounded by the Levites' territory, with the city on the south perimeter.
48.11: lakohanim hamequdash mibeni Tzadoq: 'For the consecrated priests, the sons of Zadok.' The explicit designation of the Zadokite priests (beni Tzadoq) as the holders of the central priestly territory is the eschatological vindication of their covenant faithfulness. Asher shamru et-mishmarti (who kept my charge) — the historical Zadokites remained loyal during Israel's apostasy when “the Levites went astray” (44:10-15). The land allotment permanently memorializes the principle: faithfulness in the historical order carries forward into the eschatological order.
48.12: vehayeta lahem terumiyah min-terumiat haaretz: 'It shall be theirs as a special portion within the land's contribution.' The priestly portion is a terumiyah (a lifted portion within the larger terumah) — the most holy section (qodesh qodashim) of the entire land. The spatial language of elevation (terum-) applied to the priestly land mirrors the sacrificial language of elevated offerings: just as the choicest parts of the sacrifice are lifted to YHWH, the choicest portion of the land is reserved for his sanctuary servants.
48.13: velaleviim kemodat hakohanim: 'And the Levites shall have a portion alongside the priests.' The Levites receive an equivalent strip (25,000 × 10,000 cubits), equal in size to the priestly portion but immediately adjacent to it rather than centering the sanctuary. The total sacred zone width is thus 20,000 cubits (priests 10,000 + Levites 10,000), with the city's 5,000-cubit strip making the complete 25,000-cubit terumah.
48.14: velo yimkeru mimenu velo yamiru velo ya'avir reshit haaretz: 'They shall not sell or exchange any of it; they shall not transfer the prime portion of the land.' The prohibition against selling the sacred land (lo yimkeru, lo yamiru, lo ya'avir reshit haaretz ki qodesh laYHWH) is an eschatological version of the Jubilee land laws (Lev 25:23: “the land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine”). The sacred central zone is permanently inalienable — it belongs to YHWH, and the use of it by priests and Levites is stewardship, not ownership.
48.15: vachamesh alafim hanot'rim barochav: 'The remaining five thousand cubits in width.' The city strip (25,000 × 5,000 cubits) is for chol (common/profane use) — housing, open pasture, and the city itself. The distinction chol (common) versus qodesh (holy) is not a spiritual demotion but a functional category: the city zone serves the practical needs of Israel's population rather than the specifically cultic functions of the priestly and Levitical zones.
48.16: ve'eleh midoteiha peat tzafon chamesh meot ve'arba elafim: 'These shall be the city's measurements: on the north, 4,500 [cubits].' The eschatological city is a perfect square — 4,500 × 4,500 cubits on all four sides. The perfect-square geometry (arbaat tefachim) matches the inner court's perfect square (47:47) and anticipates the New Jerusalem's four-square description in Rev 21:16. The mathematical precision of the city's dimensions communicates divine perfection — this is not a human city laid out by planners but YHWH's ideal city.
48.17: vehayah migrash ha'ir tzafon me'atayim vachamishim: 'The city shall have open land: 250 cubits on each side.' The migrash (open land, pastureland) around the city is 250 cubits on all four sides — a buffer zone between the city proper and the agricultural land. This open land provision is consistent with Levitical city regulations (Num 35:4-5) where a 2,000-cubit open area surrounded Levitical towns. The city is not crowded to its boundaries but has breathing space — a mark of abundance and order in the divine plan.
48.18: vehaNot'ar beorekh leumat terumah haqodesh: 'What remains in length alongside the holy portion.' The agricultural land flanking the city — 10,000 cubits east and 10,000 cubits west — will produce food for the city's workers (avodat ha'ir). The integration of agricultural productivity with the sacred zone and city ensures that the eschatological community is self-sustaining: the land itself supports the city that serves the sanctuary. There is no tension between sacred and agricultural space — they are architecturally integrated.
48.19: ve'ovdei ha'ir min-kol-shivtei Yisrael ya'avduha: 'The workers of the city shall cultivate it — workers from all the tribes of Israel.' The city's agricultural land is worked by representatives from all twelve tribes, not by a single tribe. This inter-tribal character of the city workforce mirrors the twelve gates named after all twelve tribes (vv. 31-34) — the eschatological city belongs equally to all Israel, transcending the historical tribal divisions that had fragmented the covenant community.
48.20: kol-haterumah esrim vachamesh elef besrim vachamesh elef ravia'it taqrivu: 'The entire offering is 25,000 by 25,000 cubits — a square.' The complete terumah zone is a perfect square: 25,000 × 25,000 cubits. This perfect square encompasses the priestly territory, Levitical territory, and city territory together. The geometric perfection of the sacred zone — a square within the land's horizontal strips — creates a visually centered structure: the perfect square of YHWH's dwelling in the middle of the horizontal tribal strips stretching from sea to Jordan.
48.21: vehanot'ar lanasi: 'What remains shall be for the prince.' The nasi (prince, leader — the royal figure who is not called melech/king in the eschatological vision, cf. 34:24; 37:25) receives the territory on both sides of the sacred zone — east of it to the eastern border and west of it to the Mediterranean. The prince's territory flanks the entire sacred zone on both sides. This arrangement ensures that the prince's land is defined relative to YHWH's sanctuary, not the reverse — YHWH's dwelling is primary, the prince's territory secondary.
48.22: umitoch achuzat halevi'im umitoch achuzat ha'ir betoch asher lanasi: 'From between the Levites' possession and the city's possession — in the middle of the prince's territory.' The Judah-Benjamin boundary section that contains the sacred zone and city falls within the prince's territory, but the sacred portions are excluded from his ownership. This architectural arrangement prevents the king from appropriating the sacred zone — a structural safeguard against the kind of royal abuse of sacred space that characterized the historical monarchy (cf. the complaint of 43:7-9 against the kings' proximity to YHWH's threshold).
48.23: veha'shet hashvatim hanot'rim: 'As for the remaining tribes.' The southern tribes (Benjamin through Gad, vv. 23-27) occupy the strips south of the sacred central zone. Benjamin is placed immediately south of the sacred zone — the historical location of Jerusalem's earliest territory — giving Benjamin the honor of flanking the divine dwelling place from the south as Judah does from the north.
48.24: ve'al gevul Binyamin mipeat qadimah ad-peat yamah Shim'on echad: 'Adjoining Benjamin, from east to west: Simeon, one portion.' Simeon — historically the tribe that dispersed within Judah's territory (Josh 19:1; Gen 49:7: “I will divide them in Jacob”) — receives its own defined territory in the eschatological order. The reversal of Jacob's curse on Simeon (Gen 49:5-7) in the restored order is one of the subtle signals that the eschatological allotment is not merely political reorganization but covenant renewal that undoes historical judgments.
48.25: ve'al gevul Shim'on Yissachar echad: 'Adjoining Simeon: Issachar, one portion.' Issachar and Zebulun (v. 26) — the two tribes of Leah's sons who are historically associated with the northern region — are placed in the south in Ezekiel's eschatological arrangement. The tribal placement throughout (north and south of the sacred zone) does not simply replicate historical geography but creates a new order centered entirely on the sanctuary, with tribes repositioned according to the logic of the new spatial center.
48.26: ve'al gevul Yissachar Zevulun echad: 'Adjoining Issachar: Zebulun, one portion.' Zebulun completes the series of Leah-tribe allotments. The placement of Issachar and Zebulun in the south is notable because Isa 9:1 specifically prophecies honor for “the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali” (the first territories darkened by Assyrian invasion) — a prophecy Matthew applies to Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matt 4:15-16). The Ezekielian placement of Zebulun and Naphtali on opposite ends of the tribal arrangement (Naphtali in the north, Zebulun in the south) reflects a different geographical logic.
48.27: ve'al gevul Zevulun Gad echad: 'Adjoining Zebulun: Gad, one portion.' Gad — historically a Transjordanian tribe (Josh 13:24-28) — receives the southernmost strip of the eschatological land. This placement replaces the Transjordanian wilderness territory of the historical Gadites with fertile Cisjordanian land within the sacred borders, completing the reversal of Transjordanian marginality. All twelve tribes are within the ideal land's borders, equally endowed with east-to-sea strips.
48.28: ve'al gevul Gad el-peat negev teimah vehayah gevul mitamar mei Merivat-Qadesh nachlah hayam hagadol: 'On the south side of Gad, the boundary runs from Tamar to the waters of Meribah-kadesh, then along the Brook of Egypt to the Great Sea.' The southern boundary of the entire land is confirmed through the description of Gad's southern border: from Tamar through Meribah-kadesh to the Wadi el-Arish and the Mediterranean. This matches the southern boundary of 47:19, closing the land description symmetrically.
48.29: zot haaretz asher tapilu benahalah leshivtei Yisrael: 'This is the land you shall distribute by lot as an inheritance among the tribes of Israel.' The summary formula closes the land allotment. Ve'eleh machlekotam ne'um adonai YHWH (these are their portions, declares the Lord YHWH) — the divine declaration authenticates the entire allotment as divinely specified. The present tense (tapilu = you shall allot) frames the allotment as a future act to be performed, not merely a vision of the past.
48.30: ve'eleh totzaot ha'ir mipeat tzafon: 'These shall be the exits/gates of the city on the north side.' Totzaot (exits, outlets) refers to the city gates, each named after a tribe. The northern wall has three gates (Reuben, Judah, Levi), the eastern wall three (Joseph, Benjamin, Dan), the southern wall three (Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun), and the western wall three (Gad, Asher, Naphtali) — twelve gates for twelve tribes. The naming of the city gates after all twelve tribes, including Levi (who had no land allotment in the historical settlement) and using Joseph (single name) rather than Ephraim/Manasseh to preserve the twelve, is the eschatological restoration of Israel's complete tribal identity.
48.31: vesha'arei ha'ir al-shemot shivtei Yisrael: 'The city's gates shall be named after the tribes of Israel.' The three northern gates are: gate of Reuben, gate of Judah, gate of Levi. Levi's inclusion in the city gates (rather than a territorial allotment) is consistent with the Levites' sacral role — they receive the sacred zone territory but also have a named gate of access to the city. Revelation 21:12 derives its twelve named gates from this passage, applying the Ezekielian vision to the New Jerusalem.
48.32: vepeat qadimah chamesh meot ve'arba alafim ushlosha sha'arim: 'On the east side: 4,500 cubits, three gates.' The eastern gates: Joseph, Benjamin, Dan. Joseph as a single name (not Ephraim and Manasseh separately) preserves the twelve-gate count while honoring Joseph's covenant significance. The arrangement of tribes around the four walls has no clear pattern derived from the Pentateuchal tribal groupings — it is a new arrangement for a new city.
48.33: vepeat negev chamesh meot ve'arba alafim midah ushlosha sha'arim: 'On the south side: 4,500 cubits in measurement, three gates.' The southern gates: Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun — three of the Leah-tribe sons. The distribution of Leah's sons across all four walls (Reuben and Judah on the north, Simeon/Issachar/Zebulun on the south) balances the matriarchal tribes around the city's perimeter.
48.34: peat yamah chamesh meot ve'arba alafim ushlosha sha'arim: 'The west side: 4,500 cubits, three gates.' The western gates: Gad, Asher, Naphtali — the sons of the handmaidens Zilpah and Bilhah. The placement of the handmaid-tribe sons on the western wall is the final element in the complete perimeter. Every tribe has a named gate; no tribe is excluded from access to the eschatological city. The walls' total perimeter (4 × 4,500 = 18,000 cubits) matches the explicit statement of v. 35.
48.35: saviv shmoneh asar elef vesheme ha'ir miyom YHWH shammah: 'The circumference shall be eighteen thousand cubits. And the name of the city from that day shall be: YHWH-Shammah.' YHWH shammah (YHWH Is There) is the climactic final verse of Ezekiel and the resolution of the book's central crisis. The book opened with the divine glory departing the defiled temple (chs. 8-11) — its entire theological energy has been directed toward the question of whether YHWH would return to dwell among his people. The answer is given in two words: YHWH Is There. The city name is not a human honorific but a divine state of affairs — YHWH's presence is the defining characteristic of the eschatological city. The NT's echo: Revelation 21:3 (“the dwelling place of God is with man”) and 22:3-4 (“the throne of God and of the Lamb will be in it, and his servants will worship him; they will see his face”) are both direct fulfillments of YHWH-Shammah.
